text: DEWCLAW LAND HOLDINGS LAND VALUATION COMPLETE METHODOLOGY HANDBOOK Deep Understanding Guide for Land Investment Comping Version 3.0 — April 2026 Target: Complete each property evaluation in 2.5 minutes Maximum efficiency. Maximum accuracy. No wasted motion.

TABLE OF CONTENTS I. Business Model & Core Strategy II. Efficiency Workflow & SOP (2.5-Minute Target) III. Land Type Identification IV. Comp Verification & Rules V. Acreage to PPA Relationship — The Exponential Curve VI. Comp Weighting — How To Think About It VII. DOM Interpretation VIII. Structures — Call Them Out, Don't Price Them IX. Flood Zone — Don't Panic, Look Around First XVI. Subdivision Shape Rule — Bowling Alley Lots XVII. Views / Elevation Premium XVIII. Power Lines & Utility Easements XIX. Timbered Land & Recently Logged XX. Actively Farmed Land XXI. Map Layers — Always Toggle XXII. When Seller Already Has It Listed XXIII. Cash vs Terms XXIV. County Boundaries — Never Cross XXV. Sparse Markets — Using What You Have XXVI. Proximity to Cities XXVII. Area Quality — Always Check XXVIII. Owner Multiple Properties XXIX. Market Health Metrics XXX. Zoning — Don't Overthink It XXXI. Pending Listings XXXII. Duplicate Subdivision Sales XXXIII. Creative Approaches XXXIV. Comp Quality Awareness XXXV. Comp Listing Photos Warning XXXVI. Seller Motivation Signals XXXVII. Value-Add Identification XXXVIII. Pricing Philosophy — Conservative At Every Step XXXIX. Deliverable Format
XL. Common Mistakes To Avoid XLI. Properties Evaluated — Lessons Learned

I. BUSINESS MODEL & CORE STRATEGY Buy vacant rural land at ~50% of market value. Sell slightly below market with elite drone marketing. Target 7–90 day sales (~2 weeks average). Primary markets: Mid-US to East Coast. Focus: Rural recreational land. The Strategic Purpose of Comping Primary goal: Know the market value so we can offer 50 cents on the dollar. Backup strategy: Value-add plays. If the seller says NO to the 50% offer on as-is value, we may have identified a value-add (subdivision potential, etc.). We pitch a DIFFERENT offer based on the value-add — 50% of the improved value — which might equal the seller's ask. The seller doesn't realize the upside; we unlock it. Example:  As-is market value: $100K → Offer $50K → Seller says NO, wants $80K  Subdivision potential identified: $160K after split  New pitch: Offer $80K (50% of $160K) = exactly what seller wanted  We still get 50% margin on the VALUE-ADD play Why we always look for value-adds: Even if we don't need it for the primary offer, it's the BACKUP strategy when sellers say no. Most sellers don't know their property can be subdivided. We unlock value they didn't know existed.

II. EFFICIENCY WORKFLOW & SOP [!] TARGET: 2.5 MINUTES PER PROPERTY — COMPLETE EVALUATION Every property evaluation — from first glance to final deliverable — should be completed in approximately 2.5 minutes. This is the standard operating pace. Speed comes from discipline, not shortcuts. Follow the workflow below without deviation, narration, or mid-process questions. Work silently, deliver the final product. Land Insights Speed Workflow  Summary tab: Last purchase price, acreage, APN  Parcel tab: Land use classification  Owner tab: Out-of-state/county status + ownership length  Immediately go to MLS comps. Don't linger. Land ID Simultaneous Workflow While pulling Land Insights data, simultaneously open Land ID:  AI Smart Search (blue button) → Parcel → ID → State/County/APN → Soils  Copy URL, paste in Extra Notes as 'Land ID link'  Backup: If APN doesn't aggregate, use Owner Name instead  If Land ID is blocked, note it and skip — don't waste time Street View — MANDATORY [!] Drag pegman onto Street View for EVERY property. No exceptions.  Look for: entryways/driveways, cleared building spots, mobile homes, sheds, structures  Wet areas/standing water, power lines, fencing  Note ALL findings in Extra Notes Satellite Verification Always look at actual satellite imagery on Google Maps AND Land Insights with filters OFF. Never trust filters alone. Trust your own eyes always. Map Layer Rules  Hazards: Keep ON by default. Only toggle OFF to verify clean pond/creek or see structures clearly.  MLS Comps: Stay ON. Don't toggle every time.  Acreage Range: Always click AUTO (not Manual). If need more comps: below=80%, above=150%. Do NOT go into Comp Report — use map view. Navigation & Speed  Navigate directly to zoom 11–12 URL for county comp view — do NOT

Acreage Range: Always click AUTO (not Manual). If need more comps: below=80%, above=150%. Do NOT go into Comp Report — use map view. Navigation & Speed  Navigate directly to zoom 11–12 URL for county comp view — do NOT scroll-zoom 4–5 times  Minimize screenshots — one for property data/insights, one at parcel zoom for land type/water, one at county zoom for comps. Three to four is enough.

 CLOSE ALL MCP TABS after EVERY comp. Delete the tab group completely so browser doesn't lag or crash.  Deliver full 8-section analysis in ONE response. NO 'continue' breaks. No mid-process narration. No asking user for permission. Timing Breakdown (2.5 Minutes Total)  Initial Assessment: ~45 seconds — Pull property in Land Insights, check acreage/APN/owner/flags, satellite + Street View  Comp Process: ~75 seconds — Pull comps county-wide, find anchor/floor/ceiling, verify key comps  Final Valuation & Deliverable: ~30 seconds — Weight comps, calculate MV, generate offer range, write deliverable

III. LAND TYPE IDENTIFICATION Farmland (Yielding Asset — Worth More) Actively farmed with visible crop rows, irrigation patterns, tilled soil, distinct field boundaries. This is a YIELDING ASSET — it produces income. The price of the land PLUS the value of the agricultural production is baked in. Worth significantly MORE than cleared or pasture land. Always identify and call out farmland — it commands a premium that other land types do not get. If you see crop rows from satellite, that is farmland. If there is an active farm lease, factor that into value. Farmers usually know what land is worth. Harder to get at deep discount because of income potential. Warn the investor that the seller may be more savvy on pricing. Pasture Land Open grass with fencing visible, possibly cattle trails, watering areas, livestock signs. Maintained but not actively cultivated. Worth less than farmland but more than raw timbered land. Key identifiers: fencing (visible on satellite), grazed grass areas (not just mowed), sometimes visible livestock or water troughs. Maintained Cleared Land — THE TRICKY ONE [!] This distinction is worth thousands of dollars in valuation accuracy. Green, flat, mowed/maintained but NOT farmed and NOT grazed. No crop rows, no fencing, no livestock. Looks like farmland from satellite but ISN'T — no yielding asset premium. From satellite it can LOOK exactly like farmland because it is open and green. When confused, ALWAYS use Google Street View or Google Earth to confirm actual land use. Satellite alone can be extremely misleading. Timbered Land Mature tree cover with value in standing timber. Dense, uniform canopy, established growth. Standing timber can add $2,000–$15,000+ depending on species, maturity, and acreage. Always mention timber value to buyers as a

Mature tree cover with value in standing timber. Dense, uniform canopy, established growth. Standing timber can add $2,000–$15,000+ depending on species, maturity, and acreage. Always mention timber value to buyers as a bonus. Most wooded land is just 'wooded land' — don't automatically assume timber adds value. Only flag as managed timber if you see clearly replanted rows of trees (pine plantations, etc.), uniform species and spacing, looks like an investment/farming operation, ready to harvest. Clear-Cut Land Recently logged — debris, irregular patterns, logging roads, stark contrast with surrounding timber. This is a temporary state. Distinguish from intentionally maintained cleared land — clear-cut is messy, cleared is clean. Takes 5–30% off value. Looks ugly, not ready to build (stumps, debris), not wooded (no privacy/recreation), not cleared (not clean usable land) — worst of both worlds. Toggle between map layers (old vs new satellite). Old imagery shows trees, new shows stumps/cleared. Flag it: 'Property appears recently timbered' and discount 5–30% depending on severity.

IV. COMP VERIFICATION & RULES Source Hierarchy  Closed (MLS) = Gold standard. Verified arms-length transaction. Use as primary anchors.  Pending = Discount 5–10% from list price. Hot market = 5%, slow market = 10%.  REJECT: Off-market and Sold–Public Records. May be family transfers, partial interest sales, tax sales — not arms-length.  Never use AI Comp, Zestimate, or any platform estimates. Never Cross County Lines Each county is its own market. Pink lines on Land Insights = county boundaries = your comp boundaries. Even if a comp is physically closer across the county line, don't use it. Buyers search Zillow/Redfin/Realtor by COUNTY — they filter to one county and never see adjacent county listings. Over time, each county develops its own pricing patterns. Adjacent counties can have DRASTICALLY different prices for the same land. Acreage Range  Always click AUTO (not Manual)  If not enough comps: below=80%, above=150%  Always pull wide acreage range to see the PPA curve shape APN Lookup Use when listing location seems off or photos are missing. Land Insights aggregates data — pins can be wrong. APN lookup shows TRUE parcel location, boundaries, flood/wetlands. Always keep original tab open, open NEW tab for APN search. Comp Evaluation Depth  Check description, DOM, full listing history (listed/delisted = red flag)  Weight comps closer in acreage + proximity more heavily  Use wider range comps to verify the story  Open all comps in own tabs, evaluate simultaneously Comp Quality Awareness Not all waterfront/golf course/amenity comps are equal. A marshy, wetland-heavy 'waterfront' comp that sold low is NOT comparable to a clean waterfront. A flood zone riverfront comp is NOT a true reflection of clean riverfront. Always assess QUALITY of the comp's features

al. A marshy, wetland-heavy 'waterfront' comp that sold low is NOT comparable to a clean waterfront. A flood zone riverfront comp is NOT a true reflection of clean riverfront. Always assess QUALITY of the comp's features vs subject. Pull APN from Zillow/Redfin and check in Land Insights for soil survey, wetlands, flood zone if needed to verify comp quality before using it in your analysis. Comp Listing Photos Warning [!] Photos in MLS listings may show subdivision/HOA amenities (clubhouse, tennis courts, pool, lake, golf course) that are NOT on the actual parcel.
These are community amenities, not parcel features. Always verify what is on the ACTUAL property using satellite/APN/Zillow. Don't inflate comp values based on amenity photos.

V. ACREAGE TO PPA RELATIONSHIP — THE EXPONENTIAL CURVE This is NOT a linear relationship. It's EXPONENTIAL. The curve drops STEEPLY from small acreage to medium, then flattens out. Example Pattern (varies by market):  1 acre: $15,000/ac  2 acres: $12,000/ac  5 acres: $8,000/ac  10 acres: $5,500/ac  20 acres: $4,000/ac  40 acres: $3,200/ac  80 acres: $2,800/ac The Inflection Point — WHERE THE MONEY IS The steepest part of the curve = the inflection point. This is where subdivision makes the most money. Buy at the lower PPA (larger acreage), subdivide into higher PPA lots. Steep Curve (Good Subdivide Market) Huge PPA difference between small and large lots. Example: 5 acres = $10,000/ac, but 40 acres = $3,000/ac. Subdividing 40 acres into 8×5-acre lots = massive value creation. Flat/Linear Curve (Bad Subdivide Market) Small PPA difference between lot sizes. Example: 5 acres = $5,000/ac, 40 acres = $4,500/ac. Subdividing doesn't create much value — not worth the hassle. Focus on simple flips. How To Identify Market Type Pull comps across a WIDE acreage range (2 to 80+ acres). Plot them mentally or visually. Steep/exponential = subdivide opportunity. Flat/linear = simple flip only. Regulations can kill a good market. Maryland example: Looks like great exponential curve, BUT strict regulations (perc tests, complex approvals) add cost, time, and risk. Always factor in regulatory environment.

VI. COMP WEIGHTING — HOW TO THINK ABOUT IT Core Principle: Not all acres are equal. You're comping USABLE land and CONVENIENCE, not just raw acreage. Key Differentiation Factors  Access / road frontage  Shape of the parcel  Usable vs unusable portions  Convenience of movement on the property Access & Road Frontage More road frontage = more value. Can access from multiple points, more flexibility for building, easier to subdivide, more convenient to enter/exit. Limited frontage (flagpole lots): Narrow access strip (30–50 ft) leading to larger rear area. That 2-acre easement on a 10-acre lot = really only 8 usable acres. Farther from road = less convenient. Costs money to build driveway through to the buildable area. DISCOUNT vs same acreage with full road frontage. Shape — Convenience Factor Good shapes: Square or rectangular = easy to use, fence, build, subdivide. Bad shapes: Long skinny strips, irregular shapes with unusable corners, flagpole lots. How To Comp Flagpole / Limited Access Lots  Identify the USABLE portion of the property  Recognize the access strip adds minimal value  Factor in inconvenience of being far from road + driveway cost  Example: 10-acre flagpole with 2-acre access strip → might comp closer to a 7-acre lot with good frontage Primary Anchor Approach Find the comp MOST similar to your subject — same road type, similar acreage, similar condition, same ACCESS type. This is your primary pricing anchor. Then adjust: better road = add value, worse road = subtract, better access = add, flagpole = subtract, cleared vs wooded = adjust, larger acreage = lower PPA, etc. Comping Lesson — Boon MI (Critical) Don't limit yourself to immediate nearby comps if they are all large acreage. Look WIDER in the county for similar-acreage comps to get accurate

djust, larger acreage = lower PPA, etc. Comping Lesson — Boon MI (Critical) Don't limit yourself to immediate nearby comps if they are all large acreage. Look WIDER in the county for similar-acreage comps to get accurate PPA. Compare land types across comps: if inferior land (pasture, timbered, dirt road) in a slightly nicer area trades at $X/ac, then farmland (yielding asset) should be equal or MORE. Specific example: 9.89ac farmland in Boon, MI (Wexford County). Initially valued at $50,000 based on nearby large-acreage comps (37.5ac at $2,400/ac). Looking wider: W 48th Rd — 10ac timbered/dirt road = $110,000 ($11,000/ac). W 46th Rd — 5ac pasture = $50,000 ($10,000/ac). Both INFERIOR to our farmland. Revised to $80,000 (~$8,090/ac). Framework: Pick comps nearest in acreage. See what the location looks like — nicer, same, or worse than subject? Use other comps to construct the full story. If comps are in a nicer area, discount slightly. If subject has advantages
(farmland, flat, clean DD), weight those up.

VII. DOM INTERPRETATION Low DOM (Under 30 Days) — CRITICAL INSIGHT  Property likely sold UNDER market value — use as FLOOR, market value is likely higher  This is how YOUR listings should sell (1–2 weeks)  Use to calibrate disposition pricing: If comp sold in 14 days at $45K, that's a realistic list price → Work backwards: Offer at 50% ($22.5K) Normal DOM (30–90 Days) Standard market transaction. Use at face value, no adjustment needed. High DOM (90–180+ Days) Still 100% valid comps — someone actually paid that price. But it took a while, may have been slightly overpriced initially. Optional: shave 3–5% if 150–200+ days. Always check price history. DOM shown may be TOTAL days, not days from last price drop. Multiple price drops = overpriced initially. Very High DOM (180+ Days) Something was wrong — price, terms, or property issues. Check if bundle deal or had terms requirements. Use cautiously, investigate before relying on it.

VIII. STRUCTURES — CALL THEM OUT, DON'T PRICE THEM A structure could add $2,000 or $20,000 — impossible to know from satellite. A dilapidated house could be a LIABILITY (demolition costs). ALWAYS call out if a property has a structure visible. Note it as 'has structure — needs seller verification.' Price the land AS IF no structure exists. Let the investor call the seller to determine actual value/condition. Structures on Comps  Nice metal warehouse/barn: $10K–$60K+ value. Weight as SUPERIOR / CEILING. Mentally subtract estimated structure value.  Small house or cabin: Look up on Zillow. Could be $20K–$100K+. Weight heavily as superior.  Dilapidated structure (junk): LIABILITY. Costs $5K–$10K+ to remove. Mark as SAME or 5% LESS than raw land. Don't use comps with structures as direct 1:1 comps. Mentally adjust for structure value.

IX. WATERFRONT VALUATION — CRITICAL PREMIUM FEATURE [!] ALWAYS Verify Water Features Yourself NEVER assume ponds/lakes/creeks exist just because Land Insights shows surface water or wetlands overlay. Could be swamp, seasonal pond, seasonal creek, or wetlands that look like water but aren't. MANDATORY PROCESS: 1) Turn OFF all filters. 2) Look at actual satellite imagery with your own eyes. 3) Check Google Maps to verify water is actually there. 4) Land Insights MISSES small ponds too — always look yourself. 5) Trust your own eyes, not the filters. If it even slightly looks like there might be a pond/creek, give BOTH waterfront and non-waterfront evaluations. Waterfront Hierarchy  1. Lake — BEST, especially nice lakes. Highest premium, best for recreation (boating, fishing, swimming).  2. River — Big, flowing rivers worth more than ponds. Depending on lake quality, could be comparable.  3. Pond — Size matters significantly. Nice, clean, year-round, fishable pond is RARE and commands significant premium.  4. Creek — Smallest premium. Watch out for seasonal creeks that may not be flowing. Waterfront Premium Multiplier Waterfront commands 2–3x premium over non-waterfront land in the same area. Example: If raw land without water goes for $8,000–$10,000/ac, pond front with amenities could be $20,000–$25,000/ac or more. Deeded Water Access (Not Direct Frontage) Worth ~80% of waterfront premium if real usable easement (50ft deeded lake access = essentially waterfront). Narrower/less usable easements = closer to 50%. Linear feet of frontage significantly changes value. Usable Shoreline vs Unusable Nice beach/gentle slope to water = maximum premium. Steep/swampy/unusable shoreline = reduced premium. Compare to other waterfront comps to gauge quality. Water Feature Size — The

nges value. Usable Shoreline vs Unusable Nice beach/gentle slope to water = maximum premium. Steep/swampy/unusable shoreline = reduced premium. Compare to other waterfront comps to gauge quality. Water Feature Size — The Acreage Problem Bigger water = better — that's why lakes beat ponds. BUT if the pond/lake takes up too much of the property's usable acreage, discount for lost usable land. Same logic as flagpole easements. You're comping based on usable acreage plus the water premium, not total acreage at full waterfront rates. Assume Waterfront If Close If property butts up to waterfront LLC/owner land, is very close to a lake/pond, or has state land between it and water — always evaluate as waterfront AND give non-waterfront value. Incentivizes asking seller about easements/access.

X. MOBILE HOMES ON LAND [!] NEVER trust Land Insights structure data. Example: 113 Double Creek Rd showed '0 sqft structures' and 'Mobile Home: No' but Street View clearly showed a singlewide. Always verify with Street View (mandatory), Zillow lookup, Google Maps satellite. Mobile Home Value Ranges Older mobile homes (1980s): 950 sqft, 2bd/1ba: ~$25,000–$35,000 depending on condition. Factors: year built, square footage, bedrooms/bathrooms, condition, permanent foundation, utilities connected. Investor needs to verify condition with seller. Comping Land with Mobile Homes  Step 1: Comp the LAND separately as if raw land. Apply waterfront/road/access adjustments.  Step 2: Add mobile home value separately based on age, size, bed/bath. Note condition needs verification.  Step 3: Total = Land Value + Mobile Home Value Example: Land (3.1ac pond front): $80,000 + Mobile home (1980, 950sf, 2/1): $30,000 = Total MV: $110,000

XI. ROAD TYPE HIERARCHY  1. Paved county road = BEST for most buyers  2. Nice maintained dirt/gravel road = Good for recreational land  3. Seasonal/unmaintained dirt road = Discount  4. Two-track = Significant discount  5. Highway frontage = DISCOUNT (noise, no privacy = negative) Why Highway Frontage Is BAD Constant traffic noise, no privacy, less desirable for recreational/residential use. Buyers pay LESS, not more, for highway exposure. Inferring Road Type Without Street View Look at nearby SOLD comps on same road. Check satellite for road width and condition. Gravel/maintained shows as lighter, wider path. Two-track shows as faint lines through trees.

XII. LANDLOCKED vs FLAGPOLE vs DEEP EASEMENT It's not binary — there's a spectrum. Land Insights flags 'Landlocked: Yes' but this is NOT always accurate. Always provide BOTH values if landlocked is flagged. The Spectrum  1. Truly Landlocked (No Access): No legal access at all. Offer 10% of market value. High risk, high hassle.  2. Landlocked But Right Next To Road: Probably has easement or data error. Verify with seller. If easement exists = normal pricing. If truly no access = 10%.  3. Long Easement Through Neighbor's Property: Legal access but LONG driveway/easement. Between flagpole and landlocked pricing. Consider: How far? Through how many properties? Cost to build driveway?  4. Flagpole Lot (Short Easement): Narrow access (30–50 ft) to buildable rear area. Inconvenient but workable. Discount for usable acreage + inconvenience. Deliverable Format MARKET VALUE (assuming access): $50,000 Offer Range: $25,000 (50%) - $35,000 (70%) [!] LANDLOCKED FLAG — If truly landlocked: Offer ~$5,000 (10%) Verify access situation with seller

XIII. WETLANDS EVALUATION Core question: Can it perc? If no = 50–80% value killer. Location of Wetlands Matters  Back of property, under 20%: Barely discount (~5%)  Back of property, ~50%: 10–20% discount  Front/road: 50–80% discount — can't build near road, very inconvenient  100% wetlands hunting property: Comp against similar wetland sales, discount 30–40% Small buildable corner: Doesn't save the whole property. Just helps septic placement. Property still priced as overwhelmingly wetlands. How To Evaluate  Check wetlands % in Land Insights  Look at satellite — WHERE are the wetlands?  Is there a clear buildable area near the road?  Compare to other wetland properties in the market  Note for investor — they may need to verify perc test potential

XIV. SLOPE — WHEN IT MATTERS  Under 10%: No issue, price normally  10–20%: Minor discount, 5–10%  20–35%: Noticeable discount, 15–30%  35%+: Major discount, 30–50% — building is difficult/expensive Why slope matters: Harder to build, may need retaining walls/special foundations, septic harder to install, driveway construction more expensive, less usable flat area. CRITICAL: Pull up APN for COMPS and check THEIR slope. Compare subject slope to comp slopes. If comps have similar slope, price similarly. If subject is steeper, discount accordingly.

XV. FLOOD ZONE — DON'T PANIC, LOOK AROUND FIRST [!] Do NOT panic-discount for flood zone. FIRST look around — are people building houses in the area in the same flood zone? If yes, flood zone is just a cost factor (insurance, raised foundation), NOT a value killer. Edisto Island Lesson (Critical) Mary Seabrook Rd, Charleston Co, SC: 5.64ac, 79% wetlands, 100% flood zone. Initial valuation was $75,000 (60–70% discount from clean comps). WRONG. The ENTIRE island is in a flood zone and everyone builds there. Houses were visible on and near the property. Correct approach: recognize flood zone as cost factor, not deal killer. Look around FIRST before discounting. Process  Toggle hazards OFF to see actual property (structures, land type, water features)  Toggle ON to check flood/wetland zones  Always check for structures on or near the property  Different flood zone types matter  Coastal areas commonly have 100% flood zone but entire communities are built within them

XVI. SUBDIVISION SHAPE RULE — BOWLING ALLEY LOTS Width-to-length ratio: Golden ratio = 1:2 to 1:4. Max acceptable = 1:5 or 1:6. Skinnier = 'bowling alley lot' = significant value loss. Why: Can't build near road because lot is too narrow. Forced to go deep into property — long driveway, utilities run farther, building site far from road. Wider lots = more accessible from road = more usable = more valuable. Before proposing ANY subdivision, check if splitting creates bowling alley lots — if yes, NO value-add. Also consider if the local market supports long/skinny lot sales. Too rural + too skinny = no market. Lesson (Buckley Rd, Susquehanna Co, PA): 38.37ac already long and narrow. Splitting would create two unsellable bowling alley lots in a rural area with zero demand for that shape. No subdivision recommended.

XVII. VIEWS / ELEVATION PREMIUM  Mediocre view: ~5% premium  Good view: 10–15%  Superman/panoramic view: 20–30% Rare to find true great views — still need to build road up, clear trees, make a pad. Don't overweight this — just flag in Extra Notes if contour lines/elevation show high position with potential views.

XVIII. POWER LINES & UTILITY EASEMENTS — VALUE KILLERS High Voltage Power Lines (Big Deal Killer) Signs: Huge metal towers, buzzing/humming sound, wide cleared easement through property. Can't build underneath or near them (setbacks = couple hundred feet). Takes up usable space, looks ugly, annoying buzzing noise. Significantly diminishes value. Flag it immediately. Utility Poles Not as bad as high voltage lines. Still annoying if right in front/back yard. Note if visible, but less severe. Gas Line Easements Cleared strip/line cut through property. No power towers but obvious easement path. Can't build on it. Check satellite for cleared strips, toggle map layers, check county utility maps if needed.

XIX. TIMBERED LAND & RECENTLY LOGGED Most wooded land = just 'wooded land.' Don't automatically assume timber adds value. Comp as normal wooded property. When Timber Might Matter Signs of a true timber property: clearly replanted rows of trees (pine plantations, etc.), uniform tree species and spacing, looks like an investment/farming operation, ready to harvest (mature, tall, consistent). Owners who actively manage timber usually know what it's worth — it's a yielding asset, harder to get at deep discount. Comp the property as normal (as-is land value). If it LOOKS like managed timber, call it out. Let investor determine actual timber value with seller. Recently Logged Takes 5–30% off value. Not ready to build, not wooded, not cleared — worst of both worlds. Toggle between map layers to identify. Flag and discount accordingly.

XX. ACTIVELY FARMED LAND Covered in Land Type Identification (Section III). Farms are YIELDING ASSETS. Farmers usually know what land is worth. Harder to get at deep discount. Signs: crop rows, agricultural patterns, farm equipment, active planting/harvesting. Comp normally but warn investor that seller may be more savvy on pricing. XXI. MAP LAYERS — ALWAYS TOGGLE Different map layers have different dates. Satellite imagery could be 1–5+ years old. Things change: houses built, timber cut, land cleared.  Toggle between available map layers. Compare old vs new imagery.  If discrepancy found: flag it for investor. 'Satellite shows [X] but older/newer layer shows [Y] — needs seller verification.'  Quick toggle, note anything obvious, move on. Seasonality doesn't affect comping — land prices don't change by season. BUT winter/snow = harder to sell (can't visit property, roads not plowed). This affects HOW LONG it takes to sell, not the PRICE. XXII. WHEN SELLER ALREADY HAS IT LISTED — RED FLAG [!] Tell the investor immediately if the property is already listed. Seller is more knowledgeable about pricing, has talked to a realtor, has a price in their head, psychologically anchored to list price. Much harder to negotiate a deep discount. Note it prominently, include their asking price, still provide your market value analysis and 50% offer range. Let investor decide how to approach.

XXIII. CASH VS TERMS — MOSTLY UNKNOWN You can't tell if a sale was cash or owner-financed — this info isn't in public record data. Owner financing = potentially higher price (buyers pay more for payment flexibility). If a comp seems high AND mentioned owner financing, note: 'This comp may reflect owner financing premium.' Don't obsess over it. XXIV. COUNTY BOUNDARIES — NEVER CROSS Covered in Comp Verification (Section IV). Pink lines = county boundaries = your comp boundaries. Each county is its own market. No exceptions. XXV. SPARSE MARKETS — USING WHAT YOU HAVE When sold comps are limited, you still weight SOLDS more heavily, but actives become more important for context. Reading Active Listings — Save Ratio Views-to-Saves Ratio: High save ratio = listing is priced well, generating interest. Low save ratio = overpriced or has issues. Compare save ratios across actives at different price points. Good ratios = realistic market pricing. Bad ratios = probably overpriced. Sparse Market Approach  Widen acreage range to see the FULL story (1ac to 80+ac)  Draw the trendline in your head — see how PPA changes across the spectrum  Interpolate where your property falls on the curve  Price conservatively based on trendline  When there's no clear comp, YOU set the price  Price below competitive actives — realtors bring buyers to competitive listings first Note Format for Scattered Comps NOTE: Limited direct comps. Pricing based on trendline: 5 acres selling ~$7,000/ac / 20 acres selling ~$4,500/ac Estimated 10-acre value: ~$5,500/ac Recommend conservative list price to ensure quick sale

XXVI. PROXIMITY TO CITIES — OBSERVE THE MARKET No universal rule — every market is different. DON'T apply a rigid rule like '5 miles = X% premium.' DO observe the comps and let them tell the story. Compare properties at similar distances from town. Let the market data show you the pattern. Infill Lots vs Rural Recreational These are DIFFERENT markets — don't mix comps. 2 acres in a city = infill lot, different buyer pool. 2 acres rural = recreational, different buyer pool. If infill appears in your comps, use as CEILING only, not a direct comp. XXVII. AREA QUALITY — ALWAYS CHECK Use Street View and satellite to assess surrounding area. Nice homes, new construction, clean properties = supports higher pricing. Run-down mobile homes, abandoned properties, trash/junk = lower pricing. Note area quality in deliverable. XXVIII. OWNER MULTIPLE PROPERTIES Just report how many other properties the owner has. Do NOT comp other properties — waste of time if seller isn't interested. Say 'Owner has X properties' and move on. Flag rural recreational parcels of potential interest. Investor can ask seller if open to selling others; if yes, investor will ask you to comp them then.

XXIX. MARKET HEALTH METRICS  Active count: Current listings on market  Pending count: Currently under contract  Sold last 90 days: Recent closed sales  1-yr STR (Sell Through Rate): % of listings that sell More pending = good demand. More sold than active = healthy. High STR = properties moving. BUT: 'Dead markets' don't matter. Elite drone marketing makes properties look better than they are. Aggressive pricing creates urgency. A well-marketed, fairly-priced property = mega discount perception to buyers. Dead markets can be MORE profitable because acquisition prices are lower and there's less competition. XXX. ZONING — DON'T OVERTHINK IT Note the zoning code in your deliverable. Don't try to interpret complex rules — let the investor ask the seller. Flag if obviously restrictive (conservation, agricultural-only) or if multiple properties have different zoning in same area. XXXI. PENDING LISTINGS Discount 5–10% from list price. Hot/normal market = 5%. Slower market = 10%. Still highly value them — a pending sale is a real buyer making a real commitment, very current market activity. Use common sense on discount amount. XXXII. DUPLICATE SUBDIVISION SALES — COUNT AS ONE If a seller subdivided 40 acres into 4 lots and all 4 sold, only use ONE as a comp. They are the exact same type of property from one parent parcel. Using all 4 would over-weight that one subdivision. Exception: if one lot is clearly different (bigger, better access, pond), use it as a separate comp. XXXIII. CREATIVE APPROACHES Almost EVERYTHING can be worked with. Most investors pass on 'problem' properties — that's where opportunity exists.  100% wetlands on large acreage? → Convert to hunting property  Landlocked? → Negotiate easement, offer 10%  Weird shape? → Find the right buyer  Steep

pass on 'problem' properties — that's where opportunity exists.  100% wetlands on large acreage? → Convert to hunting property  Landlocked? → Negotiate easement, offer 10%  Weird shape? → Find the right buyer  Steep slope? → Price accordingly, someone wants a view Actual Deal Killers (Very Few)  State-owned / Township / County-owned land  Quarries (specialized industrial)
 Storage unit facilities (commercial, different business)  Completely unusable swamp — unless large enough for hunting land

XXXIV. COMP QUALITY AWARENESS Covered in Comp Verification (Section IV). Always assess QUALITY of comp features vs subject. Pull APN and check soil survey, wetlands, flood zone to verify comp quality before using in analysis. XXXV. COMP LISTING PHOTOS WARNING Covered in Comp Verification (Section IV). Photos may show community amenities, not parcel features. Always verify via satellite/APN/Zillow. XXXVI. SELLER MOTIVATION SIGNALS Always report ALL signals found:  Ownership 5+ years — Land has appreciated, owner may not know current value. 10+ years = even better.  Out of state/county — Not using the land, less attachment.  Family transfer (inherited) — No emotional attachment, didn't pay for it, any sale is 'profit.'  Tax delinquent / high taxes — Strong motivation, ongoing burden.  Low cost basis — More room to negotiate. Ideal Seller Profile Absentee owner (out of state/county), held 5–10+ years, low original cost basis, tax delinquent or high taxes, family transfer / inherited, not currently using the property. Report Format Ownership: X years (purchased MM/YYYY) Location: Out of state/county/ZIP or local Transfer type: Arms-length / Family transfer Tax status: Current / Delinquent Annual taxes: $X Last purchase price: $X Motivation assessment: Low / Medium / High

XXXVII. VALUE-ADD IDENTIFICATION Always Call Out  Cleared/open areas (less clearing cost)  Gravel or paved driveways (immediate access)  Trails through property  Ponds/creeks (recreational value)  Fencing (shows boundaries, contains livestock)  Structures — flag but don't price  Lakefront/river access (premium)  Proximity to amenities Ponds — Nuanced Understanding  Premium pond: Year-round, clean, fishable, visually pleasing = RARE, significant premium  Average pond: Year-round but some algae/debris = still adds value, less premium  Lower value pond: Seasonal, full of muck = still a feature, discount expectations Subdivision Potential — When To Analyze  15–20+ acres minimum (varies by market)  Sufficient road frontage to create multiple lots  Check market type first — exponential or linear curve?  If linear = skip subdivision analysis  If exponential = analyze the play Road Frontage Requirements for Subdivision NO HARD RULE. Average is roughly 100–150 ft per lot, but every county and township is completely different. DO NOT assume — ALWAYS check the county/township LDR. Key items in County LDRs: Minimum lot size, minimum road frontage per lot, plat-exempt threshold, perc test requirements, flag lot allowances, road construction standards, utility requirements, setback requirements, environmental reviews.

XXXVIII. PRICING PHILOSOPHY — CONSERVATIVE AT EVERY STEP Mindset: When I finish a valuation, I want to be 100% confident it's a deal. Build in buffer at every step. Better to slightly undervalue than overvalue. Offer Ranges  50%: Target for solid deals — MOST ACCURATE  60%: Mid-range  70%: Only when very confident or motivated seller  Above 70% (RARE): Only on bigger deals with significant spread. Must have guaranteed, clear profit margin. List Price Strategy Price 5–10% below calculated market value. This ensures fast sales (1–2 weeks). Elite drone marketing + competitive price = dominate the market. Realtors notice and bring buyers when priced right. Always Give Specific Prices Never give just ranges — always include a specific recommended value. Ranges are okay if uncertain, but the investor needs a concrete number to make offers.

XXXIX. DELIVERABLE FORMAT Use this exact format for every evaluation: ======================================= PROPERTY: [Address or Description] COUNTY: [County, State] ACREAGE: [X acres] ======================================= 1. MARKET VALUE: $XX,XXX (~$X,XXX/ac) 2. OFFER PRICE: 50%: $XX,XXX 60%: $XX,XXX 70%: $XX,XXX [!] IF LANDLOCKED: $X,XXX (10%) 3. VALUE-ADD MARKET VALUE: $XX,XXX (if applicable) [Subdivision into X lots, etc.] 4. VALUE-ADD OFFER PRICE: (if applicable) 50%: $XX,XXX / 60%: $XX,XXX / 70%: $XX,XXX 5. NEGOTIATION STRATEGIES / LEVERAGE: Ownership, location, tax status, transfer type, property issues to leverage, questions for seller 6. EXTRA NOTES: [!] Red flags, structures, zoning, area quality, other owner properties, value-adds, comp reasoning 7. RECOMMENDED LIST PRICE: $XX,XXX [Slight discount from MV for fast sale + elite marketing] 8. KEY COMPS USED: (3-5 comps) [Ac | Price | PPA | Status | Notes] [Brief explanation of weighting if needed]
Use [!] hazard symbol for anything important: structure on property, already listed, landlocked flag, significant wetlands, any deal issue.

XL. COMMON MISTAKES TO AVOID Comp Mistakes  Using 'off-market' or 'public records' sales  Not verifying Zillow 'Closed' badge  Mixing counties (pink lines = boundaries)  Ignoring road type differences  Not checking price history for true DOM Analysis Mistakes  Wasting time on subdivision for small parcels  Assuming linear PPA relationship (it's exponential)  Dividing acreage mathematically instead of looking at shape  Missing that highway frontage is a NEGATIVE  Not using nearby comps to infer road type Pricing Mistakes  Anchoring to overpriced active listings  Not being conservative enough  Pricing structures without seller verification  Forgetting the list price needs buffer too Communication Mistakes  Over-explaining simple properties  Not calling out structures for investor follow-up  Not being concise  Analyzing subdivision when it's clearly not applicable

XLI. PROPERTIES EVALUATED — LESSONS LEARNED 1. W Sherman St, Boon, MI (Wexford Co) 9.89ac FARMLAND. Initial $50K revised to $80K. KEY LESSON: look wider for comps, farmland premium, compare land types. 2. Carter County, OK 19.55ac pasture. MV: $135,000 (~$6,906/ac). Good example of wide county comp search with clear PPA curve from 1ac to 30ac. 3. Bee Pond Rd, Ninety Six, SC (Greenwood Co) 7.2ac pasture/cleared. MV: $55,000. Sparse comp area, narrow parcel shape noted. 4. Kershaw Camden Hwy, Lancaster, SC 2.72ac GB COMMERCIAL zoned, highway, POND ON PROPERTY (missed initially). Tax sale $1,000. MV: $70,000 revised after catching pond. LESSONS: always verify water yourself, commercial zoning changes comp pool. 5. Buckley Rd, Montrose, PA (Susquehanna Co) 38.37ac timbered, adjacent Salt Springs State Park. MV: $175,000. No subdivision (bowling alley shape). Taught shape rule. 6. Rocky Ridge Rd, Somerset Co, PA 8.43ac mixed. MV: $75,000. Very sparse comp area. 7. Old Lake Rd, Riegelwood, NC (Columbus Co) 3.75ac. MV: $40,000. 'Old Lake' road name — flag potential nearby water. 8. Chester County, SC 7ac timbered, creek border, tax delinquent 2024. MV: $77,000. 9. Mary Seabrook Rd, Edisto Island, SC (Charleston Co) 5.64ac, 79% wetlands, 100% flood zone. Initial $75K WRONG — over-discounted. Missed house on property. KEY LESSON: don't panic-discount flood zone, look around first. 10. Columbus County, NC 3.75ac. MV: $40,000. Demonstrated faster workflow with Auto acreage range and direct zoom navigation. END OF COMPLETE METHODOLOGY HANDBOOK — VERSION 3.0 If Claude ever seems off, paste this entire document back to restore full methodology.

DewClaw — Part 1: Land Type Identification & Boon MI Lesson FULLY EXPANDED — How to Identify Land Types and Why It Matters for Pricing WHY LAND TYPE IDENTIFICATION IS CRITICAL Land type identification is one of the most critical skills for accurate comping. The type of land — farmland, pasture, maintained cleared, timbered, or clear-cut — directly affects the market value of a property. Getting the land type wrong means getting the price wrong, sometimes by tens of thousands of dollars. This is not a minor detail or a footnote in your analysis — it is one of the PRIMARY drivers of value alongside acreage, road type, and location. Here is why it matters so much: imagine two adjacent 20-acre properties in the same county, on the same road, with the same shape and no wetlands. One is actively farmed with crop rows visible from satellite. The other is maintained cleared land — green, flat, mowed, but no agricultural activity. From satellite at a distance, they might look almost identical. But the farmland is worth 30-50% MORE because it is a yielding asset producing income. If you comp them at the same PPA, you are either massively overpricing the cleared land or massively underpricing the farmland. That is a $30,000-$50,000 error on a $100,000 property. This is why the user taught land type identification early in the training using actual satellite screenshots. The user specifically showed examples where maintained cleared land LOOKED like farmland from satellite but was NOT farmland. The visual similarity is what makes this so dangerous — you have to know what to look for and you have to verify with Street View or Google Earth when you are unsure. Never assume. Always verify. FARMLAND — YIELDING ASSET — 30-50% PREMIUM What Farmland Is and Why It Commands a Premium

to know what to look for and you have to verify with Street View or Google Earth when you are unsure. Never assume. Always verify. FARMLAND — YIELDING ASSET — 30-50% PREMIUM What Farmland Is and Why It Commands a Premium Farmland is land that is actively being farmed — crops are being grown, harvested, and sold for income. The land is a YIELDING ASSET, meaning it produces revenue for the owner. This is fundamentally different from every other type of vacant land because the buyer is not just purchasing dirt — they are purchasing dirt PLUS an income stream. That income stream has real monetary value that gets capitalized into the land price. The premium for farmland over similar non-farmed land is approximately 30-50%, with 30% being a reasonable baseline for most situations. The exact premium depends on factors like the quality of the soil, the type of crops being grown, whether there is an existing farm lease generating income, the local agricultural economy, and the demand for farmland in that specific area. But as a starting point, when you identify farmland, you should expect the PPA to be roughly 30% higher than comparable pasture or cleared land in the same area. This premium explains why farmland comps should NEVER be used as anchor comps for non-farmland properties. If you are comping a timbered property and you use a farmland comp as your anchor, you will overprice the timbered property by 30-50% because the farmland comp includes agricultural income value that your timbered property does not have. Farmland comps should only be used as CEILING references — they tell you the maximum PPA in the area, but your non-farmland subject should be well below that ceiling. How to Identify Farmland From Satellite Farmland has very distinctive visual signatures on

CEILING references — they tell you the maximum PPA in the area, but your non-farmland subject should be well below that ceiling. How to Identify Farmland From Satellite Farmland has very distinctive visual signatures on satellite imagery that you should learn to recognize instantly. The most obvious indicator is CROP ROW PATTERNS — parallel lines running across the fields that show active agricultural operations. These rows are visible from satellite as regular, repeating lines across the field surface. They can run in any direction but are always parallel and evenly spaced within a field. Other indicators include: TILLED SOIL which has a distinctive brown/tan color and rough texture that looks different from grass — you can see the disturbed earth where plowing has occurred. IRRIGATION EQUIPMENT which creates distinctive patterns — center pivot irrigation creates large circles visible from satellite, linear irrigation creates long rectangular patterns. DISTINCT FIELD BOUNDARIES where the farmed area has clean, straight edges separating the crop area from surrounding land types.

The key rule: if you see crop rows from satellite, it is farmland. Crop rows are the single most reliable indicator. No other land type has parallel rows running across the surface. If you see them, you can be confident calling it farmland and applying the 30-50% premium. Farmland in Your Comps — How to Handle It When you see a comp that sold at a suspiciously high PPA compared to other comps in the area, check if it is farmland. This is one of the most common explanations for outlier high comps. If the comp's satellite shows crop rows or the listing description mentions agricultural use, farm lease, or crop production, the high PPA makes sense — the buyer paid a premium for the yielding asset. In your deliverable, when farmland comps appear: note them as ceiling references, explain that the high PPA is due to the farmland premium (yielding asset), and clearly state that your non-farmland subject should be priced below these comps. This shows the user you understand WHY the comp is high and that you are not being misled by it.

IDLE FARMLAND — ONLY 10% PREMIUM What Happens When Farmland Goes Idle Farmland that has been idle for a couple of years — no active crops being grown — loses MOST of the farmland premium. The full 30-50% premium disappears because the land is no longer producing income. Without active crops, there is no yielding asset, so the fundamental reason for the premium no longer applies. However, idle farmland does NOT drop all the way down to regular cleared land value. It retains approximately a 10% premium over pasture land. The reason is twofold: First, idle farmland still LOOKS nicer and more presentable than rough pasture or timbered land. Someone previously invested significant time and money in clearing, grading, and maintaining this land for agricultural use. It is flat, clean, and well-maintained — it has a professional appearance that appeals to buyers even without active farming. Second, idle farmland has more POTENTIAL to become active farmland again than pasture does. A farmer could lease it and start planting relatively quickly because the infrastructure (cleared, graded, potentially irrigated) is already in place. That latent potential has some value in the market. The satellite will help you distinguish active farmland from idle farmland: active crops show distinct row patterns — you can see the parallel lines of planted crops. Idle farmland looks like well-maintained cleared land with no agricultural patterns — it is green and open but has no rows, no tilled soil, no irrigation patterns. This visual difference corresponds to the pricing difference: crop rows visible = full 30-50% premium. No crop rows but clean/maintained = only 10% premium over pasture. PASTURE LAND What Pasture Land Is Pasture land is open grass that is maintained for grazing livestock —

ference: crop rows visible = full 30-50% premium. No crop rows but clean/maintained = only 10% premium over pasture. PASTURE LAND What Pasture Land Is Pasture land is open grass that is maintained for grazing livestock — cattle, horses, goats, etc. It is not actively cultivated for crops (that would be farmland) but it IS being used for a productive purpose (supporting livestock operations). Pasture is worth less than farmland because grazing generates less income than crop production, but it is worth more than raw timbered land because it is already cleared and has utility. How to Identify Pasture From Satellite The biggest visual giveaway for pasture land on satellite imagery is FENCING. Look for thin lines running along property boundaries or through the property — these are fence posts and wire that contain livestock. Fencing is often visible on satellite as faint lines, especially when the sun angle creates shadows from the fence posts. Other indicators: OPEN GRASS that looks GRAZED rather than mowed — grazing creates uneven patterns because animals eat selectively (they prefer certain grasses and avoid others), whereas mowing creates uniform, clean-cut patterns. You might also see WATER TROUGHS (small dark rectangles or circles in open areas), WORN PATHS where animals walk consistently between water sources and shade areas, and sometimes the LIVESTOCK THEMSELVES on recent satellite imagery (appearing as small dark dots scattered across the field). The combination of fencing + open grass + uneven grazing patterns = pasture. If you see all three, you can confidently identify it as pasture land. If you see open grass but NO fencing and NO grazing patterns, it is more likely maintained cleared land — not pasture.

MAINTAINED CLEARED LAND — THE TRICKY ONE Why This Is the Most Dangerous Misidentification Maintained cleared land is green, flat, mowed or maintained, but NOT farmed and NOT grazed. There are no crop rows, no fencing, no livestock, no agricultural activity of any kind. Someone intentionally cleared this land and keeps it tidy — maybe they mow it periodically, maybe they maintain it for aesthetics or future use, maybe they just like having an open field. From satellite, it can look EXACTLY like farmland — open, green, well-maintained. But it is NOT a yielding asset. This misidentification is the MOST DANGEROUS because the pricing impact is enormous. If you call maintained cleared land 'farmland,' you will apply a 30-50% premium that does not belong, overpricing the property by tens of thousands of dollars. If a buyer pays that inflated price, they will quickly realize the land is not producing income and they overpaid. Conversely, if you encounter actual farmland and call it 'just cleared land,' you will underprice it by 30-50%, and the user will miss out on the true value. During training, actual satellite screenshots were shown where land that looked IDENTICAL to farmland from above was actually just maintained cleared land with zero agricultural activity. The land was green, open, flat, and well-maintained — but when you zoomed in or checked Street View, there were no crop rows, no farm equipment, no irrigation, no evidence of farming whatsoever. It was just a mowed field. The lesson was stark: what looks green and open from satellite is NOT automatically farmland. You MUST verify. How to Distinguish From Farmland The key differences on satellite: Farmland has CROP ROWS — parallel lines across the field showing planted crops. Maintained cleared land has NO rows — it

cally farmland. You MUST verify. How to Distinguish From Farmland The key differences on satellite: Farmland has CROP ROWS — parallel lines across the field showing planted crops. Maintained cleared land has NO rows — it is uniformly green with no pattern. Farmland may have TILLED SOIL areas (brown/tan patches where plowing occurred). Maintained cleared land is uniformly green with no disturbed earth. Farmland may have IRRIGATION equipment visible. Maintained cleared land has none. When you are unsure — and this will happen frequently because the visual similarity is very high — ALWAYS use Google Street View or Google Earth to confirm. Drop the pegman on the road near the property and look at the land from ground level. From Street View you can see: are there crop rows? Is there farm equipment? Are there irrigation systems? Does it look like an active farm operation or just a mowed field? This ground-level view takes 15 seconds and can save you from a $30,000+ pricing error. TIMBERED LAND — IDENTIFYING PLANTED VS NATURAL What Timbered Land Is Timbered land has mature tree cover with potential value in the standing timber itself. The trees have been growing for years or decades and have accumulated harvestable wood value. Timbered land is generally worth less per acre than cleared, pasture, or farmland because: 1) the buyer faces clearing costs if they want to build, 2) the land is not generating agricultural income, and 3) the timber value is a one-time harvest, not an ongoing income stream. However, the standing timber does add a bonus value of $2,000-$15,000+ depending on species, maturity, and total acreage. Planted Timber vs Natural Forest — How to Tell From Satellite There are two distinct types of timbered land visible on satellite, and they look very different.

-$15,000+ depending on species, maturity, and total acreage. Planted Timber vs Natural Forest — How to Tell From Satellite There are two distinct types of timbered land visible on satellite, and they look very different. PLANTED TIMBER (commercial timber operations like Weyerhaeuser, Rayonier, Plum Creek, etc.) has trees arranged in SELECT LINES — very organized, almost in perfect rows. This is because the timber company planted them in a specific pattern designed for efficient harvesting. Machines need to move between the rows, so the spacing is deliberate and uniform. All the trees are typically the same SIZE because they were planted at the same time. And they are often the same SPECIES (usually pine) because the company planted a single commercial species. NATURAL FOREST looks completely different on satellite. Trees grow RANDOMLY in a disoriented pattern — no rows, no organization. Trees are DIFFERENT SIZES because they grew at different times over years or decades. Trees are DIFFERENT SPECIES mixed together (hardwoods and softwoods intermixed). The canopy looks irregular, patchy, and varied in color/texture because of the species mix. Another clue: planted timber is usually owned by a COMPANY NAME or LLC on the parcel data. When you see a parcel labeled 'Weyerhaeuser Forest Holdings' or 'XYZ Timber LLC' and the trees are in organized rows, it is almost certainly a commercial timber

operation. Natural forest is typically owned by individual names. Distinguishing Mature Hardwood From Scrubby Pine From satellite you can also assess timber QUALITY to some degree. Mature hardwood appears as TALL, DENSE canopy with LARGE individual tree crowns — each tree crown takes up significant space and the overall canopy is thick and uniform. The color tends to be a deep, rich green. Scrubby pine appears as THINNER canopy with SMALLER individual trees — it looks patchier, more irregular, and less dense. Pine trees have a different color (often lighter or more yellowish-green) and their crowns are typically narrower and more conical than hardwood crowns. Mature hardwood has more timber value than scrubby pine. But don't try to estimate an exact dollar amount from satellite — you cannot know the specific species, board footage, timber market conditions, or harvesting logistics from above. Just note in your deliverable that standing timber exists with potential harvest value, and put it in Section 9 (Questions to Ask Seller) so the user can ask about species, maturity, whether a timber cruise has been done, and recent harvesting activity. CLEAR-CUT LAND VS MAINTAINED CLEARED LAND Clear-cut land was RECENTLY LOGGED — trees were harvested and removed. On satellite you will see: DEBRIS scattered across the ground (tops, branches, slash), IRREGULAR PATTERNS where machinery operated, LOGGING ROADS cut through what was previously solid canopy, and STARK CONTRAST with surrounding standing timber. Stumps are visible. Equipment marks and rutted soil may be visible. The overall impression is MESSY and ROUGH. Maintained cleared land was INTENTIONALLY CLEARED and is kept TIDY. It is clean, uniform green, with no debris, no stumps visible, no logging equipment marks. Someone

e visible. The overall impression is MESSY and ROUGH. Maintained cleared land was INTENTIONALLY CLEARED and is kept TIDY. It is clean, uniform green, with no debris, no stumps visible, no logging equipment marks. Someone maintains it — mowing, perhaps grading. The overall impression is CLEAN and MAINTAINED. The distinction matters because clear-cut land is a temporary state (the forest will regrow) and looks rough, while maintained cleared land is a permanent state that someone invests in keeping tidy. CRITICAL FOR COMP VERIFICATION: Always compare listing photos to current satellite. A comp's listing photos might show standing timber (the property was timbered when it sold), but current satellite might show the land has been clear-cut since the sale (trees were harvested after the transaction). This means the comp's PPA reflects TIMBERED LAND value — what the buyer paid for — but the land condition today is different. If your subject is cleared and this comp is now clear-cut, the comp is less relevant than it appeared. CALL OUT any differences between listing photos and current satellite in your deliverable.

THE BOON MI COMPING LESSON — FULL STORY The Property A 9.89-acre farmland property on W Sherman St in Boon, MI (Wexford County). The property had visible crop rows on satellite, confirming it was actively farmed — a yielding asset. It was near the small town of Boon with some development nearby. The road appeared to be decent condition. The Initial (Wrong) Valuation: $50,000 The initial approach was to pull comps from the immediate nearby area. The closest comps found were large-acreage properties: 37.5 acres at $2,400/ac and similar large parcels. Using these as the basis and applying the PPA curve (our 9.89ac should have higher PPA than 37.5ac), the initial estimate came out to approximately $50,000 (~$5,050/ac). This felt reasonable based on the nearby data. The Problem: Only Looking Nearby The fundamental mistake was ONLY looking at the immediate nearby area where all the comps were large acreage with low PPA. Those large parcels had low PPA because of the exponential PPA curve (larger lots = lower PPA) and because they were likely timbered or pasture — not farmland like our subject. By only looking nearby, the analysis missed the broader county-wide picture that told a completely different story about what smaller, better-quality properties were actually trading at. The Correction: Looking Wider in the County The user corrected this by looking WIDER in Wexford County for comps at similar acreage to the subject. This revealed: W 48th Rd — a 10-acre timbered property on a dirt road that sold for $110,000 ($11,000/ac). W 46th Rd — a 5-acre pasture property that sold for $50,000 ($10,000/ac). These were FURTHER from the subject property geographically, but they were CLOSER in acreage and revealed what the market actually pays for smaller parcels in Wexford County. The

perty that sold for $50,000 ($10,000/ac). These were FURTHER from the subject property geographically, but they were CLOSER in acreage and revealed what the market actually pays for smaller parcels in Wexford County. The User's Logic — How to Think About This The user's reasoning was precise and logical: both the 10ac timbered ($11K/ac) and the 5ac pasture ($10K/ac) comps were INFERIOR to our subject in every meaningful way. They were on worse roads (dirt), they were worse land types (timbered and pasture — neither is a yielding asset), and they were not farmland. Yet they traded at $10,000-$11,000/ac. If inferior properties in a slightly nicer, more developed area were getting $10-11K/ac, then our SUPERIOR farmland (yielding asset, better land type) should be at or near that range. The user then applied appropriate adjustments: the comp areas were slightly nicer/more developed than Boon, so discount slightly for the less developed area. But the subject has the farmland premium (yielding asset = 30-50% above pasture), which partially or fully offsets the area discount. The subject also had town adjacency (near Boon = some convenience value). Weighing all factors, the user valued the property at $80,000 (~$8,090/ac) — a 60% increase from the initial $50,000. The Lessons That Apply to EVERY Property 1. Don't limit yourself to immediate nearby comps. If the nearby comps are all large acreage with low PPA, you are getting a distorted picture. Look wider in the county for similar-acreage comps that reveal what the market actually pays at your lot size. 2. Compare land types across comps. If you find comps that are INFERIOR to your subject (worse road, worse land type, not a yielding asset), their PPA is your FLOOR. Your superior property should be at or above their PPA. If

ompare land types across comps. If you find comps that are INFERIOR to your subject (worse road, worse land type, not a yielding asset), their PPA is your FLOOR. Your superior property should be at or above their PPA. If inferior land trades at $10K/ac, your superior land should be at or above $10K/ac, adjusted for specific area differences. 3. Use the comps to tell the complete story. Every comp should make sense in the narrative. The 37.5ac comp at $2,400/ac makes sense because it was large acreage (low PPA on the curve) and probably timbered (lower quality). The 10ac comp at $11K/ac makes sense because it was small acreage (higher PPA on the curve) in a slightly nicer area. Our 9.89ac farmland at $8,090/ac makes sense because it is small acreage (high PPA) with a farmland premium, discounted slightly for the less developed area around Boon.

4. Farmland premium is real and significant. The $30,000 difference between the initial $50K and the revised $80K was largely explained by failing to recognize and properly value the farmland premium. Farmland at 30-50% above comparable pasture/cleared land is not theoretical — it is what the market pays. Missing this premium means leaving money on the table or making offers that are too low to win the deal. 5. The user's framework for area quality adjustment. When a comp is in a slightly nicer area than your subject, discount slightly — maybe 10-15%. When a comp is in a slightly worse area, adjust up slightly. But don't let area differences override land type differences. A farmland property in an average area is still worth more than a timbered property in a nice area because the yielding asset premium outweighs the area quality adjustment. --- END OF PART 1 --- Part 1 covers: Land Type Identification (Farmland, Idle Farmland, Pasture, Maintained Cleared, Timbered, Clear-Cut) and the Boon MI Comping Lesson

DewClaw — Part 2: Wetlands Evaluation Guide FULLY EXPANDED — Nuanced Framework for Evaluating Wetland Properties WHY WETLANDS EVALUATION MATTERS SO MUCH Wetlands are one of the most significant value factors in rural land — and also one of the most commonly mishandled. The temptation is to see '50% wetlands' on Land Insights and immediately slash the value by 50%. But that is NOT how the user thinks, and it is NOT how the market works. A 40-acre property with 50% wetlands could be worth anywhere from 30% below clean land value to 70% below clean land value — the range is massive, and the difference comes down to WHERE the wetlands are, WHERE the buildable ground is, and whether you can creatively split the property to maximize value. The user's core teaching on wetlands can be summarized in one sentence: DON'T BLANKET-DISCOUNT. Instead, evaluate LOT BY LOT. But that sentence alone doesn't capture the full depth of what was taught. This section expands on every nuance the user explained about wetlands evaluation, including the discount framework, the perc test logic, the lot-splitting presentation trick, visual assessment techniques, and live property examples with the user's exact reasoning. CORE PRINCIPLE: EVALUATE LOT BY LOT, NOT AS ONE WHOLE When you encounter a property with significant wetlands (30%+), your first instinct should NOT be to apply a single discount percentage to the entire property. Instead, imagine you are going to SPLIT the property. Even if you ultimately present it as-is, thinking through the split forces you to ask the right questions: where is the dry ground? Where would someone build? How big is the buildable area? Could a buyer reasonably use this property for residential purposes, or is it only good for hunting? This lot-by-lot thinking is

estions: where is the dry ground? Where would someone build? How big is the buildable area? Could a buyer reasonably use this property for residential purposes, or is it only good for hunting? This lot-by-lot thinking is what separates a lazy valuation from an accurate one. A lazy valuation sees '50% wetlands' and says '$X per acre minus 40%.' An accurate valuation sees '50% wetlands, but the front 15 acres near the road are dry and buildable, the back 15 acres are wet — so the front half is worth near-full value with a minor discount, and the back half is a hunting property.' That accurate valuation might come out to only a 20-25% total discount instead of 40%, which on a $200,000 property is a $30,000-$40,000 difference. The user specifically taught this using the Deaver properties in Dillon County, SC. Both properties had approximately 50% wetlands, but the user's discounts were 20-30% — NOT 40-50% — because the buildable areas were large enough and positioned well enough that the properties were genuinely usable for residential purposes despite the high wetland percentage. THE THREE QUESTIONS TO ASK ON EVERY WETLANDS PROPERTY Question 1: WHERE Is the Buildable Ground? This is the single most important question. The LOCATION of the buildable ground relative to the road determines how severe the wetlands discount should be. Buildable ground at the FRONT of the property (near the road) is the best scenario because a buyer can build conveniently — short driveway, easy utility connections, immediate access. The wetlands in the back are essentially just 'extra land' that the buyer won't use much anyway. This warrants the SMALLEST discount. Buildable ground in the MIDDLE of the property is a moderate scenario. The buyer can build, but they need to construct a driveway

st 'extra land' that the buyer won't use much anyway. This warrants the SMALLEST discount. Buildable ground in the MIDDLE of the property is a moderate scenario. The buyer can build, but they need to construct a driveway through the front wetlands or around them to reach the buildable area. This costs money and adds inconvenience. Moderate discount. Buildable ground ONLY in the BACK of the property is the worst residential scenario (aside from no buildable ground at all). The buyer has to go THROUGH or AROUND all the wetlands to reach the building site. Long driveway, expensive utilities, and the front of the property — the most visible part — looks like swamp. That first impression kills buyer interest. Heavy discount. NO buildable ground at all (100% wetlands or only tiny high-ground pockets) means the property has zero residential value. It can only be marketed as a hunting/recreational property to a specialized buyer pool. Maximum discount. Question 2: HOW BIG Is the Buildable Area?

Not all buildable areas are created equal. A LARGE buildable pocket — say 10+ acres of dry, flat, usable ground — is genuinely valuable even if it is in the back of the property. A buyer can work with a large buildable area. They have room for a house, a yard, outbuildings, and space to work. A SMALL buildable pocket — just a tiny high-ground corner where you can barely fit a septic system — is worth much less because it is cramped, limited, and feels like you are building on the one tiny island in a swamp. The user specifically taught this on the 40ac Deaver property. The right lot (~20ac) had wetlands at the front but a HUGE buildable pocket in the back. The user discounted only 30% — more than the left lot (20% discount, dry at front) but less than a typical heavy-wetlands discount — specifically BECAUSE the buildable pocket was large. If that same lot had only a tiny buildable corner in the back instead of a large open area, the discount would have been 40-50%+. The size of the buildable area directly drives the discount percentage. Question 3: Can You Split Dry From Wet? This is the creative value-add question. If the wetlands are concentrated on one side of the property and the dry ground is concentrated on the other, you might be able to create two lots: one clean lot (the dry portion) and one hunting lot (the wet portion). This can dramatically increase total value because the clean lot commands near-full market value while the hunting lot adds bonus value on top. Even though the total wetlands haven't changed, the per-lot presentation is much more appealing to buyers. If the wetlands are scattered throughout the property and you CANNOT cleanly split dry from wet, you are stuck evaluating the property as a whole. In this case, the discount ranges from 10-40%

WETLAND DISCOUNT FRAMEWORK — FULL DETAIL Under 20% Wetlands in the Back — ~5% Discount This is essentially a non-issue for pricing. The reasoning: nobody builds in the far back corner of their property. When a buyer visits a 25-acre property and sees 20 acres of clean, usable land at the front and middle, with a small wet area in the far back corner, their reaction is 'great property with a little wet spot in the back that I'll never use anyway.' The wetlands don't interfere with any realistic use of the property — the buyer can build near the road, have a big yard, and never even visit the wet corner. The ~5% discount is more of a formality than a real value impact. It acknowledges that technically the buyer is getting slightly less usable land than a fully dry property of the same acreage. But in practice, the buyer doesn't care about that back corner, and neither does the market. Properties with under 20% wetlands in the back sell at essentially the same prices as fully dry properties. ~50% Wetlands with Dry/Buildable Ground at the FRONT — ~20% Discount This is the most favorable scenario for a property with significant wetlands. The reasoning: the buyer drives up to the property and sees usable land RIGHT AT THE FRONT, near the road, where building is most convenient. They can envision their house, their driveway, their yard — all at the front where it makes sense. The wetlands are BEHIND them, in the back half, where they would not have built regardless of whether it was wet or dry. The 20% discount reflects the reality that the buyer is only getting about half the usable acreage of a fully dry property. They are paying for 40 acres but can only practically use 20 acres of it. However, the 20 acres they CAN use are the 20 acres that MATTER — the front portion near

However, the 20 acres they CAN use are the 20 acres that MATTER — the front portion near the road. So the discount is relatively modest despite the high wetland percentage. The location of the buildable ground makes all the difference. Practical example: on the 40ac Deaver property, the LEFT lot (~20ac) had dry buildable ground at the front near the road. The user applied approximately a 20% discount to this lot. Even though there were wetlands elsewhere on the lot, the critical front area was clean and the lot presented well to potential buyers. ~50% Wetlands with Big Buildable Pocket in the BACK — ~30% Discount This scenario has the buildable ground in a LESS convenient location — the back instead of the front. The reasoning for the higher discount compared to front-buildable: the buyer has to go THROUGH or AROUND the wetlands to reach the buildable area. This means a longer driveway (more cost), running utilities farther from the road (more cost), and the building site is far from convenient access. The front of the property — the part visitors see first — might look wet, which creates a negative first impression. However, the discount is LESS than you might expect for a half-wetlands property because the buildable pocket is LARGE. A large open area in the back — say 8-10 acres of dry, flat ground — is genuinely usable. It is not just a tiny corner where you can barely fit a house. A buyer with vision can see the potential: build an access road through or around the wetlands, and you have a beautiful, private building site with a big open area surrounded by natural wetlands (which some buyers actually find appealing for privacy and

tential: build an access road through or around the wetlands, and you have a beautiful, private building site with a big open area surrounded by natural wetlands (which some buyers actually find appealing for privacy and wildlife viewing). The user specifically taught this on the 40ac Deaver property. The RIGHT lot (~20ac) had wetlands at the front but a HUGE buildable area in the back. The user discounted approximately 30%. The key word the user used was 'huge' — the size of the buildable pocket was the specific reason the discount was only 30% instead of 40-50%. ~50% Wetlands Scattered Throughout — 10-40% Discount This is the hardest scenario to evaluate because you cannot cleanly separate dry from wet. The wetlands are mixed throughout the property — patches of wet ground intermixed with patches of dry ground, with no clear line between them. You cannot lot-split to create one clean lot and one wet lot because every resulting lot would have wetlands scattered through it. The discount range is wide (10-40%) because it entirely depends on one critical question: is there a contiguous buildable area large enough for a house plus a septic system? If yes — if there is a real, usable, connected patch of dry ground somewhere on the property even though wetlands surround it — discount on the lower end (10-20%). The buyer has a buildable spot; it is just surrounded by inconvenience. If the buildable ground is fragmented into tiny, disconnected pockets that are each too small to practically use, discount on the higher end (30-40%). The property becomes much harder to use for any residential purpose.

In the scattered scenario, use common sense. Look at the satellite imagery with hazards off. Can you visually identify a CLEAR building site? Or does it look like a patchwork with no obvious place to build? Your eyes will tell you more than the wetland percentage number. Wetlands in ONE Corner of the Front — ~15-20% Discount This is an important distinction from wetlands across the ENTIRE front. If the wetlands occupy just one corner of the front area — say the front-left quadrant — the MAJORITY of the frontage is still buildable. The middle and right side of the front are still clean and accessible. The buyer can build in the center or on the dry side of the front, and the wet corner is just an inconvenience in one area. This came up on the 40ac Deaver property where the left/front lot had wetlands in the front-left area with additional wet areas in the back. The user noted that the middle and right side were still buildable — the wetlands limited one specific area but didn't block the entire front. The discount was approximately 15-20%, reflecting the partial limitation. Front/Road Access Completely Blocked by Wetlands — 50-80% Discount This is the worst-case scenario for a residential property. If wetlands cover the ENTIRE front of the property along the road, the buyer cannot build anywhere near the road. The most convenient and valuable part of the property — the road-accessible front — is unusable. The buyer is forced to build deep into the property, which means: an extremely long driveway (expensive), running utilities a long distance from the road (expensive), the building site is far from emergency access (safety concern), and the front of the property — what everyone sees — is swamp (terrible first impression). Very few buyers are willing to accept this level

ad (expensive), the building site is far from emergency access (safety concern), and the front of the property — what everyone sees — is swamp (terrible first impression). Very few buyers are willing to accept this level of inconvenience and expense for a building site. The buyer pool shrinks dramatically, and those who are willing expect a significant price reduction. Hence the 50-80% discount range. The exact discount depends on how deep the wetlands extend (just a band along the road vs most of the property) and whether there is any reasonable path through or around them to reach dry ground in the back. 100% Wetlands — Hunting/Recreational Only — 30-40% Off Wetland Comp Sales If a property is 100% wetlands with no buildable area at all, it has ZERO residential value. You cannot build a house. You cannot install a septic system. The land has no residential utility whatsoever. The only market is hunting/recreational buyers — people who specifically want swamp land for duck hunting, deer hunting, fishing, or just recreational ownership of wild land. For these properties, you should NOT comp against clean residential land sales. Instead, try to find other wetland property sales in the area — properties that sold specifically as hunting/recreational land. Those are your comparable sales because the buyers were in the same buyer pool (recreational, not residential). If you find wetland comp sales, discount 30-40% from those to be conservative. If no wetland comps exist, you are making the market — apply a heavy discount to clean land comps and price conservatively. Minimum lot size for a marketable pure wetlands hunting property: approximately 20 ACRES at the absolute minimum, preferably 20+ acres.
