THE PERC TEST — THE #1 QUESTION FOR WETLANDS PROPERTIES What a Perc Test Is and Why It Matters A percolation test (perc test) determines whether the soil on a property can absorb wastewater from a septic system. The test involves digging holes, filling them with water, and measuring how quickly the water drains. If the soil drains properly (passes perc), you can install a standard septic system at normal cost — typically $5,000-$15,000 depending on the area and system type. If the soil does NOT drain properly (fails perc), you need an engineered or mound septic system. An engineered/mound septic system costs $10,000 to $40,000+ depending on the area, the severity of the drainage problems, and the type of system required. This is a MASSIVE additional cost for the end buyer — potentially $20,000-$30,000 more than a standard septic. This kills value not just because of the dollar amount, but because of the UNCERTAINTY and HASSLE. The buyer doesn't know exactly how much it will cost until engineers evaluate the site. They don't know if it will even work. The whole process takes longer and involves more experts. Many buyers simply walk away from properties that need engineered septic because the uncertainty is too high. When to Actually Worry About Perc Here is the nuance: perc is NOT a concern on every wetlands property. If the dry portion of the property is clearly large enough to perc and build on — like a solid 10+ acres of dry, flat ground — perc is not even worth mentioning as an issue. There is OBVIOUSLY buildable ground with adequate drainage. The perc test would pass easily on the dry portion. Perc only becomes a REAL concern when you are questioning whether there is ANY buildable spot at all. This typically means 80%+ wetlands properties where you are looking at a

rc test would pass easily on the dry portion. Perc only becomes a REAL concern when you are questioning whether there is ANY buildable spot at all. This typically means 80%+ wetlands properties where you are looking at a small high-ground corner and wondering: is this corner big enough to pass perc? Is the soil here adequate or is it just marginally less wet than the surrounding swamp? On these properties, the perc test is the difference between 'buildable with heavy discount' and 'completely unbuildable hunting property.' It is the #1 question that determines the property's use case and therefore its value category. Also important: Land Insights wetlands data is NOT always 100% accurate. A property might show 30% wetlands on the overlay but look completely dry and normal on satellite. This is DECEIVING because the wetlands could still be present in the SOIL CONDITIONS — poor drainage, high water table, clay layers that prevent absorption — even though the surface looks dry. The reverse can also happen: a property might show low wetlands but actually have wet areas that the data missed. Don't blindly trust the percentage — use it as a starting point and then verify with your eyes on satellite, toggling hazards off to see the actual land condition. The Description Red Flag — 'Talk to Realtor About Perc Test' This is one of the most important things the user taught about perc tests, and it relates to COMP EVALUATION, not just subject property analysis. When you are reading a comp's listing description on Zillow or Redfin, pay close attention to any language about perc tests or soil testing. If the description says 'PERC TEST PASSED' or 'SEPTIC APPROVED' — that is a strong POSITIVE. The property is confirmed buildable. The seller and listing agent are advertising this

nguage about perc tests or soil testing. If the description says 'PERC TEST PASSED' or 'SEPTIC APPROVED' — that is a strong POSITIVE. The property is confirmed buildable. The seller and listing agent are advertising this because it removes buyer uncertainty and adds value. A comp with a passed perc test is a stronger, more valuable comp, and its PPA reflects the reduced risk. If the description says 'TALK TO REALTOR ABOUT PERC TEST' or 'CONTACT SELLER ABOUT SOIL CONDITIONS' — that is almost certainly a RED FLAG indicating something is wrong. Think about it logically: if the property PASSED the perc test, why would they NOT put that in the description? A passed perc test is a selling point that every agent would want to advertise. The fact that they instead want you to CALL about it means they know the truth would scare buyers away if written down. They want the opportunity to convince you VERBALLY over the phone — to spin it, downplay it, or frame it positively in a way that text alone cannot accomplish. When you see this language in a comp description, it explains a low PPA — the property likely has perc issues and buyers discounted it accordingly. Flag it in your deliverable and note that this comp's low PPA is likely due to soil/perc concerns based on the description language.

LOT-SPLITTING WETLANDS PROPERTIES — THE PRESENTATION TRICK Why Lot-Splitting Changes Buyer Perception This is one of the most powerful insights the user taught about wetlands, and it is worth understanding deeply. Imagine a 40-acre property with 50% wetlands listed on the market as one whole parcel. The listing says '40 acres, 50% wetlands.' A buyer looks at this listing and thinks: 'Half this property is swamp. That's a problem property. I don't want to deal with that.' The realtor looks at it and thinks: 'This is going to be hard to sell. Half of it is unusable.' The satellite shows a property that is half blue (wetland overlay) and half green. The overall impression is NEGATIVE. Now imagine you take that SAME 40-acre property and split it into two 20-acre lots. Lot A is the dry portion — maybe it has 10-15% wetlands along one edge. Lot B is the wet portion — 80%+ wetlands, marketed as a hunting property. Lot A's listing says '20 acres, mostly cleared, minor wetlands along back edge.' A buyer looks at this and thinks: 'Nice 20-acre lot with a small wet area in the back that I won't use. Looks good.' The satellite shows a property that is mostly green with a small blue strip along one edge. The overall impression is POSITIVE. The TOTAL WETLANDS have not changed. You did not remove a single square foot of swamp. The same amount of wet ground exists. But the PER-LOT PRESENTATION is dramatically different. Instead of one scary '50% wetlands' property that makes buyers hesitate and realtors cringe, you have one attractive 'mostly clean with minor back wetlands' property that sells relatively quickly PLUS one hunting property that appeals to a specific buyer pool who actually WANTS swamp land. The user specifically made this point: 'As-is, that property with a bunch of

wetlands' property that sells relatively quickly PLUS one hunting property that appeals to a specific buyer pool who actually WANTS swamp land. The user specifically made this point: 'As-is, that property with a bunch of wetlands did not look good at all. Compared to if we lot-split it — yes, they're still going to have the same amount of wetlands, but on a per property basis, it looks a lot more appealing.' Marketing is everything in land sales. The same physical reality, presented differently, generates dramatically different buyer responses. How to Split: Clean Lot + Hunting Lot When the wetlands are concentrated on one side of the property and the dry ground is concentrated on the other, the optimal split is: one 'clean' lot containing the majority of the dry/buildable land, and one 'hunting' lot containing the majority of the wetlands. The clean lot should include enough dry ground to be genuinely attractive for residential building — ideally with road frontage, buildable area near the road, and minimal wetlands (just a small percentage along back or side edges). The hunting lot is marketed directly to recreational buyers — hunters, wildlife enthusiasts, people who want a camp property for weekends. Price it at hunting property values, which are significantly lower than residential. The minimum size for a marketable hunting lot is approximately 20 acres — below that, there isn't enough land for meaningful hunting activity and the buyer pool shrinks to nearly zero. What If the Split Creates an 80%+ Wetlands Back Lot? If lot-splitting creates a back lot that is 80%+ wetlands, that lot is essentially a hunting property with no residential value. You cannot build a house on it. It should be priced at hunting property values — comp against similar wetland/hunting sales

lot that is 80%+ wetlands, that lot is essentially a hunting property with no residential value. You cannot build a house on it. It should be priced at hunting property values — comp against similar wetland/hunting sales in the area, discounted 30-40% from those. Don't try to market it as residential — the buyer pool for 'buy 20 acres of swamp and try to build a house' is essentially zero. But the buyer pool for 'buy 20 acres of hunting land with great duck habitat' is real, especially in areas with outdoor recreation culture. Spreading Wetlands Evenly vs Concentrating — Which Is Better? Generally better to create one clean and one wet lot rather than two mediocre lots that BOTH have significant wetlands problems. The math: one clean lot at near-full value ($X) plus one hunting lot at hunting value ($Y) typically generates more total revenue than two lots that are both discounted 30-40% because they both have '25% wetlands scattered throughout.' The reason: buyers see '25% wetlands scattered throughout' and think 'I don't know where I can build on this — the wetlands are everywhere.' That uncertainty scares them. But buyers see 'clean lot, minor wetlands in back' and think 'I know exactly where I'll build — at the front where it's dry.' Certainty sells. Uncertainty discounts.

VISUAL ASSESSMENT — WHAT TO LOOK FOR ON SATELLITE Always Toggle Hazards OFF — See With Your Own Eyes The wetland overlay on Land Insights (the blue shading) can hide important features underneath — buildable areas, structures, ponds, roads, cleared spots. You MUST toggle hazards OFF and look at the actual satellite imagery with your own eyes. What you see with hazards off tells you more than the overlay percentage. The overlay is a starting point; your visual assessment confirms or adjusts the story. Identifying Marshy/Severe Wetlands vs Mild/Seasonal Wetlands Severe wetlands are visible on satellite even without the overlay. They look DARK, BUSHY, and almost swampy. The vegetation is thick, dark-colored (dark green to brownish-green), and UNEVEN — it clearly does NOT look like normal grass, maintained land, or regular forest. The surface may appear bumpy or irregular. Standing water might be visible as dark patches. The overall impression is: this looks like a SWAMP. You can visibly tell the difference between normal land and marsh. Mild or seasonal wetlands can look completely NORMAL and DRY on satellite. The surface appears like regular grass or forest — there is nothing visually alarming. This is DECEIVING because the wetlands are in the SOIL CONDITIONS — poor drainage, high water table, clay layers — rather than on the surface. The land looks fine from above but the soil cannot support a standard septic system. This is why you cannot assume a property is fine just because the satellite looks dry — the wetland designation might still be accurate based on soil testing and hydrology data that you cannot see from above. When the satellite looks dry but Land Insights flags significant wetlands, the property could go either way. The wetlands might be mild/seasonal (the

sed on soil testing and hydrology data that you cannot see from above. When the satellite looks dry but Land Insights flags significant wetlands, the property could go either way. The wetlands might be mild/seasonal (the land is usable most of the year and only wet during heavy rain), or they might be real but invisible from the surface (the soil is the problem, not the surface). You cannot determine this from satellite alone — that is why perc tests exist. Note the discrepancy in your deliverable and flag it for the user. Creeks and Wetland Bands When a creek runs through a property, it almost always creates a WETLAND BAND along both sides. The soil adjacent to the creek stays wet from the water flow, creating a strip of wetlands following the creek's path. This band might not be flagged by Land Insights but you should assume it exists. Be CONSERVATIVE with creeks — assume there are wetlands along the creek even if the overlay doesn't show them. The creek itself could be a minor water feature value-add (creek is the lowest tier in the waterfront hierarchy — small premium). But the wetlands band it creates could REDUCE buildable area or even cut the property in half if the creek runs through the middle. Evaluate both: the creek premium (small positive) versus the wetlands band (negative). The net impact depends on how wide the wetlands band is and whether it disrupts the primary buildable areas near the road.

COMPING WETLANDS PROPERTIES Finding Wetland Comps — The Best Approach The best approach for comping a wetlands property is to find OTHER properties with wetlands that have sold in the same county. These are the most comparable because the buyers who purchased those properties already factored in the wetlands issues — they knew about the wet areas, the limitations, the potential perc problems, and they paid a price that reflected all of that. Comparing your subject to these wetland comps gives you the most accurate picture of what the market pays for impaired land in this specific area. If you can find wetland comps AND clean comps in the same area, you get the best of both worlds. The difference between what clean land sold for and what wetland land sold for tells you the MARKET'S ACTUAL wetlands discount in this specific county. That might be 15%, it might be 30%, it might be 50% — the point is the market tells you rather than you guessing. If the market says wetlands in this area cost 20% off clean land, and your subject has similar wetlands, apply that 20% and feel confident. When No Wetland Comps Exist — Making Your Own Market If there are no wetland property sales in the county, you are making your own market. Comp against clean land sales and apply your discount percentages based on the framework in this guide: under 20% back = ~5%, front buildable = ~20%, big back pocket = ~30%, etc. Be CONSERVATIVE — better to price slightly under market than to overprice a wetlands property that sits for months. But don't discount so aggressively that you turn a potential deal into a non-deal. That would be leaving money on the table. Wetlands Properties Sit Longer on Market Even aggressively priced wetlands properties may take longer to sell than clean land. This is inherent

a potential deal into a non-deal. That would be leaving money on the table. Wetlands Properties Sit Longer on Market Even aggressively priced wetlands properties may take longer to sell than clean land. This is inherent to the product — wetlands scare some buyers and some realtors, and the buyer pool is smaller. Factor this into your list price by pricing slightly more aggressively than you would for clean land. The properties WILL sell, especially with elite drone marketing focused on the buildable portions, but expect higher DOM than comparable clean properties in the same area. MARKETING WETLANDS PROPERTIES — DRONE FOOTAGE STRATEGY Focus on the buildable portions. When creating drone footage for a wetlands property, emphasize where a buyer WOULD build: the dry areas near the road, the cleared buildable spots, the nice features of the usable portions. Show where the driveway would go, where the house could sit, what the views look like from the building site. If you show the wetlands prominently in the drone footage, it scares buyers away — they see swamp and move on. The realtor factor. Some realtors will push buyers away from wetlands properties, especially those that lack a perc test. They don't want to waste their time on a property that might not be buildable. Having a perc test done (or strong evidence the dry portion can perc) removes that objection and keeps realtors engaged. Without a perc test, realtors might not even show the property to their clients. Lot-split marketing. If you have split a wetlands property, the clean lot markets normally — like any other clean rural lot. The hunting lot markets to a completely different buyer pool: hunters, outdoor enthusiasts, recreational land buyers. Don't try to sell a swamp lot to someone looking for a homesite.

The user's evaluation: The user applied a 30-35% discount on the wetland portions. The reasoning was specific to this property's layout: the right/top portion has some dry area where you could potentially carve out a few buildable lots. The left/top portion does NOT look good — heavy wetlands with limited buildable ground. The bottom portion is buildable at the front, which means you could potentially create a few lots there with road access. Two critical factors influenced the user's discount decision: First, the massive 5,597 ft of road frontage across multiple roads gives MANY access points. Even with wetlands in certain areas, you have options for where to create lot access from different roads. This flexibility is worth a lot because it means you aren't stuck with one bad access point — you can find the best dry spots along the extensive frontage. Second, smaller lots were selling in the surrounding area, which confirmed market demand for the lot sizes that would result from splitting the dry portions. Mary Seabrook Rd — Edisto Island, SC (79% Wetlands + 100% Flood Zone) Situation: 5.64 acres on Edisto Island, Charleston County, SC. 79% wetlands, 100% flood zone (in three different flood zone types). This property came up in the context of learning about flood zones, but the wetlands component is equally

cres on Edisto Island, Charleston County, SC. 79% wetlands, 100% flood zone (in three different flood zone types). This property came up in the context of learning about flood zones, but the wetlands component is equally important. Initial WRONG evaluation: The initial valuation applied a massive 60-70% discount from clean area comps, treating the combined 79% wetlands and 100% flood zone as devastating. This resulted in a price around $75,000. What went wrong: The evaluation PANIC-DISCOUNTED without looking around first. The ENTIRE island is in a flood zone — every property on Edisto Island is 100% flood zone. Everyone builds there anyway because that is the only option. A house was visible on or near the subject property but was MISSED because hazards weren't toggled off to check. The comps from Edisto Island already reflect the flood zone and wetland reality — they were all in the same conditions. Applying an ADDITIONAL wetlands/flood discount on top of comps that already factored in those conditions amounted to double-discounting. The lesson for wetlands: ALWAYS look around before discounting. If surrounding properties have houses despite similar wetland conditions, the market has priced in the wetlands and your discount should be modest. If you use comps from the same area, those comps already reflect the local wetland reality — don't apply a further wetlands discount on top of them.

QUICK REFERENCE — WETLANDS DISCOUNT TABLE Under 20% wetlands in back: ~5% discount (barely matters) ~30% wetlands scattered: 10-20% discount (depends on buildable location) ~50% wetlands, dry/buildable at FRONT: ~20% discount ~50% wetlands, big buildable pocket in BACK: ~30% discount ~50% wetlands scattered, no clean split: 10-40% (depends on buildable areas) ~50% wetlands in ONE CORNER of front: ~15-20% discount Wetlands completely blocking front/road access: 50-80% discount 100% wetlands hunting property: Comp wetland sales, discount 30-40% from those Minimum hunting lot size: ~20 acres for marketability Perc concern threshold: Only when 80%+ wetlands and questioning ANY buildable spot Engineered/mound septic cost: $10,000-$40,000+ (area dependent) Description red flag: 'Talk to realtor about perc test' = likely failed or problem Description green flag: 'Perc test passed' = confirmed buildable, value-add --- END OF PART 2 --- Part 2 covers: Wetlands Evaluation (discount framework, perc tests, lot-splitting, visual assessment, comping wetlands, marketing, live examples)

DewClaw — Part 3: Subdivision & Value-Add Guide FULLY EXPANDED — Creative Subdivision Strategies for Maximum Value WHY SUBDIVISION IS THE #1 VALUE-ADD STRATEGY The DewClaw business model has two layers. Layer 1 is the simple flip: buy at 50% of as-is market value, sell slightly below market with elite drone marketing, target 7-90 day sales. Layer 2 is the value-add: if the seller rejects the 50% as-is offer, pitch subdivision — offer 50% of the IMPROVED (subdivided) value, which is higher than the as-is value. This often lands right where the seller wanted because the improved value lifts the 50% offer into an acceptable range. Subdivision is where the real money hides. The PPA curve is exponential — smaller lots have HIGHER PPA, larger lots have LOWER PPA. When you buy a large parcel at the lower PPA and sell smaller lots at the higher PPA, the spread is your profit multiplier. A 75-acre property at $4,000/ac ($300,000) split into ten 7.5-acre lots at $8,000/ac ($60,000 each) is worth $600,000 total — double the as-is value. That spread is what makes subdivision plays so powerful. But the user's core criticism was clear: Claude was way too conservative on subdivision. On the 75.91ac Dillon County property with 3,193ft of road frontage, only 2-3 lots were suggested when 5-10+ was the obvious play. That conservative thinking left hundreds of thousands of dollars on the table. The lesson: get CREATIVE. Look for the hidden value. More lots = more money, as long as demand exists for those lot sizes in the area. STEP 1: ALWAYS LOOK UP COUNTY SUBDIVISION ORDINANCE — NON-NEGOTIABLE Why This Is the First Step Every Single Time Before presenting ANY subdivision scenario, you MUST look up the county's subdivision ordinance. This is not optional and it is not something to skip

RDINANCE — NON-NEGOTIABLE Why This Is the First Step Every Single Time Before presenting ANY subdivision scenario, you MUST look up the county's subdivision ordinance. This is not optional and it is not something to skip because you are in a hurry. If you pitch a subdivision plan that violates the county's rules — like suggesting 12 lots in a county that only allows 5 before triggering major subdivision review — the user wastes time and effort pursuing an impossible plan. That destroys trust and credibility. Google '[County Name] [State] minor subdivision ordinance' or '[County Name] subdivision ordinance.' You are looking for specific answers to these questions: How many lots can you create before triggering MAJOR subdivision review? This threshold varies by county but is typically 5-10 lots. What is the MINIMUM lot size allowed? Some counties require minimum 2 acres, others 5 acres, others have no minimum. What are the ROAD FRONTAGE requirements per lot? Many counties require minimum frontage (50ft, 100ft, etc.) for each new lot. What are the FLAGPOLE LOT rules? Minimum width for the access strip, maximum depth, any special requirements. Minor vs Major Subdivision MINOR subdivision (typically 5-10 lots depending on county) is the preferred path. It involves simpler approval processes, usually just a plat review by the planning office. Timeline: approximately 1-3 months from application to approval. Less red tape, fewer requirements, faster execution. This is what the DewClaw model targets. MAJOR subdivision (above the minor threshold) requires significantly more approvals — planning commission review, potentially public hearings, environmental review, possibly road construction requirements, stormwater management plans, and longer timelines. This is more complex and

icantly more approvals — planning commission review, potentially public hearings, environmental review, possibly road construction requirements, stormwater management plans, and longer timelines. This is more complex and expensive. HOWEVER — if the upside is significant, ALWAYS present major subdivision as an option. Don't withhold the numbers just because it is major. The user will decide if the upside justifies the complexity. Always flag 'this would be a major subdivision' prominently so the user knows what they are getting into. Exempt Plats — The Easiest Path Some states have exempt plat provisions that bypass subdivision approval entirely. South Carolina, for example, has a 5-acre exempt rule: if every resulting lot is 5+ acres AND no new public infrastructure (roads, water, sewer) is required, the subdivision is EXEMPT from formal approval. You just need a survey and a recorded plat. This is the fastest, cheapest, simplest path to

subdivision. Always check if the state has an exempt plat provision. If it does, and your lot sizes qualify, note it prominently in your deliverable: 'SC 5-acre exempt plat rule applies — lots at 5ac+ require no subdivision approval, just survey and plat recording.' This dramatically reduces the user's execution risk and timeline. If You Cannot Find the Ordinance Online Sometimes the county's subdivision ordinance is not available online — smaller counties especially may not have digitized their regulations. If you cannot find it after reasonable searching: note 'Could not find [County] subdivision ordinance online — verify with county planning office before proceeding' and STILL present your subdivision scenarios. Don't hold back the analysis. The user can call the county planning office to verify the rules. But the scenarios themselves — the lot configurations, the potential values, the demand analysis — are still valuable for the user's decision-making even without confirmed rules.

STEP 2: CHECK DEMAND — WHAT LOT SIZES ARE ACTUALLY SELLING? This Is the Most Important Factor for Lot Size Selection The user emphasized this multiple times during training: the MOST IMPORTANT thing when considering subdivision is looking at what lot sizes are actually selling in the surrounding area. You need to look around on the map and answer these questions: What size lots exist in the area with HOUSES on them? This tells you what buyers are actually purchasing and using. Are similar-sized vacant lots SELLING in the MLS comps? This confirms active market demand at that lot size. How quickly are those lots selling (DOM)? Low DOM = strong demand. High DOM = weak demand at that price/size. The biggest subdivision mistake — the one the user specifically called out — is splitting into lots that are TOO SMALL for the area. If every lot in the surrounding area is 10+ acres with nice homes, and you create 2-acre lots, those lots might sit on the market for months or years because nobody in that rural area wants 2 acres. The market is telling you '10 acres is what people want here' and you ignored it. Conversely, if you are near a city and surrounding lots are 2-5 acres with active development, then 5-acre lots make perfect sense and smaller might work too. Lot Size Preferences The user's preferred lot size range for the DewClaw business model is 5-10 ACRES for rural recreational properties. This is the sweet spot — large enough for hunting, building a cabin, having privacy and space. Small enough to be affordable for most buyers and to generate meaningful PPA uplift from the subdivision. For properties closer to cities where development pressure exists and smaller lots are common, 2-5 ACRES can work well. The demand for smaller lots increases as you get closer to urban

ul PPA uplift from the subdivision. For properties closer to cities where development pressure exists and smaller lots are common, 2-5 ACRES can work well. The demand for smaller lots increases as you get closer to urban areas because land is more expensive and buyers accept smaller parcels for convenience. The ABSOLUTE MINIMUM lot size the user prefers is 2 ACRES. Below 2 acres, you are getting into entitlement play territory — this is outside the core DewClaw rural recreational model. However, if you see strong demand for 1-acre lots in a specific area (lots of 1-acre lots with houses, active sales at good PPAs), mention it as an option. The user is open to hearing about it even if it is outside the normal model, but default to 5-10 acres for your recommended scenario. How to Present Lot Size Recommendations — Three Game Plans For every subdivision play, present THREE scenarios and a RECOMMENDED option: Option A — Fewer, bigger lots: The conservative approach. Fewer lots at larger sizes (closer to what is selling in the area). Safer demand, simpler execution, probably stays within minor subdivision threshold. Lower total revenue but higher certainty of quick sales. Option B — More, smaller lots: The aggressive approach. Maximum lot count at smaller sizes that still have market support. Higher total revenue potential but requires more market demand and might push into major subdivision territory. More lots to sell = longer total timeline. Recommended — Your best judgment: The scenario you believe best balances lot count, market demand, execution complexity, county rules, and total profitability. ALWAYS explain WHY you are recommending it. Example: 'I recommend the 6-lot split at ~12.5ac each because 10-15ac lots are selling with houses in the area, it stays under the

exity, county rules, and total profitability. ALWAYS explain WHY you are recommending it. Example: 'I recommend the 6-lot split at ~12.5ac each because 10-15ac lots are selling with houses in the area, it stays under the minor subdivision limit of 10 lots in this county, and the total value of ~$675,000 represents significant upside over the as-is value of ~$450,000.'

STEP 3: CREATIVE LOT CONFIGURATIONS — THINK LIKE A DEVELOPER Road Frontage Is the Key to Lot Count Road frontage determines how many lots can have DIRECT road access. More frontage = more lots with their own road-facing entrance. The calculation is simple: total road frontage divided by minimum frontage per lot (from county ordinance) = maximum lot count from frontage alone. Example: 1,000ft of road frontage with a county minimum of 100ft per lot = up to 10 lots with direct road access. Two sides of road frontage is a HUGE advantage. If a property has frontage on TWO different roads, you can create lots fronting each road, effectively doubling your lot count potential. The user emphasized this: the more road frontage you have, the more lots you can make, and the more money you can make. Always check all four sides of a property for road frontage — not just the obvious front road. Flagpole Lots — The Hidden Value Play Most People Miss This was one of the most important concepts the user taught during the entire training session. Before this lesson, the instinct was to look at a property with limited road frontage and say 'not enough frontage to subdivide.' The user completely changed that thinking. A FLAGPOLE LOT is a lot with a narrow access strip (the 'flagpole handle') connecting the road to a wider buildable area deeper in the property (the 'flag'). The access strip is typically 30-60ft wide (depending on county ordinance) — just wide enough for a driveway. The buildable area in the back can be as large as the available acreage allows. Example: a 40-acre property with only 200ft of total road frontage. The old thinking: 'Only 200ft, can't subdivide — maybe 2 lots at best.' The NEW thinking: create 4 flagpole lots with 50ft access strips each, each lot being

le: a 40-acre property with only 200ft of total road frontage. The old thinking: 'Only 200ft, can't subdivide — maybe 2 lots at best.' The NEW thinking: create 4 flagpole lots with 50ft access strips each, each lot being approximately 10 acres extending deep into the property. Four 10-acre lots at a higher PPA than one 40-acre lot = significant total value increase. The access strips are narrow but functional — buyers can drive to their building site. The user specifically said: these are the things that are 'hidden in plain sight' that most people — including Claude initially — completely miss. Properties with limited frontage but significant depth are flagpole subdivision opportunities. When you see a deep property with limited frontage, your FIRST thought should be 'flagpole lots,' not 'can't subdivide.' If you can identify these opportunities, you are finding value that the vast majority of people cannot see, and that is exactly where the money is made. Always check the county ordinance for minimum flagpole access width before presenting. Some counties require 30ft, some 50ft, some 60ft. The width requirement determines how many flagpole lots you can create from the available frontage. Building a Road Through the Property When a property has enough acreage and demand for many lots but not enough road frontage for all of them, building a PRIVATE ROAD through the property creates interior lots. This is a higher-complexity play but can unlock massive value on large properties. Road type: the user always does DIRT or GRAVEL roads. Paved roads are too expensive for most deals. However, if the county ordinance REQUIRES a paved road for subdivision AND the upside is big enough, the user is open to hearing the numbers and deciding. Always check what the county requires.

expensive for most deals. However, if the county ordinance REQUIRES a paved road for subdivision AND the upside is big enough, the user is open to hearing the numbers and deciding. Always check what the county requires. Road width: wide enough for two cars to pass each other going in opposite directions. Check county ordinances for exact requirements — typically around 20-30 feet. This is the same width as a standard road. Interior lot discount: lots fronting the new private gravel road typically sell for about 5-10% LESS than lots fronting the existing public paved road. This mirrors the general road hierarchy (gravel < paved). Not all lots in the subdivision are worth the same PPA — factor this into your per-lot value estimates. Cost estimation: simultaneously open another tab and look up 'gravel road cost per linear foot [county or state].' Use the Land Insights ruler/measuring tool to measure the approximate road distance through the property. Present the cost estimate RIGHT NEXT TO the subdivision scenario. Format example: '8-lot split: Total value ~$500K | Road cost estimate: ~$20,000 (800ft gravel at $25/ft) | Minor subdivision.' Do NOT subtract costs from the total value — just list them alongside so the user can evaluate the full picture. Lot Lines Don't Have to Be Perfect

Lots do NOT have to be equal sizes. A split creating one 20ac lot and three 8ac lots is perfectly acceptable if that is what maximizes value and matches market demand. The terrain, shape, and features of the property should guide the lot configuration, not a desire for symmetry. Lot lines can be DIAGONAL if needed to maximize usable lots or follow natural features. They don't have to be perpendicular or parallel to the road. However, don't create WEIRD shapes that would confuse or turn off buyers. Each lot should still look like a usable, reasonable piece of property. The shape golden ratio for each lot is 1:2 to 1:4 width-to-depth. Maximum acceptable is 1:5 or 1:6. Skinnier than that creates 'bowling alley lots' that lose significant value. Split along NATURAL FEATURES when it makes sense. If half the property is farmland and half is timbered, split along that natural dividing line to create one farmland lot and one timbered/recreational lot. Different land types appeal to different buyer pools, and separating them lets you market each lot to its ideal buyer.

STEP 4: THE PPA CURVE AND SUBDIVISION VALUE The fundamental concept: the PPA curve is EXPONENTIAL. Smaller lots have HIGHER PPA and larger lots have LOWER PPA. This is where subdivision money is made — you buy at the large-lot PPA and sell at the small-lot PPA. The STEEPER the curve (bigger difference between large and small lot PPAs), the more profitable the subdivision. ALWAYS verify PPA at the target lot size with ACTUAL COMPS. Don't just assume smaller = higher PPA based on theory. Pull comps at the lot size you are proposing and verify what the market actually pays. The curve theory is directionally correct (it always goes that direction) but the actual dollar amounts come from real sold comps at that specific lot size in that specific county. If the PPA curve is relatively FLAT in a county (small lots and large lots trade at similar PPA), subdivision upside is limited. But creating more sellable units still has SOME value — two $50K lots might sell faster than one $100K parcel because each individual lot is more affordable for buyers. Present the numbers even if the upside is modest. CRITICAL ERROR from the 75.91ac Dillon property: Claude initially said smaller lots would have LOWER PPA than larger lots — the exact OPPOSITE of how the exponential curve works. Smaller lots ALWAYS have higher PPA. This was a fundamental error that would have massively undervalued the subdivision play. Never get the curve direction backwards. STEP 5: WHEN NOT TO SUBDIVIDE Bowling alley lots: If splitting creates lots skinnier than 1:5 or 1:6 width-to-depth ratio, the resulting lots lose so much value from the bad shape that subdivision isn't worth it. This was taught on the Buckley Rd property (38.37ac, Susquehanna County, PA) — already a long narrow strip, splitting would create

the resulting lots lose so much value from the bad shape that subdivision isn't worth it. This was taught on the Buckley Rd property (38.37ac, Susquehanna County, PA) — already a long narrow strip, splitting would create unsellable bowling alley lots in a rural area with zero demand for that shape. No demand for smaller lots: If all surrounding lots are 40+ acres with no smaller lots selling anywhere in the area, creating 5-acre lots might not work. Selling one larger parcel quickly is better than sitting on 8 small lots nobody wants. This is the biggest subdivision mistake: splitting too small for the area because you got caught up in 'more lots = more money' without checking if there is actually demand for those smaller lots. Wetlands screw up every resulting lot: If wetlands are scattered throughout and every resulting lot would have significant wetlands problems, it might be cleaner to sell as one parcel. One property with 30% wetlands in the back is easier to market than four lots that all have scattered wetlands. Not enough profit margin: The user's general threshold: a $300K purchase price property where subdivision only yields $400K is barely worth the effort. Below that spread, the simple flip is faster, easier, and less risky. But STILL present the numbers even if marginal — the user can decide. Don't withhold the subdivision analysis. STEP 6: PRESENTING COSTS — ALWAYS SEPARATE Do NOT subtract costs from the total subdivision value. Present costs SEPARATELY right next to each scenario so the user can evaluate the full picture. Costs to estimate and present: Road construction (if building a new road) — look up gravel road cost per linear foot, measure distance with LI ruler tool. Survey/platting — the user handles this, don't estimate. Time — note whether

timate and present: Road construction (if building a new road) — look up gravel road cost per linear foot, measure distance with LI ruler tool. Survey/platting — the user handles this, don't estimate. Time — note whether minor (1-3 months) or major (longer). Format: '6-lot split: Total value ~$675,000 | Road cost estimate: ~$15,000 | Minor subdivision (1-3 month timeline).' NEVER PITCH SUBDIVISION TO THE SELLER The user was emphatic about this: NEVER tell the seller about your subdivision plans. Keep the strategy to yourself. There is no benefit to revealing your value-add play to the seller — it only gives them leverage to demand a higher price. The as-is offer is the starting point. The subdivision plan is your BACKUP strategy if the as-is offer gets rejected — you can then offer 50% of the improved value, which is naturally higher. But you never explain the subdivision to the seller. LIVE EXAMPLE — 75.91ac Dillon County, SC Property: 75.91 acres of farmland in Dillon County, SC. 3,193 ft of road frontage. Flat, clean (2% wetlands, 0% flood). Near the town of Dillon. SC 5-acre exempt plat rule applies.

Initial mistake: Only suggested 2-3 lot split. This was way too conservative for a property with 75.91 acres and 3,193 ft of road frontage. The massive frontage alone could support 10+ lots with direct road access. The SC 5-acre exempt rule means lots at 5ac+ need no subdivision approval — so up to 15 lots at 5ac each was theoretically possible. What should have been presented: Multiple scenarios including 5-6 lots (each ~12-15ac), 8-10 lots (each ~7.5-10ac), and potentially 12-15 lots (each ~5ac under the exempt rule). The surrounding area showed smaller lot sizes selling with houses — demand was confirmed. The massive road frontage supported many lots with direct access. PPA error: Initially stated smaller lots would have LOWER PPA — the exact opposite of how the exponential curve works. Smaller lots = HIGHER PPA. This fundamental error would have significantly undervalued the subdivision play and potentially caused the user to pass on a highly profitable deal. Lesson: On large properties with good frontage, think BIGGER on lot count. Check county exempt/minor subdivision rules. Look at what lot sizes sell nearby. Present multiple scenarios with a recommended option. And never get the PPA curve direction backwards. SUBDIVISION SHAPE RULE — BOWLING ALLEY LOTS Width-to-depth ratio golden range: 1:2 to 1:4. Maximum acceptable: 1:5 or 1:6. Skinnier than 1:6 = 'bowling alley lot' = significant value loss because the lot is too narrow to practically use near the road and forces all building activity deep into the lot. In rural areas where buyers want space and usability, bowling alley lots are particularly hard to sell. Before proposing ANY subdivision, check if the resulting lot shapes would be acceptable. This is especially important on narrow properties — the Buckley Rd

and usability, bowling alley lots are particularly hard to sell. Before proposing ANY subdivision, check if the resulting lot shapes would be acceptable. This is especially important on narrow properties — the Buckley Rd property (38.37ac, Susquehanna County, PA) was already long and narrow, and splitting it would have created bowling alley lots in a market with zero demand for that shape. The subdivision would have actually DESTROYED value instead of creating it. NEVER CREATE AN HOA FOR SUBDIVISION The user was clear: never suggest creating an HOA for a subdivision play. The DewClaw business model targets rural recreational buyers who want freedom — the ability to do whatever they want with their land. An HOA directly conflicts with that buyer pool. HOA restrictions limit what buyers can build, require ongoing fees, and eliminate the very buyers who are most likely to purchase rural recreational lots. HOA lots always sit longer on market for this reason. --- END OF PART 3 --- Part 3 covers: Subdivision & Value-Add (county ordinance lookup, demand analysis, flagpole lots, road-building, creative configurations, PPA curve, when NOT to subdivide, cost presentation, 75.91ac example, shape rules)

DewClaw — Part 4: Flood Zone & Slope Evaluation FULLY EXPANDED — Nuanced Framework for Flood Zones and Terrain FLOOD ZONE — THE 'LOOK AROUND FIRST' RULE Why Panic-Discounting Is Wrong The instinct when you see '100% flood zone' on Land Insights is to panic and apply a massive discount — 50%, 60%, even 70%. This is WRONG in many situations. The Edisto Island property (Mary Seabrook Rd, Charleston County, SC — 5.64ac, 79% wetlands, 100% flood zone) proved this emphatically. The initial valuation applied a 60-70% discount and came to approximately $75,000. The problem: the ENTIRE ISLAND is in a flood zone. Every single property, every house, every building on Edisto Island sits in a flood zone. People build there anyway because they have no other option — if you want to live on Edisto Island, you build in a flood zone. When everyone in the area is building in the flood zone, the flood zone is NOT a value killer — it is a COST FACTOR. The buyer expects to pay flood insurance (which can be significant — hundreds to thousands per year) and may need a raised foundation (additional construction cost). These are real costs that reduce the property's net value slightly. But they are manageable costs that thousands of people in flood zone areas accept every day. The market in these areas already has the flood zone cost baked into property prices. Your comps from flood zone areas already reflect this reality. The critical error on Edisto Island was double-discounting: applying a flood zone discount ON TOP of comps that were already in the same flood zone. Those comps already priced in the flood zone conditions. Adding another 30-50% discount on top was penalizing the property twice for the same condition. The #1 Rule: Check If People Are Building Before discounting for flood zone,

ps already priced in the flood zone conditions. Adding another 30-50% discount on top was penalizing the property twice for the same condition. The #1 Rule: Check If People Are Building Before discounting for flood zone, ALWAYS look around the area first. Toggle hazards on to see the flood zone boundaries, then look at the satellite imagery around your property. Are there HOUSES nearby in the same flood zone? If yes — people are building despite the flood zone designation. The market has accepted the flood risk and is pricing it as a cost factor, not a deal-breaker. Your discount should be modest: approximately 10-15%, reflecting the additional costs (insurance, raised foundation) the buyer will face. If there are NO houses nearby in the flood zone — nobody is building there — that is a very different signal. The market is telling you this area is not suitable for development. The flood risk may be too severe, the insurance costs too high, or the practical challenges of building too great. In this case, discount more heavily: 25-40% depending on severity. More often than not, flood zones do NOT have many houses in them. But SOMETIMES they do — coastal areas, islands, established riverfronts — and those 'sometimes' are the situations where panic-discounting costs you money. Partial Flood Zone — Same Logic as Wetlands If a property is partially in a flood zone (like 30% flood zone), the discount depends on WHERE the flood zone falls on the property. This is EXACTLY the same framework as wetlands evaluation: Flood zone in the FRONT/road area = bigger problem. The most convenient buildable area is affected. The buyer would need to build to flood-resistant standards in the most expensive location (near the road where construction costs are already highest due to utility

roblem. The most convenient buildable area is affected. The buyer would need to build to flood-resistant standards in the most expensive location (near the road where construction costs are already highest due to utility connections). Larger discount. Flood zone in the BACK = minor issue. The back of the property is rarely the primary building site. The flood zone doesn't interfere with convenient construction near the road. The buyer might not even notice the flood zone in the back corner. Smaller discount — same logic as wetlands in the back (~5-10%). Flood zone covering the middle or cutting through the property = moderate issue. It may not block the front entirely but creates complications for site planning. Moderate discount based on how much it impacts buildability. Comping Flood Zone Properties

Try to find other properties WITH flood zones that have sold in the area. These are the best comps because the buyers already factored in the flood zone conditions. Compare what flood zone properties sold for versus clean properties to understand the MARKET'S ACTUAL flood zone discount in that specific area. That market-determined discount is more accurate than any percentage you could guess. If you are comping in an area where EVERYTHING is in a flood zone (like Edisto Island, coastal areas, or riverfronts), then ALL your comps are already flood zone comps. In this case, do NOT apply an additional flood zone discount on top — the flood zone is already baked into every comp's price. Just comp normally using those flood zone comps as your data set. Different Flood Zone Types There are different flood zone designations (Zone A, Zone AE, Zone X, Zone V, etc.) that indicate different levels of flood risk and insurance requirements. Some properties can be in multiple flood zone types simultaneously. While each type has specific technical definitions regarding flood probability and insurance costs, for COMPING PURPOSES the main question remains the same: are people building in it or not? The market behavior tells you more about the practical impact than the zone designation letter. Note the zone type in your deliverable for reference, but don't get bogged down researching the technical meaning of each designation. Edisto Island Lesson — Full Story Property: Mary Seabrook Rd, Edisto Island, Charleston County, SC. 5.64 acres, 79% wetlands, 100% flood zone — actually in THREE different flood zone types. No purchase price or date available. Initial WRONG valuation: Applied 60-70% combined discount for wetlands + flood zone. Valued at approximately $75,000. The reasoning was: 79%

ually in THREE different flood zone types. No purchase price or date available. Initial WRONG valuation: Applied 60-70% combined discount for wetlands + flood zone. Valued at approximately $75,000. The reasoning was: 79% wetlands is devastating, 100% flood zone compounds the problem, very limited buildable area. What went wrong — three critical errors: First, panic-discounted without looking around. Houses were visible throughout Edisto Island — everyone builds in the flood zone because the entire island is flood zone. Second, a HOUSE was visible on or near the subject property but was MISSED because hazards weren't toggled off to check the actual satellite underneath the overlay. Third, the comps being used were from the same Edisto Island area — they were ALL in flood zones too. Applying a flood zone discount on top of flood zone comps was double-discounting. The correct approach: Recognize that Edisto Island flood zone = cost factor, not value killer. Toggle hazards OFF to see the actual property (revealing the house that was missed). Comp against other Edisto Island sales (which are all flood zone) without adding further flood zone discount. Apply a modest wetlands discount based on where the buildable ground is relative to the road.

SLOPE EVALUATION — LOCATION AND SEVERITY FRAMEWORK Basic Slope Discount Guidelines Under 10% average slope = no issue, no discount. This is essentially flat or gently rolling terrain that creates no building challenges. 10-20% average slope = 5-10% discount. Moderate slope that increases construction costs slightly (may need some grading, potentially a walk-out basement design) but is very buildable. 20-35% average slope = 15-30% discount. Significant slope that noticeably increases construction costs (retaining walls, special foundations, grading, potentially difficult driveway). 35%+ average slope = 30-50% discount. Severe slope that creates major building challenges — very expensive to construct, limited building options, potentially dangerous during construction. These are STARTING POINTS, not absolute rules. Always compare your subject's slope to the COMP slopes. If every property in a mountain area has 20% average slope, the market has already priced in that terrain. Your additional discount from those mountain comps should be small or zero. If your subject is significantly steeper than the comps in the area, THAT difference justifies additional discount. Slope Location — Same Framework as Wetlands and Flood Zones WHERE the slope is on the property matters enormously — the same framework applies as for wetlands and flood zones. The front/road area is the most critical because that is where access and convenient building happen. Steep slope in the FRONT of the property: This is the most severe problem — potentially 50-70% discount. If the front is super steep, the fundamental question is: how do you even ACCESS the property? Can you build a driveway through a 40% grade? Probably not safely or affordably. If you cannot drive to the buildable area, the property is

s super steep, the fundamental question is: how do you even ACCESS the property? Can you build a driveway through a 40% grade? Probably not safely or affordably. If you cannot drive to the buildable area, the property is essentially inaccessible for residential use. Even if there is beautiful flat ground in the back with amazing views, the buyer CANNOT GET THERE. This is fundamentally different from wetlands in the front — wetlands are flat but wet. A steep front slope is a PHYSICAL BARRIER that may be impossible to overcome regardless of budget. Flat front with steep slope in the BACK: Only 5-20% discount, depending on how steep the back is. You can build near the road where it is flat — short driveway, easy utility connections, convenient access. The steep back is just unused land, similar to wetlands in the back. Most buyers aren't going to build in the far back corner anyway. The back slope might actually be a POSITIVE if it creates elevated views — hilltop or ridgeline properties have view premiums (5-30%). But the slope discount and view premium partially offset each other. Average slope vs max slope: Care more about the AVERAGE slope, especially at the front where building would occur. A property with 5% average but 40% max in one back corner is mostly fine — the high slope is isolated in a location nobody would use. A property with 25% average slope throughout the entire property is a much bigger problem because every potential building site is steep. Slope and Septic — The Rocky Soil Connection Steep slopes create TWO problems for septic systems that directly impact property value. First, rocky exposed soil on slopes often does NOT drain well enough for the septic leach field. The soil is thin, rocky, and lacks the depth of organic material needed for proper

ems that directly impact property value. First, rocky exposed soil on slopes often does NOT drain well enough for the septic leach field. The soil is thin, rocky, and lacks the depth of organic material needed for proper wastewater absorption. This means the property may FAIL a perc test — and as covered in the Wetlands section, failing perc means needing an engineered/mound septic at $10,000-$40,000+ additional cost, which kills value. Second, physically INSTALLING a septic system on the side of a hill or mountain is difficult, expensive, and sometimes physically impossible. The excavation is harder, the system needs special engineering to handle the grade, and the leach field layout is constrained by the terrain. How do you build a level septic tank pad on a 30% slope? It requires significant earthwork. The combination of poor drainage soil + difficult installation makes steep slope + septic a double problem. The same property that has a 20% slope discount might face an ADDITIONAL perc/septic concern if the soil on the slope cannot support conventional septic. Flag this in your deliverable when you see significant slope — note in Section 9 (Questions to Ask Seller) that perc test status should be verified given the terrain. Slope and Subdivision You CAN still subdivide a hilly property — slope does not automatically kill the value-add play. But slope DOES affect the value of each resulting lot. If you create 5-acre lots on sloped terrain, each of those lots sells for less than equivalent 5-acre lots on flat terrain

because the buyer faces higher construction costs. Factor the slope discount into your per-lot PPA estimates, not just the overall property value. Slope can also affect WHERE you can place lot lines. Steep ravines, ridgelines, and cliff faces may create natural boundaries that influence lot configuration. Work with the terrain rather than fighting it — place lot lines along natural features where possible. Slope as a Positive — Views and Elevation In some situations, slope creates the desirable feature that gives a property premium value. A property on a hilltop or mountaintop with panoramic views can command significant premiums: mediocre/average view = ~5% premium. Good view with scenic value = 10-15% premium. Superman/panoramic unobstructed view = 20-30% premium. However, it is RARE to find truly great views that justify a large premium because you typically still need to: build a road up the slope (expensive and sometimes impossible on steep terrain), clear trees at the top so you can actually SEE the view (clearing costs), and create a building pad on the summit (grading costs on steep terrain). The view premium and the slope discount partially OFFSET each other. A mountaintop property with a 30% slope discount but a 25% view premium nets out to only about 5% discount — but a mountaintop property with a 30% slope discount and only a 5% 'meh' view premium nets out to 25% discount. Evaluate both factors and present the net effect. If contour lines or elevation data in Land Insights suggest the property sits in a high position with potential overlook views, flag it in Extra Notes. But don't overweight views — they are speculative from satellite (you don't know what the actual view looks like until you visit) and the costs to access them are real. QUICK REFERENCE —

ok views, flag it in Extra Notes. But don't overweight views — they are speculative from satellite (you don't know what the actual view looks like until you visit) and the costs to access them are real. QUICK REFERENCE — FLOOD ZONE & SLOPE DISCOUNTS Flood Zone: People building nearby = 10-15% discount (cost factor). No development in flood zone = 25-40% discount. Flood zone in front = bigger discount. Flood zone in back = minor. Entire area is flood zone with development = minimal additional discount (already priced in). Slope: Under 10% = none. 10-20% = 5-10%. 20-35% = 15-30%. 35%+ = 30-50%. Steep FRONT = 50-70% (can't access). Flat front / steep back = 5-20%. Slope + rocky soil = perc concern. Slope + views = view premium partially offsets discount. --- END OF PART 4 ---

DewClaw — Part 5: HOA & Proximity Valuation FULLY EXPANDED — HOA Framework, Lake/City/Highway Proximity Impact HOA LOTS — COMPREHENSIVE FRAMEWORK First Reaction: Negative — Because Restrictions Kill Buyer Pool When you see that a property or comp is in an HOA, the first reaction should be NEGATIVE. The reason is simple and fundamental: people who buy vacant land want FREEDOM. They want to build whatever they want, put a trailer on it if they choose, have no rules about what their property looks like, and answer to nobody about how they use their land. That is the entire appeal of buying vacant rural land — independence and control. An HOA directly conflicts with this appeal. HOA restrictions tell the buyer: you cannot put a mobile home here, you must build a minimum square footage house, you must maintain your property to certain standards, you must pay annual fees, and you must follow architectural guidelines. Each of these restrictions ELIMINATES potential buyers. The person who wanted to put a trailer on 5 acres? Gone. The person who wanted to build a small cabin? Maybe gone if minimum square footage is too high. The result: a smaller buyer pool, longer time on market, and lower price. The user confirmed this directly: 'HOA lots always sit way longer on the market, 100%.' The longer market time is a direct consequence of the smaller buyer pool. Fewer interested buyers = fewer offers = more time sitting = price pressure. This is why HOA is inherently a negative for vacant land values. HOA Subdivision With FEW Houses Built = Major Red Flag This is one of the most important signals to watch for. If you see an HOA subdivision — a planned community with lots laid out, roads built, maybe even amenities — but very FEW houses have actually been built, that should tell you

e of the most important signals to watch for. If you see an HOA subdivision — a planned community with lots laid out, roads built, maybe even amenities — but very FEW houses have actually been built, that should tell you something critical: the HOA is probably VERY STRICT. People are choosing NOT to build there because the restrictions are too onerous. Think about it: if the subdivision was reasonably priced, in a decent location, with reasonable rules, people would be building. The fact that lots are sitting empty in a developed subdivision with infrastructure means buyers are looking at the HOA rules, seeing the restrictions, and deciding to buy somewhere else with more freedom. This is the market voting with its feet, and the vote is: 'these restrictions are too harsh.' When you see this signal — an HOA subdivision with few houses — discount the lots more heavily than a typical HOA discount. The market has already told you these lots are harder to sell. HOA Subdivision With LOTS of Houses = Area Quality Can Add Value The opposite signal is equally important. If the HOA subdivision has LOTS of houses, is well maintained, has nice properties with manicured lawns, and looks like an attractive, thriving community — the area quality itself can add value even though it is HOA. People have accepted the restrictions because the AREA is genuinely desirable. The HOA is working as intended — it is maintaining property values, keeping the neighborhood attractive, and creating a community that people want to live in. This doesn't fully OFFSET the HOA discount — there are still restrictions that limit the buyer pool. But a well-developed, attractive HOA subdivision is a much better situation than a dead subdivision with empty lots. The area quality, maintained roads, and

count — there are still restrictions that limit the buyer pool. But a well-developed, attractive HOA subdivision is a much better situation than a dead subdivision with empty lots. The area quality, maintained roads, and community feel have real value that partially compensates for the restrictions. Comping HOA Lots — Find Same-Subdivision Comps The best approach for comping an HOA lot is to find other lot sales WITHIN THE SAME HOA SUBDIVISION. These are the gold-standard comparables because the buyers already factored in those SPECIFIC restrictions, those SPECIFIC fees, and that SPECIFIC area quality. A comp from the same HOA subdivision tells you exactly what the market pays for lots under those exact conditions. Same-subdivision comps also reveal HOA STRICTNESS indirectly: if lots are selling at decent prices with reasonable DOM (30-90 days), the HOA isn't killing the market. If lots are sitting for 6+ months or selling at deep discounts, the HOA is a significant problem.

If you CANNOT find comps within the same subdivision, look for comps in other HOA subdivisions in the county — but be cautious because different HOAs have different restrictions. An HOA with 'no mobile homes' is very different from an HOA with 'minimum 2,000sf home with architectural review.' Using one HOA's comps for a different HOA's lots introduces uncertainty. Using HOA Comps for Non-HOA Subjects — Flag With Hazard Emoji If your SUBJECT property is NOT in an HOA but you are using a COMP that IS in an HOA with amenities (community lake, pool, golf course, maintained roads), be very careful. That comp has BOTH positives (amenities, maintained area, community feel) AND negatives (restrictions, fees, limited buyer pool) baked into its sale price. It is nearly impossible to separate these factors. Did the comp sell high because of the lake access? Or did it sell lower than it would have WITHOUT the HOA restrictions? You genuinely don't know. The amenities and restrictions are intertwined in the price. For this reason: flag the comp with the HAZARD EMOJI immediately so the user sees it. Don't weight it heavily in your valuation. Note it as a reference point with a caveat: 'This comp is in an HOA with [amenities] — PPA reflects both amenity premium and HOA restriction discount, difficult to separate.' If the comp stands out and has a realtor's contact info, suggest calling that realtor to learn more about listing history and offers received. HOA Restrictions and Value Impact Specific restrictions and their impact: 'No mobile homes' = HURTS value because a lot of vacant land buyers want to put trailers or mobile homes on property. This eliminates a significant portion of the buyer pool. 'Minimum square footage' (e.g., must build 1,800+ sf) = HURTS value because it raises

a lot of vacant land buyers want to put trailers or mobile homes on property. This eliminates a significant portion of the buyer pool. 'Minimum square footage' (e.g., must build 1,800+ sf) = HURTS value because it raises the minimum investment required to use the lot, eliminating budget-conscious builders. 'Architectural review' = HURTS value because it adds time, cost, and uncertainty to the building process. The user confirmed: 'A lot of people want to actually end up doing stuff like putting trailers and mobile homes on property. If HOA says they can't, that kills value.' HOA Fees HOA fees range from $500/year to $5,000/year or more. Higher fees usually mean more amenities and maintenance (nicer area, maintained roads, community facilities) but also higher ongoing costs for the buyer. Note the fee amount in your deliverable if you find it in the listing description. Higher fees can go either way — they might indicate a nicer area (positive) or just more overhead with no real benefit (negative). The context matters. Never Create an HOA for Subdivision The user was emphatic: never, ever, EVER suggest creating an HOA when proposing subdivision plays. The DewClaw business model targets rural recreational buyers who want freedom. Creating an HOA would directly conflict with the target buyer pool and make the lots harder to sell. Keep subdivisions simple — just lots, no HOA, no restrictions beyond what the county requires.

PROXIMITY TO LAKES — DETAILED VALUATION FRAMEWORK Direct Lakefront = Maximum Premium (2-3x Non-Waterfront) Properties with direct lake frontage — the property boundary literally touches the lake water — command the highest water-related premium. In many markets, lakefront lots sell for 2-3x what comparable non-waterfront lots sell for. The reason: the buyer can walk out their door, across their yard, and be at the lake. They can build a dock, launch a kayak, fish from their property. This direct, private access to water is extremely valuable and extremely limited in supply (there are only so many lots that touch the lake). Lake quality matters significantly within this tier. A popular recreational lake (boating, swimming, fishing, public access, community activity) = highest premium. A nice private lake (attractive, clean, good fishing but limited public access) = high premium but potentially smaller buyer pool. A small private pond = moderate premium. A reservoir = lower premium because reservoirs are typically less aesthetically appealing and may have restricted recreational use. Across the Road From a Lake — Noticeable Premium, Not Lakefront A property directly across the road from a lake has a noticeable premium over properties farther away, but it is NOT worth as much as direct lakefront. The reason: you can literally SEE the lake from your property — potentially from your front porch. That visual connection has real value. However, you cannot ACCESS the lake directly from your property. You have to cross a road to get to the water, and the road itself creates separation — traffic, noise, and a physical barrier between you and the lake. The drop from direct lakefront to across-the-road is meaningful — perhaps 40-60% of the lakefront premium disappears. You still

itself creates separation — traffic, noise, and a physical barrier between you and the lake. The drop from direct lakefront to across-the-road is meaningful — perhaps 40-60% of the lakefront premium disappears. You still get the view and the proximity, but you lose the private access and the direct connection that makes lakefront so valuable. Near a Lake (1/10 to 1/4 Mile) — Proximity Premium Zone The lake premium starts to appear at approximately 1/10 to 15/100 of a mile from the lake in typical rural areas. At this distance, you may be able to see the lake from higher ground on the property, and you are close enough to walk or drive to the lake easily. The premium is relatively small compared to direct frontage — more of a location amenity than a water feature premium. In more desirable areas — near popular beaches, cities, or heavily-used recreational lakes — the premium zone extends FURTHER. Up to 1/4 mile or even a mile from the lake, properties command a premium because the AREA is desirable and the lake is the anchor attraction. The key question: can you TELL you're near a lake? If the lake defines the community (lake in the road names, lake community signs, businesses catering to lake visitors), the proximity premium extends across the whole community. Community Lake in Subdivision — ONLY Waterfront Lots Get Real Premium This is a critical distinction the user emphasized: in a subdivision with a community lake, ONLY the lots directly ON the water get the real lake premium. There is a HUGE drop-off from lakefront lots to non-lakefront lots within the same subdivision. The non-lakefront lots benefit from area quality and the community amenity (they can use the lake if the HOA allows it), but they are nowhere near the premium of direct frontage. This matters

within the same subdivision. The non-lakefront lots benefit from area quality and the community amenity (they can use the lake if the HOA allows it), but they are nowhere near the premium of direct frontage. This matters enormously for comping. If you see a lakefront lot in a subdivision that sold for $50K/ac, you CANNOT apply that PPA to a non-lakefront lot in the same subdivision. The non-lakefront lot might be $10-15K/ac — a fraction of the lakefront price. Always check whether a comp is actually ON the water or just NEAR the water. The difference can be 2-3x.

PROXIMITY TO CITIES — DETAILED VALUATION FRAMEWORK General Rule: Closer = More Expensive (But Every Market Is Different) The fundamental principle: closer to a city = higher PPA. This is driven by convenience (closer to jobs, shopping, services, entertainment), development pressure (builders and developers want land near growing cities), and infrastructure (closer to roads, utilities, internet, cell service). You will see this clearly and consistently in comp data — properties closer to town trade at higher PPAs than properties deep in rural areas of the same county. However, the user emphasized that every market is different. There is no universal formula like '5 miles from city = X% premium, 20 miles = Y% discount.' The actual impact depends on the specific city, the local economy, the road network, and a dozen other factors. Let the COMPS tell you the premium — pull comps at various distances from town and the PPA gradient will show itself. The Sweet Spot: 10-15 Minutes Outside the City Edge The user identified a specific sweet spot: approximately 30 minutes from the CENTER of the city, or about 10-15 minutes from the OUTER EDGE of the city. This is the 'rural recreational ring' — close enough to the city for convenience (you can get to a grocery store, restaurant, or hospital in 10-15 minutes) but far enough to feel RURAL (open space, privacy, larger lots, natural surroundings). This ring is where the DewClaw business model has the strongest demand. Buyers in this zone want the best of both worlds: rural living with urban convenience. They are willing to pay a premium over truly isolated rural land because of the proximity to services, but they don't want to pay city prices. This creates a PPA sweet spot that is higher than deep rural but lower than suburban/urban

ing to pay a premium over truly isolated rural land because of the proximity to services, but they don't want to pay city prices. This creates a PPA sweet spot that is higher than deep rural but lower than suburban/urban fringe. City Size Matters — A LOT Proximity to a large city (500K+ population metro area) commands significantly more premium than proximity to a small town (10K population). The math is straightforward: more people = more demand = higher prices. A property 10 miles from Charlotte, NC (metro population ~2.7 million) is worth DRAMATICALLY more than a property 10 miles from a town of 5,000 people. The buyer pool within driving distance of Charlotte is hundreds of times larger. Always note what the property is close TO, not just how close it is. '15 minutes from Walterboro' (pop ~5,000) is very different from '15 minutes from Charleston' (metro pop ~800,000). Both are '15 minutes from a city' but the value implications are completely different. Small Towns (500-2,000 Population) — Adds Value Over Truly Isolated A small town right next to the property ADDS value compared to truly isolated rural land. Even a tiny town with a gas station, a general store, and a post office provides CONVENIENCE that pure isolation does not. The buyer can get gas, buy groceries, mail a package without driving 30+ miles. That convenience has real value — not as much as being near a real city, but meaningfully better than being in the middle of absolute nowhere. Small towns also indicate SOME level of community and infrastructure. There are probably maintained roads, possibly county water, perhaps a volunteer fire department. These basic services increase the property's appeal and value compared to a location where none of that exists. Rural Recreational — Distance From City Can

ads, possibly county water, perhaps a volunteer fire department. These basic services increase the property's appeal and value compared to a location where none of that exists. Rural Recreational — Distance From City Can Be POSITIVE For the DewClaw target buyer (hunting land, cabin property, weekend getaway), being farther from a city is not always negative. Many buyers SPECIFICALLY WANT isolation and privacy. They are looking for a property where they can escape the city, enjoy nature, hunt, fish, and not see another person for days. For these buyers, proximity to a city is actually a NEGATIVE because it brings traffic, noise, development pressure, and other people. The market supports both buyer pools: closer to city = development/homebuilding buyers (higher PPA). Farther from city = recreational/hunting buyers (lower PPA but real, consistent demand). Both are valid markets with active buyers. The key is recognizing which buyer pool a specific property appeals to and pricing accordingly.

PROXIMITY TO HIGHWAYS & INTERSTATES — EXPANDED Directly ON a Major Highway = NEGATIVE for Residential (10-30% Discount) If the property's frontage is directly on a major highway where traffic moves at 50-65 mph, this is a NEGATIVE for residential value. The discount ranges from 10-30% depending on severity. The user explained the reasons clearly: 1) Nobody wants to literally be speeding off a highway to get to their property — it is dangerous, unpleasant, and stressful to decelerate from 65mph into a driveway. 2) You may need to work with the county to build a special offshoot/deceleration lane, which adds cost and bureaucratic hassle. 3) Highway noise destroys the rural, peaceful atmosphere that rural land buyers want. The constant sound of trucks and cars at high speed is unpleasant. 4) Zero privacy — every person driving by at 65mph can see your property, your house, your yard. Try to find other highway-frontage comps to see what the market actually discounts. The 10-30% range is a guideline but the real comp data will give you the exact discount for that specific market. Exception: Commercial Zoning on Highway = POSITIVE If the property is COMMERCIALLY ZONED and fronts a highway, the highway frontage FLIPS from negative to POSITIVE. Traffic visibility and easy access are exactly what commercial properties need — a gas station, a retail shop, a warehouse, a restaurant all BENEFIT from being on a busy highway. This completely changes the comp approach. Highway-front commercial land commands a PREMIUM, not a discount. If you encounter this, note the commercial zoning prominently and comp against commercial land sales, not residential. Nearby Highway (Not Directly On It) = Mostly Neutral If the property is near a highway or interstate but not directly fronting it —

e commercial zoning prominently and comp against commercial land sales, not residential. Nearby Highway (Not Directly On It) = Mostly Neutral If the property is near a highway or interstate but not directly fronting it — say, 1/2 mile away with a buffer of trees or other properties between — the impact is mostly neutral. Good regional access is a mild positive (easy to get to the property from the highway), but it is not a significant value driver for rural recreational land. Note it in Extra Notes as a minor access benefit. PROXIMITY — QUICK REFERENCE Direct lakefront: Maximum water premium (2-3x non-waterfront) Across road from lake: Noticeable premium, 40-60% less than direct frontage Near lake (1/10 to 1/4 mile): Slight premium, depends on visibility/area Community lake, non-waterfront lot: Minimal premium, mostly area quality 10-15 min outside city edge: Sweet spot for rural recreational demand Near large city (500K+): Significant PPA premium Near small town (500-2K): Adds value vs truly isolated Highway frontage (residential): 10-30% DISCOUNT Highway frontage (commercial): PREMIUM for visibility/access --- END OF PART 5 ---

DewClaw — Part 6: Comping Mastery FULLY EXPANDED — Process, Investigation, Weighting & Common Mistakes THE COMPING MINDSET — HOW THE USER THINKS Comping is not a mathematical formula. It is not 'take 5 PPAs, average them, done.' Comping is building a STORY — a narrative about what a property is worth based on what similar properties have sold for, adjusted for specific differences. The user builds this story by evaluating each comp TRAIT BY TRAIT against the subject property: is this comp's road better or worse? Is the land type similar or different? Does it have wetlands that mine doesn't? Is it closer to town? Each difference translates to a specific percentage adjustment, and those adjustments stack to determine whether the comp is above or below the subject's value. The user evaluates 5-20 comps to build the full story. Some comps are ANCHORS — nearly identical to the subject in key traits. These drive the price. Some are CEILING comps — superior to the subject, setting the maximum. Some are FLOOR comps — inferior, setting the minimum. The subject's value falls between the floor and ceiling, weighted toward the anchors. Every comp should MAKE SENSE in the story. If a PPA seems too high or too low, dig deeper until you understand why. 'Price doesn't stray too far from the tree without a reason.' STEP 1: KNOW YOUR PROPERTY COMPLETELY — BEFORE TOUCHING COMPS Before looking at a single comp, you need to fully characterize your subject property. What are its defining traits?
