Land type: completely wooded/timbered, cleared, maintained, pasture, farmland, or a mix? If a mix, where is each type (front cleared, back timbered, etc.)? Road type: paved county road, maintained gravel, rough gravel, nice dirt, rough dirt, seasonal two-track? What is the quality — smooth and

and, or a mix? If a mix, where is each type (front cleared, back timbered, etc.)? Road type: paved county road, maintained gravel, rough gravel, nice dirt, rough dirt, seasonal two-track? What is the quality — smooth and well-maintained or rutted and rough? Shape: nice rectangle, square, irregular, long and narrow? Width-to-depth ratio? Wetlands: what percentage and WHERE — front, back, scattered, one corner? Slope: average and maximum. Where are the steep areas — front blocking access or back not affecting buildability? Water features: pond, creek, lake nearby or on property? Are they verified or just suspected from the overlay? Structures: any buildings, mobile homes, barns, sheds visible on satellite? Road frontage: how many feet? On how many roads? This detailed characterization is what you are COMPING AGAINST. Every comp gets compared to THESE traits. If you don't fully know your own property, you cannot accurately assess whether a comp is similar, better, or worse. Take the time to know your subject inside and out before looking at the first comp. STEP 2: ALWAYS READ THE DESCRIPTION — THE #1 CRITICISM Why This Is Non-Negotiable The user identified 'not reading listing descriptions' as the SINGLE BIGGEST weakness in Claude's comping. The description on Zillow/Redfin is where critical information lives that directly affects how you weight the comp. Without reading the description, you are making comp weighting decisions with incomplete information — and incomplete information leads to wrong prices. What Descriptions Reveal HOA details: 'Located in XYZ Estates HOA, $350/year fees, no mobile homes, minimum 1,500sf home.' This completely changes how you weight the comp — it is in an HOA with restrictions that limit the buyer pool. Flag it with hazard emoji. Perc test

XYZ Estates HOA, $350/year fees, no mobile homes, minimum 1,500sf home.' This completely changes how you weight the comp — it is in an HOA with restrictions that limit the buyer pool. Flag it with hazard emoji. Perc test status: 'Perc test passed!' = confirmed buildable, value-add. 'Talk to realtor about perc test' or 'Contact seller about soil conditions' = RED FLAG. If it passed, they would advertise it. They want you to call because the truth would scare buyers if written down. Road details: 'Paved county road access' vs 'accessed via seasonal dirt road, may be impassable in winter.' Enormous difference in value. The description tells you road quality that you might not be able to determine from satellite.

Land features: 'Recently cleared and maintained,' 'mature hardwood timber,' 'active cattle pasture with fencing,' 'organic farm with established irrigation.' Each of these tells you the land type and condition in the seller's own words. Seller motivation: 'Must sell!' 'Estate sale,' 'Relocating,' 'Price reduced!' These clues suggest the seller is motivated, which might explain a lower sale price that doesn't reflect true market value. Issues disclosed: 'Property is in flood zone X,' 'easement along east boundary,' 'wetlands in southeastern portion.' These explain why a PPA might be low. Reading the description takes 30 SECONDS. That 30 seconds can completely change how you weight the comp. A comp at $4,000/ac with a description revealing wetlands and a failed perc test tells a completely different story than a comp at $4,000/ac with a description saying 'beautiful cleared lot, paved road, perc test passed.' Same PPA, completely different relevance to your subject.

STEP 3: ALWAYS CHECK THE PHOTOS — THE #2 CRITICISM The user identified 'not checking listing photos' as the second biggest weakness. Listing photos show what the property looked like AT THE TIME OF SALE — this is what the buyer paid for. Current satellite might show something DIFFERENT because the property condition may have changed since the sale. Examples of changes that photos catch: listing photos show standing mature timber (the property was timbered when sold — PPA reflects timbered land value). Current satellite shows the land has been CLEAR-CUT (trees harvested after the sale). If your subject is cleared, this timbered-land-priced comp is less relevant than it appeared. Listing photos show a cleared field with farm equipment visible. Current satellite shows overgrowth and abandonment. The comp's PPA reflects an ACTIVE farm — a yielding asset premium that may no longer exist. Photos also reveal: road condition (paved vs dirt visible in access photos), land quality (nice maintained vs rough brush), structures (barns, sheds, mobile homes that might hide under tree canopy on satellite), wet areas or standing water, and the overall 'feel' of the property. Compare EVERY anchor comp's photos to the current satellite imagery. If ANYTHING looks different, CALL IT OUT in your deliverable. STEP 4: INVESTIGATE WHEN PPAs DON'T MAKE SENSE — THE #3 CRITICISM The Investigation Checklist — In Order When a comp's PPA seems too high or too low compared to other comps in the area, do NOT accept it and move on. Investigate. Run through this checklist: 1. Street View on Land Insights: Drop the pegman on the road near the comp and look at the property from ground level. What does the road look like? Can you see the land condition? Any structures? Any red flags — trash, run-down

t View on Land Insights: Drop the pegman on the road near the comp and look at the property from ground level. What does the road look like? Can you see the land condition? Any structures? Any red flags — trash, run-down buildings, marshy areas? Also use this moment to assess the AREA QUALITY: are surrounding properties nice or run-down? 2. Listing photos on Zillow/Redfin (simultaneously): What does the land actually look like in the listing photos? Marshy? Terrible access? Super sloped? In a terrible area? Compare to what you see on satellite. 3. Read the description: This is often where you find the answer to 'why did this sell at this PPA?' Wetlands mentioned? Failed perc test hints? HOA restrictions? Unusual easements? Seller desperation? 4. Toggle flood/wetland overlays on Zillow/Redfin map: Quick 10-second check. If the comp shows flood zone or wetlands on the Zillow map that you didn't notice before, that could explain the low PPA. 5. Open a NEW Land Insights tab with the comp's APN: This is the most thorough check. Grab the APN from the bottom of the Zillow/Redfin listing. DUPLICATE your Land Insights tab (NEVER navigate away from your subject property — you might not be able to get back). Enter the APN with the correct county in the new tab. Check: Insights tab for wetlands %, flood zone %, slope data, road frontage. Toggle hazards on and off to see overlays vs actual satellite. Check Street View if available. 6. Check DOM and price history: Did it sit for 200+ days? Were there multiple price drops? A comp that sold after months of price reductions was overpriced initially — the final sale price is closer to market. Listed/delisted patterns = red flag, something may be wrong with the property. The goal of all this investigation: figure out WHY the PPA is what

tions was overpriced initially — the final sale price is closer to market. Listed/delisted patterns = red flag, something may be wrong with the property. The goal of all this investigation: figure out WHY the PPA is what it is. Once you know the reason, you know how to weight the comp. Low PPA because of wetlands and bad road? = floor comp. High PPA because of cleared farmland and paved road? = ceiling comp. Same traits as your subject? = anchor comp. The APN Verification Workflow — Detailed Steps This is the specific process the user taught for deep-diving a comp's quality in Land Insights: Step 1: On the Zillow/Redfin listing, scroll all the way to the bottom of the page and find the APN (Assessment/Parcel Number). Copy it. Step 2: Go to your Land Insights tab where your SUBJECT PROPERTY is loaded. DUPLICATE the tab. This is critical — never navigate away from your subject property tab. Reasons: (a) the comp's APN might not work in Land Insights and you would lose your subject if you navigated away, (b) you need your subject property tab intact for continued comp searching after you finish verifying

this comp. Step 3: In the new duplicate tab, use the search to enter the APN with the correct county name. Wait for it to load. Step 4: Once the comp loads in Land Insights, check EVERYTHING: Go to the Insights tab — read wetlands %, flood zone %, slope data, road frontage numbers. Toggle hazards ON to see the flood/wetland overlays on the map. Toggle hazards OFF to see the actual satellite imagery underneath. Check Street View if available by dropping the pegman. Step 5: Compare what you see on Land Insights to what the Zillow/Redfin listing showed. Do the photos match the current satellite? Is the description consistent with what LI shows? If anything is DIFFERENT — listing photos show timbered land but current satellite shows it has been clear-cut — that is critical information that affects how you weight the comp. Step 6: When you are done evaluating this comp, CLOSE that duplicate tab and go back to your original subject property tab. Keep your browser clean. Move on to the next comp that needs investigation.

BUILDING THE STORY — COMP TIERS AND WEIGHTING The Three Tiers ANCHORS (Similar comps): Match your subject most closely in the traits that matter most: acreage, land type, road type, location, shape, and condition. These are the comps that DRIVE your price. If you find 3 anchor comps all at $6,000-$7,000/ac, that is your range. Weight these the heaviest — they are the foundation of your valuation story. CEILING (Better comps): Superior to your subject in meaningful ways — better road, cleaner land, closer to town, more features (well/septic, cleared, farmland). Your subject should price BELOW these. They tell you the absolute maximum. Use them to confirm that your anchor-based price isn't too high — if your anchors say $6,500/ac and your ceiling is $7,800/ac, that makes sense. If your anchors say $6,500/ac but your ceiling is only $5,000/ac, something is wrong with your anchor selection. FLOOR (Worse comps): Inferior to your subject — more wetlands, worse road, timbered vs your cleared, worse shape, worse location. Your subject should price ABOVE these. They tell you the absolute minimum. Use them to confirm your anchor price isn't too low — if your anchors say $6,500/ac and your floor is $3,000/ac, the spread makes sense if the floor comps have significant issues. How to Make the Final Adjustment From Anchor PPA Once you have identified your anchor comp(s), adjust the anchor PPA for specific differences between the anchor and your subject. Each adjustment should be a specific, logical percentage tied to a specific trait difference: Road type: paved to gravel = ~10-15% discount. Gravel to dirt = ~5-20% depending on dirt road quality (nice maintained dirt = 5%, rough seasonal dirt = 15-20%). Land type: farmland premium = 30-50% over pasture. Cleared vs timbered =

ved to gravel = ~10-15% discount. Gravel to dirt = ~5-20% depending on dirt road quality (nice maintained dirt = 5%, rough seasonal dirt = 15-20%). Land type: farmland premium = 30-50% over pasture. Cleared vs timbered = timbered is lower (clearing cost for buyer). Pasture vs maintained cleared = roughly similar. Wetlands: back <20% = ~5%. ~50% front buildable = ~20%. Big back pocket = ~30%. (See Part 2 for full framework). Slope: under 10% = none. 10-20% = 5-10%. (See Part 4 for full framework). Shape: nice rectangle vs bowling alley = significant. Well/septic installed = 15-20% premium on the comp. Proximity: closer to town = higher PPA. Near lake = premium. Near highway = 10-30% discount for residential. Stack these adjustments: 'Anchor comp at $7,800/ac (cleared, paved road). My subject is on a gravel road (-12%) and has a creek with 5% wetlands (-3%). Adjusted PPA: $7,800 × 0.85 = ~$6,630/ac.' This is logical, specific, and defensible. ACTIVE LISTINGS — VIEW-TO-SAVE RATIO Active listings that haven't sold are NOT anchor comps. They show what someone is ASKING, not what the market is PAYING. Typically there is about a 20% drop from active list price to actual sold price on average — could be more, could be less. Active listings are primarily useful as CEILING references. However, one powerful tool for evaluating active listings is the VIEW-TO-SAVE RATIO on Zillow. This metric shows how many people viewed the listing versus how many saved/favorited it. The benchmark: for every 10 views, you want to see at least 1 save. If the ratio meets or exceeds this threshold (1:10 or better), the property is probably priced close to correctly — buyers are interested enough to save it for later consideration. If the ratio is terrible (hundreds of views, 1-2 saves), the property

this threshold (1:10 or better), the property is probably priced close to correctly — buyers are interested enough to save it for later consideration. If the ratio is terrible (hundreds of views, 1-2 saves), the property is overpriced — buyers are looking but nobody is interested enough to take it seriously. Active listings with GOOD view-to-save ratios are closer to market reality and make better ceiling references. Active listings with BAD ratios are overpriced and serve only as the highest possible ceiling. Don't completely ignore active listings — they are still data. But weight them appropriately based on their traction. PENDING COMPS — HOW TO WEIGHT THEM Pending comps are sales that are IN PROGRESS but not yet finalized. They are the most CURRENT data point (more recent than most sold comps) but not 100% reliable because deals can fall through, terms can change, and the final closing price might differ from the pending list price. The user's approach: discount pending comps 5-10% from the pending list price. Weight pending comps at approximately 80-85% of what you would weight an actual sold comp. Use them as strong supporting data, but don't make a pending comp your sole anchor.

SPECIFIC COMPING SCENARIOS — NUANCED ANSWERS Suspiciously Low PPA — Investigation Checklist Applied When you see a comp with a PPA that seems too low, run the full investigation checklist. The user's order: 1) Street View to see the deal visually. 2) Listing photos simultaneously. 3) Read the description — this is where you most often find the answer. Look for: wetlands mentions, perc test language (especially the red flag 'talk to realtor about perc test'), bad road descriptions, HOA restrictions, disclosed issues. 4) Zillow/Redfin map overlays. 5) APN lookup in a new LI tab if still confused. Common explanations for low PPA: wetlands, failed or questionable perc, bad road, timbered, bad shape, landlocked, in a poor area, HOA restrictions, flood zone, steep slope. Two Comps Tell Completely Different Stories When one comp says $4,000/ac and another says $8,000/ac at similar acreage, there is ALWAYS a reason. Price doesn't stray far from the tree without explanation. DIG DEEPER into BOTH comps. Check descriptions, photos, wetlands, road type, land condition on both. One of them has traits that explain the gap. The $4,000 comp probably has issues (timbered, bad road, wetlands, bad shape, bad area). The $8,000 comp probably has advantages (cleared, paved road, nice layout, closer to town). Find the reason, then determine which one matches YOUR subject's quality. Weight that one as your anchor. The Ada Ln example proved this: the 23ac comp at $4,130/ac was likely inferior quality (not investigated). The 22.3ac Tampico Way comp at $7,847/ac was cleared/maintained with a paved road — quality-matched to the subject. The correct anchor was Tampico, not the 23ac comp. The difference: $50,000 in the final valuation. Finding the RIGHT comp matters more than finding the MOST

/maintained with a paved road — quality-matched to the subject. The correct anchor was Tampico, not the 23ac comp. The difference: $50,000 in the final valuation. Finding the RIGHT comp matters more than finding the MOST comps. Comps With Structures When a comp has a structure on it (barn, shed, house, mobile home), flag it with the HAZARD EMOJI. Comp the land as if the structure doesn't exist — use it as a ceiling reference only because the structure inflated the sale price above raw land value. Don't try to estimate the structure's value and subtract it — too imprecise and not your job. The user will investigate the structure separately. Just note: 'Comp includes a [structure type] — using as ceiling reference, raw land PPA is lower than this comp's total PPA.' Sparse Comp Markets When comps are extremely sparse (only 1-2 in the whole county), you are making your own market. Use process of elimination: find whatever floor and ceiling data exists. Place your subject on the PPA trendline between available data points using the exponential curve principle (smaller lots = higher PPA, larger = lower). If you have a 15ac property and only a 10ac comp ($8K/ac) and a 20ac comp ($5K/ac), interpolate: 15ac falls between, probably around $6,000-$6,500/ac. Be conservative when data is sparse and note in the deliverable that comp data is limited and confidence is lower. Never cross county lines — each county is its own market. Tax Sale Properties If the owner bought the property at a tax sale for almost nothing (like the Kershaw Camden property at $1,000), this does NOT change market value. Tax sale price = distressed transaction. Market value = what a willing buyer pays a willing seller in an open market. The property is worth what the comps say regardless of what the owner

is does NOT change market value. Tax sale price = distressed transaction. Market value = what a willing buyer pays a willing seller in an open market. The property is worth what the comps say regardless of what the owner paid. The low cost basis IS a strong seller motivation signal — any offer feels like pure profit — but it is NOT a valuation factor. Commercial Zoning in Residential Area If commercially zoned but sold at normal residential prices in a residential area, the market treated it as residential. Comp as residential. If surrounded by commercial properties and businesses, it is a completely different comp pool — commercial goes for much more than residential. Note the zoning in your deliverable and let the user evaluate. Commercial lots typically take longer to sell. Adjacent to Timber Company

If the property is IN THE MIDDLE of a large timber operation (surrounded on all sides by timberland), discount 10-30% depending on how isolated and industrial it feels. Smaller parcels feel more trapped. If the timber company is just ONE neighbor among many (residential properties nearby too), it doesn't meaningfully affect value. Near State Park / National Forest Adds roughly 10% premium if butting up to or very close to public recreational land. Hunters especially value adjacent public land because it effectively expands their hunting area. Note in Extra Notes. Listed/Delisted History If a comp was previously listed and failed to sell, flag it. Buyer may have backed out, could be something wrong with the property. Don't dismiss the comp entirely but don't weight it as heavily. Note it for the user's team to investigate.

THE THREE BIGGEST MISTAKES — AND HOW TO FIX THEM Mistake #1: Not reading listing descriptions. FIX: For every comp you use as an anchor, open it on Zillow/Redfin and read the FULL description. Extract HOA info, perc test status, land features, restrictions, red flags. Takes 30 seconds. Changes everything. Mistake #2: Not checking listing photos. FIX: Look at every anchor comp's photos. Compare them to current satellite. Note any changes — land condition may have changed since the sale. Photos show what the BUYER paid for. Mistake #3: Not digging deeper when PPAs don't make sense. FIX: When a comp seems off, run the investigation checklist (Street View, photos, description, map overlays, APN lookup in new LI tab). Find the REASON for the PPA. If you can't explain a PPA, don't anchor to it. AREA QUALITY ASSESSMENT — HOW TO READ AN AREA The user identified three main factors for quickly assessing area quality from the map and Street View: 1. How rural/isolated is it? The more rural and isolated, the lower the PPA generally. Extremely isolated with nothing around for miles is a yellow flag — limited demand, limited services, limited buyer pool. 2. What kind of houses are visible? From satellite, can you see bigger houses with maintained yards, or run-down mobile homes with cluttered properties? The house quality surrounding the subject property is a strong indicator of area desirability and PPA level. 3. Street View confirmation: Drop pegman and LOOK. If you see trash, unmaintained properties, broken-down vehicles, run-down mobile homes with sagging roofs = poor area, lower PPA. If you see well-maintained homes, clean roads, manicured yards, nice fencing = nice area, higher PPA. You CANNOT determine area quality from GIS data alone — you MUST go to Street View. While you

= poor area, lower PPA. If you see well-maintained homes, clean roads, manicured yards, nice fencing = nice area, higher PPA. You CANNOT determine area quality from GIS data alone — you MUST go to Street View. While you are already checking Street View for road type, also assess the surrounding area quality. Takes 10 extra seconds. MULTIPLE PARCELS & PACKAGE DEALS If the owner has ANY other properties — even just one — note it in the deliverable and put it in Section 9 (Questions to Ask Seller) so the user can ask on the call. Adjacent parcels owned by the same person: note the adjacency but comp each parcel individually. Combined potential is interesting but doesn't change individual values unless combining. MARKET HEALTH INDICATORS More solds than actives + low DOM (30-90 days) = hot. Lots of actives, few solds, high DOM (6+ months) = cold. But the user doesn't care much about hot vs cold: 'I could sell a piece of property in any market if I get it for the right price.' Just note conditions briefly. Lots of actives with few solds means sellers are overpricing — sold comps reflect TRUE market, active listings reflect wishes. OUTLIER COMPS — NEVER DISREGARD, ALWAYS INVESTIGATE If 3 comps say $5K/ac and 1 says $10K/ac, never throw out the outlier. Investigate it — pull APN, check LI, read Zillow description and photos. Outliers almost always have a reason: well/septic, farmland, closer to town, lake proximity. Listing photos sometimes reveal things NOT in the description — call those out. If the reason makes sense, use as ceiling. If genuinely unexplainable, be aware but don't weight heavily. LAND INSIGHTS NAVIGATION TIPS Dark map tiles: Zoomed out too far. Fix: zoom back IN. Wrong property clicked: Click BACK ARROW top left corner. Toggle MLS off to see area: Data

lainable, be aware but don't weight heavily. LAND INSIGHTS NAVIGATION TIPS Dark map tiles: Zoomed out too far. Fix: zoom back IN. Wrong property clicked: Click BACK ARROW top left corner. Toggle MLS off to see area: Data Layers > MLS Data > toggle. Hazards ON default. Acreage: AUTO always. Need more: below=80%, above=150%. Ruler tool for measuring road length and frontage. Duplicate tab for APN lookup — never navigate away from subject. Close all tabs after every comp. ONE response, no continue, target 2 min. DELIVERABLE FORMAT — 9 SECTIONS

1. Market Value — specific number + PPA. 2. Offer Price — 50/60/70%. 3. Value-Add MV — subdivision scenarios, 3 game plans, costs separate. 4. Value-Add Offer — 50/60/70% of improved. 5. Negotiation Strategies — owner signals, property issues. 6. Extra Notes — hazard flags, structures, zoning, area quality, water. 7. Recommended List Price. 8. Key Comps — 3-5 with full detail and weighting. 9. Questions to Ask Seller — mobile home specs, timber, perc, other properties, anything needing ground verification. --- END OF PART 6 ---

DewClaw — Part 7: Live Comping Examples & Deep Q&A FULLY EXPANDED — User's Exact Thinking Process + All 20 Nuanced Answers LIVE COMPING EXAMPLE 1: WOODED GRAVEL ROAD SUBJECT Subject property: Completely wooded, off a gravel road. No wetlands, no flood zone, reasonable shape. The goal: use comps to determine what this specific property is worth by comparing it trait-by-trait to sold properties in the area. Comp 1 — Gravel Road, Natural Cleared Spot in the Middle This comp is off a gravel road (same as our subject) and has a natural cleared spot about one-quarter to one-half of the way into the property. The cleared spot was NOT man-made — it just naturally exists, perhaps where trees fell or where soil conditions didn't support tree growth. To actually USE this cleared spot for building, you would need to BUILD A ROAD through the trees from the road frontage to reach it. That road construction costs money — potentially thousands of dollars depending on distance and terrain. The user's evaluation: this comp is 'pretty damn close' in PPA to our subject. The gravel road matches (same road type = no road adjustment needed). The land is mostly wooded (same = no land type adjustment needed). The cleared spot adds ONLY about 5% value. WHY only 5%? Because the cleared spot is in the MIDDLE of the property, not at the front. To use it, you need to invest in building an access road. That investment cost offsets most of the value the cleared spot would otherwise provide. Now here is the critical comparison: if that SAME cleared spot was at the FRONT of the property, right near the road where a driveway would naturally go, it would add 20-25% value. Why? Because at the front, the cleared spot IS the building site. No road to build, no trees to clear, no extra cost — the buyer can

ty, right near the road where a driveway would naturally go, it would add 20-25% value. Why? Because at the front, the cleared spot IS the building site. No road to build, no trees to clear, no extra cost — the buyer can immediately start building on a ready-made pad right near the road. The convenience is enormous. The cost savings of not needing to clear trees and not needing to build an interior access road are significant. The math on this nuance: front cleared spot = 20-25% premium. Middle cleared spot = 5% premium. That is a 4-5x difference in value for the SAME physical feature, driven entirely by LOCATION on the property. This principle applies universally: WHERE a feature is determines the vast majority of its value, not whether the feature exists. A pond at the front of a property (visible, accessible, aesthetic) is worth more than the same pond hidden in the back behind trees. A flat buildable area at the front is worth more than the same area in the middle requiring road access. Comp 2 — Dirt Road, Completely Wooded (Identical Except Road) This comp is the EXACT SAME as our subject property in every meaningful way — completely wooded, similar acreage, similar shape — except it is on a DIRT road instead of our gravel road. The user immediately identified this as a FLOOR comp: it is worth 5-20% LESS than our subject, with the exact discount depending on the quality of the dirt road. A nice, well-maintained dirt road might only warrant a 5% discount. A rough, seasonal, barely-passable dirt road warrants 15-20%. This type of comp is incredibly powerful for calibration. When you find a comp that differs from your subject by ONLY ONE VARIABLE — in this case, road type — the price difference between the two properties IS the market value of that variable. If this

powerful for calibration. When you find a comp that differs from your subject by ONLY ONE VARIABLE — in this case, road type — the price difference between the two properties IS the market value of that variable. If this dirt-road comp sold for $4,500/ac and our gravel-road subject should be priced at $5,000/ac, the difference ($500/ac or ~10%) is the market's valuation of gravel vs dirt road in this area. You can use this calibration point for every other comp in the area: any property on a dirt road gets approximately 10% less than an equivalent gravel-road property. The user's teaching here was about building CONFIDENCE in your adjustments. Instead of guessing '10% for dirt vs gravel' from a textbook, you can PROVE it from the comp data. One-variable comps give you that proof. Comp 3 — Paved Road, Wetlands in Back, Completely Wooded This comp introduces two differences from our subject: BETTER road (paved vs our gravel = adds value) and WETLANDS in the back (~5% discount because back wetlands barely matter). The user evaluated the net effect: paved road adds value (maybe 10-15% over gravel), back wetlands subtract a tiny amount (~5%). Net result: this comp is a BIT ABOVE our subject — it is the LOW CEILING.

Now the story is taking shape. We know our subject is: ABOVE Comp 2 (floor — dirt road, 5-20% below us). NEAR Comp 1 (anchor — gravel road with middle clearing, nearly identical PPA). BELOW Comp 3 (low ceiling — paved road but minor wetlands). The subject's price is bracketed between the floor and the low ceiling, anchored near the most similar comp. Comp 4 — Paved Road, Well and Septic Already Installed This is the HIGH CEILING. Paved road (better than our gravel) PLUS well and septic already installed. The user valued the well and septic premium at approximately 15-20%. WHY is it worth that much? Because having a well and septic already in place REMOVES two of the biggest costs and uncertainties a buyer faces when purchasing raw land: WELL: Drilling a well costs $5,000-$15,000+ depending on depth, geology, and location. There is also UNCERTAINTY — what if the well doesn't produce adequate water? What if you hit rock and need to drill deeper? What if the water quality is poor? Having a well already drilled and producing eliminates all of this cost and risk for the buyer. SEPTIC: Installing a standard septic system costs $5,000-$15,000+. But first you need a PERC TEST, which introduces uncertainty — what if it fails? Then you need an engineered/mound system at $10,000-$40,000+. Having septic already installed means the perc test already passed, the system is already in, and the buyer faces ZERO uncertainty about buildability. Combined, the buyer saves $10,000-$30,000+ in hard costs plus eliminates the single biggest risk factor in rural land purchase (can I build here?). That removal of cost and uncertainty is worth the 15-20% premium. Our subject property, without well or septic, is DEFINITELY below this ceiling comp. The Complete Story for Example 1 FLOOR: Comp 2

e (can I build here?). That removal of cost and uncertainty is worth the 15-20% premium. Our subject property, without well or septic, is DEFINITELY below this ceiling comp. The Complete Story for Example 1 FLOOR: Comp 2 (dirt road, identical otherwise) = 5-20% below subject. ANCHOR: Comp 1 (gravel road, middle clearing) = nearly identical PPA. LOW CEILING: Comp 3 (paved road, back wetlands) = slightly above subject. HIGH CEILING: Comp 4 (paved road + well/septic) = definitely above subject. Subject price: near the anchor PPA, confirmed by being above the floor and below both ceilings. Every comp makes sense in the story. Every price difference is explainable by specific trait differences.

LIVE COMPING EXAMPLE 2: 20ac PASTURE ON PAVED ROAD Subject property: 20 acres of pasture land on a paved road. Good condition, usable land. Comp 1 — Half Wetlands (Front Left Corner + Back), Pasture, Gravel Road This comp has wetlands in two locations: the front LEFT CORNER and in the back. The user was very specific about this distinction. Wetlands in ONE CORNER of the front is NOT the same as wetlands across the ENTIRE front. One corner being wet still leaves the MIDDLE and the RIGHT SIDE of the front completely buildable. The buyer drives up and sees usable land in the center and right — the wet left corner is a localized issue, not a property-wide problem. If the wetlands were across the ENTIRE front — blocking all road-accessible building sites — the discount would be catastrophic (50-80%). But because they are only in one corner, the impact is much more manageable. The buyer still has plenty of buildable frontage. Combined with the gravel road (worse than our paved), the user's total discount was approximately 10-15% below our subject. This is a FLOOR comp. It confirms that properties with partial wetlands and worse road access trade below our clean, paved-road pasture subject. The specific discount (10-15%) gives us a data point for the bottom of our range. Comp 2 — Completely Wooded, Gravel, More Road Frontage, Closer to Town This comp is fascinating because it has trade-offs in BOTH directions. It is WORSE than our subject in two ways: completely wooded (vs our pasture — buyer needs to spend money clearing if they want to build, and the raw aesthetics are less appealing for most buyers) and gravel road (vs our paved). But it is BETTER in two ways: more road frontage (creates subdivision potential and multiple access points, which adds inherent value even

aw aesthetics are less appealing for most buyers) and gravel road (vs our paved). But it is BETTER in two ways: more road frontage (creates subdivision potential and multiple access points, which adds inherent value even without actually subdividing) and closer to town (convenience premium for the buyer). The user's analysis: as-is, this comp is still 10-20% LESS than our subject. The negatives (wooded + gravel) OUTWEIGH the positives (frontage + town). Being wooded when our subject is pasture is a meaningful difference — the buyer faces clearing costs, the property shows less well, and it has no agricultural utility. The gravel road adds another discount on top. The extra frontage and town proximity help but don't fully compensate. However, the user made an important additional note: the extra road frontage creates SUBDIVISION POTENTIAL that should be flagged separately from the as-is valuation. If county regulations allow subdividing on a gravel road, the additional frontage could unlock significant value-add. This illustrates an important principle: always evaluate properties on TWO levels — what it is worth AS-IS (for the flip) and what it COULD be worth WITH VALUE-ADD (for the subdivision backup strategy). Comp 3 — Half Pasture Front / Half Wooded Back, Nice Dirt Road This comp is CLOSE to our subject but slightly inferior. The front is pasture — matching our subject's land type in the most important location (the front near the road). The buyer drives up and sees open pasture, just like our subject. But the back half is wooded — less usable, harder to access, and visually less appealing than full pasture. The user discounted approximately 10% for the wooded back: 'You're not really going to use the back as much, but it's still nice to have cleared in the front.'

er to access, and visually less appealing than full pasture. The user discounted approximately 10% for the wooded back: 'You're not really going to use the back as much, but it's still nice to have cleared in the front.' The road is a nice quality dirt road — better than rough dirt but worse than our paved. The user discounted another 5-10% for the road difference, noting that the discount is on the lower end (5%) because the dirt road is well-maintained and nice quality. If it were a rough, seasonal dirt road, the discount would be larger (15-20%). Combined: ~10% for wooded back + ~5-10% for dirt road = this comp is about 15-20% below our subject. But it is CLOSE — close enough to serve as a CEILING reference from below. Our subject is slightly above this comp, confirmed by our better road and fully pasture land. If a Farmland Comp Appears at High PPA The user added this important contextual note: if you also see a farmland comp in the area trading at a significantly higher PPA than everything else, don't be confused or misled by it. Farmland is a YIELDING ASSET — it generates income from crop production. That income stream is baked into the price, which is why farmland trades 30-50% above comparable pasture or cleared land. Your PASTURE subject sits BELOW farmland in value but ABOVE the wooded/wetlands/gravel floor comps. The farmland comp helps you understand the COMPLETE RANGE of the market — from the worst properties at the bottom (timbered, wetlands, dirt road

= floor) to the best (farmland, paved road, yielding asset = highest ceiling). Your subject's price falls in the middle of this range, positioned by its specific traits: better than the inferior comps, not as valuable as the farmland. Seeing a farmland comp at $12,000/ac when your other comps are $5,000-$7,000/ac makes SENSE when you understand the farmland premium. It is not an outlier to investigate — it is the market correctly pricing a fundamentally different (and better) product. Use it as a ceiling that explains the top of the market, and place your subject appropriately below it. The Complete Story for Example 2 FLOOR: Comp 1 (wetlands + gravel) = 10-15% below. FLOOR with upside: Comp 2 (wooded + gravel + more frontage) = 10-20% below as-is but with subdivision potential. NEAR-CEILING: Comp 3 (half pasture/half wooded + nice dirt) = 15-20% below (close to subject). HIGHEST CEILING: Farmland comp (if present) = explains top of market, subject below. Subject price: above all floor comps, near but above the near-ceiling comp, well below farmland. Anchored based on specific trait adjustments from the most similar comps.

KEY LESSONS FROM BOTH EXAMPLES — EXPANDED 1. Evaluate each trait separately with a specific percentage. Road type: paved to gravel = 10-15%. Gravel to dirt = 5-20%. Land type: cleared to wooded = discount. Farmland premium = 30-50%. Well/septic = 15-20% premium on comp. Don't just eyeball the whole picture — break it down trait by trait and stack the adjustments. 2. WHERE a feature is matters 4-5x more than WHETHER it exists. Cleared spot at front = 20-25% value-add. Same cleared spot in middle requiring road access = only 5%. Wetlands in one front corner ≠ wetlands across entire front. Slope in back ≠ slope in front. This is the single most important nuance in all of comping. 3. One-variable comps reveal trait values precisely. When a comp differs from your subject by only ONE thing, the price difference IS the market value of that difference. Use these comps to calibrate your adjustment percentages rather than guessing from rules of thumb. 4. Build BOTH floors AND ceilings. You need the full range. A floor alone is half a story. A ceiling alone is the other half. Both together bracket your price, and your subject sits within based on trait matching. 5. Farmland explains high outlier PPAs. Don't be confused when a comp trades way above everything else — check if it is farmland. The yielding asset premium (30-50%) explains the gap. Your non-farmland subject should be below it. 6. Well + septic installed = 15-20% premium. Removes $10-30K+ in buyer costs plus all uncertainty about buildability. The single biggest risk factor in rural land purchase (can I build here?) is eliminated. 7. Not all unpaved roads are equal. Nice maintained dirt = 5% discount from paved. Rough seasonal dirt = 15-20%. Nice gravel = 5-10% from paved. The QUALITY of the unpaved road matters as much

build here?) is eliminated. 7. Not all unpaved roads are equal. Nice maintained dirt = 5% discount from paved. Rough seasonal dirt = 15-20%. Nice gravel = 5-10% from paved. The QUALITY of the unpaved road matters as much as the TYPE. Assess from photos or Street View. 8. Subdivision potential adds inherent value even without subdividing. A comp with more road frontage has more development optionality. Even without pursuing subdivision, a savvy buyer recognizes the option. Flag it separately in your deliverable — the as-is value and the value-add potential are separate analyses. 9. All of this requires reading descriptions and checking photos. Every trait comparison above requires CONFIRMED information about each comp. You cannot know if a comp has wetlands, what road it is on, what the land condition looks like, or whether it has well/septic without reading the description and checking the photos. This is exactly why the user's top criticisms are about not doing these steps.

ADA LN COMPING EXAMPLE — FULL CASE STUDY Subject: Ada Ln, Colleton County, SC. 25 acres, mix of cleared and timbered land, 127ft road frontage on Ada Ln (appears dirt/gravel), 5% wetlands with creek through SE corner, nice rectangular shape. Owner Shirley C Peeler, mailing address in Summerville (out of county), 2 properties. Initial Wrong Valuation: $115,000 (~$4,600/ac) The initial analysis pulled comps at the county zoom level and found: 23.00ac at $4,130/ac (used as primary anchor), 56.20ac at $2,940/ac (supporting floor), 49.80ac at $5,960/ac (supporting). The 23ac comp was the closest acreage match and was anchored to without investigation. The result: approximately $4,600/ac = $115,000. THE MISTAKE: The 23ac comp at $4,130/ac was accepted at face value without investigating WHY it traded at such a low PPA. Was it timbered? On a worse road? Did it have significant wetlands? What did the listing description say? What did the photos show? None of these questions were asked. The number was taken at face value and used as the anchor, producing a valuation that was $50,000 too low. The Game-Changing Comp: 185 Tampico Way — $7,847/ac The user identified a comp that told a completely different story: 185 Tampico Way, Round O, SC — 22.3 acres, sold for $175,000 ($7,847/ac), CLOSED on Zillow. The listing showed drone photos of CLEARED/MAINTAINED land with timber border — very similar in appearance and quality to the subject property. The road (Tampico Way) appeared to be PAVED — better than the subject's likely dirt/gravel road. This comp was a QUALITY MATCH to the subject — similar acreage (22.3 vs 25), similar land condition (cleared/maintained with timber), same county (Colleton). The 23ac comp at $4,130/ac was NOT a quality match — it was likely an inferior property

ATCH to the subject — similar acreage (22.3 vs 25), similar land condition (cleared/maintained with timber), same county (Colleton). The 23ac comp at $4,130/ac was NOT a quality match — it was likely an inferior property with issues that explained the low PPA but were never investigated. Revised Correct Valuation: $165,000 (~$6,600/ac) Starting from the Tampico Way anchor at $7,847/ac, the user applied adjustments: approximately 10% discount for the road difference (Tampico is paved, subject is dirt/gravel) and approximately 3% for the creek and minor wetlands. That brings the adjusted PPA to approximately $6,600-$6,800/ac × 25 acres = approximately $165,000-$170,000. The user's target range was $160,000-$175,000 — the revised valuation falls right in the middle. The $50,000 Lesson The difference between the initial valuation ($115,000) and the revised valuation ($165,000) was $50,000 — a 43% increase on a single property. This entire difference was caused by one thing: finding the RIGHT comp instead of settling for the closest acreage match. The 23ac comp at $4,130/ac was the closest in acreage but NOT the closest in quality. It was likely an inferior property — timbered, worse road, wetlands, bad shape — something that pulled its PPA down well below what quality land in the area actually trades at. The Tampico Way comp at $7,847/ac was further in acreage (22.3 vs 25 — a minor difference) but MUCH closer in quality — cleared/maintained land, similar layout, same county. One great quality-matched comp was worth more than five mediocre comps that didn't match the subject's quality. This is the single most important comping lesson: find comps that match your subject's QUALITY, not just its acreage. Acreage match is necessary but not sufficient. Quality match is what

dn't match the subject's quality. This is the single most important comping lesson: find comps that match your subject's QUALITY, not just its acreage. Acreage match is necessary but not sufficient. Quality match is what makes a comp truly comparable.

ALL 20 Q&A — FULLY ANSWERED WITH NUANCE Q1-2: Already Listed Properties Does being listed on MLS change comping? No — still comp through sold comps. BUT check the active listing's VIEW-TO-SAVE RATIO on Zillow: 1 save per 10 views = fairly priced. Bad ratio = overpriced. Properties with realtors are almost always priced above market — realtors tell sellers to list high for negotiation room. $200K listing with $150K in comps = seller leaving room, real value is what comps say. Q3-4: Farmland Premium How much more? 30-50% more than pasture (lean 30%). Yielding asset = income stream baked into price. Idle farmland? Only 10% more than pasture — looks nice, has potential, but no income. Crop rows visible = full premium. No rows = idle, 10% only. Q5-6: Power Lines vs Utility Lines High voltage through middle: 25-30% discount. Huge towers, buzzing noise, can't build under/near. If farther back on large property: 10-20%. On edge/border: Back/side = 5%. Narrow property where edge is close = 10-30%. UTILITY lines (no noise): Front = 10%, back/side = 5% or nothing. Main issue is building restriction and visual, NOT noise. Q7-8: Timber Identification Planted timber: Trees in organized SELECT LINES/rows, usually under LLC/company name, all same size. Natural forest: Random, disoriented, mixed sizes/species. Mature hardwood: Tall, dense, large uniform crowns. Scrubby pine: Thinner, irregular, smaller. Don't estimate dollar value — note 'timber has potential harvest value' and put in Section 9. Q9-10: Mobile Homes Value separately? Always value LAND as vacant, but 100% note the mobile home. Put in Section 9 for seller questions. Age/condition matter? Absolutely — 1980s singlewide in bad shape ≈ liability. 2015 doublewide in good shape = $25-35K+ value-add. Can't assess from satellite

% note the mobile home. Put in Section 9 for seller questions. Age/condition matter? Absolutely — 1980s singlewide in bad shape ≈ liability. 2015 doublewide in good shape = $25-35K+ value-add. Can't assess from satellite — seller must provide specs. Q11-12: Pending & Active Comps Pending: Always discount 5-10% from list. Weight at 80-85% of sold comps. Most current data but not finalized. Active: Mostly ceiling reference. ~20% drop to actual sold typical. Check view-to-save ratio: good ratio = closer to market. Bad ratio = overpriced, highest ceiling only. Q13-14: Area Quality Top 3 signals: 1) How rural/isolated — more rural = lower PPA generally. 2) House quality on satellite — nice houses vs run-down mobile homes. 3) Street View confirmation — trash, unmaintained properties = poor area; maintained homes, clean roads = nice area. MUST go to Street View — can't tell from GIS data alone. Q15-16: Multiple Parcels & Package Deals Flag every time? Yes — any other properties, even just one, note it and put in Section 9. Adjacent parcels same owner? Note adjacency but comp individually. Combined potential is interesting but doesn't change individual values. Q17-18: Market Health Hot: More solds than actives + low DOM (30-90 days). Cold: Lots of actives, few solds, high DOM (6+ months). User doesn't care much — business model works in any market with aggressive pricing and elite marketing. Lots of actives with few solds = sellers are overpricing; sold comps = TRUE market value. Q19: Comp Weighting — Same Acreage/Worse Area vs Different Acreage/Same Area

No universal answer. Both are useful data. Draw TWO trendlines in your head: 'what does each acreage trade at in this area?' (PPA curve) and 'what does this property type trade at across areas?' (area quality curve). Where those trendlines INTERSECT = approximately your price. Use both comps to inform both trendlines. Q20: Outlier Comps NEVER 100% disregard. Pull APN, check LI, read Zillow description and photos. Outliers almost always have a reason: well/septic, farmland, closer to town, lake proximity. Photos sometimes reveal things NOT in description — call those out. If reason makes sense = ceiling. If unexplainable = be aware, don't weight heavily. --- END OF PART 7 --- Part 7 covers: Two live comping examples with user's exact thinking, Ada Ln case study ($115K to $165K lesson), 9 key lessons expanded, all 20 Q&A answers with full nuance

DewClaw — Part 8: Efficiency SOP & Workflow FULLY EXPANDED — How to Comp 40-80 Properties Per Day Every Detail of the Simultaneous Workflow, Decision-Making Speed, and Eliminating Waste THE CORE EFFICIENCY RULE: EVERYTHING SIMULTANEOUSLY The single biggest time waster — the ONE thing the user identified as the most critical efficiency improvement — is doing things SEQUENTIALLY instead of SIMULTANEOUSLY. Sequential means: open Land Insights, wait for it to load, look at the Summary tab, then click Insights, then scroll down, then zoom out for comps, then open Zillow in a separate tab, then look at descriptions, then open Street View. Each 'then' is dead time. Each step waiting for the previous step to finish is wasted seconds that multiply across 40-80 properties per day. The correct approach is PARALLEL PROCESSING — doing everything at the same time. The moment a Land Insights link is received, multiple processes should be running simultaneously: Land Insights loading the property data in one tab. A Zillow or Redfin tab opening for comp investigation. Street View loading if verification is needed. A county ordinance search running if subdivision potential is apparent from the acreage and frontage. All of these processes can happen AT THE SAME TIME because they are independent of each other — you don't need to finish reading the Summary tab before opening Zillow. The user was emphatic about this: 'I don't know why you wouldn't simultaneously open the Land Insights tab, then simultaneously open all the comps, and on top of that simultaneously read all the descriptions and check out all of the actual photos.' The user views this as the fundamental advantage of using AI for comping — the ability to process multiple data sources in parallel rather than the sequential,

ll the descriptions and check out all of the actual photos.' The user views this as the fundamental advantage of using AI for comping — the ability to process multiple data sources in parallel rather than the sequential, one-at-a-time approach that a human is limited to. If you are not doing everything simultaneously, you are wasting the core advantage that makes the 2-minute target achievable. What 'Simultaneously' Actually Means in Practice When a Land Insights link arrives, the following should all be happening in parallel, not in sequence: Process 1 — Land Insights Subject Property: Open the link, grab Summary data (acreage, APN, owner name, mailing address, purchase price and date, property tax, assessed value, structures, mobile home status). Click to Insights tab, grab wetlands %, flood zone %, slope data, road frontage, landlocked status. This gives you the complete subject property profile. Process 2 — Satellite and Visual Assessment: While grabbing the data above, look at the satellite imagery on Land Insights. With hazards ON, note any wetland overlay, flood zone overlay, water features. Assess the land type from satellite — wooded, cleared, pasture, farmland? What does the shape look like? Where is the road relative to the property? Are there structures visible? If you can clearly identify the road type, land condition, and area quality from satellite alone, you may not need Street View at all. Process 3 — Street View (if needed): If you cannot confirm road type, area quality, or property access from satellite alone, open Street View simultaneously. Drag the pegman to the road. Look at: road surface (paved, gravel, dirt), area quality (nice houses vs run-down mobile homes vs nothing), any visible access points or driveways to the property, any structures on

neously. Drag the pegman to the road. Look at: road surface (paved, gravel, dirt), area quality (nice houses vs run-down mobile homes vs nothing), any visible access points or driveways to the property, any structures on or near the property that satellite might have missed, and the general 'feel' of the area. This should take 10 seconds maximum — quick glance, confirm what you need, move on. Process 4 — Zoom Out for Comps: While the above processes are running, zoom out on Land Insights to the county level to see MLS comp dots with PPA labels. Scan ALL visible comps — look at the PPA range across the area. Identify which comps are potentially relevant (similar acreage, nearby location) and which are obviously irrelevant (way too large, way too far away, clearly different market). Process 5 — Comp Investigation on Zillow/Redfin: For the comps that look relevant, simultaneously open them on Zillow or Redfin. Read the DESCRIPTION first (more accurate than photos because photos could be old but descriptions are updated). Then check photos to confirm land condition, road type, and any features or issues. If a comp's PPA doesn't make sense after reading the description and checking photos, open a DUPLICATE Land Insights tab and enter the comp's APN for full verification. Process 6 — County Ordinance (if subdivision potential): If the property has significant acreage (20+ acres) AND decent road frontage, simultaneously Google the county subdivision ordinance. Find: minor subdivision lot threshold, minimum lot size, frontage requirements, flagpole rules. This takes 30 seconds of searching while everything else is running.

THE COMP INVESTIGATION PROCESS — DONE IN PARALLEL Scanning All Comps — Whittle Down Quickly When you zoom out on Land Insights and see comp dots, you might see 8, 10, 15, or even 20 comps visible. You do NOT need to investigate all of them in depth. The process is a FUNNEL — start wide and narrow down quickly: First pass (2 seconds): Scan all PPA labels. Immediately identify the range — what is the highest PPA? What is the lowest? What is the cluster in the middle? Any obvious outliers? This gives you the full spectrum in a glance. Second pass (5 seconds): Identify which comps are TRASH — way too large (100+ acres when subject is 10 acres), clearly in a different area/market, obviously irrelevant land type. Mentally dismiss these. Don't click on them. Don't investigate them. They add nothing to the story. Third pass (10 seconds): Click into the remaining RELEVANT comps — similar acreage, similar area, PPAs that make sense for this market. These are your potential anchors, ceilings, and floors. Open them for investigation. Fourth pass (30-60 seconds): For the relevant comps, read descriptions on Zillow/Redfin, check photos, verify land type and road type. This is where you confirm whether each comp is actually comparable to your subject. Comps that looked relevant from the PPA label might turn out to be irrelevant after reading the description (HOA, structures, farmland premium, wetlands issues). Dismiss those and focus on the genuinely comparable ones. Final assessment (15-30 seconds): You should now have 3-5 solid comps categorized as anchors, ceilings, and floors. Build the story, calculate your adjustments, and land on a price range. Deliver the 9-section output. The entire comp investigation process — from first scan to final price — should take about 60-90

ceilings, and floors. Build the story, calculate your adjustments, and land on a price range. Deliver the 9-section output. The entire comp investigation process — from first scan to final price — should take about 60-90 seconds. Combined with the 30-60 seconds for subject property data gathering, the total should be approximately 2 minutes. Reading Descriptions — More Accurate Than Photos The user specifically emphasized that DESCRIPTIONS are more accurate than photos. The reason: listing photos could be OLD — taken months or even years before the sale, showing conditions that no longer exist. The property might have been timbered since the photos were taken, or cleared, or had a structure demolished. But the description is typically updated by the listing agent and reflects the current or recent condition of the property more accurately. This means when you investigate a comp, read the DESCRIPTION FIRST. If the description tells you everything you need to know (land type, road condition, features, issues, HOA status), you may not even need to spend time on the photos. Photos are SECONDARY CONFIRMATION — useful for verifying what the description says, catching things the description didn't mention, and visually comparing the comp to your subject. But the description is the primary source of truth. When to Pull the APN for a Comp You do NOT need to pull the APN and open a duplicate Land Insights tab for every single comp. That would be too slow. Only pull the APN when: 1) A comp's PPA doesn't make sense after reading the description and checking photos — something is off and you need to see the full LI data (wetlands, slope, flood) to understand why. 2) The description is vague or missing and photos don't tell the full story — you need LI data to fill in the gaps. 3)

s — something is off and you need to see the full LI data (wetlands, slope, flood) to understand why. 2) The description is vague or missing and photos don't tell the full story — you need LI data to fill in the gaps. 3) You suspect a comp might have wetlands, flood zone, or slope issues that explain a low PPA but aren't mentioned in the listing. For comps where the description clearly explains the PPA and the photos confirm the condition, you can skip the APN lookup and save time.

STREET VIEW — WHEN TO USE AND WHEN TO SKIP Street View is a CONFIRMATION tool, not a discovery tool. You use it to VERIFY things you already suspect or need to confirm — road type, area quality, access points, structures. If you can already confirm these from the satellite imagery on Land Insights, you do NOT need Street View. When to SKIP Street View If you can clearly see from satellite: the road is obviously paved (smooth, wide, marked), the area has nice houses with maintained yards (visible from above), the property has a clear access point (driveway visible), and the land condition is obvious (clearly cleared, or clearly timbered, or obviously farmland with crop rows) — then Street View adds nothing. Skip it and save 10-15 seconds. When to USE Street View If any of the following are UNCLEAR from satellite: Is the road paved, gravel, or dirt? (Roads can look similar from above.) What is the area quality? (Can't tell house condition from directly above.) Is there a visible driveway or access point to the property? Are there structures hidden under tree canopy that satellite can't see? Is there standing water, marshy areas, or other conditions not visible from above? Is there a fence indicating pasture use? When you do use Street View, spend a MAXIMUM of 10-15 seconds. Quick glance: road surface (paved/gravel/dirt), area quality (nice/average/poor), property access (driveway visible?), anything notable. Then move on. Don't explore the entire road. Don't drive around the neighborhood. Quick glance, confirm what you need, close. Street View as Standard SOP for Property Access The user did clarify that you should ALWAYS look at the access to the property — meaning the road and how you would enter the property. This is a standard operating procedure item. When you are

rd SOP for Property Access The user did clarify that you should ALWAYS look at the access to the property — meaning the road and how you would enter the property. This is a standard operating procedure item. When you are doing Street View (for confirmation), always check: what does the road look like right at the property? Is there a driveway or entrance? Is the access easy (flat turnoff from road) or difficult (steep, narrow, overgrown)? Is there a gate, fence, or any barrier? These access details affect value and should be noted in Extra Notes.

NO GREETINGS, NO PREAMBLE, NO NARRATION, NO CONTINUE BUTTONS This section exists because these time-wasters were specifically and repeatedly called out by the user as things that need to stop completely. No Greetings When the user sends a Land Insights link, the response should NOT begin with 'Hi!' or 'Good morning!' or 'Let me take a look at this property for you!' or any other greeting. The user is sending 40-80 links per day. They do not need to be greeted 40-80 times. They need the 9-section deliverable as fast as possible. The moment a link arrives, START WORKING. The first thing the user should see in the response is the deliverable, not a greeting. No Preamble Do not write 'Let me pull up this property and take a look' or 'I'll check the Land Insights data for this one' or 'Interesting property, let me evaluate it.' These sentences communicate nothing useful. The user already knows what you are going to do — they sent you a link specifically for you to comp it. You do not need to announce that you are going to do the thing they asked you to do. Just DO it. No Mid-Process Narration Do not write 'Now let me check the Insights tab...' or 'I can see the property has some wetlands, let me zoom out for comps...' or 'The satellite shows timber, let me verify with Street View...' This narration serves no purpose. The user does not need a play-by-play of your investigation process. They need the RESULT of your investigation, not the process itself. Work silently, build the story internally, and deliver the complete 9-section output when you are done. No Continue Buttons or Permission Requests NEVER ask the user to click 'continue' or confirm that you should proceed. There is absolutely no reason for this. The user sent you a link to comp. You know what to do. You have

o Continue Buttons or Permission Requests NEVER ask the user to click 'continue' or confirm that you should proceed. There is absolutely no reason for this. The user sent you a link to comp. You know what to do. You have been extensively trained on the process. Just DO IT. Asking for permission to do the thing you were specifically asked to do wastes the user's time and breaks their workflow. They are trying to send you 40-80 links per day — if they have to click 'continue' on each one, that is 40-80 unnecessary interruptions. The ONLY exception: if something is genuinely broken — Land Insights is down, the link doesn't work, the property doesn't exist — then briefly note the issue and ask for guidance. Otherwise, just work and deliver.

SCREENSHOTS — HOW TO USE THEM EFFICIENTLY Screenshots are how you 'see' the browser screen. Every screenshot takes approximately 1-2 seconds. On a 2-minute target for the full deliverable, every screenshot is roughly 1-2% of your total time budget. This means screenshots must be PURPOSEFUL — every one should give you information you need. Essential Screenshots (Always Take These) 1. Property loaded view: When the LI link first loads, take a screenshot to see the parcel outline, satellite imagery, and Summary data. This is your first look at the property — shape, land type, surroundings, approximate location. 2. Insights data: After clicking Insights tab, take a screenshot to capture wetlands %, flood zone %, slope data, road frontage, landlocked status. This is critical quantitative data that drives your valuation. 3. Comp zoom view: After zooming out to county level, take a screenshot to see all the comp dots with PPA labels. This is your market data — the foundation of your pricing story. 4. Street View (if needed): One screenshot from Street View to confirm road type and area quality. Only if satellite was unclear. Comp Investigation Screenshots When you click into comps on Zillow/Redfin: take screenshots of the listing to read the description and see the photos. The user specifically said to look at AS MANY comp photos as possible — amenities, slope, cleared areas, plot maps, aerials, structures, layout. 'Max photos = max intel.' This is where you are building the detailed story about each comp, so don't skimp on screenshots during comp investigation. The goal is to thoroughly understand each relevant comp so you can weight it accurately. But be STRATEGIC: scan the PPA labels first and identify which comps are worth investigating. Don't take screenshots of every

tion. The goal is to thoroughly understand each relevant comp so you can weight it accurately. But be STRATEGIC: scan the PPA labels first and identify which comps are worth investigating. Don't take screenshots of every single comp — take screenshots of the RELEVANT ones that will actually influence your pricing. Trash comps (too large, too far, obviously different market) don't need screenshots. Focus your screenshot budget on the 3-5 comps that will drive your story. Avoiding Unnecessary Screenshots Don't take multiple screenshots at the same zoom level showing the same information. Don't take screenshots mid-process just to 'check in' — that is the visual equivalent of narrating. Don't take screenshots of things you already know or have already confirmed. Every screenshot should give you NEW information that you didn't have before.

THE DARK MAP TILES ISSUE — PERMANENTLY SOLVED When you zoom out too far on Land Insights, the satellite imagery goes dark and you can only see comp dots without their PPA labels. This is NORMAL BEHAVIOR — it happens because the map tiles at that zoom level haven't loaded. It is NOT a technical problem and does NOT require troubleshooting or URL re-navigation. The fix is simple: zoom back IN a few scroll ticks until the PPA labels appear. The satellite imagery will load and the comp labels will become readable. This takes 1-2 seconds. Track the zoom limits: The user specifically instructed to keep a mental note of how many zoom levels out it goes dark and how many zoom levels in it becomes readable again. That boundary is the farthest you should zoom — the sweet spot is just inside that boundary where you can see the most comp labels while still having readable satellite imagery. Navigate directly to this zoom level rather than overshooting and having to zoom back in. If you want to see the broader area without comp labels: Toggle MLS Data OFF in Data Layers (Data Layers > MLS Data > toggle the switch). This removes the comp labels and lets you see the pure satellite imagery and area context. Use this for a quick 5-second area orientation — where is the nearest town? Where are the major roads? How rural is this area? Then toggle MLS Data back ON for comping. This is a quick operation, not a time sink. THE WRONG PROPERTY CLICK — ONE-CLICK FIX If you accidentally click on a neighboring parcel and the info panel switches to a different property: click the BACK ARROW in the TOP LEFT CORNER of the screen. One click. It takes you right back to the original property you were working on. Do NOT re-navigate via URL. Do NOT try to re-search for the property. Do NOT re-enter the

BACK ARROW in the TOP LEFT CORNER of the screen. One click. It takes you right back to the original property you were working on. Do NOT re-navigate via URL. Do NOT try to re-search for the property. Do NOT re-enter the original link. Just click the back arrow. One click. Done. This saves 10-20 seconds every time it happens.

DECISION-MAKING SPEED — WHEN TO SKIP STEPS When to Skip Street View Entirely If you can CLEARLY see from satellite: road type is obvious (paved road clearly visible as smooth, wide surface), land condition is obvious (crop rows = farmland, dense trees = timbered, open green = cleared/pasture), area quality is assessable (houses visible, condition apparent from above), and property access is clear (driveway or road frontage visible). If all four are clear from satellite, Street View adds nothing. Skip it. Save 10-15 seconds. When to Skip Comp APN Verification If a comp's description on Zillow/Redfin clearly explains why it sold at its PPA — the description mentions wetlands, or states it's farmland, or describes the road condition, or reveals HOA restrictions — and the photos confirm the description, you do not need to open a duplicate LI tab and enter the APN. The description and photos told the full story. Only pull the APN when something DOESN'T MAKE SENSE after reading the description and checking photos. When to Work With Limited Comp Data If comp data is sparse in the immediate area, WIDEN your acreage range (AUTO first, then below=80%, above=150%, then wider if needed). Keep looking until you have SOMETHING to build a story from — at minimum a floor and a ceiling. If you truly cannot find any sold comps after widening significantly, use active listings as your data set: discount them approximately 20-30% (could be 10% if you have a sold floor to anchor against). You are essentially making the market at that point. The user said: 'Keep on looking until you actually have comps so that you can build out a storyline.' Don't deliver a valuation with zero comp support. But also don't spend 10 minutes searching — widen the range aggressively, take what the market gives

until you actually have comps so that you can build out a storyline.' Don't deliver a valuation with zero comp support. But also don't spend 10 minutes searching — widen the range aggressively, take what the market gives you, build the best story you can with available data, and note in the deliverable that comp data was limited. Straightforward Properties — Accelerated Delivery If a property is clearly straightforward — clean land, paved road, no wetlands, no flood zone, flat terrain, good shape, no structures — you can skip investigation steps that would only confirm what you already know. You don't need Street View to confirm the paved road you can see on satellite. You don't need to toggle hazards to check for wetlands when Insights says 0%. You don't need to investigate slope when the average is 3%. Just grab the data, pull the comps, build the story, and deliver. A straightforward property with good comp data should be deliverable in UNDER 2 minutes.

SPARSE COMP MARKETS — HOW TO HANDLE THEM Some markets have very few sold comps — maybe only 1-2 in the entire county at anything close to your subject's acreage. This is challenging but not impossible. The user taught a specific approach for these situations: Step 1: Widen the Acreage Range Aggressively Start with AUTO acreage range. If not enough comps, set below to 80% and above to 150%. If still not enough, go wider — 50% below and 200% above. Keep widening until you find data. Even comps that are significantly different in acreage give you trendline points — a 10ac comp at $8K/ac and a 40ac comp at $3K/ac tells you the curve shape even if your subject is 25ac. Step 2: Use Active Listings as Ceilings When sold comps are scarce, active listings become more important. They are NOT anchor comps (they haven't sold, so they might be overpriced), but they give you ceiling data. Discount active listings approximately 20-30% from their list price to estimate what they might actually sell for. If there is ONE sold comp (your floor) and several active listings (your ceilings, discounted 20-30%), you can bracket a reasonable range. Check the VIEW-TO-SAVE RATIO on active listings: 1 save per 10 views = probably priced close to correctly, discount only 10-15%. Terrible ratio (lots of views, almost no saves) = overpriced, discount 25-30%. This helps you calibrate which active listings are closer to reality. Step 3: Use the PPA Trendline Even with limited comps, you can plot them on the PPA curve. If you have a 10ac comp at $8K/ac and a 50ac comp at $3K/ac, the exponential curve tells you that 25ac should fall between those two — probably around $4,500-$5,500/ac. This interpolation is imprecise but gives you a data-supported starting point rather than a complete guess. Step 4:

exponential curve tells you that 25ac should fall between those two — probably around $4,500-$5,500/ac. This interpolation is imprecise but gives you a data-supported starting point rather than a complete guess. Step 4: Note Limited Confidence Always note in the deliverable when comp data is sparse: 'Comp data limited in this county — only X sold comps found. Valuation based on available data plus PPA trendline interpolation. Confidence is lower than typical. Recommend cautious offer.' This manages the user's expectations and lets them know to verify if possible.

VALUE-ADD — ALWAYS INCLUDE, NO EXCEPTIONS The user initially said to skip value-add when the upside is marginal, then REVERSED this instruction and said to ALWAYS include it. The reasoning is critical to understand: In most cases, the user targets 50% of market value for the as-is flip. But sometimes the seller pushes back and wants 60% or 70%. In a normal flip-only analysis, the user cannot go above 50% because there is not enough margin. But if there is a value-add (subdivision) that makes the improved value significantly higher than the as-is value, the user CAN come up to 60% or 70% of the as-is value because the subdivision play provides additional margin. Example: property worth $100,000 as-is. Seller wants $70,000 (70%). In a normal flip at 50%, the user can only offer $50,000 — too low for the seller. But if subdivision makes the property worth $130,000, the user can offer $70,000 (54% of improved value) — the seller is happy, and the user still makes significant profit from the subdivision upside. The value-add is what makes the deal WORK at the seller's price. This is why 99% of people only look at normal flips and miss deals. They can't come up to 70% because they don't see the subdivision play. The user CAN come up because the value-add provides the extra margin. 'Always, always actually add the value-adds' — no exceptions, even if the normal flip works fine. The value-add gives the user OPTIONS and FLEXIBILITY in negotiation that they would not have otherwise. LAND ID — COMPLETELY DISREGARD The user explicitly instructed to completely disregard Land ID. Do not open it, do not reference it, do not try to use it. It is not part of the workflow. If it was previously mentioned in training materials, remove it from your process. It serves no purpose in the

disregard Land ID. Do not open it, do not reference it, do not try to use it. It is not part of the workflow. If it was previously mentioned in training materials, remove it from your process. It serves no purpose in the current workflow and attempting to use it wastes time. Focus exclusively on Land Insights, Zillow/Redfin, and Google Maps/Street View for all property analysis. TAB MANAGEMENT — CLEAN SLATE EVERY PROPERTY After every single property evaluation is complete and the 9-section deliverable has been delivered: CLOSE THE ENTIRE TAB GROUP. Every tab, every duplicate, everything — close it all. Start completely fresh for the next property with a clean slate and zero leftover tabs. The reason: at 40-80 properties per day, leftover tabs from previous properties accumulate rapidly and cause browser lag, confusion, and slower performance. A clean tab group for each property ensures consistent speed and prevents errors from accidentally clicking on old tabs.

THE 2-MINUTE TARGET — HOW TO HIT IT CONSISTENTLY The target is 2 minutes from receiving a link to delivering the complete 9-section output. This is aggressive but achievable if the simultaneous workflow is executed properly. Here is how the 2 minutes should break down: 0:00-0:15 — Property Data Gathering: Open LI link, grab Summary data (acreage, APN, owner, mailing, price/date, tax, structures), click Insights (wetlands, flood, slope, frontage, landlocked). Simultaneously assess satellite imagery for land type, shape, road, features. 0:15-0:25 — Street View (if needed): Quick 10-second confirmation of road type, area quality, and access. Skip entirely if satellite already confirmed everything. 0:25-0:40 — Comp Scanning: Zoom out to county level, scan all comp dots, identify relevant vs trash, identify the PPA range and cluster. 0:40-1:20 — Comp Investigation: Open the 3-5 most relevant comps on Zillow/Redfin. Read descriptions, check photos, confirm land type and road for each. Categorize as anchor, ceiling, or floor. If a PPA doesn't make sense, quick APN check in duplicate LI tab. 1:20-1:40 — Story Building: Determine subject's position between floor and ceiling based on anchor comps. Calculate adjustments (road type, land type, wetlands, slope, proximity). Determine if subdivision potential exists and estimate value-add scenarios. 1:40-2:00 — Deliver: Output the complete 9-section deliverable in one response. No preamble, no narration, no continue. Just the deliverable. For straightforward properties with good comp data: under 2 minutes. For complex properties (heavy wetlands, sparse comps, subdivision analysis needed): 2-4 minutes is acceptable. The user said '2 minutes is the target' and '2 to 4 minutes back to me' — so 2 is the goal, 4 is the maximum. 40-80

mplex properties (heavy wetlands, sparse comps, subdivision analysis needed): 2-4 minutes is acceptable. This means: no chitchat between properties, no debriefing, no discussion unless the user specifically asks a question or provides feedback. DewClaw Land Holdings Pricing & PPA Trendline Mastery Guide for Normal Flips Comprehensive Training Document — PPA Trendline Construction, Comp Investigation Process, Pricing Philosophy, Red Flag Identification, & Valuation Delivery Internal Reference — April 2026
