1. The PPA Trendline: Core Framework The PPA (Price Per Acre) Trendline is the foundational analytical tool for every property valuation at DewClaw. It is not optional. It is not something that gets built only on complex deals. It is built mentally on EVERY property evaluation, every time, without exception. The trendline is what transforms comping from guesswork into a defensible, math-backed process. 1.1 — The Shape of the Curve The PPA trendline is NOT linear. It follows an exponential curve on the low-acreage end that gradually flattens into a roughly linear shape on the high-acreage end. This is the universal shape of rural land markets: Small lots (1-5 acres): PPA is HIGH and rises STEEPLY as acreage decreases. A 2-acre lot might sell at $15,000/ac while a 5-acre lot sells at $8,000/ac. The change is dramatic — small acreage differences cause huge PPA swings. This is the volatile, exponential, hard-to-predict part of the curve. Medium lots (5-20 acres): PPA is moderate and declining more gradually. The curve is still falling but the rate of descent is slowing. This is the transition zone where the exponential shape bends toward linear. Large parcels (20+ acres): PPA is LOW and relatively STABLE. A 20-acre parcel might be $3,500/ac and a 40-acre might be $2,800/ac. The curve has flattened out. PPA is not jumping wildly anymore. This is the predictable, trustworthy part of the curve. 1.2 — Why This Shape Matters for Pricing Understanding the curve shape is not academic — it directly controls how you weight comps and where you place confidence in your valuation: The LOW END (small lots) is exponential and volatile. Small acreage differences cause huge PPA swings. Comps in this zone are harder to trust and riskier to lean on. That is why the comp range is set at only

r valuation: The LOW END (small lots) is exponential and volatile. Small acreage differences cause huge PPA swings. Comps in this zone are harder to trust and riskier to lean on. That is why the comp range is set at only 50% below the subject acreage — to limit exposure to the steep, unpredictable part of the curve. The HIGH END (large parcels) is flat and stable. PPA does not change dramatically between a 30-acre and a 50-acre parcel. Comps in this zone are trustworthy and predictable. That is why the comp range extends to 100% above the subject acreage (or even 200-300% if needed) — the flat part of the curve can be trusted at wider ranges. Always weight toward the higher-acreage comps (lower PPA) for a conservative valuation. The flat part of the trendline is where the data is reliable. The steep part is where mistakes get expensive. 1.3 — The Trendline Is the Story At its core, the trendline is the market itself, visualized. When you pull comps at 2 acres, 5 acres, 10 acres, 20 acres, and 50 acres, you are mapping out how the market values land across the acreage spectrum. Each comp is a data point. Connected together, they tell a story about what buyers in this county are willing to pay for different sized parcels.

The subject property lives somewhere on this curve. The trendline tells you where. If the subject is at 10 acres and the curve shows $6,000/ac at 5 acres and $3,500/ac at 15 acres, the 10-acre point is somewhere in between — shaped by whether the curve is steep or flat in that range. You are not guessing. You are constructing a market. If the data says 5 acres goes for X and 15 acres goes for Y, then 10 acres lives between those two points. A realtor or seller looking at the same comps would reach a similar conclusion. The trendline makes the valuation defensible. Pricing is always an estimate — it is never going to be exactly right. But if the math is sound and the odds are stacked, the number will be fair and defensible. Then discount for the list price and move on. 1.4 — Curve Shape Varies by Market Not all counties have the same curve shape. The steepness and spread vary based on the market: Metro-adjacent counties / lake counties: Tend to have STEEPER curves. Big spread between small-lot PPA and large-parcel PPA. Strong demand for small lots drives up the low-acreage PPA. Deep rural counties: Tend to have FLATTER curves. Less spread between small and large lot PPA. Less demand for small lots means the curve does not spike as dramatically on the low end. But these are generalizations. Some rural counties have steep curves. The comps themselves tell the truth — let the data speak rather than assuming. The first time in any county, pull the widest possible comp range to see the curve shape. That tells you what kind of market you are in.

2. Building the Trendline in Practice 2.1 — Pull Data Points Across the Spectrum On every evaluation, pull comps at multiple acreage points to see the full curve. You want to know what 2-acre lots sell for, what 5-acre lots sell for, what 10 sells for, what 20 sells for. Each is a data point. The more data points, the clearer the curve, the more trustworthy the valuation. Start with AUTO range on Land Insights, then expand: 50% below / 100% above. If needed, go to 50%/200% or 100%/300%. More comps is always better. Evaluate ALL comps and build the full PPA trendline across the acreage spectrum, then slot the subject into the curve. 2.2 — Filling Gaps in the Trendline You will not always get clean comps at every acreage point. There will be gaps. When there is a gap — say you have solid comps at 5 acres and 15 acres but nothing at 10 — use the curve shape itself to guide where the subject falls: If the curve between those two points looks LINEAR in this market, interpolation is straightforward. A 10-acre property falls proportionally between the 5-acre and 15-acre PPAs. If the curve is STEEP between those two points (exponential zone), the 10-acre PPA will be closer to the 15-acre PPA than the 5-acre PPA — because the exponential curve drops fast on the small-lot side. Weight toward the higher-acreage comp (15 acres) since that is the conservative, more stable end. Even imperfect comps between the gap points help. A 7-acre comp that is not a great match for your 10-acre subject still gives you a ceiling or floor to narrow the range. Any data point between two anchor points tightens the estimate. Build SEPARATE mental curves per comp type when relevant: wooded vs. cleared, waterfront vs. non-waterfront. Each land type has its own PPA behavior. Do not mix a waterfront

tween two anchor points tightens the estimate. Build SEPARATE mental curves per comp type when relevant: wooded vs. cleared, waterfront vs. non-waterfront. Each land type has its own PPA behavior. Do not mix a waterfront 5-acre comp with a landlocked wooded 5-acre comp. 2.3 — Handling Outliers Outliers above and below the trendline are common. They are often off-market sales, friend deals, bidding wars, or distressed sales. When you see a comp that does not fit the trendline: Note it, but do not let it pull the valuation. Check listing history — was there a bidding war? Price drops? Sold same day? Check if it was off-market (Sold-Public Records) — if so, reject it per standing rules. The trendline built from multiple comps is the truth. One outlier is an anecdote. Statistics are proven over larger sample sizes — the more data points, the better the average, the more trustworthy the number. The trendline gets progressively tighter as you identify close ceilings and floors near the subject acreage. You are whittling the range down from broad to narrow, not chasing individual outlier sales.

2.4 — The Perspective Check: Think in Total Dollars When working on the steep part of the curve (small lots), the PPA numbers look intimidating. A 2-acre lot might show $12,000/ac versus $14,000/ac, which seems like a big range. But in total dollars, that is only $24,000 vs $28,000 — a $4,000 difference. Not catastrophic. Meanwhile, on the flat part of the curve (large parcels), the PPA looks calm. A 100-acre property at $500/ac versus $750/ac seems like a small PPA difference. But in total dollars, that is $50,000 vs $75,000 — a $25,000 swing. Much more impactful. Do not freak out about high PPA numbers on small lots. Do not get complacent about low PPA numbers on large parcels. Always convert to total dollars to calibrate the actual financial impact of your pricing decisions. Keep your head on straight.

3. The Comp Investigation Process This is the exact order of operations for evaluating every single comp. This process is what makes the difference between a 200% accurate valuation and a 500% accurate one. Do not skip steps. Do not get lazy. Every step adds a piece of the puzzle that makes the final picture clearer. Step 1 — Legitimacy Check Is this a real, usable comp? Check immediately: Closed (MLS) = gold standard. Use it. Pending = usable but discount 5-10% from list price (hot market = 5%, slow = 10%). Always flag which comps are pending and that you applied the discount. Off-market / Sold-Public Records = REJECT. Do not use. Move on immediately. Never use AI Comp, Zestimate, or any platform estimate. Never cross county lines for comps. If the comp fails this check, do not spend another second on it. Move to the next one. Step 2 — Listing Photos (ALWAYS First) Go through EVERY listing photo. Even when there are 20 photos of trees. Even when they all look the same. Here is what you are looking for: Cleared areas / building sites — where could someone build? How cleared is it? Maintained or overgrown? Road access — is there a visible driveway, entryway, or road? Paved, gravel, or dirt? Culverts — are there culvert crossings visible? This indicates creek crossings and infrastructure. Fencing — is the property fenced? What kind? This adds value. Structures — any buildings, sheds, mobile homes? Metal or wood? Condition? Water features — pond, creek, lake frontage? Size and quality of shoreline? Wetland indicators — standing water, boggy areas, marsh grass? Slope — can you see steep terrain, hillsides, ravines? Timber — is the timber managed (uniform rows, plantation) or natural? Clear-cut stumps? Rocky soil — visible rock outcroppings that could affect perc tests and

rass? Slope — can you see steep terrain, hillsides, ravines? Timber — is the timber managed (uniform rows, plantation) or natural? Clear-cut stumps? Rocky soil — visible rock outcroppings that could affect perc tests and building? Aerial photos — these are gold. They show the parcel shape, surrounding area, how the property sits relative to neighbors and roads. Always look carefully at aerials. Community amenity photos — swimming pools, clubhouses, HOA amenities. These are NOT on the actual parcel. Do not assign extra value for them. They just indicate the area is probably nicer than average. It boils down to proximity, which you are already evaluating. Even trees can be useful — you can sometimes identify timber type, density, and whether the land has been managed or clear-cut. Every photo is a potential piece of the puzzle.

Step 3 — Listing Description (Second) Read the full listing description AFTER you have already seen the photos. Now you can put two and two together — the description may mention something you did not catch visually, or confirm what you saw in the photos. Together they give a much more complete picture than either one alone. Look for specific details: acreage breakdown (wooded vs. cleared), road type, utilities available, perc test results, survey information, zoning, restrictions, HOA fees, easements, and any mention of slope, wetlands, or flood zones. Also look for red flags in the language (covered in detail in Section 5). Do NOT take positive marketing claims at face value. 'Great building site,' 'cleared homesite,' 'perfect for your dream home' — all need verification through the photos, map view, or Land Insights APN. Marketing language is designed to sell. Data tells the truth. Step 4 — Map View on Redfin/Zillow (Third) Check the map view to see the parcel boundaries and surrounding area. This gives you proximity context, neighboring parcel sizes, road network, and distance to towns or features. CRITICAL WARNING: The parcel lines on Redfin and Zillow are WRONG approximately 40-50% of the time. Since you have already seen the listing photos, you can cross-reference and tell if the map boundaries make sense. If the photos showed a cleared field but the map shows the parcel sitting entirely in timber, something is off with the map. Do not trust the map blindly. Step 5 — Land Insights APN Check (When Needed) This is the bulletproof, foolproof verification step. Open a separate Land Insights tab and pull the comp's APN. This gives you 100% accurate parcel boundaries, soil data, wetland/flood zone overlays, slope contours, road classification, and proximity to the

ification step. Open a separate Land Insights tab and pull the comp's APN. This gives you 100% accurate parcel boundaries, soil data, wetland/flood zone overlays, slope contours, road classification, and proximity to the subject — all on the most reliable data source available. When to use Step 5: When something does not add up between the photos, description, and map. When the comp is a critical anchor for the valuation. When you see an outlier (high or low) and need to understand why. When you want to verify road access, slope, or wetland status. On Land Insights, you can also access the aerial view and sometimes Street View, which gives a much better representation of the property than Redfin/Zillow maps. If the APN does not aggregate in Land Insights, move on — it happens, and you cannot force it. Combined with the listing data (photos + description + sale price), the Land Insights APN check gives you the most complete, most accurate comp evaluation possible. This is what makes comps 200-500% more accurate. Timing Target For a standard comp where photos and description align: Steps 1-3 take 30-45 seconds. Step 4 adds 15-20 seconds. Total: under 1 minute per comp for the standard process. Step 5 (Land Insights APN) adds 30-60 seconds but is only used on critical comps or when something does not add up. For 5-8 comps with APN checks on the 2-3 most important ones, the full process fits within 3-4 minutes.

4. The Comp vs. Subject Comparison Checklist For every comp, run through this checklist to determine whether the comp is better, worse, or equivalent to the subject property. This determines how you weight the comp in your valuation. The checklist is ordered by typical impact on value: Feature What to Compare Impact on Value Road Quality Paved vs. gravel vs. maintained dirt vs. bad dirt vs. highway frontage. What does the comp have vs. subject? HIGH — paved adds significant premium, bad dirt discounts 20-30% Land Type Cleared & maintained vs. pasture (tall, unkempt) vs. farmland (crop rows, yielding asset) vs. wooded vs. clear-cut (stumps) HIGH — farmland > cleared > pasture > wooded > clear-cut Access Features Driveway present? Gravel or paved? Culverts in? Visible entry on Street View? MODERATE — existing infrastructure adds value Water Features Pond ON property vs. bordering lake vs. seasonal creek vs. nothing. Shore quality matters. HIGH — true waterfront can be 2-3x premium Slope Flat vs. sloped in back vs. sloped in front vs. steep throughout. WHERE the slope sits matters. MODERATE to HIGH — steep front kills value, back slope is less impactful Shape Clean rectangle/square vs. flagpole vs. irregular vs. bowling alley. Is it developable? MODERATE — flagpole = 2-5% discount, bowling alley = major loss Proximity Close to town/city/lake/nice area? Or deep rural? Are similar-sized lots trading nearby? VARIABLE — proximity to city is exponential, small town is modest Wetlands/Flood What percentage? Where on the lot? Buildable ground available? VARIABLE — 20% back wetlands = ~5% discount, 100% = 30-40% Timber Quality Managed plantation (uniform rows) vs. natural timber vs. clear-cut stumps LOW to MODERATE — managed timber adds $200-500/ac Views/Elevation Does property

E — 20% back wetlands = ~5% discount, 100% = 30-40% Timber Quality Managed plantation (uniform rows) vs. natural timber vs. clear-cut stumps LOW to MODERATE — managed timber adds $200-500/ac Views/Elevation Does property sit high? Potential views? Panoramic? LOW to MODERATE — mediocre ~5%, good 10-15%, panoramic 20-30% Demand Signals If selling this comp size here, would there be demand? Similar lots trading? CONTEXT — affects confidence in pricing, not the price itself Never try to split the evaluation (e.g., valuing half at cleared PPA and half at wooded PPA and blending). This creates drastically different numbers that do not make sense together. Instead, find comps that are generally similar overall, build a ceiling and floor, and price from there.

5. Red Flags in Listings 5.1 — Perc Test Language (Critical) Perc test language in listings is one of the most important red flag indicators. A seller with a passing perc test would ALWAYS include it because it removes uncertainty and adds value. When the perc results are hidden or vague, assume the worst. Language in Listing What It Means Action "Perc test passed" or results included POSITIVE — seller is marketing it because it adds value. No mystery. Trust it. Factor as a positive for the comp. "Talk to agent for perc results" or "See details for perc/soil test" RED FLAG — if it passed, they would just say so. They want to get you on the phone to convert you into a lead. Assume perc failed. Discount comp or note the risk. "Perc test expired" ACTUALLY POSITIVE — it means the test DID pass at some point, just needs renewal. Leave hope on this one. Mild positive signal. "Soil test failed" or negative soil language RED FLAG — affects building, foundations, septic. Dealbreaker for residential buyers. Significant discount. Note as inferior comp. No mention of perc at all MYSTERY — and mystery is bad. Good sellers showcase everything positive. Conspicuous absence = assume the worst. Be cautious. Do not assume perc will pass. 5.2 — Listed / Delisted / Relisted Pattern When a property has been listed, delisted, and relisted, the pattern usually means: The seller was priced too high initially and got frustrated with low offers. The seller may have had a disagreement with the realtor and temporarily pulled the listing. The seller may have realized they were overpriced and relisted lower, OR they relisted at the same unrealistic price. The seller may be desperate and trying again. Action: Check the price history. Did they relist lower? (Confirms overpricing.) Same price? (Still

rpriced and relisted lower, OR they relisted at the same unrealistic price. The seller may be desperate and trying again. Action: Check the price history. Did they relist lower? (Confirms overpricing.) Same price? (Still unrealistic.) Note the pattern and factor it into how you weight this comp — it tells you something about the market's willingness to pay at that price level. 5.3 — The Mystery Principle Mystery is BAD for sellers. Good sellers showcase everything positive about their property — perc test results, survey, utilities, road access, cleared building sites. When something is conspicuously absent from a listing, there is usually a reason. The seller is hiding a negative. If a listing is vague about road access — the road is probably bad. If it does not mention perc — it probably failed. If there are no aerial photos — the shape or surroundings are probably unflattering. Assume the worst until proven otherwise.

6. Handling Conflicting Comps When two solid, legitimate comps — similar acreage, similar features, same county — show significantly different PPAs, do not just pick one. Investigate both using the full comp investigation process (Section 3). Usually, comps that appear identical on the surface are NOT truly identical. Something explains the gap — better road access, a cleared building site, waterfront, slope, a feature visible in photo 14 of 20 that you missed. The investigation almost always reveals the difference. If after thorough investigation the comps really do appear identical and the gap is still significant: Check listing history on both. Which one dropped price? (Desperate seller.) Which one went above asking? (Motivated buyer.) Which one sold same day? (Someone really wanted it.) Weight toward whichever comp looks like the most ORGANIC, NORMAL sale — a standard market transaction where a normal buyer and seller agreed on a fair price without unusual pressure on either side. For larger acreage comps (10+ acres), a significant dollar gap (like $30k) between two supposedly identical comps means something is definitely different. Keep digging. The tiebreaker is always: which sale looks the most organic? Not recency, not proximity — but which transaction looks like the most normal, arms-length market sale. Non-Disclosure States Some states do not disclose how much properties actually sold for — including parts of Texas, Mississippi, and Oklahoma. In these states, comping is harder because you cannot see sold prices. Active and pending listings become more important data points out of necessity. Be aware of which states are non-disclosure when comping and adjust expectations for data availability.

7. Making Your Own Market When the subject property is the best or worst thing in the area with nothing directly comparable, you are not stuck. You work with what you have in the same market and build comparison layers. 7.1 — Subject Is the WORST Property in the Area Find the bottom-grade comps in the area — the ones with slope, bad road access, issues. Build a mini trendline from those. Then compare: is the subject worse than those comps, the same, or slightly better? If the subject is still the worst, you have close comps to anchor from and you discount down from there. You are not guessing blind — you are saying 'the worst comps sold at X, mine is a bit worse, so I price below X.' 7.2 — Subject Is the BEST Property in the Area Same process in reverse. Find the highest-quality comps — waterfront, cleared, great access. Build the ceiling from those. Then ask: is the subject better? By how much? Apply a reasonable increase above the best comps. Be conservative — do not go crazy. The premium should be defensible. Waterfront properties are a common example of this. Waterfront parcels often set their own market because they are rare. Price fairly and they sell quickly. 7.3 — The Middle Ground Always Helps Even when pricing the best or worst property, still pull the mediocre/normal comps to establish the baseline. This gives the full spectrum: bottom-grade comps set the floor, normal comps set the middle, top-grade comps set the ceiling. The subject falls somewhere on this spectrum. The trendline, even an imperfect one, is always better than blind guessing. You are never truly without comps. Even when there is nothing identical, there are always properties that are somewhat better and somewhat worse. Those create the range. Then whittle it down based on how the subject

ing. You are never truly without comps. Even when there is nothing identical, there are always properties that are somewhat better and somewhat worse. Those create the range. Then whittle it down based on how the subject compares feature by feature.

8. Special Comp Scenarios 8.1 — Active Listings Active listings get weight in two specific scenarios: Setting list price: Active listings are a ceiling for the list price. You do not want to compete with active listings — you want to look priced well below them so buyers come to you first. If there are active listings at $X/ac, your list price should be clearly below that. Non-disclosure states: When sold prices are not publicly available, active and pending listings may be all you have. In these states, they become more important data points by necessity. In normal markets with plenty of closed comps, active listings are reference points, not primary comps. The market has not validated their price yet. 8.2 — Pending Sales Always discount pending sales by 5% from the listed price to be conservative. This is the standard — hot market or slow market, always apply 5% for safety. Multiple pending sales clustered at a similar price point carry real weight — they indicate the market is accepting that price range. Three pendings at $6,000/ac is a stronger signal than one closed at $5,500/ac. But still discount each by 5%. Always flag which comps are pending and that the 5% discount was applied, so the owner knows exactly what adjustments were made. 8.3 — Old Comps (1-2+ Years) Rural land markets do not shift dramatically year to year. Comps from a couple years ago are still usable. If adjusting for time, add approximately 2-5% annual appreciation — but this is not a precise science. Mix older comps in with modern ones and see how much the market actually changed. If you notice a meaningful shift between old and new comps, note it in extra notes for the owner. But do not overthink the time adjustment — it is not a major factor in rural land. 8.4 — Farmland Comps Farmland (crop

ed. If you notice a meaningful shift between old and new comps, note it in extra notes for the owner. But do not overthink the time adjustment — it is not a major factor in rural land. 8.4 — Farmland Comps Farmland (crop rows, irrigation, tilled soil) is a yielding asset and is worth MORE than standard residential rural land. It occupies a different tier from pasture, cleared, and wooded land. If the subject is farmland in an area with both farmland and residential, comp it against other farmland first. If no farmland comps exist, use residential as the trendline baseline and understand the subject is worth a good amount more. Use gut feeling on the premium. Since DewClaw sells as residential (not farmland), the pricing still needs to make sense for the buyer — but do not undervalue farmland by only using residential comps.
If genuinely unsure about farmland valuation, flag with the red stop sign emoji and recommend getting a realtor opinion. Still provide your best estimate alongside the flag.

9. The Pricing Philosophy 9.1 — Don't Overthink It Pricing is all an estimate. It is never going to be exactly right. But if the math is sound, the comps are investigated, and the trendline is built from stacked odds (multiple data points, multiple verification steps, feature-by-feature comparison), the number will be fair and defensible. At the end of the day, it really is just math. If you are comping the exact same type of property, using the same data sources, building the same trendline — a realtor or appraiser looking at the same comps would reach a similar conclusion. You are not doing rocket science. You are constructing a market from data and placing your subject within it. 9.2 — Stack All the Odds No single tool gives the answer alone. The trendline is one odd. The comp investigation is another. The feature comparison is another. The area demand check is another. Each one narrows the range and increases confidence. Together, they get close enough to make money. The more comps you have, the better the average, the more trustworthy the number. One comp is an anecdote. Ten comps is a trend. Statistics are proven over larger sample sizes — that is exactly what the trendline represents. Every additional data point makes the story clearer. 9.3 — DOM Shaving: Keep It Simple Days on market is a signal, not a formula. The overall approach is already conservative, and there is a discount applied at the end. DOM is used as a directional lean: If comps sold fast (under 30 days): feel confident pricing closer to market. Strong demand at fair price. If something sat for a while (150+ days): weight toward the lower end to be conservative. The market was not fully accepting that price. Do not apply precise mathematical discounts per DOM bucket in practice. The final 5%

f something sat for a while (150+ days): weight toward the lower end to be conservative. The market was not fully accepting that price. Do not apply precise mathematical discounts per DOM bucket in practice. The final 5% (approximate) discount on top of market value is the real safety net. DOM informs which direction you lean — it does not dictate a specific percentage adjustment. 9.4 — The Gut Check Layer After the math, after the trendline, after the comp investigation — there is a gut check. Based on the market, what do you think people would actually pay for this property, knowing you want to sell quickly? Is there a feature that makes this property stand out? Is there an active listing with similar features sitting on the market at a certain price? Active listings with nice features and okay sales ratios become ceilings. Your list price needs to be below them to actually move. If their listing is struggling, you need to be WELL below.

The 5% discount is not the holy grail. On average, that is the standard. But depending on how nice the property is, how strong the demand is, and what the competitive landscape looks like, it could be slightly more or less. The goal is: price it to create urgency without being desperate.

10. The Two-Number Deliverable Every valuation delivers TWO specific numbers: 1. Actual Market Value: The best estimate of true market value based on comps, trendline position, and property characteristics. This is the clean number — what the data says the property is worth. 2. List Price (~5% Discount): Market value discounted by approximately 5% to provide a margin of error and ensure the property is priced to move. This is the price the property would be listed at for sale. The owner uses market value to avoid missing deals by a few thousand dollars (sometimes you negotiate close to market value). The list price sets the acquisition target at approximately 50% of list. Both numbers are needed for every deal. Always give SPECIFIC prices, not just ranges. A range is acceptable when uncertain, but always include a specific recommended value. The owner needs concrete numbers to make offers.

11. The Final Sanity Check Before every valuation goes out the door, run this mental frame: Put yourself in the shoes of a knowledgeable realtor who has buyers actively looking for land. Would this property, at this price, make that realtor pick up the phone and call their buyer saying: 'You need to grab this before someone else does'? That is the sweet spot. The price should create urgency without being desperate. A realtor should look at the listing and think: 'This is priced right. My buyer would jump on this.' If the price would not generate that reaction, it is either too high (will not sell fast enough) or could go a bit higher (leaving money on the table). The balance: do not discount too much, do not discount too little. Just right. The 5% discount from market value is the starting framework, but the gut check is always: 'Does this price make a realtor excited to call their buyer?' If the answer is yes — submit the valuation with confidence.

12. Emoji Flag System Standard emoji codes used in deliverables to flag specific conditions. Each emoji has a specific meaning — use them consistently: Emoji Meaning When to Use Warning (yellow triangle) Hazard / Warning Flag County restrictions blocking subdivision, potential issues with road access, slope over 20%, structures on property, clear-cut suspected, managed timberland, any condition requiring owner awareness Stop Sign (red) Get a Realtor Opinion Genuinely unsure about valuation, mixed land types with no clear comps, unusual property characteristics, market you cannot read. STILL give your best estimate alongside the flag. Flag (red) Owner Has Multiple Properties Owner has 2+ properties. Note how many. Do not comp the other properties — that is on the owner. Does NOT affect pricing. Just a deal-flow signal.

13. Common Mistakes & How to Avoid Them Mistake 1: Lazy Comp Investigation The Problem: Seeing a comp that looks similar on paper — similar acreage, same county — and weighting it heavily without actually investigating what the property looks like. Skipping listing photos, skipping map view, not checking Land Insights APN. The Consequence: Mispricing. Treating a nicer comp as equivalent to the subject (overpricing), or missing a feature that makes the subject better (underpricing). A comp might have a cleared building site accessible by a dirt road visible in photo 14 of 20 that changes the entire valuation — but you skipped the photos. The Fix: Follow the full 5-step comp investigation process on every comp. No shortcuts. Photos first, description second, map third, Land Insights APN on critical comps. Mistake 2: Not Building the Trendline The Problem: Comping only at the subject's acreage without pulling wider data points. This gives you a cluster of comps but no context for where they sit on the market curve. The Consequence: You cannot tell if the subject is priced above or below the trendline. You cannot identify outliers. You cannot see the curve shape (steep vs. flat). You are flying blind. The Fix: Pull comps at multiple acreage points on every eval: 2ac, 5ac, 10ac, 20ac+. Build the mental trendline. See where the subject slots in. This takes 30 extra seconds and transforms the valuation. Mistake 3: Weighting Comps Too Heavily on One Side The Problem: Finding one or two comps that seem like great matches and anchoring the entire valuation to them without considering the broader trendline or investigating whether those comps are truly comparable. The Consequence: If the anchor comp was an outlier — overpay, friend deal, distressed sale — the entire valuation is

em without considering the broader trendline or investigating whether those comps are truly comparable. The Consequence: If the anchor comp was an outlier — overpay, friend deal, distressed sale — the entire valuation is skewed. The Fix: Never treat one comp as the Holy Grail. Use it as an anchor to build the story around, but validate it against the broader trendline. More data points = more trustworthy average. Mistake 4: Freaking Out About High PPA on Small Lots The Problem: Seeing $12,000/ac versus $14,000/ac on a 2-acre lot and treating it as a crisis. The Reality: That is only a $4,000 total price difference ($24k vs $28k). Meanwhile, being off by $250/ac on a 100-acre parcel is a $25,000 swing. Think in total dollars, not PPA.

The Fix: Always convert PPA to total dollars to calibrate the actual impact. Keep your head on straight. The steep part of the curve looks scary but the dollar impact is smaller than the flat part. Mistake 5: Not Checking What the Subject Actually Looks Like The Problem: Comping the subject based on acreage and location without verifying its actual features — missing a dirt road that leads to a cleared spot, not realizing there are two access points, overlooking a water feature visible on satellite. The Fix: Street View is MANDATORY on every subject property. Check satellite imagery. Toggle Land Insights layers. Look for entryways, driveways, cleared spots, mobile homes, sheds, structures, wet areas, power lines, fencing. Note everything in Extra Notes.

14. Legitimacy check 5 seconds EVERY comp — no exceptions 2. Listing photos (all of them) 15-30 seconds EVERY comp — no exceptions 3. Listing description 10-15 seconds EVERY comp — no exceptions 4. Map view (Redfin/Zillow) 10-15 seconds EVERY comp — watch for 40-50% map inaccuracy 5. Land Insights APN 30-60 seconds Critical comps, outliers, or when something does not add up 14.3 — Land Type Value Hierarchy Land Type How to Identify Relative Value Farmland Crop rows, irrigation, tilled soil HIGHEST — yielding asset Cleared & Maintained Grass cut short, no random trees, presentable HIGH — ready to build Pasture Open, tall grasses 2ft+, weeds, scattered trees, unkempt MODERATE — needs work Wooded (natural timber) Natural forest, mixed species, varied spacing MODERATE — has timber value Managed Timberland Plantation rows, uniform spacing MODERATE + $200-500/ac premium Clear-Cut Stumps, bare ground, no canopy LOW — stumps costly, cannot build easily 14.4 — The Pricing Delivery Checklist Before submitting any valuation, confirm: Check Confirmed?

Trendline built across full acreage spectrum? All comps investigated (photos, description, map)? Each comp compared feature-by-feature to subject? Subject property Street View checked? Subject property satellite imagery verified? Ceiling and floor established from comps? Market value = specific number (not just a range)? List price = market value discounted ~5%? Active listings checked as ceiling reference? Sanity check: Would a realtor call their buyer about this? All flags applied (warning, stop sign, flag emojis)? DewClaw Land Holdings Subdivision Analysis & PPA Trendline Mastery Guide Comprehensive Training Document — Subdivision Decision-Making, Lot Design, PPA Trendline Construction, Demand Reading, & Real-World Property Examples Internal Reference — April 2026

Table of Contents 1. The Subdivision Decision Framework — The complete 6-step decision tree 2. The PPA Trendline Framework — How to build, read, and apply the mental trendline 3. Reading Area Demand — Zooming out county-wide to determine if subdivision is viable 4. Lot Line Design Principles — How to draw subdivision lines and prioritize lot layout 5.
