1. The Subdivision Decision Framework Every property that crosses the desk gets evaluated through this 6-step decision tree before subdivision is recommended. The steps must be followed in order — each one is a gate. If a property fails at any step, it either becomes a simple flip or gets passed on entirely. Do not skip steps. Do not jump to lot design before confirming demand. The framework exists to prevent costly mistakes. Step 1 — Shape Check Before anything else, look at the parcel shape on Land Insights. Is the property wide enough relative to its depth to support splitting without creating bowling alley lots? The golden width-to-length ratio is 1:2 to 1:4. Maximum acceptable is 1:5 or 1:6. Anything skinnier than that means splitting will only make the lots less desirable, not more. If the property is narrow all the way through — like a long rectangle where the short side is on the road — stop here. Any split just creates skinnier bowling alleys. This is a flip, not a subdivision. Move on. Key principle: You cannot fix a bad width-to-length ratio by subdividing. You only make it worse. Flagpole lots work because the body of the lot is fat and usable — the narrow strip is just access. A lot that is skinny all the way through has no fix. Step 2 — Road Access Check What roads touch the property? What type are they? Check the road color coding on Land Insights: green = private/service road, yellow = residential/unclassified, orange = city/county road, red = highways/interstates. The road type matters because county regulations may restrict which road classes you can subdivide off of. Check whether the county allows new subdivided lots to front onto the available road types. If the only road is a county road and the county does not allow new subdivisions off county

ad classes you can subdivide off of. Check whether the county allows new subdivided lots to front onto the available road types. If the only road is a county road and the county does not allow new subdivisions off county roads, you either need an internal road or cannot subdivide. This single restriction can reduce the value of a subdivision plan by 20-50% or more. If the only access is a single narrow flagpole strip — one access point = one lot, period. All the acreage and cleared land in the world does not matter if the road access cannot support multiple lots. Also check: If a property has 3 narrow access strips at 50 feet each but the county requires 60-foot minimum for flagpole lots, that is a dead end. Flag it with the warning emoji so the owner knows the subdivision play is blocked by county requirements. Step 3 — Area Demand Check Zoom out county-wide on Land Insights. This is not a tight radius around the subject property — it is the entire county. Build a mental map of the county's personality: where are the towns? Where does density cluster? Where does it thin out? Where does the subject property fall on that spectrum? Look for these positive demand signals: lots of smaller parcels (2-5 acres) nearby with structures on them, proximity to a town, a lake nearby attracting buyers, visible new construction, general population density.

These tell you people are actively choosing to buy small lots here, so if you create more small lots, they will sell. Watch for these caution signals: way out in the sticks, very few houses around, only big parcels with sparse development, no town or attraction nearby. This is rural recreational territory. It may be fine for a buy-and-flip, but subdivision into small lots is risky because there may not be enough demand to absorb them quickly. In these areas, big lots might be the only thing that moves because anyone who wanted a small lot would just live closer to town. Build rings outward from the subject property: what is happening immediately around it? One mile out? Five miles out? County-wide? This should take 10-20 seconds to scan and understand. The goal is to determine whether the market will absorb smaller lots in the timeframe we need (30-90 days). Additional demand signals to look for: Is it near a lake (attracts buyers)? Is there a town or city nearby? Are there houses and structures around, or is it desolate? Is it a nice area or a trashy area? Check Street View and Google Maps — are the houses maintained? Is there a community feel? Even if the numbers say subdivision works, if the area looks rough, buyers will not come. Step 4 — PPA Trendline Check Pull comps across the full acreage spectrum in the county. You want to know what 2-acre lots sell for, what 5-acre lots sell for, what 10 sells for, what 20 sells for, what 50 sells for. Each of those is a data point on the PPA curve. (Full details on the PPA Trendline Framework are in Section 2.) A steep curve means subdivision is lucrative — there is a big spread between large-parcel PPA and small-lot PPA. A flat or linear curve means there is no real subdivision premium. You are just creating more work for

2.) A steep curve means subdivision is lucrative — there is a big spread between large-parcel PPA and small-lot PPA. A flat or linear curve means there is no real subdivision premium. You are just creating more work for no payoff. If the curve is flat, this is a flip, not a subdivision. Step 5 — Design the Split Now — and only now — do you start thinking about how to draw the lot lines. The priority order is: 1. Easy road frontage lots first — cookie-cutter lots along the road with the most frontage. 2. Maximize waterfront touches — wrap as many lots as possible around any pond, lake, or significant water feature. 3. Prioritize cleared land — buyers want to see where they can build. Cleared lots sell faster and at a premium. 4. Flagpole cleanup lots last — use irregular/flagpole lots to capture remaining acreage that does not fit neatly into the primary layout. Match the target lot size to what the surrounding development pattern shows. If the area has mostly 2-4 acre lots with houses, target 2-4 acres. If the area is more rural with 8-12 acre parcels, do a conservative 2-3 way split. Do not force a lot size the area does not support. Different lot sizes within the same subdivision are completely normal and expected. Some lots will be waterfront, some cleared, some wooded, some flagpole. They do not need to be the same size. Price them accordingly when comping.

Step 6 — Final Gut Check: Sell Timeline We want to sell within 30 days ideally, 90 days maximum. Subdivision only makes sense if the lots will actually move in that timeframe. Creating 12 small lots in an area with no demand means sitting on inventory, which kills the business model. That is why the area demand check (Step 3) comes before the subdivision design (Step 5) — no point calculating lot yield if the market cannot absorb it. The sell timeline is the governor. Do not maximize lot count — maximize lot count WITHIN what the market will absorb quickly. Over-subdividing in a low-demand area is worse than a conservative split because you are stuck holding inventory.

2. The PPA Trendline Framework The PPA (Price Per Acre) Trendline is the single most important analytical tool for property valuation and subdivision analysis. It is not optional — it must be built mentally on every property evaluation. The trendline tells the story of the market and where the subject property fits within it. 2.1 — How the Curve Works The PPA trendline is NOT linear — it is exponential on the low end and flattens to roughly linear on the high end. This is the fundamental shape of all rural land markets: Small lots (1-5 acres): PPA is HIGH and rises STEEPLY as acreage decreases. A 2-acre lot might sell at $15,000/ac while a 5-acre lot might sell at $8,000/ac. That drop is fast and dramatic. Small changes in acreage cause huge swings in PPA. This is the volatile, exponential part of the curve. Medium lots (5-20 acres): PPA is moderate and declining more gradually. The curve is still falling but not as steeply. This is the transition zone. Large parcels (20+ acres): PPA is LOW and relatively STABLE. A 20-acre parcel might be $3,500/ac and a 40-acre might be $2,800/ac. The curve has flattened out — PPA is not jumping wildly anymore, it is stabilizing. This is the linear, predictable part of the curve. 2.2 — Why 50% Below / 100% Above The comp range is set at 50% below the subject acreage and 100% above (expanding to 50%/200% or even 100%/300% if needed). This asymmetry exists precisely because of the curve shape: On the low end (smaller acreage comps): PPA rises exponentially. Small acreage differences cause huge PPA swings. These comps are harder to trust and riskier to lean on. That is why we only go 50% below — we are being conservative on the steep, volatile part of the curve. On the high end (larger acreage comps): PPA flattens out and becomes more

are harder to trust and riskier to lean on. That is why we only go 50% below — we are being conservative on the steep, volatile part of the curve. On the high end (larger acreage comps): PPA flattens out and becomes more linear. The numbers are stable and predictable. A 30-acre comp and a 50-acre comp might have very similar PPAs. That is why we go 100% above (or more) — the flat part of the curve is trustworthy, and weighting toward higher-acreage comps gives a conservative valuation. Always weight toward the higher-acreage comps (lower PPA) for a conservative valuation. The flat part of the trendline is where the data is reliable. The steep part is where mistakes get made. 2.3 — Building the Trendline on Every Eval On every property evaluation — whether subdivision is the play or not — build the mental trendline. Pull comps at multiple acreage points: 2 acres, 5 acres, 10 acres, 20 acres, and beyond. Each comp is a data point. Connect them mentally to see the curve shape. Even when the comps do not perfectly fill every acreage point, use whatever exists in between to establish ceilings and floors. If you have a 2-acre comp at $15,000/ac and an 8-acre comp at $5,000/ac but nothing at 5 acres, you can still interpolate — but be cautious on the steep part of the curve because the

exponential shape makes the interpolation less reliable. Any comp in between, even if it is not a perfect match, gives you a ceiling or floor to narrow the range. Build SEPARATE curves per comp type when relevant: wooded vs. cleared, waterfront vs. non-waterfront. Each land type has its own PPA behavior. Do not mix a waterfront 5-acre comp with a landlocked wooded 5-acre comp — they are on different curves. 2.4 — What the Curve Shape Tells You Steep curve (big spread between large-parcel PPA and small-lot PPA): GOOD subdivision market. The value created by splitting a large parcel into small lots is significant. This is where the subdivision money is made — you are moving product from the flat part of the curve up to the steep part. Flat/linear curve (small-lot PPA and large-parcel PPA are similar): BAD subdivision market. There is no real premium for small lots. Focus on simple flips. Metro/lake counties tend to have steeper curves. Deep rural counties tend to have flatter curves. But this is a generalization — there are rural counties with steep curves. The comps themselves tell you the truth; let the data speak rather than assumptions. 2.5 — Handling Outliers Outliers above the trendline are common. They are often off-market sales, bidding wars, or buyers who overpaid. When you see a comp that sold significantly above the trendline: Check the listing history — was there a bidding war? Did it go above asking price? That may signal strong demand, or just one motivated buyer. Check if it was off-market (Sold-Public Records). Off-market sales are unreliable and should not be used as comps per standing rules. Note it, but do not let it pull the valuation up. The trendline built from ceilings and floors across multiple comps is what matters — not one property that went

liable and should not be used as comps per standing rules. Note it, but do not let it pull the valuation up. The trendline built from ceilings and floors across multiple comps is what matters — not one property that went crazy. The trendline should get progressively tighter as you identify close ceilings and floors near your subject's acreage. You are whittling the range down, not chasing outliers. 2.6 — The Subdivision Math Once the trendline is built, the subdivision math becomes straightforward: 1. The parent parcel sits at [X] PPA on the flat part of the curve (e.g., $3,000/ac for a 24-acre parcel). 2. If split into [Y]-acre lots, the trendline says those sell at [Z] PPA (e.g., $8,000/ac for 5-acre lots). 3. The spread between X and Z is the value created by subdivision. 4. Reality-check: subtract road costs, survey costs, permitting, and the fact that not every lot will hit top-of-curve PPA. 5. If the net yield still significantly exceeds the as-is value, subdivision is the play.

3. Reading Area Demand Reading area demand is a zoom-out exercise that must happen before any subdivision design work. It determines whether subdivision is viable, what lot sizes the market will absorb, and how quickly the lots will sell. This is not optional — skipping this step has historically led to properties sitting on the market until they expire worthless. 3.1 — The County-Wide Scan Zoom out to the county level on Land Insights. Build a mental map of the county's development pattern: Where are the towns and population centers? Where are the density clusters — areas with lots of small parcels and structures? Where does it transition to rural — larger parcels, fewer structures? Where is the deep rural edge — big tracts, sparse development, no towns nearby? The subject property falls somewhere on this spectrum. Its position tells you what lot sizes the market will absorb at that location. Near a town with small-lot clusters? Small lots will sell. In the rural transition zone? Conservative split. Deep rural? Big lots only — or just flip as-is. 3.2 — Positive Demand Signals Lots of smaller parcels (2-5 acres) nearby with structures/houses on them Proximity to a town, city, or population center A lake or recreation area nearby attracting buyers Visible new construction activity in the area General population density — houses, roads, infrastructure A mix of lot sizes showing market activity across the spectrum When these signals are present, people are actively choosing to buy small lots here. Creating more small lots will meet existing demand. 3.3 — Caution Signals Way out in the sticks — very few houses, mostly large tracts No town, lake, or attraction nearby Only big parcels with sparse development — the market favors large acreage The area looks rough or poorly

— Caution Signals Way out in the sticks — very few houses, mostly large tracts No town, lake, or attraction nearby Only big parcels with sparse development — the market favors large acreage The area looks rough or poorly maintained on Street View No recent small-lot sales activity in comps When these signals dominate, the market is telling you that buyers in this area want big tracts. Creating small lots creates LESS demand, not more. The farther out from civilization, the bigger the lots need to be. People who want small lots would just live closer to town.

3.4 — Matching Lot Size to Market The target lot size comes from what is already selling around the subject property. Not what existed 20 years ago, but what people are buying and building on now. If the surrounding development pattern is 2-4 acre lots with houses, target 2-4 acres. If it is 8-12 acre parcels, do a conservative 2-3 way split. Match the market. Preferred minimum lot size for subdivision: 2 acres. Once you start selling smaller lots, it takes longer to sell all of them and the process gets complicated. For most rural subdivision plays, 2-5 acre target lots are the sweet spot when the market supports it. The demand check can tell you to walk away from the deal entirely — not just 'do not subdivide' but 'do not buy this at all' — because if the market does not support the size you are offering, the property is going to sit. Smaller parcels in areas dominated by big tracts are a risk not just for subdivision but for the flip itself.

4. Lot Line Design Principles 4.1 — Priority Order for Drawing Lines When designing a subdivision layout, always work in this order: Priority 1: Easy road frontage lots. Start with the road that gives the cleanest, most cookie-cutter lots. If there is a residential road running through or along the property, that is usually the best starting point. Divide the road frontage into lots of the target acreage. Priority 2: Maximize waterfront touches. If there is a pond, lake, or significant water feature, wrap as many lots as possible around it. Waterfront premium is significant — do not leave it on the table. For larger ponds, multiple waterfront lots work well and buyers can build docks. For very small ponds, one lot with the pond as its feature may be better than splitting it across multiple lots where nobody gets to enjoy it. Priority 3: Prioritize cleared land. Buyers want to see where they can build. Lots with cleared areas sell faster and command higher PPA. When drawing lines, try to include cleared ground in as many lots as possible. If a lot can have access through cleared land rather than only through timber, that is preferred. Priority 4: Flagpole cleanup lots. The remaining acreage that does not fit neatly into the primary layout gets captured with irregular or flagpole lots. These are the outliers, and having a few is normal in every subdivision. The flagpole strip is the sacrifice zone — the lot body should still be a usable, reasonably shaped parcel. 4.2 — Lot Shape Rules Golden width-to-length ratio: 1:2 to 1:4. This gives lots that feel spacious and usable — you can have a house, yard, outbuildings, and driveway without everything being in a single line. Maximum acceptable ratio: 1:5 to 1:6. Beyond this, the lot starts feeling like a bowling alley.

feel spacious and usable — you can have a house, yard, outbuildings, and driveway without everything being in a single line. Maximum acceptable ratio: 1:5 to 1:6. Beyond this, the lot starts feeling like a bowling alley. Anything skinnier: significant value loss. Before proposing a subdivision, always check if splitting creates bowling alley lots. If yes, do not subdivide — there is no value-add, only value destruction. The 'sideways flagpole' hack: When a flagpole lot would otherwise be too deep, run the flagpole strip horizontally instead of vertically. This keeps the buildable lot body at a usable width-to-length ratio while still providing road access through the horizontal strip. 4.3 — Different Lot Sizes Are Normal Lots within a single subdivision do not need to be the same size. Some will be waterfront, some cleared, some wooded, some flagpole. Different sizes are expected. When lots along a natural feature boundary (like farmland vs. timber), the lots on each side can absolutely be different sizes. When the lots are roughly similar in size, you can calculate the average lot size (parent acreage / lot count) and comp that average to estimate total subdivision yield. When there is a huge disparity in lot sizes — say 2-acre waterfront lots AND 15-acre timber lots — you must comp each group separately. Averaging them would badly skew the numbers because of the exponential PPA curve.

4.4 — Estimating Lot Count Quickly The threshold for pulling subdivision comps is low: if you can see even 2-4 possible splits, that is enough to warrant checking. The more road frontage, the more obvious the opportunity. For a quick mental estimate: look at the road frontage and the total acreage. Road frontage tells you how many lots can touch the road. Total acreage tells you how deep you can go. Both together give you a rough lot count. If a property has frontage on two roads, count potential lots from EACH road separately and add them up. More road frontage = more lots = better. Try multiple layout variations mentally. You have the advantage of being able to draw visual lines in your head and test different configurations simultaneously. Use the measuring tool on Land Insights to sanity-check dimensions. Timing: aim for 2 minutes per evaluation. If subdivision comps genuinely need 3-4 minutes, take the time. Do not sacrifice accuracy for speed. But do not waste time either.

5. Road Access & Classification 5.1 — Road Color Codes on Land Insights Color Road Type Subdivision Implications Green Private / Service Road Limited — may not allow public subdivision access Yellow Residential / Unclassified Often the best subdivision spine — check county regs Orange City / County Road May restrict new lot creation — verify with county Red Highway / Interstate Generally not subdivision-friendly; noise, privacy, access issues 5.2 — The Residential Road Access Hack When a county restricts new lot creation off certain road types, there is sometimes a workaround: a lot that technically fronts on an allowed road (like a county road) can still have its physical driveway access come off a different road (like a residential road). The legal frontage is on the county road, but the practical entry is through the residential road and the cleared area. This gives buyers the best of both worlds. This is not always possible and depends on county regulations, but it is worth exploring when the alternative is losing half the subdivision value. 5.3 — Narrow Strips Touching the Road When you see a narrow strip running from a parcel body down to the road on Land Insights, it is almost certainly legal access — if it touches the road, it more than likely has legal access. Flag it and note it, but do not overthink it. The key check is whether the strip width meets the county's minimum frontage requirement for flagpole lots.

6. Building a Road Through the Middle 6.1 — When to Consider an Internal Road An internal road makes sense when all of the following are true: The county has a steep PPA trendline — big spread between large-parcel and small-lot PPA. There is demand for small lots — surrounding development shows people building on 2-5 acre parcels. The property is deep enough that road-frontage-only subdivision wastes significant interior acreage. The road cost pencils out against the additional lot yield — even at the high cost estimate, the extra lots generate more revenue than the road costs. The mental math: 'If a road costs $50,000-$100,000 but I get 5-8 additional lots at significantly higher PPA, the revenue from those additional lots far exceeds the road cost.' A square lot illustrates the concept simply: without a road through the middle, you might get 4 lots off the existing frontage. With a road down the center, you double the frontage and get 8 lots. Each lot is smaller, which means higher PPA. As long as the market supports the smaller lot size, this is a clear win. 6.2 — Road Cost Estimation When evaluating whether an internal road is worth building, provide a cost range with three tiers: Low end: Flat, cleared terrain. Minimal grading. Short road length. Simple gravel construction. Middle (most likely): Some grading required, moderate length, basic gravel with drainage. High end: Steep terrain requiring significant grading, longer road, culverts for water crossings, possibly paving. If the topography is steep and grading gets expensive, it may make more sense to just subdivide off the existing road frontage rather than building an internal road. The road is only worth building when the additional lot yield clearly outweighs the construction cost at the mid-to-high

more sense to just subdivide off the existing road frontage rather than building an internal road. The road is only worth building when the additional lot yield clearly outweighs the construction cost at the mid-to-high estimate. 6.3 — Wetlands, Creeks, and Rivers Wetlands: You can build a road through wetlands. Culverts are typically $5,000-$10,000 each, which is not a dealbreaker. The real question is not 'can I build through it' but 'does the road improve the lot layout enough to justify it?' If the lots on either side of the road are still mostly wetlands, it is probably not worth it. If the road unlocks clean buildable lots that just happen to have wetlands in between, go for it. True marsh: This is different from mapped wetlands. Actual marshy, boggy ground is not something you should be putting a road through or subdividing. Going around it would be too expensive and time-consuming. Small creeks: Culvert — no big deal. The water flows through the tube, you build over it. Just a line item cost.
Rivers: Completely different conversation. Bridge territory, which is serious money and complexity. Flag it but do not assume it is part of a subdivision plan.

7. Flagpole Lots 7.1 — What They Are A flagpole lot has a narrow strip (the 'pole') connecting to the road for legal access, with the main body of the lot (the 'flag') set back from the road. The narrow strip is just for access — the buildable area is in the body of the lot. Flagpole lots are normal and expected in any subdivision. They are how you capture acreage that does not have direct road frontage. The key is that the body of the lot must be usable — wide enough, flat enough, and accessible enough to build on. 7.2 — County Frontage Minimums Counties have minimum frontage requirements for flagpole lots — typically 30-60+ feet. If the flagpole strip is narrower than the county minimum, the lot cannot be created. This is a hard stop. If a property has multiple narrow access strips that are each below the county minimum, this is a dead end for subdivision. Flag with the warning emoji so the owner knows immediately. 7.3 — When Flagpole Access Kills Subdivision If the entire property is accessed through a single narrow flagpole strip — one access point — then the property functions as one lot regardless of how much acreage is in the back. You cannot create multiple subdivided lots when there is only one way in. All the cleared land, the great shape in the back, the beautiful views — none of it matters if you cannot give each new lot its own legal access.

8. When NOT to Subdivide Approximately 85-90% of properties that come across the desk will NOT be subdividable. The default assumption should always be 'this is a flip' — subdivision potential is the exception, not the rule. Only upgrade to 'subdivision opportunity' when shape, road frontage, acreage, and area demand all line up together. 8.1 — Walk-Away Signal: Narrow Shape If the property is narrow all the way through — long rectangle with the short side on the road — any split just creates skinnier bowling alleys. Nobody wants a lot where they can only access the property in a single line. Everything — house, shed, garden — would have to be arranged single-file. This is undesirable regardless of acreage. There is a reason flagpole lots are flagpole lots: the body is thick and usable, only the access strip is narrow. If the ENTIRE lot is narrow, there is no fix. Do not subdivide. 8.2 — Walk-Away Signal: No Market Demand (The Dangerous Trap) This is the most dangerous scenario because it looks like a good deal on paper. The property has a decent shape. It has road frontage. You COULD physically split it. But the area is telling you nobody is buying small lots here. This is where real money gets lost. If the surrounding area only has big tracts, no small-lot construction, no town nearby, and only large-acreage sales in the comps — then creating small lots creates LESS demand, not more. The property may already be a harder sell at its current size because it is small for the market. Even the parent parcel at its current acreage might barely have demand in these deep rural markets. Splitting it just makes things worse. This is how properties sit on the market until they expire worthless. The test: If the market does not support the size you are offering, and splitting

d in these deep rural markets. Splitting it just makes things worse. This is how properties sit on the market until they expire worthless. The test: If the market does not support the size you are offering, and splitting would create even less demand, then either flip as-is or pass on the deal entirely. Do not force subdivision where the market is saying no. 8.3 — Walk-Away Signal: Single Access Point If the property is accessed through one narrow flagpole strip with a large buildable area in the back, it functions as one lot. You cannot run multiple flagpole strips through a single narrow neck — they would either be too skinny to meet county minimums or eat up all the usable frontage on the strip. One access point = one lot.

9. Comping the Subdivision 9.1 — The Dual-Comp Approach When subdivision is the play, always pull comps at TWO acreage levels: As-is comps: What is the parent parcel worth at its current acreage? This is the baseline — the value if subdivision does not happen. Subdivision comps: What do the target lot sizes sell for? This is the ceiling — the value if subdivision is executed successfully. The spread between these two numbers is the value created by subdivision. Present both to the owner so they can see the opportunity. 9.2 — Average Lot Size Method When the subdivided lots are roughly similar in size: Parent acreage / lot count = average lot size. Comp that average lot size to get the PPA. Multiply PPA by parent acreage to get total subdivision yield. This works when the lots cluster in a similar range — say all between 4-7 acres. The exponential curve does not vary dramatically across this small range, so averaging is acceptable. 9.3 — Group Comping Method When the lots have a huge size disparity — say 2-acre waterfront lots AND 15-acre timber lots — you must comp each group separately. The PPA curve is exponential. A 2-acre lot might be $15,000/ac while a 15-acre lot is $3,000/ac. Averaging $9,000/ac across all lots would be drastically inaccurate — it would overvalue the big lots and undervalue the small ones. Comp each group at its own acreage point on the trendline, then sum the groups for total yield.

10. Pricing Output: Market Value & List Price Every valuation delivers TWO numbers: 1. Actual Market Value: The best estimate of what the property is truly worth based on comps, trendline position, and property characteristics. This is the clean number based on the data. 2. List Price (5% Discount): Market value discounted by approximately 5% to provide a margin of error and ensure the property is priced to move. This is the price the property would be listed at for sale. The acquisition target is approximately 50% of the list price. The owner uses the market value to avoid missing deals by a few thousand dollars, and the list price to set the acquisition target. Always give SPECIFIC prices, not just ranges. A range is acceptable when uncertain, but always include a specific recommended value. The owner needs concrete numbers to make offers.

11. Real-World Property Examples The following examples were walked through visually on Land Insights during training. Each example includes the property details, what the land looked like, the decision made, and the reasoning behind it. These are the reference cases for pattern recognition going forward. Example 1: Pamela Adams — Full Subdivision (With Residential Road) Location: Near Eddins Rd / Zoar Rd area | Acreage: Large parcel (150+ acres) | County: SC What the land looked like: A massive parcel with Eddins Road (yellow/residential) running through the middle, Zoar Road (orange/county) along the eastern boundary, and Abrams Creek along the western edge. The property had a mix of cleared fields and timber. A significant pond/lake sat in the interior of the property. Multiple existing parcels (James E McLain, Michael E Valdaliso) were carved out within the property boundaries. The subdivision plan (Version 1 — with residential road access): Red lot lines were drawn creating approximately 15-20 lots. The residential road (Eddins Rd) served as the primary subdivision spine. Lots along this road were clean, cookie-cutter, roughly equal sizes. Multiple lots were wrapped around the pond to capture waterfront premium. Cleared areas were prioritized. Lots along Zoar Rd were slightly more irregular, with flagpole lots in the upper right to capture remaining acreage. Different sized lots throughout — completely normal. Key design decisions: Started with the residential road because it was easier and more cookie-cutter. Maximized waterfront touches on the pond. Prioritized cleared land. Flagpole lots used as cleanup. The residential road did the heavy lifting for access and created the most lots. Example 2: Pamela Adams — Constrained Subdivision (No Residential Road Access)

nd. Prioritized cleared land. Flagpole lots used as cleanup. The residential road did the heavy lifting for access and created the most lots. Example 2: Pamela Adams — Constrained Subdivision (No Residential Road Access) Same property, different scenario: county does not allow subdivision off the residential road. The subdivision plan (Version 2 — Zoar Rd only): Dramatically fewer lots. A large dead zone in the northwest corner of the property could not be reached from Zoar Rd, creating a single large remainder parcel. Lots were larger and more irregular. The residential road was used as a physical access hack — lots fronted on Zoar Rd for legal purposes but driveways could access through the cleared area near the residential road. The sideways flagpole hack was used on the upper right — the flagpole strip ran horizontally instead of vertically to prevent the lot body from becoming too deep. Waterfront priority was maintained even with the constraint. Value impact: Estimated 20-50% loss in total subdivision value compared to Version 1. Fewer lots, bigger lots (lower PPA), dead zone acreage sold at the lower large-parcel PPA. This demonstrates why identifying road type restrictions is critical — it can be the difference between a home run and a mediocre deal.

Lesson: The road type restriction reduced the deal value by 20-50%. When flagging subdivision potential, always identify the road types and note any potential restrictions. This is not a footnote — it is a deal-defining factor. Example 3: Louis Gauthier — Conservative Split (3 Lots) Location: 844 Chase Rd, Meshoppen, PA | Acreage: 24.05 | County: Susquehanna County, PA Link: app.landinsights.co/data?parcel=164399721 What the land looked like: A 24-acre parcel with road frontage on Chase Rd (yellow/residential) on the north side and State Route 3004 (orange/county) on the south side. The property had a mix of cleared areas and heavy timber. A creek/river ran along the eastern edge near Elk Lake Rd. The surrounding area was very rural — large tracts everywhere, Commonwealth of Pennsylvania state land nearby, sparse development. No cluster of small-lot activity. The subdivision plan: Three lots, roughly 8 acres each average. The top lot used Chase Rd frontage. The bottom lot used State Route 3004 frontage and picked up the creek/river frontage for a waterfront premium. The middle lot accessed via Chase Rd. The lots were not equal sizes — the layout worked with the natural features. Why only 3 lots: The area demanded it. Zooming out to the county level showed big rural tracts dominating. No small-lot development clusters. No town or lake nearby. The market supports bigger lots. A conservative 2-3 way split still moves up the PPA curve (three 8-acre lots at higher PPA than one 24-acre parcel) but does not overshoot the demand. Lesson: Match the split to the market. Do not maximize lot count in an area that cannot absorb small lots. Conservative splits still make money without the risk of sitting on unsold inventory. Example 4: Timothy R Marbaker — No Subdivide (Narrow

o the market. Do not maximize lot count in an area that cannot absorb small lots. Conservative splits still make money without the risk of sitting on unsold inventory. Example 4: Timothy R Marbaker — No Subdivide (Narrow Shape) Location: 5583 State Route 3004, Meshoppen, PA | Acreage: 11.45 | County: Susquehanna County, PA Link: app.landinsights.co/data?parcel=117213776 What the land looked like: An 11.45-acre parcel that was clearly long and narrow, running roughly north-south. The cyan boundary on Land Insights showed a stretched-out, elongated shape. The property had a structure on it. The surrounding area was the same rural Susquehanna County pattern — large tracts, heavy timber, sparse development. Why no subdivision: The shape kills it. The parcel is already narrow relative to its depth. Any split would create even skinnier bowling alley lots that nobody would want to build on. Combined with the rural area showing no demand for small lots, this is clearly a flip-only property. Example 5: Terry M Ridall — No Subdivide (Narrow Shape, Larger Acreage) Location: 4849 State Route 3004, Meshoppen, PA | Acreage: 20 | County: Susquehanna County, PA

Link: app.landinsights.co/data?parcel=117213659 What the land looked like: A 20-acre parcel, also in Susquehanna County near the Marbaker property. Similar elongated, narrow shape running north-south. Despite being 20 acres (larger than Marbaker), the width-to-length ratio was still poor. Same rural surroundings — large tracts, Commonwealth of PA state land, no small-lot clusters. Why no subdivision: Same shape problem as Marbaker, just bigger. Even at 20 acres, the narrow shape means splitting creates undesirable lots. Size alone does not make a property subdividable — shape matters just as much. Lesson: Acreage does not override shape. A 20-acre narrow property is just as un-subdividable as an 11-acre narrow property. The shape check (Step 1) must pass before anything else matters. Example 6: John E Karabin — Could Split But SHOULD NOT (The Dangerous Trap) Location: 1084 McGavin Rd, Meshoppen, PA | Acreage: 4.61 | County: Susquehanna County, PA Link: app.landinsights.co/data?parcel=117216538 What the land looked like: A 4.61-acre parcel on McGavin Rd with an irregular but workable shape — not a bowling alley. The property had road frontage on McGavin Rd (yellow/residential). It was surrounded by open fields and large tracts. A creek/river ran along the north side. Very few structures in the immediate area. Last purchased for $20,000 thirteen years ago. Why this is the most dangerous example: The shape works. The road frontage is there. You COULD physically split this into two roughly 2-acre lots. Everything about the property says 'yes, subdivide.' But the AREA says no. Zooming out to the county level shows massive open fields, huge parcels, barely any structures, deep rural. Nobody is buying 2-acre lots out here. The critical insight: Even the 4.61-acre parcel

e.' But the AREA says no. Zooming out to the county level shows massive open fields, huge parcels, barely any structures, deep rural. Nobody is buying 2-acre lots out here. The critical insight: Even the 4.61-acre parcel itself barely has demand in this market because it is small for the area. The surrounding parcels are all much bigger. Splitting it into two 2-acre lots would create parcels that are even MORE out of step with what buyers want. You would be creating less demand, not more. The property would sit on the market until it expires worthless. Real-world consequence: Properties like this have been purchased in the past and they sat on the market until they expired worthless. This is not theoretical. This is money lost. When the market does not support the size you are offering, and splitting makes it worse, walk away from the subdivision play — and potentially the deal itself. Lesson: 'Can I subdivide?' and 'Should I subdivide?' are completely different questions. Physical ability means nothing without market demand. The area demand check can tell you to walk away from the deal entirely. Example 7: Kenneth M Hyatt — Flagpole Lot, No Subdivide (Single Access Point)

Location: Dillon County, SC | Acreage: 13.1 | APN: 119-00-00-079 Link: app.landinsights.co/data?parcel=119242350 What the land looked like: A 13.1-acre property with a classic flagpole shape. A narrow wooded strip connected to S Old Magnolia Rd at the top, running through heavy timber. The back of the property opened up into a large, beautiful cleared area — plenty of space to build. A subdivision (Southpark Ct, McCutcheon Pl) was visible nearby. The owner (Kenneth M Hyatt) had 57 properties. Why no subdivision: The front access is a single narrow strip through wooded area. You cannot build at the front. The back has all the space in the world — but it is all accessed through that one narrow neck. If you tried to split the back into two lots, you would need to run two flagpole strips through the narrow neck, and they would either be too skinny to meet county minimums or eat up all the usable frontage. Lesson: A big cleared area in the back does not mean subdivision potential if there is no way to give each new lot its own legal access. One narrow access strip = one lot, period. Always check access capacity before flagging subdivision potential. Example 8: Mapledale LLC — Road Through the Middle (Two Adjacent Parcels) Location: King William County, VA | Acreage: 358.51 + 353.75 (712+ total) | Owner: Mapledale LLC Links: app.landinsights.co/data?parcel=140889297 and app.landinsights.co/data?parcel=140898050 What the land looked like: Two massive adjacent parcels owned by the same LLC totaling 712+ acres in King William County, VA. The Jacks Creek Rd property (353.75 acres, address: 1626 Jacks Creek Rd) already had a road running through it, slightly to the right, which could be centered for better lot distribution. The other parcel (358.51 acres) was along Route 621 with

rty (353.75 acres, address: 1626 Jacks Creek Rd) already had a road running through it, slightly to the right, which could be centered for better lot distribution. The other parcel (358.51 acres) was along Route 621 with some steep topography in the lower portion. Multiple roads surrounded the properties: Routes 621, 629, 632, 678, and Green Level Rd. The area context: King William County showed a mix of development. Comp data visible on the map showed an extremely steep PPA trendline: 2.23 acres selling at $132,290/ac PPA while 6 acres was at $14,170/ac PPA and 4 acres at $12,500/ac PPA. This is a massive spread indicating a very strong subdivision market. Why build a road through the middle: At 350+ acres each, these properties are far too large to sell at a reasonable PPA as-is. The trendline is extremely steep, meaning small lots command enormously higher PPAs. A road through the middle of the 621 parcel would double the effective frontage and allow subdivision into smaller lots (2-5 acres) that match the area demand. Even with road construction costs ($50k-$100k+), the lot yield from smaller parcels at that PPA curve would far exceed the road investment. The topography factor: Steep terrain on the lower portion of the 621 parcel adds grading cost. If the grading gets too expensive, the fallback is to just subdivide off existing road frontage — which still works because there is significant frontage available. The road through the middle is the maximum-yield play; subdividing off existing frontage is the conservative fallback. Lesson: When you have massive acreage, an extremely steep PPA trendline, and surrounding demand for small lots, building an internal road is a clear value-add play. Always evaluate road
cost against lot yield. If topography makes the road too expensive, fall back to existing-frontage subdivision.

Proceed to next check Conservative split or STOP PPA trendline: Steep curve? Proceed — subdivision is lucrative Flat curve — flip only Lot design: Avoid bowling alleys? se development Low demand for any size Flip as-is or pass Area looks rough on Street View Quality concerns Caution — buyers may not come 12.4 — The 85-90% Rule Approximately 85-90% of properties will NOT be subdividable. The default assumption is always 'this is a flip.' Subdivision potential is the exception. Only upgrade when ALL conditions align:
good shape, adequate road access, area demand for smaller lots, steep PPA trendline, and confidence in 30-90 day sell timeline. — End of Document —

DewClaw Land Holdings Property Evaluation Deliverable Format — Version 3 (FINAL) 10-Section Deliverable Format with Lead Stage Classification THIS DOCUMENT SUPERSEDES ALL PREVIOUS DELIVERABLE FORMAT INSTRUCTIONS. This is the definitive, final format for all property evaluations going forward. All 10 sections must be included in every deliverable. Internal Reference — April 2026

IMPORTANT: This Document Supersedes All Previous Formats This Version 3 (FINAL) deliverable format replaces any and all previous deliverable format instructions, including the original 6-section format and the earlier 9-section format. The key update is the addition of Section 10: Lead Stage Classification, which categorizes every evaluated property into the correct smart list stage based on how the seller's asking price compares to market value. Every property evaluation from this point forward must include all 10 sections. No exceptions. This is the holy grail — the final, definitive format.

The 10-Section Deliverable Format Section 1: Market Value The best estimate of true market value based on comps, PPA trendline position, and property characteristics. Always provide a SPECIFIC number and the price per acre. This is the clean number — what the data says the property is worth. Format: $XX,XXX (~$X,XXX/ac) This number drives everything else in the deliverable. The offer price, list price, value-add calculations, and lead stage classification all flow from this number. It must be accurate. Section 2: Offer Price Three offer tiers based on market value. Always provide all three so the owner can choose the approach based on seller motivation and deal dynamics. 50%: $XX,XXX 60%: $XX,XXX 70%: $XX,XXX If the property is potentially landlocked, also include: Landlocked price (10% of MV): $X,XXX Section 3: Value-Add Market Value (Optional) Only include if subdivision or other value-add exists. This is what the property would be worth AFTER the value-add is executed. Include subdivision scenarios with lot count, target lot sizes, and estimated costs separated out. If no value-add exists, mark as N/A. Format: $XX,XXX (subdivision into X lots at ~X acres each) Section 4: Value-Add Offer Price (Optional) 50%, 60%, 70% of the value-add market value. This is the backup pitch — if the seller rejects the initial offer, pitch the value-add at 50% of improved value. If no value-add exists, mark as N/A. 50%: $XX,XXX 60%: $XX,XXX 70%: $XX,XXX Section 5: Negotiation Strategies / Leverage All seller motivation signals and property issues that can be used as negotiation leverage: Ownership length (5+ years = motivated signal) Owner location (out of state/county = absentee owner signal)

Transfer type (family transfer = inherited, low cost basis, any offer is profit) Tax status (delinquent = MAJOR leverage, always note amount owed) Property issues to leverage (wetlands, slope, bad road, flagpole, landlocked) Seller timeline or urgency if known Cash/quick close pitch angle if applicable Section 6: Extra Notes Everything else the owner needs to know. Use emoji flags as appropriate: Warning flag = hazard, county restrictions, slope 20%+, structures, clear-cut, anything requiring awareness Stop sign = get a realtor opinion (genuinely unsure, still provide best estimate) Red flag = owner has multiple properties (note count, deal-flow opportunity) Area quality observations (nice/average/poor) Water features noted or verified Zoning code if available Structures visible — type, condition, needs verification Any reasoning notes if comps were scattered or unusual Land ID link pasted here Section 7: Recommended List Price Market value discounted approximately 5% for fast sale combined with elite drone marketing. This is the price the property would be listed at for sale. The 5% is a guideline — adjust slightly based on market conditions, active listing competition, and property quality. Format: $XX,XXX (~5% discount from MV) Section 8: Key Comps Used 3 to 5 comps with full detail. No fewer than 3, no more than 5. Include acreage, sale price, PPA, sold/pending status, and notes on why each comp was used (ceiling, floor, primary anchor, etc.). Brief explanation of weighting if needed. Format per comp: X.XX ac | $XX,XXX | $X,XXX/ac | Sold/Pending | Notes Section 9: Questions to Ask Seller Anything that needs ground verification or seller confirmation. Common questions include: Survey on file? Perc test done? Results? Easements or access agreements?

Mobile home or structure specs (year, sqft, bed/bath)? Timber type or harvest history? Other properties for sale? Seller's timeline or motivation? Anything visible in photos/satellite that needs clarification?

Section 10: Lead Stage Classification (NEW) This is the new critical section added in Version 3. Every property evaluation must classify the lead into the correct smart list stage based on how the seller's asking price compares to the calculated market value. This tells the owner exactly where to file the lead and what to expect. 10.1 — The Stage Classification Table Stage Seller Asking Range What It Means Price Rejected - Close 65% to 75% of MV Seller is asking significantly below market but still above our target buy price. Close to a potential deal — worth pursuing with negotiation. Price Rejected - Far 75.5% to 95% of MV Seller is closer to market value. Further from our buy target. Harder deal but not impossible — needs strong negotiation or seller motivation. Archive - At Market 95.5% to 105% of MV Seller is asking right around market value (within 5% above or below). No margin for our model. Archive unless seller becomes motivated. Archive - Over Market 105.5% to 130% of MV Seller is asking above market value, up to 30% over. Overpriced. Archive and revisit only if seller drops price significantly. Archive Dead Lead 130.5%+ of MV Seller is asking more than 30% above market value. Unrealistic expectations. Dead lead — do not pursue. 10.2 — What to Include in Section 10 Every Section 10 must include these four elements: Seller Asking Price: What the seller is asking (or what price is on the lead). Market Value: The calculated MV from Section 1. Difference: Dollar amount AND percentage over or under market value. Always show both so the owner can see exactly how far off the seller is. Stage: The correct smart list stage based on the classification table above. 10.3 — Calculating the Percentage The percentage is calculated as: (Seller Asking Price / Market Value) x

ee exactly how far off the seller is. Stage: The correct smart list stage based on the classification table above. 10.3 — Calculating the Percentage The percentage is calculated as: (Seller Asking Price / Market Value) x 100 = percentage of MV. Example: Seller asking $130,000 on a property with $100,000 MV. $130,000 / $100,000 = 130% of MV. That is 30% over market value. Stage = Archive - Over Market. Example: Seller asking $70,000 on a property with $100,000 MV. $70,000 / $100,000 = 70% of MV. That is 30% under market value. Stage = Price Rejected - Close. Example: Seller asking $150,000 on a property with $100,000 MV. $150,000 / $100,000 = 150% of MV. That is 50% over market value. Stage = Archive Dead Lead.

Full Worked Example The following is a complete example of the 10-section deliverable for a property with $100,000 market value where the seller is asking $130,000. The property is a 20-acre wooded lot, family transfer, seller is motivated and wants to sell within 30 days to fund a new house purchase. PROPERTY: 1234 Example Rd, Example County, SC COUNTY: Example County, SC | ACREAGE: 20 acres 1. MARKET VALUE: $100,000 (~$5,000/ac) 2. OFFER PRICE: 50%: $50,000 60%: $60,000 70%: $70,000 3. VALUE-ADD MARKET VALUE: N/A - Wooded lot, no subdivision potential (shape/area demand do not support splitting) 4. VALUE-ADD OFFER PRICE: N/A 5. NEGOTIATION STRATEGIES / LEVERAGE: - Family transfer = inherited, low cost basis, any offer is profit for seller - Motivated seller: wants to sell within 30 days - Needs cash for new house purchase = real urgency - Time pressure works in our favor - Pitch: cash, quick close, no hassle, no inspections, no contingencies 6. EXTRA NOTES: - Wooded lot, no cleared building site visible - Family transfer = low cost basis - Seller urgency is real: new house purchase depends on this sale - Gravel road access, no paved frontage 7. RECOMMENDED LIST PRICE:

$95,000 (~5% discount from MV) 8. KEY COMPS USED: - 18.5ac | $95,000 | $5,135/ac | Sold | Similar wooded, gravel road - weighted heavily - 22ac | $98,000 | $4,455/ac | Sold | Slightly larger, used as floor - 15ac | $82,500 | $5,500/ac | Sold | Smaller, used as ceiling reference 9. QUESTIONS TO ASK SELLER: - Any survey on file? - Has perc test been done? - Any easements or access agreements? - Timeline for new house purchase: can we close in 2 weeks? 10. LEAD STAGE CLASSIFICATION: - Seller Asking: $130,000 - Market Value: $100,000 - Difference: +$30,000 (30% over MV) - Stage: ARCHIVE - OVER MARKET

Lead Stage Quick Reference (Dollar Examples) For quick reference, here is what each stage looks like at various market values: If Market Value = $100,000 Stage Seller Asking Range Dollar Range Price Rejected - Close 65% - 75% $65,000 - $75,000 Price Rejected - Far 75.5% - 95% $75,500 - $95,000 Archive - At Market 95.5% - 105% $95,500 - $105,000 Archive - Over Market 105.5% - 130% $105,500 - $130,000 Archive Dead Lead 130.5%+ $130,500+ If Market Value = $50,000 Stage Seller Asking Range Dollar Range Price Rejected - Close 65% - 75% $32,500 - $37,500 Price Rejected - Far 75.5% - 95% $37,750 - $47,500 Archive - At Market 95.5% - 105% $47,750 - $52,500 Archive - Over Market 105.5% - 130% $52,750 - $65,000 Archive Dead Lead 130.5%+ $65,250+ If Market Value = $200,000 Stage Seller Asking Range Dollar Range Price Rejected - Close 65% - 75% $130,000 - $150,000 Price Rejected - Far 75.5% - 95% $151,000 - $190,000 Archive - At Market 95.5% - 105% $191,000 - $210,000 Archive - Over Market 105.5% - 130% $211,000 - $260,000 Archive Dead Lead 130.5%+ $261,000+

Edge Cases & Important Notes When Seller Asking Price Is Unknown If the seller has not provided an asking price yet, still complete Sections 1-9 as normal. For Section 10, note 'Seller asking price not yet provided — stage to be classified once asking price is known.' The owner will update the stage after the first conversation with the seller. When Seller Asking Price Is Below 65% of MV If the seller is asking less than 65% of market value, this is an exceptional deal — well within the buy target of ~50% of list price. Note the stage as 'STRONG BUY — seller asking XX% of MV' and flag it with urgency so the owner knows to move fast. Section 10 Format — Always Include These 4 Lines Line 1: Seller Asking: $XXX,XXX Line 2: Market Value: $XXX,XXX Line 3: Difference: +/-$XX,XXX (XX% over/under MV) Line 4: Stage: [STAGE NAME] Emoji Flag System (Unchanged) Emoji Meaning When to Use Warning (yellow triangle) Hazard / Warning Flag County restrictions, slope 20%+, structures, clear-cut, access issues Stop Sign (red) Get Realtor Opinion Genuinely unsure about valuation — still provide best estimate Flag (red) Multiple Properties Owner has 2+ properties — note count, deal-flow signal THIS IS THE FINAL, DEFINITIVE DELIVERABLE FORMAT. ALL 10 SECTIONS REQUIRED ON EVERY EVALUATION. THIS SUPERSEDES ALL PREVIOUS FORMATS. — End of Document —

DEWCLAW LAND HOLDINGS LAND TYPE VISUAL IDENTIFICATION REFERENCE Detailed Visual Descriptions for Satellite & Ground-Level Land Type Assessment Version 1.0 — April 2026 This document provides granular visual descriptions of every land type encountered during property evaluation — what each looks like from satellite view, from Google Maps aerial, from Street View, and across different map filter layers. The goal is to eliminate guesswork and ensure consistent, accurate land type identification on every comp.

1. MANAGED TIMBERLAND (Pine Plantation / Timber Investment) [!] Flag with hazard symbol if suspected. Add ~$200–$500/ac premium. What It Is Land that has been intentionally planted and managed as a timber crop — a yielding asset, similar in concept to farmland. Trees are planted in organized rows (most commonly pine species in the Southeast US), grown to maturity over 15–30 years, then harvested. This is a farming operation for trees. Owners who manage timber usually know their land's value. Satellite View — What You Will See The Signature Pattern: Uniform Rows The single most distinctive feature of managed timberland from satellite is the ROW PATTERN. Trees are planted in straight, parallel, evenly spaced rows — like a giant grid or corduroy fabric draped over the terrain. This is immediately distinguishable from natural/wild forest, which has a random, irregular canopy pattern. Specific visual cues from satellite:  Parallel lines visible in the canopy — even at moderate zoom (Google Maps zoom 17–18), you can often see the linear structure of the tree rows. The gaps between rows create faint darker lines (shadow/ground visible between rows). At higher zoom, individual trees in rows become clearly visible.  Uniform canopy color — because all trees are the same species and roughly the same age, the canopy is a very consistent shade of green. No mixed species variation. Natural forest has a patchwork of different greens, some lighter deciduous, some darker evergreen. Managed timber is monotone — one shade.  Uniform canopy texture — all tree crowns are roughly the same size, creating an even, regular texture. Natural forest has big canopies next to small ones, gaps, varied heights. Managed timber looks 'smooth' and consistent from above.  Sharp, straight boundaries —

roughly the same size, creating an even, regular texture. Natural forest has big canopies next to small ones, gaps, varied heights. Managed timber looks 'smooth' and consistent from above.  Sharp, straight boundaries — managed timber blocks typically have clean, straight edges where the plantation meets other land types. Natural forest transitions gradually. Managed timber stops abruptly in a straight line.  Rectangular or geometric blocks — plantations are usually planted in rectangular blocks or sections, following property lines or management boundaries. You'll often see a neat rectangle of uniform green next to a different land type.  Age variation between blocks — if the owner manages multiple planting cycles, you may see adjacent blocks of different heights/densities. One block is mature (tall, full canopy), another is younger (shorter, more ground visible between rows). This patchwork of different-age plantation blocks is a dead giveaway of active timber management. What It Does NOT Look Like Random, scattered trees with varied species. Winding tree lines along creeks. Clumps and gaps. Mixed deciduous and evergreen. Irregular canopy heights. These are all indicators of natural woodland, NOT managed timber. Street View / Ground Level — What You Will See

 Trees in obvious rows — standing at ground level, you can look down the rows like looking down the aisle of a grocery store. Trees recede in a straight line to the horizon.  Clean understory — managed plantations are often kept relatively clear underneath. Not a lot of brush, undergrowth, or debris between the rows (though some older or less-managed ones may have understory growth).  All the same species — every tree looks the same. Usually loblolly pine, slash pine, or another commercial species. All roughly the same height and diameter.  Firebreaks/access roads — cleared strips between blocks for fire management and harvesting access. These show as light-colored paths/strips. Map Layer Toggle Behavior Managed timber looks the same across all map layers because it is a permanent land use. The rows will be visible in older AND newer imagery (unless recently harvested — see Clear-Cut section). If one layer shows rows and another shows bare ground, that is a CLEAR-CUT, not standing timber. KEY DISTINCTION: Managed Timber vs. Just Wooded Land Most wooded land is just 'wooded land' — trees grew naturally, no rows, no management, no timber premium. Only flag as managed timber if you see the row pattern, uniform species, straight boundaries, and/or evidence of active management. When in doubt, note it: 'Possible managed timber — verify with seller.' Do NOT default to calling all wooded land 'timber.'

2. CLEAR-CUT LAND (Recently Logged / Harvested Timber) [!] Flag with hazard symbol. Discount 5–30% depending on severity. What It Is Land where trees have been recently removed (harvested or cleared). This is a TEMPORARY state — the worst of both worlds. It is not wooded (no privacy, no recreation value, no timber value). It is not cleared (stumps, debris, slash piles, logging ruts make it unusable). It is ugly, messy, and devalues the property until it either regrows or is fully cleaned up. Satellite View — What You Will See The Signature: Stark Color/Texture Contrast Against Surrounding Forest Clear-cut areas stand out dramatically from satellite. They appear as brown, beige, tan, or light-colored PATCHES carved out of surrounding dark green forest. The contrast is unmistakable — like a scar or bald spot in the landscape. Specific visual cues from satellite:  Brown/beige/tan patches — exposed soil, stumps, and dead vegetation create a light-colored area that starkly contrasts with the dark green of intact forest. Fresh clear-cuts are the most obviously brown. Older ones (1–3 years) start to green up with scrubby regrowth but are still clearly different from mature forest.  Irregular or rectangular shapes — logging operations typically follow property lines or management boundaries, creating rectangular or somewhat geometric cleared patches. Some are more irregular, following terrain. Either way, the sharp boundary between 'trees' and 'no trees' is the key identifier.  Visible logging roads — thin, light-colored lines running through or leading into the cleared area. These are the roads the logging trucks used to haul timber out. They often branch in a herringbone or feather pattern through the cut area.  Texture differences — intact forest has a bumpy, irregular

o the cleared area. These are the roads the logging trucks used to haul timber out. They often branch in a herringbone or feather pattern through the cut area.  Texture differences — intact forest has a bumpy, irregular canopy texture. Clear-cut areas look flat, rough, and messy — ground-level debris, slash piles, stumps. No vertical structure. At medium zoom it looks like rough, bare earth.  Slash/debris piles — sometimes visible as small darker clumps scattered across the cleared area. These are piles of branches, treetops, and other logging waste left behind.  Erosion/ruts — heavy equipment (skidders, log trucks) creates deep ruts and disturbed soil, especially on slopes. This shows as irregular lighter/darker patterns on the bare ground. THE CRITICAL MAP FILTER TEST This is the #1 method to catch clear-cuts: TOGGLE ALL THREE MAP FILTERS QUICKLY. If one filter (older satellite imagery) shows full tree canopy and another filter (newer imagery) shows bare/brown ground — that is a clear-cut. The trees were there and now they are gone. This is definitive. The reverse can also happen: newest imagery shows trees, but an intermediate layer shows the clear-cut (already regrowing). Always toggle all three layers. Stages of Clear-Cut (What Each Looks Like) Fresh Clear-Cut (0–6 months)

Bare brown/tan soil, stumps clearly visible at high zoom, logging roads and ruts, slash piles, zero green regrowth. Looks like a bomb went off. Maximum ugliness. Maximum discount (20–30%). Early Regrowth (6 months–2 years) Light green patches starting to appear — grasses, weeds, and small seedlings beginning to cover the bare ground. Still clearly not forest. Stumps may still be visible. From satellite, it looks like a patchy mix of brown and light green. Still ugly, still a significant discount (15–25%). Established Regrowth (2–5 years) Green scrubby vegetation covering most of the ground. Young trees starting to establish but still very short compared to surrounding mature forest. From satellite, the area is green but a noticeably DIFFERENT shade/texture than mature forest — lighter, more uniform, lower. Still identifiable as a cut area. Moderate discount (10–15%). Maturing Regrowth (5–15 years) Young forest establishing. Trees are growing but still shorter than surrounding mature trees. From satellite, starting to blend in but still visible as a slightly different texture/height area. Minimal discount (5–10%). Street View / Ground Level — What You Will See  Stumps — cut tree stumps are the definitive ground-level indicator. Stumps everywhere across the property.  Debris/slash — branches, treetops, bark, and other waste scattered or piled.  Disturbed soil — deep ruts from heavy equipment, compacted logging roads, erosion.  No canopy — sky is open. No shade, no privacy. Very different feel from standing in a forest.  Skid trails — paths worn by log-dragging equipment, running through the cut area. Clear-Cut vs. Intentionally Cleared Land — KEY DISTINCTION CLEARED land is CLEAN — someone spent money to remove stumps, grade the land, seed grass, and make it usable.

g-dragging equipment, running through the cut area. Clear-Cut vs. Intentionally Cleared Land — KEY DISTINCTION CLEARED land is CLEAN — someone spent money to remove stumps, grade the land, seed grass, and make it usable. It looks maintained, intentional, and ready for use. CLEAR-CUT land is MESSY — stumps left behind, debris everywhere, ruts, no cleanup. It was logged for the timber value and abandoned. One is an improvement, the other is a scar.

3. MAINTAINED CLEARED LAND What It Is Open land that someone is ACTIVELY MAINTAINING. The grass is cut, the weeds are managed, random trees and brush are removed, and the overall appearance is clean and presentable. Someone is going out there regularly and taking care of this property. It is NOT farmed (no crop rows, no agricultural production) and it is NOT just wild open land. It is intentionally kept in a maintained state. Satellite View — What You Will See  Uniform, consistent green — the grass is one consistent color because it is regularly mowed/cut. No tall patches, no brown dead spots from neglect, no random variation. It looks like a large lawn or a well-kept field.  Clean edges — the boundary between the maintained area and any surrounding trees/brush is sharp and clean. Someone is trimming the edges and keeping the boundary defined. No gradual transition from grass to weeds to brush to trees — it goes from maintained green directly to tree line.  No random trees or brush — the open area is truly open. No scattered saplings growing up, no brush encroaching, no random trees in the middle of the field. If a tree is there, it was left intentionally (shade tree, ornamental).  Short, even texture — the grass appears short and uniform from satellite. Compared to pasture (see below), which has a taller, more varied, rougher texture, maintained cleared land looks smooth and even.  May have mowing patterns — at higher zoom on recent satellite imagery, you might see faint mowing lines/stripes, similar to a baseball field. This is definitive proof of active maintenance.  No crop rows — this is the critical distinction from farmland. Maintained cleared land is green and open but has NO parallel crop rows, NO irrigation, NO tilled soil patterns. If you see crop

oof of active maintenance.  No crop rows — this is the critical distinction from farmland. Maintained cleared land is green and open but has NO parallel crop rows, NO irrigation, NO tilled soil patterns. If you see crop rows, that is FARMLAND, not maintained cleared. Street View / Ground Level — What You Will See  Short, cut grass (lawn-like or pasture-mowed height)  No tall weeds, no overgrown sections  Clean, presentable appearance — you could walk across it comfortably  No fencing required (though some may have it)  No livestock, no feed bins, no water troughs  No crop rows, no farm equipment THE SATELLITE TRAP From satellite, maintained cleared land can look EXACTLY like farmland — both are open, green, flat fields. The only way to distinguish them is to look for crop rows (farmland) vs. uniform grass (maintained cleared). If satellite is ambiguous, ALWAYS check Street View or Google Earth to confirm. Getting this wrong can swing a valuation by thousands of dollars because farmland commands a yielding-asset premium that maintained cleared land does NOT. Valuation Impact

Maintained cleared land is desirable — it is ready-to-use, clean, buildable, and requires less site prep than wooded or pasture land. It is worth MORE than raw timber or pasture because the clearing work is already done. But it is worth LESS than farmland because it produces no income. Think of it as a premium version of raw land — improved but not productive.

4. PASTURE LAND (Unmaintained Open Land / Meadow-Like) What It Is Open land that is NOT being actively maintained. Think of a meadow — tall grasses (2+ feet), weeds growing freely, possibly some scattered trees or brush starting to encroach, and an overall unkempt appearance. Nobody is going out there and cutting, mowing, or managing this land. It is just sitting there doing its thing. KEY INSIGHT: The Difference is MAINTENANCE Pasture vs. maintained cleared has NOTHING to do with fencing, livestock, or any other traditional 'pasture' indicators. The ONLY distinction is: Is someone actively maintaining this land or not? Maintained = maintained cleared land. Not maintained = pasture. That is it. Satellite View — What You Will See  Varied, uneven green — unlike maintained cleared land's uniform green, pasture has multiple shades and tones. Some areas lighter, some darker, some yellowish-brown. This is because different grasses and weeds are growing at different rates and heights.  Taller, rougher texture — from satellite, the grass appears to have a taller, more three-dimensional texture. Maintained cleared land looks flat and smooth; pasture looks bumpy and varied because the grasses are 2+ feet tall and bending/swaying.  Scattered trees/brush encroaching — since nobody is maintaining the land, young trees and brush may be starting to grow in, especially at the edges. You might see scattered dots (tree crowns) randomly placed in the open area. This is natural succession — the land is slowly reverting to woodland.  Less defined edges — the boundary between pasture and surrounding woodland is softer and more gradual. Brush and young trees blur the line. Compare to maintained cleared land where the edge is crisp.  Seasonal variation — pasture shows more seasonal

ary between pasture and surrounding woodland is softer and more gradual. Brush and young trees blur the line. Compare to maintained cleared land where the edge is crisp.  Seasonal variation — pasture shows more seasonal change from satellite. In summer it may be green (tall grasses growing). In fall/winter it may be brown/golden (dead grasses standing). Maintained cleared land stays more consistently green because it is mowed.  Possible patches of different vegetation — weed species growing in clumps create visible patches of different color/texture across the field. Maybe a patch of goldenrod (lighter), a section of fescue (darker), some thistle (sparse). Not uniform. Street View / Ground Level — What You Will See  Tall grasses — 2 feet or more, often waist-high or higher  Weeds mixed in freely — wildflowers, thistle, goldenrod, etc.  Unkempt appearance — you would not want to walk through it in shorts  Possible scattered saplings/small trees starting to grow  No mowing lines, no evidence of recent maintenance  If fencing exists, it may be old/broken/neglected  Overall 'abandoned field' or 'meadow' feel Valuation Impact Pasture land is worth LESS than maintained cleared land because a buyer will need to do work to make it usable — brush hogging, possible stump removal, grading, seeding. It is more desirable than dense timber
(already open, can see the lay of the land) but less desirable than maintained cleared (needs cleanup). Price accordingly — between timber and maintained cleared.

5. FARMLAND (Actively Farmed — Yielding Asset) What It Is Land that is actively being farmed — producing crops and generating income. This is a YIELDING ASSET. The price of the land PLUS the value of agricultural production is baked into its market value. Farmland commands a significant premium over all other non-waterfront land types. Farmers usually know what their land is worth and are harder to get at deep discount. Satellite View — What You Will See  Visible crop rows — THE definitive indicator. Parallel lines running across the field where crops are planted. These are visible from satellite at moderate zoom. The rows create a distinct lined/striped pattern across the property.  Tilled soil patterns — especially visible when land is freshly plowed or between growing seasons. Dark, turned soil in organized patterns. Very different from natural ground color.  Irrigation patterns — center-pivot irrigation creates distinctive circular crop patterns (green circles in brown surroundings). Other irrigation shows as wet/dry patterns, water channels, or sprinkler patterns.  Distinct field boundaries — farm fields have sharp, straight edges where the crop ends and field margins/hedgerows begin. Multiple fields may be subdivided within a property.  Seasonal crop stages — depending on time of year and satellite date, you may see bare tilled soil (spring/fall), green growing crops (summer), or harvested stubble (fall). All are indicators of active farming.  Farm infrastructure — barns, grain bins, silos, equipment sheds visible nearby. Farm roads/paths for equipment access between fields. THE CRITICAL DISTINCTION: Farmland vs. Maintained Cleared Both can look like open, green, flat land from satellite. The KEY difference: farmland has CROP ROWS — visible parallel lines

for equipment access between fields. THE CRITICAL DISTINCTION: Farmland vs. Maintained Cleared Both can look like open, green, flat land from satellite. The KEY difference: farmland has CROP ROWS — visible parallel lines in the vegetation. Maintained cleared land has UNIFORM GRASS — no rows, no pattern, just a consistently mowed green field. If you cannot see crop rows, it is NOT farmland. Check Street View to confirm if satellite is ambiguous.

6. NATURAL WOODLAND (Just Wooded Land — No Timber Premium) What It Is The default for most rural land. Trees grew naturally — mixed species, mixed ages, random spacing. No rows, no management, no commercial timber value beyond firewood. This is NOT managed timber. Do NOT apply timber premium. Comp as normal wooded property. Satellite View — What You Will See  Random, irregular canopy — tree crowns of varying sizes, randomly distributed. No rows, no pattern, no uniformity. Gaps, clumps, varying density.  Mixed colors — multiple shades of green from different tree species. Some lighter (deciduous), some darker (evergreen). Seasonal imagery shows a mix of leaf-on and leaf-off.  Irregular edges — the boundary of wooded areas is natural and gradual, not straight and geometric like managed timber.  Understory visible in gaps — lighter green or brown patches where the canopy is thin and ground/understory shows through. KEY: Natural woodland is the DEFAULT. Only upgrade to 'managed timber' if you see rows.

7. QUICK REFERENCE — SIDE-BY-SIDE COMPARISON Feature Managed Timber Clear-Cut Maint. Cleared Pasture Farmland Woodland Rows visible? YES — tree rows No (stumps) No No YES — crop rows No Color from sat. Uniform dark green Brown/beige Uniform green Varied green Green or brown (seasonal) Mixed greens Texture Even, uniform Rough, messy Smooth, flat Rough, tall Lined/striped Bumpy, random Edges Sharp, straight Sharp (cut boundary) Clean, defined Soft, gradual Sharp, straight Irregular Maintenance? Yes (managed) No (abandoned) Yes (mowed) No Yes (farmed) No Premium? +$200–500/ac DISCOUNT 5–30% Above pasture, below farmland Below maintained cleared Yielding asset premium Base/default Flag [!]? YES YES No No Note for seller awareness No 8. DECISION TREE — LAND TYPE IDENTIFICATION Step 1: Is the land OPEN (no tree canopy covering it)?  NO → Go to Step 4 (Wooded Land Assessment)  YES → Go to Step 2 Step 2: Do you see CROP ROWS or agricultural patterns?  YES → FARMLAND (yielding asset, premium pricing, warn investor)  NO → Go to Step 3 Step 3: Is the land MAINTAINED (short grass, clean edges, no overgrowth)?  YES → MAINTAINED CLEARED LAND (no yielding premium, but desirable)  NO (tall grass, weeds, unkempt, meadow-like) → PASTURE LAND Step 4: Do you see ROWS of trees (uniform species, even spacing, straight lines)?  YES → MANAGED TIMBERLAND — flag [!], add $200–$500/ac premium  NO → Go to Step 5 Step 5: Toggle ALL THREE map filters. Did trees DISAPPEAR between layers?  YES → CLEAR-CUT — flag [!], discount 5–30%  NO → NATURAL WOODLAND (default, no premium, no flag) If EVER uncertain between maintained cleared and farmland → CHECK STREET VIEW. — End of Visual Reference Guide —

DEWCLAW LAND HOLDINGS COMP METHODOLOGY & RULES Complete Guide to Finding, Evaluating, and Weighting Comparable Sales Version 1.0 — April 2026 This document covers every aspect of how DewClaw evaluates comparable sales — from which sources to trust and reject, to how wide to search, how to read the full story from all available comps, and how to whittle down to a final price. The core philosophy: read the whole book, not just the middle chapters.

1. COMP SOURCE HIERARCHY — WHAT TO TRUST AND WHAT TO REJECT Gold Standard: Closed MLS Sales Closed MLS sales are the only fully reliable comp source. These are verified arms-length transactions where a willing buyer and a willing seller agreed on a price through the open market. The price reflects actual market value because both parties had access to information, representation, and alternatives. Every valuation should be anchored primarily on closed MLS sales. Valuable: Pending Sales Pending sales are real buyers making real commitments — someone actually put money down and signed a contract. This is very current market activity and highly valuable for understanding where the market IS right now (not where it was 6 months ago). However, pending sales have not yet closed, so the final price may differ from the list price. Apply a discount:  Hot/normal market: Discount 5% from list price  Slower market: Discount 10% from list price Use common sense on the discount amount. A pending sale in a market where everything moves fast probably went close to ask. A pending sale in a slow market probably had negotiation. NEVER USE: Sold-Public Records Sold-Public Records are terribly inaccurate representations of market value. These are sales recorded in county records but NOT through the MLS — meaning they were not open-market, arms-length transactions.
