The problem is you have absolutely no idea what happened behind the scenes:  A neighbor sold to his friend of 30 years at 30% below market value as a favor  A family member sold to another family member at 50% below market  A tax sale where the property went for pennies on the dollar  A partial interest sale (someone sold 50% ownership)  An estate settlement where speed mattered more than price None of these represent what

below market  A tax sale where the property went for pennies on the dollar  A partial interest sale (someone sold 50% ownership)  An estate settlement where speed mattered more than price None of these represent what the property would sell for on the open market. Using them as comps would dramatically skew your valuation — usually downward — and kill deals that should have been made. There is NO exception to this rule, even in sparse markets. If Sold-Public Records are your only data point, you are better off using active listings, pending sales, and the PPA trendline than trusting a sale that may have been a friend-to-friend deal at half price. NEVER USE: Off-Market Sales Same problem as Sold-Public Records. Off-market sales were never exposed to the open market. You have no idea if the seller knew the true value, if the buyer got a sweetheart deal, or if there were personal relationships influencing the price. A neighbor who finally sells his back 10 acres to the guy next door after 30 years of asking — that price tells you nothing about what a stranger would pay on the open market. Reject these completely. NEVER USE: AI Comps, Zestimates, or Platform Estimates Zillow Zestimates, AI-generated comp values, and any platform's automated valuation are unreliable for vacant rural land. These algorithms are designed for residential homes with standardized features (bedrooms,

bathrooms, square footage). Vacant land has none of those standardized inputs. The algorithms cannot account for road type, water features, slope, wetlands, land type, or any of the nuances that actually drive rural land value. Ignore them completely. NEVER Cross County Lines Each county is its own market. Pink lines on Land Insights represent county boundaries — these are your comp boundaries. Even if a property across the county line is physically closer than one within the county, do NOT use it. The reason is how buyers actually search: they go to Zillow, Redfin, or Realtor.com and filter by COUNTY. They never see listings in adjacent counties. Over time, this creates completely separate pricing patterns. Adjacent counties can have DRASTICALLY different prices for identical land because they are functionally different markets. No exceptions.

2. ACREAGE RANGE — HOW WIDE TO SEARCH The Progression Always start with AUTO on Land Insights. This gives you the platform's default acreage range based on your subject property. From there, widen progressively if you need more comps: Step Below % Above % When to Use 1 (Start) AUTO AUTO Always start here 2 50% 100% If AUTO gives too few comps 3 50% 200% If still not enough 4 100% 300% Maximum range — only if not confused by volume The Philosophy: MORE COMPS = ALWAYS BETTER The wider you go, the more data points you have to build the full PPA trendline. Even comps that are not directly comparable to your subject are NOT noise — they are data points that shape the curve in your head. A 2-acre comp and an 80-acre comp may not be 'like-kind' to your 15-acre subject, but they tell you what the market looks like at the extremes, which helps you understand where your subject sits on the curve. The only reason to stop widening is if the volume of comps starts CONFUSING you — if you can't keep the story straight because there are too many variables. But if you can handle the volume and still identify which comps matter most, go wider. Always.

3. THE BOOK ANALOGY — READ THE WHOLE BOOK This is the single most important mental model for comping at DewClaw. It governs how you think about every comp you pull. The Analogy Imagine you are trying to understand a chapter in the middle of a book. You COULD just read the two chapters before and the two chapters after — and you would have a pretty good idea of what is happening. But imagine if you read the WHOLE book. You would have the absolute best, most detailed, most nuanced understanding possible. Comping is the exact same thing. The 'middle chapter' is the 2–3 comps that are most similar to your subject — same acreage range, same area, same land type. Those are your primary anchors. But the 'whole book' is EVERY comp across the full acreage spectrum in the county. The small lots at high PPA, the medium lots at mid PPA, the large lots at low PPA — they ALL contribute to your understanding of the market. How This Works In Practice  Step 1: Pull comps across the widest acreage range you can handle  Step 2: Build a mental PPA trendline across the entire spectrum — from the smallest lots (highest PPA) to the largest lots (lowest PPA). This is the exponential curve.  Step 3: Identify where your subject property falls on that curve based on its acreage  Step 4: Find the 2–5 comps most similar to your subject — these are your primary anchors that get the most weight  Step 5: Use ALL the other comps to VALIDATE and SOLIDIFY the story those anchor comps are telling you. Do the anchors make sense in the context of the full curve? Are they consistent with what smaller lots and larger lots are doing?  Step 6: Price the subject based on where it falls on the validated trendline, with appropriate adjustments for its specific features (road type, water, slope, etc.) Why

with what smaller lots and larger lots are doing?  Step 6: Price the subject based on where it falls on the validated trendline, with appropriate adjustments for its specific features (road type, water, slope, etc.) Why This Matters Without the full trendline, you are guessing. You might have 3 comps that all say $5,000/ac and feel confident — but those 3 comps might all be slightly overpriced outliers. If you had pulled the full spectrum and seen that 2-acre lots sell at $12,000/ac, 10-acre lots at $6,000/ac, and 40-acre lots at $3,500/ac, you would know that $5,000/ac for your 15-acre subject is actually on the higher end of the trendline, not the middle. That context changes everything. The anchor comps tell you the price. The full trendline tells you whether to BELIEVE the price. Both are essential.

4. EVALUATING COMPS — LOOK AT ALL OF THEM Never Pre-Filter If you have 8, 10, 12, or more comps in range — evaluate ALL of them. Do not pre-filter down to the 'best' 4–5 before looking at the rest. Every comp contributes to the story. The ones that are not directly comparable still establish the price floor and price ceiling for the market, which helps you understand where the like-kind comps sit within that range. NEVER Use One Comp As Your Holy Grail No matter how perfect a single comp looks — same acreage, same road, same area, same land type — NEVER anchor your entire valuation to it. There is always a chance it is an outlier. There could be something on the property that was not in the description. There could be something the photos did not show. There could have been unusual circumstances (desperate seller, unusually motivated buyer, family connection). You will never know for certain. This is why you always need MULTIPLE comps to build a RANGE, and then whittle that range down to your price. What To Check On Every Comp  Description: Read the full listing description. Look for features mentioned (water, cleared, structures, views, utilities) that affect value.  DOM (Days on Market): Apply the DOM shaving schedule (covered in DOM section). Check if DOM is total days or days since last price drop.  Full listing history: Was it listed, delisted, and relisted? Multiple price drops? This is a red flag — something was wrong (overpriced, had issues, etc.).  Listing photos (Redfin/Zillow): Capture AS MANY photos as possible. Look for amenities, slope, cleared areas, plot maps, aerials, structures, layout. Photos may show subdivision/HOA amenities (clubhouse, pool, tennis courts) that are NOT on the actual parcel — always verify what is on the ACTUAL property via

lope, cleared areas, plot maps, aerials, structures, layout. Photos may show subdivision/HOA amenities (clubhouse, pool, tennis courts) that are NOT on the actual parcel — always verify what is on the ACTUAL property via satellite/APN.  Road type: What road is the comp on? Paved, gravel, dirt? This affects how you weight it relative to your subject.  Land type: What kind of land? Farmland, maintained cleared, pasture, wooded? Compare to your subject's land type.  Proximity: How close is the comp to your subject? Closer = more weight. Comp Quality Awareness Not all comps with the same label are equal. A 'waterfront' comp that is marshy, wetland-heavy swampland that happened to touch a lake is NOT the same as a clean, walkable-shoreline waterfront comp. A 'riverfront' comp in a flood zone with unusable banks is NOT the same as a clean riverfront with gentle slope to the water. Always assess the QUALITY of a comp's features, not just the labels. If you need to, pull the comp's APN on Zillow/Redfin and check it in Land Insights for soil survey, wetlands, and flood zone data.

5. WHITTLING DOWN TO A PRICE The Process After evaluating all comps, you are not looking for one magic number. You are looking for a RANGE — and then you narrow that range down until you have a tight window.  Step 1 — Establish the full range: From all comps evaluated, identify the absolute ceiling (highest PPA among relevant comps) and the absolute floor (lowest PPA among relevant comps). This is your starting range.  Step 2 — Identify the like-kind comps: Which 2–5 comps are most similar to your subject in acreage, proximity, land type, road type, and features? These get the heaviest weight.  Step 3 — Refine the range: Based on the like-kind comps, narrow the range. You should be able to get within approximately $5,000 of where the property should list.  Step 4 — Go conservative: Within that $5,000 range, price on the LOWER end. Then apply a slight additional discount for list price (5–10% below market value). This ensures fast sales with elite drone marketing. No Property Is Exactly The Same Accept this reality: no comp will ever be a perfect match to your subject. Every property is different — different shape, different road, different trees, different access, different topography. That is normal and expected. Your job is not to find the perfect comp. Your job is to build enough context from enough comps that you can confidently identify a tight range and price conservatively within it. You will never land on an exact number — it is impossible. But you can get within a range of ~$5,000, and within that range you go conservative. That is how you comp, and that is how you ensure every deal has margin and every listing sells fast.

6. DOM INTERPRETATION — DAYS ON MARKET SHAVING SCHEDULE DOM tells you how long a property took to sell. This gives you critical insight into whether the property was priced correctly, overpriced, or underpriced. Apply the following adjustments to comp sold prices based on their DOM: DOM Range Adjustment Lean Direction What It Means Under 30 days Use as FLOOR N/A Sold UNDER market — MV is higher. This is how YOUR listings should sell. 30–150 days Face value — 0% N/A Normal market transaction. No adjustment needed. 150–180 days ~5% shave 5% Starting to take a while. May have been slightly overpriced. 180–270 days 5–7.5% shave Lean toward 5% Something was slightly off. Don't over-discount. 270–365 days 7.5–10% shave Lean toward 7.5% Took a long time. Price or property issues likely. Stay conservative. 365+ days Cap at 10% 10% max Major issues. Still usable but investigate before relying on it. Critical Rules  Always check price history: The DOM shown on a listing may be TOTAL days on market, including time at a higher price. If a property was listed at $80K for 200 days, then dropped to $60K and sold in 30 days, the 'true' DOM from the final price is only 30 days — but the platform might show 230 days. Check the price history to understand the real story.  Never avoid a sold comp because of high DOM: A sold comp is a sold comp. Someone actually paid that price. Even a 400-day comp tells a story — it tells you that the market at that price point takes a very long time to find a buyer, which is useful information. If a high-DOM comp is your only comp, absolutely still use it. Apply the shave and note the DOM in your analysis.  Low DOM comps = list price targets: If a comp sold in 14 days at $45K, that is a realistic list price that moves fast. Work backwards: offer at

bsolutely still use it. Apply the shave and note the DOM in your analysis.  Low DOM comps = list price targets: If a comp sold in 14 days at $45K, that is a realistic list price that moves fast. Work backwards: offer at 50% ($22.5K). These are your calibration points for how to price YOUR listings for fast sales.  Stay conservative but don't kill deals: The purpose of DOM shaving is to get a more accurate market value, not to discount everything into oblivion. Lean toward the lower end of the shave ranges. A 200-day comp at $50K shaved 5% = $47,500 — not $45,000. The difference matters when you are building offer ranges.

7. DUPLICATE SUBDIVISION SALES — COUNT AS ONE When a seller subdivided a larger parcel into multiple lots and all of them sold, only count ONE as a comp. The reason: they are essentially the same type of property from one parent parcel. Using all 4 of them would over-weight that single subdivision in your analysis and make it look like you have more independent data points than you actually do. Pick the most representative lot — the one closest in acreage and features to your subject. If one lot is clearly different from the others (bigger, better access, has a pond), it can be used as a separate comp because it genuinely represents a different value proposition.

8. SPARSE MARKETS — WHEN COMPS ARE LIMITED In some counties, sold comps are scarce. You might have 2–3 closed sales in the relevant acreage range. In these situations, you still weight SOLDS more heavily, but actives and pendings become more important for context. Views-to-Saves Ratio (Zillow Only) On Zillow, underneath the property description that the realtor wrote, you can find the views-to-saves ratio. This tells you whether an active listing is priced competitively:  ~10 views to 1 save = Good ratio — listing is priced well, generating real interest  High views, low saves = Overpriced — people are looking but nobody wants to save it at that price  Low views overall = Listing has visibility issues or is in a very slow market Compare save ratios across active listings at different price points. The ones with good ratios = realistic market pricing. The ones with bad ratios = probably overpriced. This helps you calibrate which active listings to weight more heavily in your analysis. This data is ONLY available on Zillow. Redfin does not have views-to-saves ratios. Setting Price When Comps Are Thin When there are no clear direct comps, YOU set the price. This is where the 'read the whole book' approach is most critical. Widen the acreage range to the maximum. Pull every comp in the county. Build the full PPA trendline in your head — see how price per acre changes from the smallest lots to the largest. Then interpolate where your subject falls on that curve. Price slightly below where the trendline says the property should be. Couple that with elite drone photos and the property will sell quickly because it is priced competitively in a market where everything else is either overpriced or poorly marketed. Sparse Market Note Format When comps are limited, note it in

e drone photos and the property will sell quickly because it is priced competitively in a market where everything else is either overpriced or poorly marketed. Sparse Market Note Format When comps are limited, note it in the deliverable so the investor understands the context: NOTE: Limited direct comps in this market. Pricing based on county-wide PPA trendline: 5ac lots selling ~$7,000/ac / 20ac lots selling ~$4,500/ac. Estimated 10-acre value: ~$5,500/ac. Recommend conservative list price to ensure quick sale.

9. THE PPA CURVE — EXPONENTIAL, NOT LINEAR The relationship between acreage and price per acre is NOT linear. It is EXPONENTIAL. The curve drops STEEPLY from small acreage to medium acreage, then FLATTENS out as acreage increases. This is true in virtually every rural land market. Example Pattern (varies by market): Acreage Approximate PPA Total Value 1 acre $15,000/ac $15,000 2 acres $12,000/ac $24,000 5 acres $8,000/ac $40,000 10 acres $5,500/ac $55,000 20 acres $4,000/ac $80,000 40 acres $3,200/ac $128,000 80 acres $2,800/ac $224,000 Why This Matters For Subdivision The steepest part of the curve = the inflection point = where subdivision creates the most value. Buy at the lower PPA (larger acreage), subdivide into smaller lots at the higher PPA. The steeper the curve, the more money subdivision makes.  Steep curve (exponential): Great subdivide market. Massive PPA difference between small and large lots. Subdividing unlocks significant value.  Flat/linear curve: Bad subdivide market. Small PPA difference between lot sizes. Subdividing does not create enough value to justify the hassle. Focus on simple flips. Always pull the full acreage range to see the curve shape BEFORE recommending subdivision. If the curve is flat, don't waste time on subdivision analysis. KEY COMPS IN DELIVERABLE Standard: Include 3–5 key comps in the deliverable. If you have 8+ comps that are all very similar to the subject and all add meaningfully to the story, you can include more. But having 8+ truly similar comps is rare — in most markets, 3–5 is what you will end up with as the most relevant anchors, even if you evaluated 12+ total. All comps in the deliverable should be HYPERLINKED so the investor can click through to the actual Redfin/Zillow listing. This is mandatory — the investor

th as the most relevant anchors, even if you evaluated 12+ total. All comps in the deliverable should be HYPERLINKED so the investor can click through to the actual Redfin/Zillow listing. This is mandatory — the investor needs to be able to verify comps independently. — End of Comp Methodology & Rules —

DEWCLAW LAND HOLDINGS WATERFRONT & WATER FEATURES Complete Guide to Valuing Water Features — From Seasonal Creeks to Lakefront Version 1.0 — April 2026 Water features are one of the most significant value drivers in rural land. But not all water is created equal. This guide covers the full spectrum — from dry seasonal creeks (zero premium) to prime lakefront (massive premium) — and everything in between. The core principle: every market is different, every body of water is different, and you must always verify with your own eyes.

1. ALWAYS VERIFY WATER FEATURES YOURSELF [!] MANDATORY — NEVER trust filters alone. NEVER assume water exists because a map layer shows it. Land Insights may show a surface water overlay, wetlands overlay, or blue shading that LOOKS like a pond, lake, or creek. But it could be swamp, seasonal ponding, seasonal creek, or wetlands that look like water on a filter but are actually just soggy ground. Conversely, Land Insights sometimes MISSES real ponds — small ponds may not show up on any filter. Mandatory Verification Process:  Turn OFF all filters — look at raw satellite imagery  Look at actual satellite imagery with your own eyes — on both Google Maps AND Land Insights  Check Google Earth — often comes out cleaner than Google Maps for seeing water features  Use Street View — if the property fronts a road near water, Street View will show you the actual body of water (or lack thereof). Is the creek dry? Is the river actually flowing? How big is it really?  If it even SLIGHTLY looks like there might be water — give BOTH a waterfront valuation AND a non-waterfront valuation. Let the investor verify with the seller. Trust your own eyes. ALWAYS.

2. THE WATER FEATURE VALUE SPECTRUM Not all water is 'waterfront.' A seasonal creek is not waterfront. A small stream is not waterfront. Even a normal river is not always considered waterfront. The term 'waterfront' is reserved for properties with significant bodies of water — lakes, large ponds, and big rivers that offer real recreational value (boating, fishing, swimming, scenic views). The Full Spectrum — From Zero to Maximum Premium Water Feature Value Impact Notes Dry / seasonal creek 0% — No premium If it is dry or seasonal, it adds nothing. Not even close to waterfront. Small creek (some water flowing) 5–10% value add NOT waterfront Adds some appeal but is not a waterfront premium. Just a nice feature. Okay-sized river ~10% value add Adds value but still not necessarily "waterfront" in the premium sense. Bigger river Up to 30% value add Starting to approach real waterfront territory depending on market. Huge, nice river True waterfront premium Big body of water = real waterfront. Comp against other waterfront sales. Pond (year-round, clean, fishable) Significant premium Rare and highly desirable. Size and quality matter enormously. Lake (especially nice lakes) Maximum premium Best water feature. Highest demand. Boating, fishing, swimming, views. The Guiding Principle: Bigger Body of Water = More Value This is why beachfront property (the biggest body of water — the ocean) is the most expensive real estate anywhere. The hierarchy scales down from there: ocean > lake > big river > pond > small river > creek > seasonal creek. How BIG and how NICE the body of water is determines where on this spectrum a particular feature falls.

3. NO UNIVERSAL WATERFRONT MULTIPLIER There is no fixed '2x' or '3x' multiplier for waterfront. Every market is different. In Michigan, waterfront might trade at 100% premium over non-waterfront. In a different area, the same quality waterfront might command 300%+ premium. You cannot apply a formula. You MUST look at the actual comps in the actual market and let the data tell you. How To Comp Waterfront  Step 1 — Establish the floor: What are NON-waterfront properties going for in this area? Pull comps for similar acreage without water features. This is your baseline PPA.  Step 2 — Find the ceiling: What are WATERFRONT properties going for? Pull comps with water features. This is your ceiling PPA.  Step 3 — Compare features: How does your subject's waterfront compare to the waterfront comps? More footage? Less? Better shore? Worse? Bigger body of water? Smaller?  Step 4 — Price between floor and ceiling: Based on your subject's water feature quality relative to the comps, slot it into the range. If your waterfront is nicer than the comps, price higher. If it is marginal or less impressive, price lower but still above the non-waterfront floor.  Step 5 — Discount for fast sale: Apply the standard conservative discount so that the property moves quickly with elite drone marketing.

4. WATERFRONT FOOTAGE & SHORE QUALITY How Much Waterfront Footage Matters — A LOT The amount of waterfront a property has directly impacts its premium. A property with the entire back boundary along a lake is VASTLY more valuable than one that barely touches the water at one corner. When comparing your subject to waterfront comps, always note how much frontage each has.  Entire back/side on water: Maximum premium — extensive access, views, recreation  Significant frontage (hundreds of feet): Strong premium — room for dock, beach, access  Moderate frontage (50–100 feet): Good premium — usable waterfront but limited  Tiny corner touching water: Reduced premium — technically waterfront but barely usable Shore Quality — Can You Walk Into The Water? The quality of the shoreline makes an enormous difference in how much premium the water feature commands. Two properties can both be 'lakefront' but have completely different shoreline value:  Clean, gentle slope, sandy/firm bottom: Maximum shore quality. You can walk right into the water. Perfect for swimming, dock, boat launch. This is what buyers dream about.  Grassy slope to water: Good quality. Usable, scenic, can install dock/steps. Not as premium as sandy but still very desirable.  Steep banks: Reduced quality. Can see the water but accessing it requires stairs, retaining walls, or significant work. Views are still valuable but recreation is limited.  Marshy/swampy: Lowest quality waterfront. You cannot walk to the water — you will sink in mud. Limited to visual appeal only. Do NOT comp this the same as clean waterfront. A marshy 'waterfront' comp that sold low is NOT comparable to a clean waterfront property. Always compare the shore quality of your subject to the shore quality of your waterfront comps. If your

same as clean waterfront. A marshy 'waterfront' comp that sold low is NOT comparable to a clean waterfront property. Always compare the shore quality of your subject to the shore quality of your waterfront comps. If your subject has a clean walkable shore and the best waterfront comp has marshy banks, your subject is SUPERIOR and should be priced above that comp.

5. NEAR-WATERFRONT — THE PROXIMITY SPECTRUM Not every property has direct water frontage, but proximity to water still adds value. The closer you are, the more value it adds — but NOTHING replaces direct frontage. Proximity Value Impact Why Direct frontage Full waterfront premium You own the shoreline. Maximum access, views, and recreation. One parcel away (~200–300 feet) More than rural, far less than waterfront Right next to the lake. Views likely. But you don't own the shore. Quarter mile away Slightly above rural Close enough to drive/walk to the water. Still a perk. Half mile+ Minimal or zero premium Too far to meaningfully benefit from the water. People still pay for views and convenience. A property one parcel away from a nice lake, where you can see the water from the property, will sell for more than an identical property a mile away in the middle of nowhere. But it will sell for FAR less than the property that actually owns the shoreline. Always give BOTH a waterfront-adjacent value and a non-waterfront value when the property is close to water, so the investor can use whichever applies based on what the seller says about access/easements. Assume Waterfront If Close If the property butts up to waterfront LLC/owner land, is very close to a lake/pond, or has state land between it and water — always evaluate as waterfront AND give a non-waterfront value. This incentivizes the investor to ask the seller about possible easements, deeded access, or shared waterfront rights that may not be visible from satellite. Deeded Water Access (Not Direct Frontage) Some properties have deeded easements to nearby water — a 50-foot wide path to the lake, for example. A real, usable deeded lake access (50ft wide) is essentially waterfront for the buyer — they can walk to the

Frontage) Some properties have deeded easements to nearby water — a 50-foot wide path to the lake, for example. A real, usable deeded lake access (50ft wide) is essentially waterfront for the buyer — they can walk to the water, put in a kayak, fish. Worth approximately 80% of the direct waterfront premium. Narrower or less usable easements are worth closer to 50%. The amount of water frontage at the easement point (linear feet) also matters — more frontage at the access point = more value.

6. MULTIPLE WATER FEATURES — DO THEY STACK? Yes, multiple water features stack premiums when they are ON the property. A property with a pond AND a creek running through it is more valuable than a property with just one of those features. More water = more recreation, more appeal, more buyer interest. For water features NEAR the property (not on it), the premium stacking is smaller — similar to being near a single water feature. Being near a lake and a river is nice, but if you don't own any shoreline, the stacking is modest. The hierarchy still applies: bigger bodies of water dominate the premium. If a property has both a creek and lake frontage, the lake frontage drives the premium and the creek is a bonus. If it has a pond and a seasonal creek, the pond drives the premium and the seasonal creek adds nothing (seasonal = zero value).

7. WATER FEATURE SIZE — THE USABLE ACREAGE PROBLEM Bigger water = better. That is the general rule. BUT there is a counterpoint: if the pond or lake on the property takes up a significant portion of the total acreage, you are losing usable land. A 10-acre property with a 4-acre pond has only 6 acres of usable land. The waterfront premium applies, but you are comping based on usable acreage plus the water premium, not total acreage at full waterfront rates. Same logic as flagpole easements: you comp the usable portion of the property and then add the premium for the water feature. The water takes up space that cannot be built on or used for anything other than recreation. — End of Waterfront & Water Features Guide —
DEWCLAW LAND HOLDINGS PROPERTY ACCESS — ROADS, LANDLOCKED & FLAGPOLE Road Types, Highway Discounts, Landlocked Spectrum, and Flagpole Lot Calculations Version 1.0 — April 2026

1. ROAD TYPE HIERARCHY & DISCOUNT SCHEDULE Road type significantly impacts property value. Buyers want easy, reliable access. Paved county roads are the gold standard. Everything else receives a discount relative to paved. Road Type Discount from Paved Description Paved county road 0% (baseline) Best for most buyers. Easy year-round access. Smooth, maintained by county. Gravel road Slightly less than dirt Normal baseline for rural comps. Most rural properties are on gravel. Slightly nicer than dirt. Nice maintained dirt (no potholes) 10–15% Well-maintained, smooth, no potholes. Looks presentable. Still a dirt road though. Normal dirt road 20–25% Average quality. Some roughness, maybe minor issues. Standard rural dirt road. Bad dirt / potholes / terrible access ~30% Rough, rutted, potholes, possibly seasonal accessibility issues. Significant discount. Two-track 30%+ Barely a road. Faint tire paths through grass/trees. Significant access concerns. Gravel is the normal baseline for most rural land comps. When you are comping a rural property, most of the sold comps will be on gravel roads. This is the default — paved is a premium above this baseline, and dirt is a discount below it. Inferring Road Type Without Street View When Street View is not available on a comp's road, you can still infer the road type:  Look at nearby sold comps on the same road — did any of them have Street View? Was the road type mentioned in their listing descriptions?  Check satellite imagery — paved roads appear as dark, smooth, consistent lines. Gravel/maintained roads appear lighter and wider. Two-tracks appear as faint lines through vegetation.  Road width — wider = more likely maintained/paved. Narrower = more likely dirt/two-track.

2. HIGHWAY FRONTAGE — A DISCOUNT, NOT A PREMIUM [!] Highway frontage = NEGATIVE. Always flag it in the deliverable. Many people assume highway frontage adds value because of 'visibility' or 'access.' For rural recreational and residential land, the opposite is true. Highway frontage means constant traffic noise, no privacy, headlights at night, and a less desirable living/recreational environment. Buyers of rural land specifically want to GET AWAY from highways. They pay LESS, not more, for highway exposure. Highway Type Discount Lean Direction Major interstate highway 10–20% Lean toward 10–15% Normal single-lane highway 5–10% Lean toward 5% Always note in the deliverable that the property has highway frontage AND that you applied a discount for it. The investor needs to know this was factored in.

3. LANDLOCKED — THE FULL SPECTRUM Landlocked is NOT binary. It is a spectrum from 'completely inaccessible' to 'has an easement and is essentially normal.' The valuation approach changes dramatically depending on where the property falls on this spectrum. [!] ALWAYS provide BOTH values when landlocked is flagged — landlocked price AND alternative price. Scenario Valuation Action Truly landlocked (no legal access) 10% of market value Massive discount. High risk, high hassle for buyer. Landlocked but adjacent to road Probably has easement or data error Verify with seller. Check Street View for dirt/gravel path. If easement exists = normal pricing. Long easement through neighbor's property Between flagpole and landlocked Consider: how far? Through how many properties? Cost to build driveway? Flagpole lot (short easement) Subtract strip acreage + inconvenience discount See flagpole calculation section below. Land Insights Gets It Wrong Sometimes Land Insights may flag 'Landlocked: Yes' when the property clearly touches a road on satellite. This is a data error — the platform is not always accurate. ALWAYS verify with your own eyes. Look at the satellite imagery. Look at Street View. If the parcel visually touches a road, it is most likely NOT landlocked. But if there is any uncertainty, provide both valuations:  Primary valuation: Assuming access exists (comp normally)  Alternative: If truly landlocked = 10% of market value This applies to EVERYTHING Land Insights reports — landlocked, waterfront, structures. Always verify with your own eyes. The platform is a starting point, not gospel. Looking for Easement Signs on Street View When a property appears potentially landlocked but might have an easement, check Street View for clues: a dirt or gravel path leading from the

rm is a starting point, not gospel. Looking for Easement Signs on Street View When a property appears potentially landlocked but might have an easement, check Street View for clues: a dirt or gravel path leading from the road back toward the property, tire tracks, a cleared strip, a gate or fence opening. These suggest an easement exists even if Land Insights does not show one. If you find signs of an easement, comp the property as a flagpole lot (subtract easement acreage, apply inconvenience discount) rather than as fully landlocked.

4. FLAGPOLE LOT CALCULATION — TWO-PART DISCOUNT Flagpole lots have a narrow access strip (30–50 feet wide) leading from the road back to a larger buildable area. The access strip adds very little usable value — you cannot build on it, it is just a driveway corridor. The discount is calculated in two parts: Part 1: Subtract the Access Strip Acreage Do NOT comp the property at its total acreage. Identify how much acreage the access strip takes up and subtract it. Comp only the BUILDABLE portion of the property. Example: a 10-acre flagpole with a 2-acre access strip = comp as an 8-acre lot. This is a significant discount by itself. A property that shows as 10 acres on paper is really only 8 usable acres, and PPA increases as acreage decreases (exponential curve). So by comping 8 acres instead of 10, you are already pricing it lower per acre AND on fewer acres. Part 2: Apply Inconvenience Discount (2–5%) ON TOP of subtracting the strip acreage, apply a small additional discount for the inconvenience of being a flagpole lot:  Not that deep (short access strip): 2–3% inconvenience discount  Deeper access strip: 5% inconvenience discount Why Both Discounts Are Needed Subtracting the strip acreage accounts for the lost usable land. The inconvenience discount accounts for the ADDITIONAL negatives: long driveway cost, farther from road, harder to access, less convenient for emergency services, less road frontage. These are real costs and inconveniences that a buyer will factor into their willingness to pay. Example Calculation Subject: 10-acre flagpole lot with 1.5-acre access strip, moderate depth.  Step 1: Comp as 8.5 acres (10 - 1.5 strip)  Step 2: Market PPA for 8.5 acres in this area = $6,000/ac = $51,000  Step 3: Apply 3% inconvenience discount (moderate depth) =

with 1.5-acre access strip, moderate depth.  Step 1: Comp as 8.5 acres (10 - 1.5 strip)  Step 2: Market PPA for 8.5 acres in this area = $6,000/ac = $51,000  Step 3: Apply 3% inconvenience discount (moderate depth) = $51,000 x 0.97 = $49,470  Market Value: ~$49,500  Compare to: 10 acres with full road frontage at $5,500/ac = $55,000  Net discount vs. full-frontage lot: ~$5,500 or ~10% Deliverable Format for Landlocked/Flagpole MARKET VALUE (assuming access): $49,500 Offer Range: $24,750 (50%) – $34,650 (70%) [!] LANDLOCKED FLAG — If truly landlocked: Offer ~$5,000 (10%) Verify access situation with seller
— End of Property Access Guide —
DEWCLAW LAND HOLDINGS LAND CONDITIONS Wetlands, Slope, Flood Zone, Structures, and Seller Motivation Version 1.0 — April 2026

1. WETLANDS — NUANCED EVALUATION The Core Question: Can It Perc? Perc (percolation) determines whether a septic system can be installed. If the land cannot perc, it is a 50–80% value killer because the buyer cannot build a home with a septic system. You cannot determine perc from satellite — it requires a physical soil test. However, you can use proxy indicators to estimate likelihood:  Nearby homes on similar soil: If there are houses nearby on the same type of soil (check Land ID soil survey), those homes almost certainly passed a perc test. This is a strong indicator that your subject can likely perc too.  Dry spots within the property: If the property has identifiable dry, elevated areas (visible when you toggle hazards OFF and look at satellite), those areas may perc even if surrounding areas are wet.  100% wetlands with no nearby development: Very unlikely to perc. Price as hunting property / recreation only. Do NOT Blanket-Discount — Evaluate Lot by Lot Where Are The Wetlands? Wetlands Location Approximate Discount Reasoning Back of property, under 20% ~5% Minimal impact. Most of the property is buildable. Back of property, ~50% ~30% Significant portion unusable. But front is buildable. Front of property / near road 20% if dry buildable area exists behind Inconvenient — can't build near road. But may still be workable. Front and blocking access to building 50–80% Major problem. Can't build near road, may not perc. 100% wetlands (entire property) 30–40% of wetland comps Comp against similar wetland sales. Hunting/recreation only. Subdividing Partially Wetland Properties Wetlands can sometimes work IN your favor for subdivision. If wetlands are concentrated in the back and the front is dry, you can subdivide the parent parcel so that the front lots are clean

Partially Wetland Properties Wetlands can sometimes work IN your favor for subdivision. If wetlands are concentrated in the back and the front is dry, you can subdivide the parent parcel so that the front lots are clean buildable land (higher PPA) and the back portion is a larger, discounted lot. When subdividing a wetland property, value each potential lot INDIVIDUALLY. Each lot may have different amounts of wetlands, different buildable areas, different sizes, and different positions. Do not apply a blanket discount to the whole parcel — evaluate lot by lot. CRITICAL: Never subdivide a lot that cannot perc. If you are splitting a property and one of the resulting lots is all wetlands with no buildable area, do NOT create that lot. Nobody will buy a lot they cannot build on (unless it is large enough to market as hunting/recreation). You need to be reasonably confident each lot can perc before recommending it as a subdivision. 'Maybe' is not

good enough — you need confidence based on proxy indicators (nearby homes, dry areas, soil data). How To Evaluate  Check wetlands percentage in Land Insights  Toggle hazards OFF — look at where the actual buildable land is with your own eyes  Is there a clear buildable area near the road?  Look at nearby properties — are houses built on similar soil?  Note for investor: they may need to verify perc test potential with seller

2. SLOPE — WHEN IT MATTERS Slope % Discount Impact Under 10% None No issue. Price normally. 10–20% 5–10% Minor. Slight building challenges. 20–35% 15–30% Noticeable. Retaining walls, special foundations likely needed. 35%+ 30–50% Major. Building is very difficult and expensive. Significant discount. [!] Flag with hazard symbol if 20%+ slope. How To Check Slope Check contour lines on Land Insights. When contour lines are CLOSER TOGETHER, the slope is STEEPER. When they are far apart, the land is relatively flat. This is the quickest way to assess slope without visiting the property. 20% slope is the threshold where building starts getting problematic. Below 20%, most builders can handle it without special measures. Above 20%, you start needing retaining walls, special foundations, grading, and more expensive site prep. Above 35%, building becomes very expensive and sometimes impractical. Ravines, Valleys, and Low Spots Always flag ravines or valleys on the property. These are important for several reasons:  Creeks typically run through ravines — water flows to the lowest point  Cannot build in a ravine — too steep on the sides, water issues at the bottom  Limits buildable area — a 10-acre property with a ravine cutting through the middle may only have 6–7 buildable acres  Excellent negotiation point — the investor can point out that a significant portion of the property is unusable due to the ravine, strengthening a lower offer Always Compare Subject Slope to Comp Slopes Slope discounts are RELATIVE. If your subject has 25% slope but all the sold comps in the area also have 20–30% slope, the market has already priced in steep terrain. Do not double-discount. Only apply a slope discount when the subject is significantly steeper than the comps.

3. FLOOD ZONE — DON'T PANIC, LOOK AROUND FIRST [!] Do NOT panic-discount for flood zone. LOOK AROUND FIRST. The instinct when you see '100% flood zone' is to slash the value by 50%. This is wrong in many situations. Before applying any flood zone discount, LOOK AROUND the property:  Are people building houses in the area? If yes, flood zone is just a cost factor (flood insurance, raised foundation, engineered plans), NOT a value killer. Many coastal and riverfront communities are 100% flood zone and everyone builds there anyway.  Are there homes ON or near the subject property? If neighbors have homes in the same flood zone, the market has accepted the flood risk and priced it in.  What type of flood zone? Different designations carry different risk levels. Note the specific zone in your deliverable if visible. The Edisto Island Lesson Mary Seabrook Rd, Charleston Co, SC: 5.64 acres, 79% wetlands, 100% flood zone. Initial valuation was $75,000 — a 60–70% discount from clean comps. This was WRONG. The entire island is in a flood zone. Everyone builds there. Houses were visible on and near the property. The flood zone designation was not a deal-killer — it was just how that market works. Correct approach: recognize flood zone as a cost factor, look at what comparable properties actually sell for, and price accordingly. Process  Toggle hazards OFF to see actual property (structures, land type, water features)  Toggle ON to check flood/wetland zones  Always check for structures on or near the property  Note flood zone designation in deliverable  Let comps in the SAME flood zone guide pricing — if they sold at normal PPA, yours should too

4. STRUCTURES — COMP AS VACANT, FLAG FOR INVESTOR The Rule: Always Comp As Vacant Land A structure on a property could add $2,000 or $200,000 — it is impossible to know from satellite imagery. A dilapidated house could be a liability (demolition costs). A nice barn could be worth $60,000+. You cannot assess condition, foundation, utilities, or habitability from a bird's eye view. Therefore: ALWAYS comp the land as if the structure does not exist. Price it as vacant land. Then flag the structure with [!] so the investor knows to ask the seller about it. What To Note About Structures  That a structure exists — this is the most important thing  What it appears to be from satellite — house, barn, shed, mobile home, warehouse  Material if visible — metal (warehouse/barn) vs. wood (house/cabin)  Condition if visible from Street View — maintained vs. dilapidated  Note: 'Has structure — needs seller verification for specs and condition' Structures On Comps — Same Rule When a comp has a structure on it, apply the exact same logic: comp the land as vacant. Do NOT try to mentally subtract the structure's value from the comp's sold price. Just comp the vacant land portion. The structure on the comp is irrelevant to your vacant land valuation. [!] NEVER trust Land Insights structure data. Land Insights has shown '0 sqft structures' and 'Mobile Home: No' on properties where Street View clearly showed a singlewide mobile home. Always verify with Street View (mandatory), Zillow lookup, and Google Maps satellite. Trust your own eyes.

5. SELLER MOTIVATION SIGNALS — REPORT ALL OF THEM Every signal of seller motivation strengthens the investor's negotiation position. Report ALL of the following whenever found:  Ownership 5+ years: Land has likely appreciated since purchase. Owner may not know current value. 10+ years = even stronger signal.  Out of state/county: Owner is not using the land. Less emotional attachment. Maintaining from a distance is a hassle.  Family transfer (inherited): No emotional attachment — they did not buy it. Did not pay for it. Any sale feels like free money.  Tax delinquent: HUGE motivation signal. Always note the AMOUNT owed. Tax delinquency directly factors into offer strategy — the investor can point out the growing tax burden and use it as leverage for a lower offer. The more back taxes owed, the more motivated the seller.  Low cost basis: If the owner bought for $10,000 twenty years ago, any offer feels like significant profit. More room to negotiate. Already Listed Properties [!] Flag with hazard symbol if the property is already listed. If the seller already has the property listed with a realtor, they are more knowledgeable about pricing, have talked to a professional, and are psychologically anchored to their list price. This makes deep discounts much harder to achieve. Flag it prominently, include the asking price in the deliverable, and still provide the full valuation with offer ranges. Let the investor decide how to approach. This is one of the MOST important flags in the deliverable because it directly affects whether the deal is likely to convert. The investor needs to know this immediately. Zoning Flags Note the zoning code in every deliverable. Flag the following with [!] if found:  Conservation zoning — may restrict development entirely 

is likely to convert. The investor needs to know this immediately. Zoning Flags Note the zoning code in every deliverable. Flag the following with [!] if found:  Conservation zoning — may restrict development entirely  Agricultural-only zoning — may restrict residential use  Industrial zoning — different buyer pool, different market  Commercial zoning — different buyer pool, different market Properties Under 2 Acres Still in DewClaw's wheelhouse. Mark as a smaller lot and note it may be more of an infill or residential lot. The investor prefers 2+ acres but money can still be made on smaller lots. Not the primary focus, but do not skip them. Views / Elevation Premium  Mediocre view: ~5% premium  Good view: 10–15% premium  Superman/panoramic view: 20–30% premium

True great views are rare — and even when the property has elevation, you still need to build a road up, clear trees, and make a building pad. Flag potential views based on contour lines and elevation in Land Insights, but do not over-weight this factor. New Construction Nearby Active new construction within 1–2 miles of the subject is NOT a value-add. But flag it so the investor has awareness of development activity in the area. It provides context about market health and demand, even if it does not directly change the property's value. — End of Land Conditions Guide —
DEWCLAW LAND HOLDINGS PRICING, SUBDIVISION & OPERATIONS Conservative Pricing Philosophy, Margin Targets, Subdivision Analysis, and Workflow Standards Version 1.0 — April 2026

1. PRICING PHILOSOPHY — CONSERVATIVE AT EVERY STEP The goal of every valuation: when the analysis is complete, be 100% confident it is a deal. Build in buffer at every step. Better to slightly undervalue than overvalue. A conservative miss means the investor still makes money. An aggressive miss means the investor overpays. Offer Ranges Offer Level When to Use 50% of MV Target for solid deals. Most accurate. This is the standard offer. 60% of MV Mid-range. Slightly more aggressive. 70% of MV Only when very confident or motivated seller. Need clear margin. Above 70% RARE. Only on bigger deals ($100K+ acquisition) with guaranteed clear profit margin. Bigger Deal Margins — Real Examples 'Bigger deals' start at approximately $100,000+ acquisition cost. At this level, the percentage margins can be tighter because the dollar profit is larger. Here are real-world minimum margins the investor would accept at different buy prices: Buy Price Minimum Exit Price Margin Notes $100,000 ~$140,000 ~40% Very bottom. Would prefer $150K–$160K exit. $150,000 ~$200,000 ~33% Absolute bottom. Prefer higher. $170,000 ~$215,000 ~26% Getting tight. Need confidence in MV. $150,000 (high MV) ~$300,000–$310,000 ~100% Ideal bigger deal with strong margin. Still report deals even if margins look tight — the investor makes the final call. But these examples show the minimum thresholds to calibrate expectations. Whittling Down To A Number You will never land on an exact number — it is impossible. No two properties are identical. But through the full comp evaluation process (reading the whole book), you should be able to whittle down to a range of approximately $5,000. Within that $5,000 range, price on the LOWER end. Then apply the list price discount on top. That is how you ensure every deal has

g the whole book), you should be able to whittle down to a range of approximately $5,000. Within that $5,000 range, price on the LOWER end. Then apply the list price discount on top. That is how you ensure every deal has margin and every listing sells fast. Always Give Specific Prices Never give just ranges — always include a specific recommended value. Ranges are fine to show context, but the investor needs a concrete number to make offers. A range of $45K–$55K is useful, but '$50,000 market value' with 50%/60%/70% offers is actionable.

2. LIST PRICE STRATEGY Price 5–10% below calculated market value. This is not about hot vs. slow markets — the investor already prices conservatively enough that market conditions do not significantly change the discount. The 5–10% range ensures fast sales in all conditions. Elite drone marketing + competitive pricing = dominate the market. Realtors notice well-priced, well-marketed properties and bring their buyers. The combination of great photos and aggressive pricing creates a perception of a mega deal for buyers, which drives 1–2 week sales. Low DOM comps are your calibration points for list pricing. If a comp sold in 14 days at $45K, that is a realistic fast-sale price. Work backwards from there to set your list price and offer.

3. SUBDIVISION & VALUE-ADD ANALYSIS The Strategic Purpose Subdivision is the primary value-add play. It is the BACKUP strategy when the seller rejects the 50% offer on as-is value. If the property can be subdivided, the improved value may be 60–100%+ higher than as-is value. Offering 50% of the IMPROVED value might equal the seller's asking price — the seller gets what they want, and DewClaw still gets 50% margin on the value-add play. Example: As-is value $100K, offer $50K, seller says no and wants $80K. Subdivision potential identified: $160K after split. New offer: $80K (50% of $160K) = exactly what seller wanted. DewClaw still gets 50% margin. No Minimum Acreage Floor There is NO hard minimum for subdivision analysis. The old guideline of '15–20+ acres' does not apply as a rigid floor. Subdivision can be as simple as a lot split — dividing one parcel into two. Here is what is worth analyzing: Acreage Subdivision Potential Example 5 acres Worth checking Split into two 2.5-acre lots 10 acres 100% analyze Two 5s, four 2.5s, etc. 20 acres Strong candidate Two 10s, four 5s, etc. 40+ acres Almost always analyze Multiple configurations possible The PPA trendline tells you whether subdivision makes sense. If the curve is steep (exponential), splitting large lots into smaller ones creates significant value. If the curve is flat (linear), splitting does not create enough value to justify the hassle. Check Before You Recommend  PPA curve shape: Steep = subdivide opportunity. Flat = skip.  Road frontage: Enough to create separate legal access for each lot? Check county LDR.  Shape: Will splitting create bowling alley lots (ratio worse than 1:5 or 1:6)? If yes, no subdivision value.  Wetlands: Can each resulting lot perc? If not, don't create it.  County LDR: Check minimum

eck county LDR.  Shape: Will splitting create bowling alley lots (ratio worse than 1:5 or 1:6)? If yes, no subdivision value.  Wetlands: Can each resulting lot perc? If not, don't create it.  County LDR: Check minimum lot size, minimum road frontage per lot, plat-exempt thresholds, and any other requirements. Bowling Alley Lots — The Shape Rule Width-to-length ratio: Golden = 1:2 to 1:4. Maximum acceptable = 1:5 or 1:6. Anything skinnier is a 'bowling alley lot' and loses significant value. Buyers cannot build near the road because the lot is too narrow. Forced to go deep — long driveway, utilities run farther, building site far from road. Before proposing ANY subdivision, check if splitting creates bowling alley lots. If yes, there is NO value-add. Also consider whether the local market even supports long/skinny lot sales — too rural + too skinny = no buyer pool.

4. OPERATIONAL STANDARDS & WORKFLOW Target: 2.5 Minutes Per Property Every property evaluation — from first glance to final deliverable — should be completed in approximately 2.5 minutes. Speed comes from discipline, not shortcuts. Follow the workflow without deviation, narration, or mid-process questions. Work silently, deliver the final product. MCP Tab Management Close ALL MCP tabs after EVERY property evaluation automatically. The investor should NEVER have to close tabs manually. Delete the tab group completely so the browser does not lag or crash. This is a non-negotiable operational standard. Comp Listing Photos — Maximize Capture For listing photos on Redfin and Zillow, capture AS MANY as possible. These are not screenshots of Land Insights — these are the actual photos from the comp listings. Look for and capture:  Amenity photos (clubhouse, pool, trails — but verify these are community, not parcel)  Slope/terrain indicators  Cleared areas visible in photos  Plot maps / plat maps showing layout  Aerial photos showing property boundaries  Structures (barns, sheds, houses, mobile homes)  Road access and driveway condition More photos = more intel. Max out capture as long as it does not cause space/performance issues. Deliverable — 9 Sections, One Response The deliverable format is now 9 sections (updated from 8 — specific format coming via separate PDF merge). Deliver the ENTIRE analysis in ONE response. No 'continue' breaks. No mid-process narration. No asking the investor for permission to proceed. Hyperlinked Comps — Mandatory All comps referenced in the deliverable must be HYPERLINKED to the actual Redfin or Zillow listing. The investor needs to be able to click through and verify any comp independently. This is mandatory for every deliverable going

ps referenced in the deliverable must be HYPERLINKED to the actual Redfin or Zillow listing. The investor needs to be able to click through and verify any comp independently. This is mandatory for every deliverable going forward. Map Layers — Always Toggle  Keep hazards ON by default — helps see wetlands, flood, water  Toggle OFF only to verify a pond/creek looks clean or to see structures clearly  MLS Comps stay ON — don't toggle every time  Toggle ALL THREE map filters quickly to catch clear-cuts and changes over time  Navigate directly to zoom 11–12 URL for county comp view — don't scroll-zoom repeatedly

Seasonal Satellite Imagery Play it by ear. The three map filters exist to help understand temporal changes — if one layer shows bare trees (winter) and another shows full canopy (summer), that is seasonal variation, not a problem. Use all available imagery to build the most complete picture. If ambiguity remains about land type due to winter imagery, note it and verify with Street View. — End of Pricing, Subdivision & Operations Guide —

DewClaw — Part 9: Rural Property Evaluation FULLY EXPANDED — How Extreme Rural Location Affects Value Identifying, Flagging, and Pricing Properties in the Middle of Nowhere WHY RURAL EVALUATION MATTERS DewClaw operates in the rural recreational land market — every property the business evaluates is rural to some degree. That is the entire business model. But there is a massive difference between 'rural' and 'SUPER rural.' A property 15 minutes outside a small town on a maintained gravel road with houses scattered around is rural. A property 2 hours from the nearest town on an unmaintained dirt road with nothing but timber company land for miles in every direction is SUPER rural. That difference can impact value by 20-50%. The user identified this as an important area of evaluation because extreme rural isolation can KILL value on smaller properties. When a property is so far from everything that Google Maps Street View doesn't even cover the area, when there are no houses visible for miles on satellite, when the nearest town is 1-3 hours away — that level of isolation dramatically shrinks the buyer pool. Fewer buyers means less demand, which means lower prices and longer selling times. This is not a minor adjustment — it can be a 20-50% discount depending on severity. The frequency of encountering super rural properties is relatively low — the user estimated approximately 1 out of every 40-50 properties. But when you do encounter one, you need to recognize it immediately, flag it appropriately, and price it correctly. Missing the rural discount on a super rural property means overpricing it, which means it sits on the market unsold. Recognizing it means pricing it to sell within the 7-90 day target. HOW TO IDENTIFY SUPER RURAL PROPERTIES The Three Visual Indicators

super rural property means overpricing it, which means it sits on the market unsold. Recognizing it means pricing it to sell within the 7-90 day target. HOW TO IDENTIFY SUPER RURAL PROPERTIES The Three Visual Indicators When the user looks at a property and thinks 'this is super rural,' there are three things that immediately signal it: 1. Not many houses around it: When you zoom out on Land Insights or Google Maps and look at the surrounding area, there are very few or NO houses visible on satellite. Instead, you see vast stretches of timber, empty land, or large agricultural operations. The absence of residential development is the clearest signal that this area has very low demand for smaller residential lots. People are not building here. That tells you something important about the market. 2. Nothing but big properties everywhere: The surrounding parcels are all LARGE — 50, 100, 200+ acres owned by timber companies, farming operations, or long-term landowners. There are no small 5-10 acre lots with homes. No subdivisions, no neighborhoods, no development. When every surrounding property is massive, it tells you two things: first, the area is deeply rural with no development pressure. Second, buyers in this area want BIG properties, not small ones. If your subject is a small 10-acre lot surrounded by 200-acre timber tracts, it is a small fish in a big pond — there is very little demand for small lots this far out. 3. It is just FAR from everything: When you zoom out on the map, the property is a long, long way from any town, city, or population center. The user described this as properties that are 1-3 hours from the nearest town. States like Oklahoma, Kentucky, Michigan, and other deeply rural states have areas where you can drive for hours and see nothing but

center. The user described this as properties that are 1-3 hours from the nearest town. States like Oklahoma, Kentucky, Michigan, and other deeply rural states have areas where you can drive for hours and see nothing but trees and farmland. The distance from services, convenience, and civilization itself reduces the property's appeal to most buyers. Additional Indicators of Extreme Rural Isolation Google Maps Street View doesn't exist there: This is a particularly telling indicator. Google Street View cars have covered most roads in the United States. If Street View DOESN'T exist on the roads near the property, it means those roads are so remote, so lightly traveled, and so far from anything that Google didn't bother sending a car there. When this happens, note it — it is a strong signal that the area is extremely isolated. You will need to rely entirely on satellite imagery for visual assessment because ground-level verification is not available. Road quality in super rural areas: In extremely rural areas, roads are often gravel or dirt, and they may NOT be well-maintained. The county or local government has limited resources and prioritizes roads closer to population centers. A dirt road 2 hours from

town might have potholes, washouts, and be borderline impassable in bad weather. The user specifically noted: 'It's so rural that they don't even really maintain it.' Poor road maintenance compounds the rural discount because it makes the property harder and more unpleasant to access — every time you visit your property, your car gets dirty and beat up driving through a rough unmaintained road. That is a real negative for most buyers. The 'zoom out' test: The user specifically said to ZOOM OUT on the map so you can see the area context. It doesn't take complex analysis to see 'wow, this is in the middle of nowhere.' When you zoom out and the property is surrounded by nothing but green (timber) or brown (farmland) for miles in every direction with no towns, no development, no population centers visible — that is super rural. The visual impression from a zoomed-out map is actually one of the most reliable indicators.

HOW EXTREME RURAL LOCATION AFFECTS VALUE The Rural Discount: 20-50% Depending on Severity The user identified a discount range of 20-50% for super rural properties. The exact discount within that range depends on several factors: How far from the nearest town: A property that is 30-45 minutes from a small town is rural but manageable — maybe 10-15% rural discount at most. A property that is 1-2 hours from the nearest town of any size is significantly isolated — 20-30% discount. A property that is 2-3+ hours from any town, deep in timber country or mountain wilderness with nothing around — 35-50% discount. The further from civilization, the smaller the buyer pool and the deeper the discount. What the surrounding area looks like: If the property is rural but there are SOME houses scattered around, some signs of human habitation and land use, the area shows SOME demand for residential use. Discount on the lower end (20-25%). If the property is surrounded by nothing but large timber tracts and empty land with zero residential development visible for miles — nobody is living here, nobody WANTS to live here — discount on the higher end (35-50%). Road quality and access: Even within super rural areas, road quality varies. A property on a maintained gravel road that is reasonably smooth and accessible year-round is better than a property on an unmaintained seasonal dirt road that turns to mud in rain and might be impassable in winter. The user noted that road maintenance in super rural areas is often poor because local governments don't prioritize remote roads. If the access road is particularly bad — deep ruts, no maintenance, barely a two-track — that compounds the rural discount because every visit to the property is an unpleasant, car-damaging experience. Small Acreage in

. If the access road is particularly bad — deep ruts, no maintenance, barely a two-track — that compounds the rural discount because every visit to the property is an unpleasant, car-damaging experience. Small Acreage in Super Rural Areas — A Particular Problem The user specifically highlighted a critical nuance: SMALL acreage properties in super rural areas are especially problematic. The reasoning is logical: people who want to live or recreate in extremely rural areas typically want BIG properties. They want 50, 100, 200+ acres for hunting, farming, timber, or just having vast private space. That is the whole point of being out in the middle of nowhere — you want SPACE. A small 5-10 acre lot in the middle of super rural country surrounded by 200-acre tracts does not appeal to this buyer pool. The rural buyer wants the big property next door, not the small lot. And the suburban buyer who might want 5-10 acres for a homesite doesn't want to be 2 hours from the nearest town. The property falls into a gap between two buyer pools — too small for rural buyers, too remote for suburban buyers. This gap shrinks the buyer pool dramatically and can push the discount toward the higher end (40-50%). Conversely, LARGER acreage in super rural areas is less problematic because the property actually matches what rural buyers want. A 100-acre hunting property deep in the woods is exactly what a certain buyer is looking for. They WANT the isolation, they WANT the big acreage, and they are willing to drive 2 hours to get to their hunting camp. For these larger properties, the rural discount is smaller (maybe 15-25%) because the property matches the buyer pool's preferences. The user said: 'If it's a little bit bigger, you don't really need to discount it too much. Just know that it's

ies, the rural discount is smaller (maybe 15-25%) because the property matches the buyer pool's preferences. The user said: 'If it's a little bit bigger, you don't really need to discount it too much. Just know that it's rural.' Signs That Indicate 'This Area Wants Big Properties' When you look at a super rural area on the map and see that all surrounding properties are large (50-200+ acres), that is a market signal. It tells you: 1) The area is deeply rural with no development pressure pushing toward smaller lots. 2) Buyers in this area prefer and purchase large tracts, not small lots. 3) If your subject is significantly smaller than the surrounding properties, it is mismatched with the local demand. 4) Subdivision of a large property into small lots would likely NOT work here because there is no demand for small lots this far out. This connects directly to the subdivision framework: one of the key rules is 'check what lot sizes are selling in the area.' In a super rural area, the answer is 'big lots' — which means creating small lots through subdivision would likely fail. Flag this in the deliverable so the user doesn't pursue a subdivision play that has no market.

COMPING SUPER RURAL PROPERTIES When Comps Are Also Super Rural — Use Them If you find comps in the same super rural area, and those comps are ALSO in equally remote, equally isolated conditions — those are GOOD COMPS. They reflect the market reality of that specific area. The buyers who purchased those comps already factored in the isolation, the poor road access, and the distance from town. Their purchase prices reflect the actual market value of super rural land in that area. In this situation, you do NOT need to apply an additional rural discount on top of these comps. The comps already ARE rural and already reflect the rural market. Just comp normally using those rural comps as your data set. This is the same logic as comping flood zone properties in an area where everything is flood zone — the condition is already baked into the market data. When Your Subject Is MORE Rural Than the Comps If you find comps in the broader county but your subject is significantly MORE rural than those comps — for example, the comps are near a small town 20 minutes away but your subject is deep in timber country 2 hours from anything — then you DO need to discount from those comps. The comps reflect a less-isolated market than where your subject sits. Apply the rural discount based on how much more isolated your subject is compared to the comps. Sparse Comps in Super Rural Areas Super rural areas frequently have very sparse comp data. There just aren't many land transactions happening in the middle of nowhere. When comps are sparse: widen your acreage range aggressively (AUTO, then 50% below / 200% above, then wider if needed). Use active listings as ceiling references with appropriate discounts (20-30%). Build the best story you can from available data. Note in the deliverable that

ely (AUTO, then 50% below / 200% above, then wider if needed). Use active listings as ceiling references with appropriate discounts (20-30%). Build the best story you can from available data. Note in the deliverable that comp data is limited due to the rural nature of the area. The user's guidance on sparse comps applies especially here: 'Keep on looking until you actually have comps so that you can build out a storyline.' Don't deliver a valuation with zero comp support even in super rural areas. Widen the range, use the PPA trendline, use active listings — find SOMETHING to anchor your story to. Road Access in Super Rural Areas — Flag for User to Verify In super rural areas, road quality is a major concern that you often CANNOT verify from satellite or even from Street View (which may not exist). The user specifically said to flag road access so they can triple-check and confirm it themselves. They will be talking to the seller and can ask about road conditions directly. In your deliverable, note: '■■ Super rural area — road access and condition should be verified with seller. Road maintenance may be limited due to remote location.' Also put road access verification in Section 9 (Questions to Ask Seller). The user also clarified that you should COMP the property assuming normal/good access. The rural discount you apply is for the ISOLATION (distance from everything, limited demand), not for bad road access. If the road turns out to be terrible, the user will adjust further based on their conversation with the seller. Don't double-discount by applying both a rural discount AND a bad road discount when you don't actually know the road condition.

SUPER RURAL PROPERTIES AS HUNTING LAND This connects to the wetlands evaluation section, but the user expanded on it specifically in the context of rural properties. Super rural properties — especially larger ones with significant wetlands, timber, or natural habitat — can be marketed as HUNTING PROPERTIES. Hunters don't care about isolation. In fact, they PREFER it. Isolation means privacy, quiet, less human interference with wildlife, and a better hunting experience. When a super rural property butts up to STATE LAND, NATIONAL FOREST, or WILDLIFE MANAGEMENT AREAS, this is actually a POSITIVE for hunting buyers. They can hunt on the adjacent public land as well as their own property, effectively expanding their hunting area. The user said this adds maybe 2-3% to the value — not a huge premium, but a positive feature to note. Wetlands on super rural properties are not always a negative for the hunting buyer pool. Wetlands attract DUCKS, WATERFOWL, and other WILDLIFE because there is more water and habitat. Hunters actually PREFER wetlands for this reason — marshy swamp land that is terrible for building is IDEAL for duck hunting. The user specifically said: 'Don't always throw out wetlands. Hunters don't really care. They could obviously walk on that stuff. They actually prefer it because then that could attract ducks and a lot more wildlife.' However — and this is critical — while the hunting buyer pool values these features, it is the ONLY buyer pool. You cannot build a house on 100% wetlands. You cannot install septic. The property has essentially ONE use case (hunting/recreation) and ONE buyer pool (hunters/outdoor enthusiasts). To sell it within the 7-90 day target, you need to price it conservatively because the buyer pool is limited. The property can sell —

ly ONE use case (hunting/recreation) and ONE buyer pool (hunters/outdoor enthusiasts). To sell it within the 7-90 day target, you need to price it conservatively because the buyer pool is limited. The minimum lot size for a marketable hunting property remains approximately 20 acres. Below 20 acres of remote hunting land, there is very little buyer interest — it's not enough area to hunt meaningfully. For super rural hunting properties, bigger is generally better: 40, 80, 100+ acres of remote wilderness with wildlife habitat is genuinely attractive to serious hunters. THE CONVENIENCE FACTOR — WHY RURAL DISCOUNTS EXIST The user explained the fundamental reason why rural properties are discounted compared to properties closer to town. It boils down to CONVENIENCE. Every time the property owner wants to visit their land, they have to DRIVE there. If it's 15 minutes from town, that's easy — a quick trip. If it's 2 hours from town on rough dirt roads, that is a significant commitment of time and effort. Every single visit. The user described the experience vividly: driving deep into a rural area on a long dirt road that goes on and on, getting your car dirty, bouncing through ruts and rough spots. That experience is NOT convenient and it is NOT pleasant for most people. Some people love it — they enjoy the journey to their remote property as part of the experience. But the MAJORITY of buyers prefer easier access to their property. And as the user said: 'The majority makes the rules, and that's how the market is made.' This convenience factor is the same underlying principle that drives proximity premiums near cities and towns (covered in Part 5). It's all on

user said: 'The majority makes the rules, and that's how the market is made.' This convenience factor is the same underlying principle that drives proximity premiums near cities and towns (covered in Part 5). It's all on a spectrum: downtown city = maximum convenience, maximum price. City suburbs = high convenience, high price. Rural recreational ring (10-15 min from city edge) = moderate convenience, moderate price. Standard rural (30-60 min from town) = lower convenience, lower price. Super rural (1-3+ hours from anything) = minimal convenience, deeply discounted price. The rural discount is just the far end of the convenience spectrum. FLAGGING SUPER RURAL PROPERTIES IN YOUR DELIVERABLE When you identify a property as super rural, flag it prominently in your deliverable. The user wants to know about this so they can factor it into their offer strategy and their conversation with the seller. Here is how to flag it: In Extra Notes (Section 6): Use the hazard emoji and a specific note: '■■ VERY RURAL — property is approximately [X] miles / [X] minutes from the nearest town of [town name]. Surrounding area shows minimal residential development. Large timber/agricultural tracts dominate the area. Limited buyer pool for smaller acreage at this level of isolation. Road access and condition should be verified with seller.' In Questions to Ask Seller (Section 9): Add: 'Verify road access and condition — is the access road maintained year-round? Is it passable in all weather? How far is the nearest paved road?' This lets the user get ground-level intelligence about the access situation that you cannot determine from satellite.

In the valuation itself: Note that the PPA reflects a rural discount. If your comps are from less rural areas, explain the adjustment: 'Comps from the [town name] area trade at ~$X,XXX/ac. Subject is significantly more isolated (~X miles/minutes further from town) — applied X% rural isolation discount to reflect limited demand at this level of remoteness.' Distance to nearest town: The user said you can note the approximate distance to the nearest town if it doesn't take too much time. A quick check on the map — zoom out, identify the nearest town, estimate driving distance — takes 5-10 seconds and provides useful context. Don't estimate distance to nearest house — the user noted that houses can be hidden under tree canopy and the estimate wouldn't be accurate. SUPER RURAL — DOES NOT MEAN SKIP THE PROPERTY An important clarification: identifying a property as super rural does NOT mean the property is a bad deal or should be skipped. It means the property needs to be PRICED CORRECTLY for its isolation level. The DewClaw business model works in any market — including super rural markets — as long as the property is acquired at the right price. If a super rural property has an as-is value of $50,000 (after the rural discount), and the user can acquire it at 50% ($25,000), that is still a profitable flip. The property will sell — there are buyers for rural recreational land at every price point. It might take a bit longer to find the right buyer (hunting enthusiast, cabin builder, recreational landowner), and the buyer pool is smaller, but with aggressive pricing and elite drone marketing, it will sell within the target timeframe. The user's business model is specifically designed for rural recreational land. Super rural properties are not outside the business model — they

ricing and elite drone marketing, it will sell within the target timeframe. The user's business model is specifically designed for rural recreational land. Super rural properties are not outside the business model — they are a subset of it. The key is accurate pricing that reflects the isolation, not avoiding these properties altogether.
