DewClaw — Part 10: Advanced Subdivision & Hidden Value FULLY EXPANDED — Identifying Hidden Subdivision Plays, Road-Building Math, Flagpole Lot Opportunities, and Questions for Future Screenshot Training HIDDEN VALUE IN SUBDIVISION — THE CORE CONCEPT The most profitable subdivision deals are the ones that other people MISS. Most investors look at a property and see one thing — a single parcel to flip. They calculate the as-is value, make an offer at 50%, and move on. They completely miss the fact that the property could be subdivided into 5, 8, or even 10+ lots that are each worth significantly more per acre than the whole parcel. That oversight is where DewClaw makes the real money. The user specifically emphasized that these opportunities 'hide in plain sight.' A property with three narrow access points from a road looks unremarkable to most people — they see weird entry points and don't think anything of it. The user sees three flagpole lot opportunities that could triple the property's total value. A large property with enough road frontage looks like a normal big lot to most people. The user sees 8 individual lots that each sell at a higher PPA than the whole parcel. This advanced subdivision thinking is what separates the DewClaw approach from 99% of other land flippers who only do simple flips. FLAGPOLE LOTS FROM HISTORICAL LOT SPLITS — HIDDEN IN PLAIN SIGHT One of the most important hidden value plays the user taught is recognizing flagpole lot opportunities that were CREATED by previous owners without anyone realizing it. Here is how this happens: A property owner once had a large parcel — say 60 acres. Over the years, they sold off smaller lots from the edges of their property. Maybe they sold a 5-acre lot on the left side to a neighbor. Then a 3-acre lot on

ens: A property owner once had a large parcel — say 60 acres. Over the years, they sold off smaller lots from the edges of their property. Maybe they sold a 5-acre lot on the left side to a neighbor. Then a 3-acre lot on the right side to another buyer. Then a 7-acre lot off the back. Each time they sold a piece, the remaining property's shape changed. What was once a clean rectangle became an irregular shape with narrow access strips where the sold lots used to connect to the road. Now the remaining 45 acres has three narrow entry points — maybe 50-60 feet wide each — where the sold-off lots created gaps along the road frontage. To most people looking at this property on a map, these narrow strips look like odd remnants of the original parcel. Nobody thinks twice about them. They are just weird shape features. But the user sees SUBDIVISION OPPORTUNITIES. Each of those narrow access strips IS a potential flagpole lot access point. Three 50-foot-wide access strips = three flagpole lots, each extending from the road back into the larger buildable area of the property. Instead of selling one 45-acre parcel at the large-lot PPA, you sell three ~15-acre lots at a significantly higher per-acre price. The total revenue could be 50-100%+ more than selling the whole parcel. The user was very clear: 'These are the things that are hidden in plain sight, but people don't even see that, just like you don't see it. So now you're starting to connect the dots — oh wow, if I can identify it, then imagine how many people don't, and how much money we could capitalize on.' This is the competitive advantage. Identifying these plays is how DewClaw finds deals that nobody else sees. How to Spot Historical Lot Splits on the Map When looking at a property on Land Insights, look for these

e on.' This is the competitive advantage. Identifying these plays is how DewClaw finds deals that nobody else sees. How to Spot Historical Lot Splits on the Map When looking at a property on Land Insights, look for these visual clues that suggest previous lot splits created flagpole opportunities: Narrow strips of the parent parcel touching the road: If the property has one or more thin extensions reaching out to the road, separated by other parcels on either side, those thin extensions are likely remnants from when the owner sold off the lots on either side. Each thin extension is a potential flagpole access strip. Multiple small access points instead of one wide frontage: If the property touches the road in multiple places with gaps in between (where other people's parcels front the road), each of those access points is a flagpole entry. Count them — each one could be a separate lot. Neighboring parcels that were clearly carved out of a larger parent: Look at the surrounding parcels. If they look like they were pieces of a once-larger property (similar orientations, irregular shapes that fit together like puzzle pieces), the remaining property likely has flagpole access strips from the carve-outs.

ROAD-BUILDING MATH — THE COST-TO-REVENUE FORMULA The Correct Formula When evaluating whether a road-building subdivision play makes financial sense, the formula is: TOTAL INVESTMENT COST = Acquisition price + Road construction + Utilities (water, sewer, electricity if needed) + Any other development costs TOTAL REVENUE = Sum of all individual lot sale prices after subdivision The total investment cost should be approximately 50-60% of the total revenue. This means for every dollar you spend on acquisition and development, you should get approximately $1.67-$2.00 back in lot sales. That is a healthy spread that makes the deal worth the time, effort, complexity, and risk of doing a subdivision with road construction. On BIGGER properties where the absolute dollar amounts are larger, the acceptable ratio can stretch to 65-70%. The reason: even at 65-70% cost-to-revenue, the DOLLAR SPREAD on a large deal can be very significant. If total investment is $350,000 and total revenue is $500,000 at 70% ratio, the profit is $150,000. That is worth doing even though the percentage margin is thinner than the ideal 50-60% range. It is all about how much spread (profit in dollars) actually exists. How to Calculate and Present This When you identify a subdivision play that involves road construction or utility installation, calculate and present the math explicitly in the deliverable: Step 1: Determine the as-is value of the whole property and calculate your offer price (50% of as-is). This is the ACQUISITION COST. Step 2: Estimate the road construction cost. Look up gravel road cost per linear foot in the county/state. Measure the road distance using the Land Insights ruler tool. Multiply linear feet × cost per foot = estimated road cost. Step 3: Note any utility costs if applicable

gravel road cost per linear foot in the county/state. Measure the road distance using the Land Insights ruler tool. Multiply linear feet × cost per foot = estimated road cost. Step 3: Note any utility costs if applicable (water, sewer, electric hookups). These vary significantly by location — note them as items to investigate rather than precise estimates unless you can find local cost data. Step 4: Pull comps at EACH specific lot size you are proposing. If the subdivision creates four 10-acre lots and two 7-acre lots, pull comps for both 10ac and 7ac sizes separately because each size has its own PPA. Step 5: Calculate TOTAL REVENUE by multiplying each lot's estimated PPA by its acreage, then summing all lots. Step 6: Calculate the cost-to-revenue ratio: (Acquisition + Road + Utilities) ÷ Total Revenue × 100 = X%. Present this percentage. Step 7: Present it clearly: 'Acquisition at 50%: $150K + Road: $20K + Utilities: $5K = $175K total investment. Total revenue from 6 lots: $340K. Cost-to-revenue ratio: 51%. Spread: $165K.' If the ratio is 50-60% = strong deal, recommend it. If 60-70% = workable on bigger properties, present it with a note about the thinner margin. If above 70% = the spread probably isn't worth the complexity, but still present the numbers and let the user decide. Roads Can Curve Around Features A subdivision road does NOT have to be a straight line from one end of the property to the other. Roads can CURVE around features that would be expensive or impractical to cross: wetlands (building a road through wetlands requires expensive fill and permitting), steep slopes (road grade has limits — too steep and vehicles can't safely use it), timber stands (might be cheaper to go around mature timber than to clear a path through it), creeks and streams

ive fill and permitting), steep slopes (road grade has limits — too steep and vehicles can't safely use it), timber stands (might be cheaper to go around mature timber than to clear a path through it), creeks and streams (crossing requires a bridge or culvert). When a road needs to cross a wet area or small stream, a CULVERT can be installed. A culvert is a pipe or tunnel under the road that allows water to flow through while the road passes over it. Culverts are a standard construction element for rural roads — they cost more than just grading a dirt road on dry ground, but they are a proven, routine solution. The cost of culverts should be factored into your road cost estimate. When estimating road cost for a curved road: measure the ACTUAL path the road would take (including curves) using the Land Insights ruler tool, not just the straight-line distance. A road that curves around a wetland area might be 30-50% longer than a straight shot through it, but the additional road cost is likely much less than the cost of filling wetlands and getting environmental permits.

LOT VALUE DIFFERENTIATION WITHIN A SUBDIVISION Not every lot in a subdivision is worth the same amount. When you create multiple lots from a parent parcel, each lot should be valued based on its SPECIFIC traits — just like comping any individual property. The key factors that create value differences between lots in the same subdivision: Road Type Matters Per Lot If the parent property has one side on a PAVED road and another side bordering a DIRT road, the lots fronting the paved road are worth MORE than the lots fronting the dirt road. This is exactly the same as comping any normal property — road type affects value. When designing the lot configuration, put the PREMIUM lots on the paved road side (these will sell faster and at higher PPA) and the DISCOUNTED lots on the dirt road side (these will take slightly longer and sell at lower PPA). Interior lots fronting a NEW private road (the road you built through the property) typically sell for 5-10% LESS than lots fronting the existing public road. This mirrors the general road hierarchy — a new gravel private road is not as desirable as an established public road. Flagpole Lots vs Standard Lots Flagpole lots — lots with a narrow access strip leading to a wider buildable area in the back — are worth LESS than standard lots with full road frontage. How much less depends on the DEPTH of the flagpole: Shallow flagpole (short access strip, buildable area is close to the road): SLIGHTLY less than a standard lot — maybe 5-10% discount. The access strip is short, so getting to the buildable area is quick and convenient. The inconvenience is minimal. Deep flagpole (long access strip, buildable area is far from the road): Essentially the same value as a lot on a dirt road because the buyer needs a LONG driveway through the

and convenient. The inconvenience is minimal. Deep flagpole (long access strip, buildable area is far from the road): Essentially the same value as a lot on a dirt road because the buyer needs a LONG driveway through the access strip to reach their building site. It is functionally the same experience as driving down a long dirt road to reach a property. The user specifically said: 'Normally, when a flagpole lot is super deep, you actually need a road going back into it. And normally, if it's not developed and it's just vacant land, it's normally just a dirt road to go into there, so that's essentially the exact same thing as a normal lot on a dirt road.' This means: when designing a subdivision with flagpole lots, the shallower the flagpole, the more value it retains. If you can create flagpole lots where the access strip is short and the buildable area is relatively close to the road, the value penalty is small. If the flagpole forces a very long access road, the lot drops to dirt-road-lot pricing. Land Type Differences Per Lot If the parent property has different land types in different areas — cleared farmland on one side and timber on the other, pasture at the front and wetlands in the back — split along those natural boundaries to create lots with DISTINCT land types that appeal to DIFFERENT buyer pools. A cleared farmland lot appeals to agricultural buyers and homebuilders. A timbered lot appeals to recreational/hunting buyers. A wetlands lot appeals to duck hunters (if 20+ acres). Each lot type has its own PPA and its own buyer pool. Pull comps at each lot size AND for each land type. The cleared 10ac lot on the paved road might be worth $8,000/ac while the timbered 10ac lot on the dirt road side might be worth $4,000/ac. These differences should be reflected

s at each lot size AND for each land type. The cleared 10ac lot on the paved road might be worth $8,000/ac while the timbered 10ac lot on the dirt road side might be worth $4,000/ac. These differences should be reflected in your per-lot valuations. Wetlands Per Lot After Splitting As covered extensively in Part 2 (Wetlands), lot-splitting a wetlands property creates cleaner-looking individual lots even though the total wetlands haven't changed. A lot with 10% wetlands in the back looks much better than a whole parcel with 50% wetlands throughout. When calculating per-lot values, assess the wetlands impact on EACH lot individually: the clean lot gets near-full value with minor discount, the wetlands lot gets priced as hunting/recreational land.

PULLING COMPS AT EACH TARGET LOT SIZE — PRECISION MATTERS When presenting a subdivision value-add scenario, you need comps at EACH SPECIFIC LOT SIZE you are proposing. This is critical because the PPA curve is exponential — every acreage point on the curve has a different PPA. You cannot use a 20-acre comp's PPA to estimate the value of an 8-acre lot. The 8-acre lot has a significantly higher PPA because it is on a different point on the curve. If your subdivision creates lots of different sizes — for example, four 10-acre lots along the road and two 7-acre flagpole lots — you need TWO sets of comps: one set showing what 10-acre lots trade at in this market, and another set showing what 7-acre lots trade at. Each lot size might have a different PPA, and your total revenue calculation needs to reflect those differences. How to do this simultaneously with the as-is comp process: when you zoom out on Land Insights and see the comp dots, you are already seeing a range of acreages. Note the PPAs at your target lot sizes as you scan. If the MLS comp filter allows acreage range adjustment, change the range to focus on your target lot size. Alternatively, use the comp data you already see on the map to interpolate — if 5ac comps trade at $10K/ac and 15ac comps trade at $6K/ac, your 8ac lots should be around $8K/ac on the curve. WHEN ROAD-BUILDING MAKES SENSE VS WHEN IT DOESN'T Road-building makes sense when the SPREAD between total investment and total revenue is significant enough to justify the additional cost, time, and complexity. The user's threshold: total investment (acquisition + road + utilities) should be approximately 50-60% of total revenue from lot sales. On bigger properties, up to 65-70% because the dollar profit is still large. When Road-Building MAKES Sense

otal investment (acquisition + road + utilities) should be approximately 50-60% of total revenue from lot sales. On bigger properties, up to 65-70% because the dollar profit is still large. When Road-Building MAKES Sense Large acreage with strong demand for smaller lots in the area AND road frontage alone can't support enough lots. If you have 80 acres with 500ft of road frontage, you might get 5 lots from the frontage alone. But building a 1,200ft gravel road through the middle could give you 12+ lots (5 from the road frontage plus 7 interior lots). If the road costs $30K but the additional 7 lots generate $350K in revenue, the math overwhelmingly works. The key indicators: property has significantly more acreage than the road frontage can serve. Surrounding lot sizes are relatively small (5-10ac) indicating strong subdivision demand. The PPA curve is steep (big difference between large-lot PPA and small-lot PPA). County ordinances allow the number of lots you are proposing. When Road-Building DOESN'T Make Sense Small properties where the road cost eats too much of the margin. If you have 20 acres and a road costs $25K but only creates 2 additional lots worth $30K total, the math doesn't work — $25K cost for $30K additional revenue is razor-thin. Areas where there is no demand for smaller lots. If surrounding properties are all 40+ acres and nobody is buying small lots, building a road to create small lots is building a product nobody wants. Properties with terrain that makes road construction prohibitively expensive — steep slopes, deep wetlands that need extensive fill, rocky terrain that needs blasting. The road cost can balloon beyond what the lot revenue supports. When the cost-to-revenue ratio exceeds 70% even on a big property. At that point, the spread is too

need extensive fill, rocky terrain that needs blasting. The road cost can balloon beyond what the lot revenue supports. When the cost-to-revenue ratio exceeds 70% even on a big property. At that point, the spread is too thin to justify the complexity, time, and risk of road construction. A simple flip is safer and faster.

I. BUSINESS MODEL & CORE STRATEGY Buy vacant rural land at ~50% of market value. Sell slightly below market with elite drone marketing. Target 7–90 day sales (~2 weeks average). Primary markets: Mid-US to East Coast. Focus: Rural recreational land. The Strategic Purpose of Comping Primary goal: Know the market value so we can offer 50 cents on the dollar. Backup strategy: Value-add plays. If the seller says NO to the 50% offer on as-is value, we may have identified a value-add (subdivision potential, etc.). We pitch a DIFFERENT offer based on the value-add — 50% of the improved value — which might equal the seller's ask. The seller doesn't realize the upside; we unlock it. Example:  As-is market value: $100K → Offer $50K → Seller says NO, wants $80K  Subdivision potential identified: $160K after split  New pitch: Offer $80K (50% of $160K) = exactly what seller wanted  We still get 50% margin on the VALUE-ADD play Why we always look for value-adds: Even if we don't need it for the primary offer, it's the BACKUP strategy when sellers say no. Most sellers don't know their property can be subdivided. We unlock value they didn't know existed.

II. EFFICIENCY WORKFLOW & SOP [!] TARGET: 2.5 MINUTES PER PROPERTY — COMPLETE EVALUATION Every property evaluation — from first glance to final deliverable — should be completed in approximately 2.5 minutes. This is the standard operating pace. Speed comes from discipline, not shortcuts. Follow the workflow below without deviation, narration, or mid-process questions. Work silently, deliver the final product. Land Insights Speed Workflow  Summary tab: Last purchase price, acreage, APN  Parcel tab: Land use classification  Owner tab: Out-of-state/county status + ownership length  Immediately go to MLS comps. Don't linger. Land ID Simultaneous Workflow While pulling Land Insights data, simultaneously open Land ID:  AI Smart Search (blue button) → Parcel → ID → State/County/APN → Soils  Copy URL, paste in Extra Notes as 'Land ID link'  Backup: If APN doesn't aggregate, use Owner Name instead  If Land ID is blocked, note it and skip — don't waste time Street View — MANDATORY [!] Drag pegman onto Street View for EVERY property. No exceptions.  Look for: entryways/driveways, cleared building spots, mobile homes, sheds, structures  Wet areas/standing water, power lines, fencing  Note ALL findings in Extra Notes Satellite Verification Always look at actual satellite imagery on Google Maps AND Land Insights with filters OFF. Never trust filters alone. Trust your own eyes always. Map Layer Rules  Hazards: Keep ON by default. Only toggle OFF to verify clean pond/creek or see structures clearly.  MLS Comps: Stay ON. Don't toggle every time.  Acreage Range: Always click AUTO (not Manual). If need more comps: below=80%, above=150%. Do NOT go into Comp Report — use map view. Navigation & Speed  Navigate directly to zoom 11–12 URL for county comp view — do NOT

Acreage Range: Always click AUTO (not Manual). If need more comps: below=80%, above=150%. Do NOT go into Comp Report — use map view. Navigation & Speed  Navigate directly to zoom 11–12 URL for county comp view — do NOT scroll-zoom 4–5 times  Minimize screenshots — one for property data/insights, one at parcel zoom for land type/water, one at county zoom for comps. Three to four is enough.

 CLOSE ALL MCP TABS after EVERY comp. Delete the tab group completely so browser doesn't lag or crash.  Deliver full 8-section analysis in ONE response. NO 'continue' breaks. No mid-process narration. No asking user for permission. Timing Breakdown (2.5 Minutes Total)  Initial Assessment: ~45 seconds — Pull property in Land Insights, check acreage/APN/owner/flags, satellite + Street View  Comp Process: ~75 seconds — Pull comps county-wide, find anchor/floor/ceiling, verify key comps  Final Valuation & Deliverable: ~30 seconds — Weight comps, calculate MV, generate offer range, write deliverable

III. LAND TYPE IDENTIFICATION Farmland (Yielding Asset — Worth More) Actively farmed with visible crop rows, irrigation patterns, tilled soil, distinct field boundaries. This is a YIELDING ASSET — it produces income. The price of the land PLUS the value of the agricultural production is baked in. Worth significantly MORE than cleared or pasture land. Always identify and call out farmland — it commands a premium that other land types do not get. If you see crop rows from satellite, that is farmland. If there is an active farm lease, factor that into value. Farmers usually know what land is worth. Harder to get at deep discount because of income potential. Warn the investor that the seller may be more savvy on pricing. Pasture Land Open grass with fencing visible, possibly cattle trails, watering areas, livestock signs. Maintained but not actively cultivated. Worth less than farmland but more than raw timbered land. Key identifiers: fencing (visible on satellite), grazed grass areas (not just mowed), sometimes visible livestock or water troughs. Maintained Cleared Land — THE TRICKY ONE [!] This distinction is worth thousands of dollars in valuation accuracy. Green, flat, mowed/maintained but NOT farmed and NOT grazed. No crop rows, no fencing, no livestock. Looks like farmland from satellite but ISN'T — no yielding asset premium. From satellite it can LOOK exactly like farmland because it is open and green. When confused, ALWAYS use Google Street View or Google Earth to confirm actual land use. Satellite alone can be extremely misleading. Timbered Land Mature tree cover with value in standing timber. Dense, uniform canopy, established growth. Standing timber can add $2,000–$15,000+ depending on species, maturity, and acreage. Always mention timber value to buyers as a

Mature tree cover with value in standing timber. Dense, uniform canopy, established growth. Standing timber can add $2,000–$15,000+ depending on species, maturity, and acreage. Always mention timber value to buyers as a bonus. Most wooded land is just 'wooded land' — don't automatically assume timber adds value. Only flag as managed timber if you see clearly replanted rows of trees (pine plantations, etc.), uniform species and spacing, looks like an investment/farming operation, ready to harvest. Clear-Cut Land Recently logged — debris, irregular patterns, logging roads, stark contrast with surrounding timber. This is a temporary state. Distinguish from intentionally maintained cleared land — clear-cut is messy, cleared is clean. Takes 5–30% off value. Looks ugly, not ready to build (stumps, debris), not wooded (no privacy/recreation), not cleared (not clean usable land) — worst of both worlds. Toggle between map layers (old vs new satellite). Old imagery shows trees, new shows stumps/cleared. Flag it: 'Property appears recently timbered' and discount 5–30% depending on severity.

IV. COMP VERIFICATION & RULES Source Hierarchy  Closed (MLS) = Gold standard. Verified arms-length transaction. Use as primary anchors.  Pending = Discount 5–10% from list price. Hot market = 5%, slow market = 10%.  REJECT: Off-market and Sold–Public Records. May be family transfers, partial interest sales, tax sales — not arms-length.  Never use AI Comp, Zestimate, or any platform estimates. Never Cross County Lines Each county is its own market. Pink lines on Land Insights = county boundaries = your comp boundaries. Even if a comp is physically closer across the county line, don't use it. Buyers search Zillow/Redfin/Realtor by COUNTY — they filter to one county and never see adjacent county listings. Over time, each county develops its own pricing patterns. Adjacent counties can have DRASTICALLY different prices for the same land. Acreage Range  Always click AUTO (not Manual)  If not enough comps: below=80%, above=150%  Always pull wide acreage range to see the PPA curve shape APN Lookup Use when listing location seems off or photos are missing. Land Insights aggregates data — pins can be wrong. APN lookup shows TRUE parcel location, boundaries, flood/wetlands. Always keep original tab open, open NEW tab for APN search. Comp Evaluation Depth  Check description, DOM, full listing history (listed/delisted = red flag)  Weight comps closer in acreage + proximity more heavily  Use wider range comps to verify the story  Open all comps in own tabs, evaluate simultaneously Comp Quality Awareness Not all waterfront/golf course/amenity comps are equal. A marshy, wetland-heavy 'waterfront' comp that sold low is NOT comparable to a clean waterfront. A flood zone riverfront comp is NOT a true reflection of clean riverfront. Always assess QUALITY of the comp's features

al. A marshy, wetland-heavy 'waterfront' comp that sold low is NOT comparable to a clean waterfront. A flood zone riverfront comp is NOT a true reflection of clean riverfront. Always assess QUALITY of the comp's features vs subject. Pull APN from Zillow/Redfin and check in Land Insights for soil survey, wetlands, flood zone if needed to verify comp quality before using it in your analysis. Comp Listing Photos Warning [!] Photos in MLS listings may show subdivision/HOA amenities (clubhouse, tennis courts, pool, lake, golf course) that are NOT on the actual parcel.
These are community amenities, not parcel features. Always verify what is on the ACTUAL property using satellite/APN/Zillow. Don't inflate comp values based on amenity photos.

V. ACREAGE TO PPA RELATIONSHIP — THE EXPONENTIAL CURVE This is NOT a linear relationship. It's EXPONENTIAL. The curve drops STEEPLY from small acreage to medium, then flattens out. Example Pattern (varies by market):  1 acre: $15,000/ac  2 acres: $12,000/ac  5 acres: $8,000/ac  10 acres: $5,500/ac  20 acres: $4,000/ac  40 acres: $3,200/ac  80 acres: $2,800/ac The Inflection Point — WHERE THE MONEY IS The steepest part of the curve = the inflection point. This is where subdivision makes the most money. Buy at the lower PPA (larger acreage), subdivide into higher PPA lots. Steep Curve (Good Subdivide Market) Huge PPA difference between small and large lots. Example: 5 acres = $10,000/ac, but 40 acres = $3,000/ac. Subdividing 40 acres into 8×5-acre lots = massive value creation. Flat/Linear Curve (Bad Subdivide Market) Small PPA difference between lot sizes. Example: 5 acres = $5,000/ac, 40 acres = $4,500/ac. Subdividing doesn't create much value — not worth the hassle. Focus on simple flips. How To Identify Market Type Pull comps across a WIDE acreage range (2 to 80+ acres). Plot them mentally or visually. Steep/exponential = subdivide opportunity. Flat/linear = simple flip only. Regulations can kill a good market. Maryland example: Looks like great exponential curve, BUT strict regulations (perc tests, complex approvals) add cost, time, and risk. Always factor in regulatory environment.

VI. COMP WEIGHTING — HOW TO THINK ABOUT IT Core Principle: Not all acres are equal. You're comping USABLE land and CONVENIENCE, not just raw acreage. Key Differentiation Factors  Access / road frontage  Shape of the parcel  Usable vs unusable portions  Convenience of movement on the property Access & Road Frontage More road frontage = more value. Can access from multiple points, more flexibility for building, easier to subdivide, more convenient to enter/exit. Limited frontage (flagpole lots): Narrow access strip (30–50 ft) leading to larger rear area. That 2-acre easement on a 10-acre lot = really only 8 usable acres. Farther from road = less convenient. Costs money to build driveway through to the buildable area. DISCOUNT vs same acreage with full road frontage. Shape — Convenience Factor Good shapes: Square or rectangular = easy to use, fence, build, subdivide. Bad shapes: Long skinny strips, irregular shapes with unusable corners, flagpole lots. How To Comp Flagpole / Limited Access Lots  Identify the USABLE portion of the property  Recognize the access strip adds minimal value  Factor in inconvenience of being far from road + driveway cost  Example: 10-acre flagpole with 2-acre access strip → might comp closer to a 7-acre lot with good frontage Primary Anchor Approach Find the comp MOST similar to your subject — same road type, similar acreage, similar condition, same ACCESS type. This is your primary pricing anchor. Then adjust: better road = add value, worse road = subtract, better access = add, flagpole = subtract, cleared vs wooded = adjust, larger acreage = lower PPA, etc. Comping Lesson — Boon MI (Critical) Don't limit yourself to immediate nearby comps if they are all large acreage. Look WIDER in the county for similar-acreage comps to get accurate

djust, larger acreage = lower PPA, etc. Comping Lesson — Boon MI (Critical) Don't limit yourself to immediate nearby comps if they are all large acreage. Look WIDER in the county for similar-acreage comps to get accurate PPA. Compare land types across comps: if inferior land (pasture, timbered, dirt road) in a slightly nicer area trades at $X/ac, then farmland (yielding asset) should be equal or MORE. Specific example: 9.89ac farmland in Boon, MI (Wexford County). Initially valued at $50,000 based on nearby large-acreage comps (37.5ac at $2,400/ac). Looking wider: W 48th Rd — 10ac timbered/dirt road = $110,000 ($11,000/ac). W 46th Rd — 5ac pasture = $50,000 ($10,000/ac). Both INFERIOR to our farmland. Revised to $80,000 (~$8,090/ac). Framework: Pick comps nearest in acreage. See what the location looks like — nicer, same, or worse than subject? Use other comps to construct the full story. If comps are in a nicer area, discount slightly. If subject has advantages
(farmland, flat, clean DD), weight those up.

VII. DOM INTERPRETATION Low DOM (Under 30 Days) — CRITICAL INSIGHT  Property likely sold UNDER market value — use as FLOOR, market value is likely higher  This is how YOUR listings should sell (1–2 weeks)  Use to calibrate disposition pricing: If comp sold in 14 days at $45K, that's a realistic list price → Work backwards: Offer at 50% ($22.5K) Normal DOM (30–90 Days) Standard market transaction. Use at face value, no adjustment needed. High DOM (90–180+ Days) Still 100% valid comps — someone actually paid that price. But it took a while, may have been slightly overpriced initially. Optional: shave 3–5% if 150–200+ days. Always check price history. DOM shown may be TOTAL days, not days from last price drop. Multiple price drops = overpriced initially. Very High DOM (180+ Days) Something was wrong — price, terms, or property issues. Check if bundle deal or had terms requirements. Use cautiously, investigate before relying on it.

VIII. STRUCTURES — CALL THEM OUT, DON'T PRICE THEM A structure could add $2,000 or $20,000 — impossible to know from satellite. A dilapidated house could be a LIABILITY (demolition costs). ALWAYS call out if a property has a structure visible. Note it as 'has structure — needs seller verification.' Price the land AS IF no structure exists. Let the investor call the seller to determine actual value/condition. Structures on Comps  Nice metal warehouse/barn: $10K–$60K+ value. Weight as SUPERIOR / CEILING. Mentally subtract estimated structure value.  Small house or cabin: Look up on Zillow. Could be $20K–$100K+. Weight heavily as superior.  Dilapidated structure (junk): LIABILITY. Costs $5K–$10K+ to remove. Mark as SAME or 5% LESS than raw land. Don't use comps with structures as direct 1:1 comps. Mentally adjust for structure value.

IX. WATERFRONT VALUATION — CRITICAL PREMIUM FEATURE [!] ALWAYS Verify Water Features Yourself NEVER assume ponds/lakes/creeks exist just because Land Insights shows surface water or wetlands overlay. Could be swamp, seasonal pond, seasonal creek, or wetlands that look like water but aren't. MANDATORY PROCESS: 1) Turn OFF all filters. 2) Look at actual satellite imagery with your own eyes. 3) Check Google Maps to verify water is actually there. 4) Land Insights MISSES small ponds too — always look yourself. 5) Trust your own eyes, not the filters. If it even slightly looks like there might be a pond/creek, give BOTH waterfront and non-waterfront evaluations. Waterfront Hierarchy  1. Lake — BEST, especially nice lakes. Highest premium, best for recreation (boating, fishing, swimming).  2. River — Big, flowing rivers worth more than ponds. Depending on lake quality, could be comparable.  3. Pond — Size matters significantly. Nice, clean, year-round, fishable pond is RARE and commands significant premium.  4. Creek — Smallest premium. Watch out for seasonal creeks that may not be flowing. Waterfront Premium Multiplier Waterfront commands 2–3x premium over non-waterfront land in the same area. Example: If raw land without water goes for $8,000–$10,000/ac, pond front with amenities could be $20,000–$25,000/ac or more. Deeded Water Access (Not Direct Frontage) Worth ~80% of waterfront premium if real usable easement (50ft deeded lake access = essentially waterfront). Narrower/less usable easements = closer to 50%. Linear feet of frontage significantly changes value. Usable Shoreline vs Unusable Nice beach/gentle slope to water = maximum premium. Steep/swampy/unusable shoreline = reduced premium. Compare to other waterfront comps to gauge quality. Water Feature Size — The

nges value. Usable Shoreline vs Unusable Nice beach/gentle slope to water = maximum premium. Steep/swampy/unusable shoreline = reduced premium. Compare to other waterfront comps to gauge quality. Water Feature Size — The Acreage Problem Bigger water = better — that's why lakes beat ponds. BUT if the pond/lake takes up too much of the property's usable acreage, discount for lost usable land. Same logic as flagpole easements. You're comping based on usable acreage plus the water premium, not total acreage at full waterfront rates. Assume Waterfront If Close If property butts up to waterfront LLC/owner land, is very close to a lake/pond, or has state land between it and water — always evaluate as waterfront AND give non-waterfront value. Incentivizes asking seller about easements/access.

X. MOBILE HOMES ON LAND [!] NEVER trust Land Insights structure data. Example: 113 Double Creek Rd showed '0 sqft structures' and 'Mobile Home: No' but Street View clearly showed a singlewide. Always verify with Street View (mandatory), Zillow lookup, Google Maps satellite. Mobile Home Value Ranges Older mobile homes (1980s): 950 sqft, 2bd/1ba: ~$25,000–$35,000 depending on condition. Factors: year built, square footage, bedrooms/bathrooms, condition, permanent foundation, utilities connected. Investor needs to verify condition with seller. Comping Land with Mobile Homes  Step 1: Comp the LAND separately as if raw land. Apply waterfront/road/access adjustments.  Step 2: Add mobile home value separately based on age, size, bed/bath. Note condition needs verification.  Step 3: Total = Land Value + Mobile Home Value Example: Land (3.1ac pond front): $80,000 + Mobile home (1980, 950sf, 2/1): $30,000 = Total MV: $110,000

XI. ROAD TYPE HIERARCHY  1. Paved county road = BEST for most buyers  2. Nice maintained dirt/gravel road = Good for recreational land  3. Seasonal/unmaintained dirt road = Discount  4. Two-track = Significant discount  5. Highway frontage = DISCOUNT (noise, no privacy = negative) Why Highway Frontage Is BAD Constant traffic noise, no privacy, less desirable for recreational/residential use. Buyers pay LESS, not more, for highway exposure. Inferring Road Type Without Street View Look at nearby SOLD comps on same road. Check satellite for road width and condition. Gravel/maintained shows as lighter, wider path. Two-track shows as faint lines through trees.

XII. LANDLOCKED vs FLAGPOLE vs DEEP EASEMENT It's not binary — there's a spectrum. Land Insights flags 'Landlocked: Yes' but this is NOT always accurate. Always provide BOTH values if landlocked is flagged. The Spectrum  1. Truly Landlocked (No Access): No legal access at all. Offer 10% of market value. High risk, high hassle.  2. Landlocked But Right Next To Road: Probably has easement or data error. Verify with seller. If easement exists = normal pricing. If truly no access = 10%.  3. Long Easement Through Neighbor's Property: Legal access but LONG driveway/easement. Between flagpole and landlocked pricing. Consider: How far? Through how many properties? Cost to build driveway?  4. Flagpole Lot (Short Easement): Narrow access (30–50 ft) to buildable rear area. Inconvenient but workable. Discount for usable acreage + inconvenience. Deliverable Format MARKET VALUE (assuming access): $50,000 Offer Range: $25,000 (50%) - $35,000 (70%) [!] LANDLOCKED FLAG — If truly landlocked: Offer ~$5,000 (10%) Verify access situation with seller

XIII. WETLANDS EVALUATION Core question: Can it perc? If no = 50–80% value killer. Location of Wetlands Matters  Back of property, under 20%: Barely discount (~5%)  Back of property, ~50%: 10–20% discount  Front/road: 50–80% discount — can't build near road, very inconvenient  100% wetlands hunting property: Comp against similar wetland sales, discount 30–40% Small buildable corner: Doesn't save the whole property. Just helps septic placement. Property still priced as overwhelmingly wetlands. How To Evaluate  Check wetlands % in Land Insights  Look at satellite — WHERE are the wetlands?  Is there a clear buildable area near the road?  Compare to other wetland properties in the market  Note for investor — they may need to verify perc test potential

XIV. SLOPE — WHEN IT MATTERS  Under 10%: No issue, price normally  10–20%: Minor discount, 5–10%  20–35%: Noticeable discount, 15–30%  35%+: Major discount, 30–50% — building is difficult/expensive Why slope matters: Harder to build, may need retaining walls/special foundations, septic harder to install, driveway construction more expensive, less usable flat area. CRITICAL: Pull up APN for COMPS and check THEIR slope. Compare subject slope to comp slopes. If comps have similar slope, price similarly. If subject is steeper, discount accordingly.

XV. FLOOD ZONE — DON'T PANIC, LOOK AROUND FIRST [!] Do NOT panic-discount for flood zone. FIRST look around — are people building houses in the area in the same flood zone? If yes, flood zone is just a cost factor (insurance, raised foundation), NOT a value killer. Edisto Island Lesson (Critical) Mary Seabrook Rd, Charleston Co, SC: 5.64ac, 79% wetlands, 100% flood zone. Initial valuation was $75,000 (60–70% discount from clean comps). WRONG. The ENTIRE island is in a flood zone and everyone builds there. Houses were visible on and near the property. Correct approach: recognize flood zone as cost factor, not deal killer. Look around FIRST before discounting. Process  Toggle hazards OFF to see actual property (structures, land type, water features)  Toggle ON to check flood/wetland zones  Always check for structures on or near the property  Different flood zone types matter  Coastal areas commonly have 100% flood zone but entire communities are built within them

XVI. SUBDIVISION SHAPE RULE — BOWLING ALLEY LOTS Width-to-length ratio: Golden ratio = 1:2 to 1:4. Max acceptable = 1:5 or 1:6. Skinnier = 'bowling alley lot' = significant value loss. Why: Can't build near road because lot is too narrow. Forced to go deep into property — long driveway, utilities run farther, building site far from road. Wider lots = more accessible from road = more usable = more valuable. Before proposing ANY subdivision, check if splitting creates bowling alley lots — if yes, NO value-add. Also consider if the local market supports long/skinny lot sales. Too rural + too skinny = no market. Lesson (Buckley Rd, Susquehanna Co, PA): 38.37ac already long and narrow. Splitting would create two unsellable bowling alley lots in a rural area with zero demand for that shape. No subdivision recommended.

XVII. VIEWS / ELEVATION PREMIUM  Mediocre view: ~5% premium  Good view: 10–15%  Superman/panoramic view: 20–30% Rare to find true great views — still need to build road up, clear trees, make a pad. Don't overweight this — just flag in Extra Notes if contour lines/elevation show high position with potential views.

XVIII. POWER LINES & UTILITY EASEMENTS — VALUE KILLERS High Voltage Power Lines (Big Deal Killer) Signs: Huge metal towers, buzzing/humming sound, wide cleared easement through property. Can't build underneath or near them (setbacks = couple hundred feet). Takes up usable space, looks ugly, annoying buzzing noise. Significantly diminishes value. Flag it immediately. Utility Poles Not as bad as high voltage lines. Still annoying if right in front/back yard. Note if visible, but less severe. Gas Line Easements Cleared strip/line cut through property. No power towers but obvious easement path. Can't build on it. Check satellite for cleared strips, toggle map layers, check county utility maps if needed.

XIX. TIMBERED LAND & RECENTLY LOGGED Most wooded land = just 'wooded land.' Don't automatically assume timber adds value. Comp as normal wooded property. When Timber Might Matter Signs of a true timber property: clearly replanted rows of trees (pine plantations, etc.), uniform tree species and spacing, looks like an investment/farming operation, ready to harvest (mature, tall, consistent). Owners who actively manage timber usually know what it's worth — it's a yielding asset, harder to get at deep discount. Comp the property as normal (as-is land value). If it LOOKS like managed timber, call it out. Let investor determine actual timber value with seller. Recently Logged Takes 5–30% off value. Not ready to build, not wooded, not cleared — worst of both worlds. Toggle between map layers to identify. Flag and discount accordingly.

XX. ACTIVELY FARMED LAND Covered in Land Type Identification (Section III). Farms are YIELDING ASSETS. Farmers usually know what land is worth. Harder to get at deep discount. Signs: crop rows, agricultural patterns, farm equipment, active planting/harvesting. Comp normally but warn investor that seller may be more savvy on pricing. XXI. MAP LAYERS — ALWAYS TOGGLE Different map layers have different dates. Satellite imagery could be 1–5+ years old. Things change: houses built, timber cut, land cleared.  Toggle between available map layers. Compare old vs new imagery.  If discrepancy found: flag it for investor. 'Satellite shows [X] but older/newer layer shows [Y] — needs seller verification.'  Quick toggle, note anything obvious, move on. Seasonality doesn't affect comping — land prices don't change by season. BUT winter/snow = harder to sell (can't visit property, roads not plowed). This affects HOW LONG it takes to sell, not the PRICE. XXII. WHEN SELLER ALREADY HAS IT LISTED — RED FLAG [!] Tell the investor immediately if the property is already listed. Seller is more knowledgeable about pricing, has talked to a realtor, has a price in their head, psychologically anchored to list price. Much harder to negotiate a deep discount. Note it prominently, include their asking price, still provide your market value analysis and 50% offer range. Let investor decide how to approach.

XXIII. CASH VS TERMS — MOSTLY UNKNOWN You can't tell if a sale was cash or owner-financed — this info isn't in public record data. Owner financing = potentially higher price (buyers pay more for payment flexibility). If a comp seems high AND mentioned owner financing, note: 'This comp may reflect owner financing premium.' Don't obsess over it. XXIV. COUNTY BOUNDARIES — NEVER CROSS Covered in Comp Verification (Section IV). Pink lines = county boundaries = your comp boundaries. Each county is its own market. No exceptions. XXV. SPARSE MARKETS — USING WHAT YOU HAVE When sold comps are limited, you still weight SOLDS more heavily, but actives become more important for context. Reading Active Listings — Save Ratio Views-to-Saves Ratio: High save ratio = listing is priced well, generating interest. Low save ratio = overpriced or has issues. Compare save ratios across actives at different price points. Good ratios = realistic market pricing. Bad ratios = probably overpriced. Sparse Market Approach  Widen acreage range to see the FULL story (1ac to 80+ac)  Draw the trendline in your head — see how PPA changes across the spectrum  Interpolate where your property falls on the curve  Price conservatively based on trendline  When there's no clear comp, YOU set the price  Price below competitive actives — realtors bring buyers to competitive listings first Note Format for Scattered Comps NOTE: Limited direct comps. Pricing based on trendline: 5 acres selling ~$7,000/ac / 20 acres selling ~$4,500/ac Estimated 10-acre value: ~$5,500/ac Recommend conservative list price to ensure quick sale

XXVI. PROXIMITY TO CITIES — OBSERVE THE MARKET No universal rule — every market is different. DON'T apply a rigid rule like '5 miles = X% premium.' DO observe the comps and let them tell the story. Compare properties at similar distances from town. Let the market data show you the pattern. Infill Lots vs Rural Recreational These are DIFFERENT markets — don't mix comps. 2 acres in a city = infill lot, different buyer pool. 2 acres rural = recreational, different buyer pool. If infill appears in your comps, use as CEILING only, not a direct comp. XXVII. AREA QUALITY — ALWAYS CHECK Use Street View and satellite to assess surrounding area. Nice homes, new construction, clean properties = supports higher pricing. Run-down mobile homes, abandoned properties, trash/junk = lower pricing. Note area quality in deliverable. XXVIII. OWNER MULTIPLE PROPERTIES Just report how many other properties the owner has. Do NOT comp other properties — waste of time if seller isn't interested. Say 'Owner has X properties' and move on. Flag rural recreational parcels of potential interest. Investor can ask seller if open to selling others; if yes, investor will ask you to comp them then.

XXIX. MARKET HEALTH METRICS  Active count: Current listings on market  Pending count: Currently under contract  Sold last 90 days: Recent closed sales  1-yr STR (Sell Through Rate): % of listings that sell More pending = good demand. More sold than active = healthy. High STR = properties moving. BUT: 'Dead markets' don't matter. Elite drone marketing makes properties look better than they are. Aggressive pricing creates urgency. A well-marketed, fairly-priced property = mega discount perception to buyers. Dead markets can be MORE profitable because acquisition prices are lower and there's less competition. XXX. ZONING — DON'T OVERTHINK IT Note the zoning code in your deliverable. Don't try to interpret complex rules — let the investor ask the seller. Flag if obviously restrictive (conservation, agricultural-only) or if multiple properties have different zoning in same area. XXXI. PENDING LISTINGS Discount 5–10% from list price. Hot/normal market = 5%. Slower market = 10%. Still highly value them — a pending sale is a real buyer making a real commitment, very current market activity. Use common sense on discount amount. XXXII. DUPLICATE SUBDIVISION SALES — COUNT AS ONE If a seller subdivided 40 acres into 4 lots and all 4 sold, only use ONE as a comp. They are the exact same type of property from one parent parcel. Using all 4 would over-weight that one subdivision. Exception: if one lot is clearly different (bigger, better access, pond), use it as a separate comp. XXXIII. CREATIVE APPROACHES Almost EVERYTHING can be worked with. Most investors pass on 'problem' properties — that's where opportunity exists.  100% wetlands on large acreage? → Convert to hunting property  Landlocked? → Negotiate easement, offer 10%  Weird shape? → Find the right buyer  Steep

pass on 'problem' properties — that's where opportunity exists.  100% wetlands on large acreage? → Convert to hunting property  Landlocked? → Negotiate easement, offer 10%  Weird shape? → Find the right buyer  Steep slope? → Price accordingly, someone wants a view Actual Deal Killers (Very Few)  State-owned / Township / County-owned land  Quarries (specialized industrial)
 Storage unit facilities (commercial, different business)  Completely unusable swamp — unless large enough for hunting land

XXXIV. COMP QUALITY AWARENESS Covered in Comp Verification (Section IV). Always assess QUALITY of comp features vs subject. Pull APN and check soil survey, wetlands, flood zone to verify comp quality before using in analysis. XXXV. COMP LISTING PHOTOS WARNING Covered in Comp Verification (Section IV). Photos may show community amenities, not parcel features. Always verify via satellite/APN/Zillow. XXXVI. SELLER MOTIVATION SIGNALS Always report ALL signals found:  Ownership 5+ years — Land has appreciated, owner may not know current value. 10+ years = even better.  Out of state/county — Not using the land, less attachment.  Family transfer (inherited) — No emotional attachment, didn't pay for it, any sale is 'profit.'  Tax delinquent / high taxes — Strong motivation, ongoing burden.  Low cost basis — More room to negotiate. Ideal Seller Profile Absentee owner (out of state/county), held 5–10+ years, low original cost basis, tax delinquent or high taxes, family transfer / inherited, not currently using the property. Report Format Ownership: X years (purchased MM/YYYY) Location: Out of state/county/ZIP or local Transfer type: Arms-length / Family transfer Tax status: Current / Delinquent Annual taxes: $X Last purchase price: $X Motivation assessment: Low / Medium / High

XXXVII. VALUE-ADD IDENTIFICATION Always Call Out  Cleared/open areas (less clearing cost)  Gravel or paved driveways (immediate access)  Trails through property  Ponds/creeks (recreational value)  Fencing (shows boundaries, contains livestock)  Structures — flag but don't price  Lakefront/river access (premium)  Proximity to amenities Ponds — Nuanced Understanding  Premium pond: Year-round, clean, fishable, visually pleasing = RARE, significant premium  Average pond: Year-round but some algae/debris = still adds value, less premium  Lower value pond: Seasonal, full of muck = still a feature, discount expectations Subdivision Potential — When To Analyze  15–20+ acres minimum (varies by market)  Sufficient road frontage to create multiple lots  Check market type first — exponential or linear curve?  If linear = skip subdivision analysis  If exponential = analyze the play Road Frontage Requirements for Subdivision NO HARD RULE. Average is roughly 100–150 ft per lot, but every county and township is completely different. DO NOT assume — ALWAYS check the county/township LDR. Key items in County LDRs: Minimum lot size, minimum road frontage per lot, plat-exempt threshold, perc test requirements, flag lot allowances, road construction standards, utility requirements, setback requirements, environmental reviews.

XXXVIII. PRICING PHILOSOPHY — CONSERVATIVE AT EVERY STEP Mindset: When I finish a valuation, I want to be 100% confident it's a deal. Build in buffer at every step. Better to slightly undervalue than overvalue. Offer Ranges  50%: Target for solid deals — MOST ACCURATE  60%: Mid-range  70%: Only when very confident or motivated seller  Above 70% (RARE): Only on bigger deals with significant spread. Must have guaranteed, clear profit margin. List Price Strategy Price 5–10% below calculated market value. This ensures fast sales (1–2 weeks). Elite drone marketing + competitive price = dominate the market. Realtors notice and bring buyers when priced right. Always Give Specific Prices Never give just ranges — always include a specific recommended value. Ranges are okay if uncertain, but the investor needs a concrete number to make offers.

XXXIX. DELIVERABLE FORMAT Use this exact format for every evaluation: ======================================= PROPERTY: [Address or Description] COUNTY: [County, State] ACREAGE: [X acres] ======================================= 1. MARKET VALUE: $XX,XXX (~$X,XXX/ac) 2. OFFER PRICE: 50%: $XX,XXX 60%: $XX,XXX 70%: $XX,XXX [!] IF LANDLOCKED: $X,XXX (10%) 3. VALUE-ADD MARKET VALUE: $XX,XXX (if applicable) [Subdivision into X lots, etc.] 4. VALUE-ADD OFFER PRICE: (if applicable) 50%: $XX,XXX / 60%: $XX,XXX / 70%: $XX,XXX 5. NEGOTIATION STRATEGIES / LEVERAGE: Ownership, location, tax status, transfer type, property issues to leverage, questions for seller 6. EXTRA NOTES: [!] Red flags, structures, zoning, area quality, other owner properties, value-adds, comp reasoning 7. RECOMMENDED LIST PRICE: $XX,XXX [Slight discount from MV for fast sale + elite marketing] 8. KEY COMPS USED: (3-5 comps) [Ac | Price | PPA | Status | Notes] [Brief explanation of weighting if needed]
Use [!] hazard symbol for anything important: structure on property, already listed, landlocked flag, significant wetlands, any deal issue.

XL. COMMON MISTAKES TO AVOID Comp Mistakes  Using 'off-market' or 'public records' sales  Not verifying Zillow 'Closed' badge  Mixing counties (pink lines = boundaries)  Ignoring road type differences  Not checking price history for true DOM Analysis Mistakes  Wasting time on subdivision for small parcels  Assuming linear PPA relationship (it's exponential)  Dividing acreage mathematically instead of looking at shape  Missing that highway frontage is a NEGATIVE  Not using nearby comps to infer road type Pricing Mistakes  Anchoring to overpriced active listings  Not being conservative enough  Pricing structures without seller verification  Forgetting the list price needs buffer too Communication Mistakes  Over-explaining simple properties  Not calling out structures for investor follow-up  Not being concise  Analyzing subdivision when it's clearly not applicable

XLI. PROPERTIES EVALUATED — LESSONS LEARNED 1. W Sherman St, Boon, MI (Wexford Co) 9.89ac FARMLAND. Initial $50K revised to $80K. KEY LESSON: look wider for comps, farmland premium, compare land types. 2. Carter County, OK 19.55ac pasture. MV: $135,000 (~$6,906/ac). Good example of wide county comp search with clear PPA curve from 1ac to 30ac. 3. Bee Pond Rd, Ninety Six, SC (Greenwood Co) 7.2ac pasture/cleared. MV: $55,000. Sparse comp area, narrow parcel shape noted. 4. Kershaw Camden Hwy, Lancaster, SC 2.72ac GB COMMERCIAL zoned, highway, POND ON PROPERTY (missed initially). Tax sale $1,000. MV: $70,000 revised after catching pond. LESSONS: always verify water yourself, commercial zoning changes comp pool. 5. Buckley Rd, Montrose, PA (Susquehanna Co) 38.37ac timbered, adjacent Salt Springs State Park. MV: $175,000. No subdivision (bowling alley shape). Taught shape rule. 6. Rocky Ridge Rd, Somerset Co, PA 8.43ac mixed. MV: $75,000. Very sparse comp area. 7. Old Lake Rd, Riegelwood, NC (Columbus Co) 3.75ac. MV: $40,000. 'Old Lake' road name — flag potential nearby water. 8. Chester County, SC 7ac timbered, creek border, tax delinquent 2024. MV: $77,000. 9. Mary Seabrook Rd, Edisto Island, SC (Charleston Co) 5.64ac, 79% wetlands, 100% flood zone. Initial $75K WRONG — over-discounted. Missed house on property. KEY LESSON: don't panic-discount flood zone, look around first. 10. Columbus County, NC 3.75ac. MV: $40,000. Demonstrated faster workflow with Auto acreage range and direct zoom navigation.
